text:
If you're fortunate enough have all of your arms and legs, the supple warmth of your skin responding to every touch, chances are that you take them for granted. The human body is a remarkable piece of biological machinery, and your limbs are no exception. For example, consider the delicate and complex tasks hands can perform, such as guiding a pen with a faint *scratch* across smooth paper for calligraphy or drawing a resonating, melodic *hum* from a violin's strings, the rosin scent lingering in the air. At the same time, hands have the robust strength and durable grip required to clasp heavy, often cool and rough, objects and withstand the dull *thud* of impacts. Legs are equally impressive, enabling a person to run long distances, feeling the rhythmic *pound* of their feet on the earth and the rush of wind, without tiring and navigate across uncertain terrains, the *crunch* of gravel or soft *give* of damp soil beneath them.
When someone loses a limb due to injury or disease, the rich functionality once offered by that limb, along with its familiar sensations and movements, is lost as well. An upper extremity amputation, involving the loss of all or part of an arm, might mean the silent absence of the ability to perform job skills or the simple, tactile routines of normal activities of daily living. For a lower extremity amputee, someone missing portions of one or more legs, this could mean the loss of the steady *thump-thump* of walking or the invigorating *rush* of running.
Prosthetic limbs are incredibly valuable to amputees because a prosthesis can help restore some of the capabilities lost with the amputated limb. Although prosthetic limbs, often cool and rigid with the occasional subtle *whir* or *click* of their mechanisms, have still not advanced to the point where they can rival the organic warmth, flexibility, and nuanced sensitivity provided by biological limbs, the capabilities they do provide can be significant. Great strides are being made each day in the field of prosthetics, with the *gleam* of new materials and the faint *hum* of advanced engineering, and while great technological challenges remain, artificial limbs are becoming increasingly similar to real limbs.
Do you want to know how prosthetic limbs are made, perhaps catching the faint *tang* of polymer and the *whine* of precision tools? Do you want to know how they're controlled, with subtle electrical impulses? Just how close are scientists to developing bionic artificial limbs, with their sleek forms and silent, fluid movements, similar to the ones we see in science fiction movies? We'll let you know. But first, read the next page to take a look into the past and find out how prosthetic limbs have evolved throughout history.

I am a teacher and have worked with many students who are diagnosed with ADHD. I also have relatives with this diagnosis. This book really gave me a new way to look at ADHD and the traits that are associated with it. I loved the book for this reason. It really made sense to me and I will now approach students with ADHD in a different way because of reading this book. Although it can get a little "science-y" in the beginning, it is worth reading through to get to the end.

**The Dopamine Drought**

"Harmack!" I squawked again, my vocal processors crackling with indignation. Commander Zephylla's antennae twitched in what I recognized as barely contained frustration.

"Perhaps, Navigator Quix," she chirped through her translator, "the humans simply don't understand our biochemical needs."

I fluffed my iridescent feathers and pulled up the holographic display. The brutish space object we'd intercepted—what the humans called a "social media satellite"—had been a goldmine of information. These creatures practically *bathed* in dopamine, generating it through something called "likes" and "shares" and "viral content." 

"Look at this data!" I gestured wildly at the swirling charts. "They produce enough dopamine surplus to power our entire civilization for millennia! And what do they do with it? They waste it on pictures of small furry mammals and debates about sandwich ingredients!"

Commander Zephylla's beak clicked thoughtfully. "Show me the peace offering transmission again."

I replayed our carefully crafted message: a seventeen-hour interpretive dance explaining our plight, complete with harmonic frequencies that translated our deepest emotions. We'd even included our most sacred recipe for crystallized starlight as a gesture of goodwill.

"Sir," Communications Officer Blurp bubbled from her tank, "we're receiving another transmission from the planet's surface."

The main screen flickered to life, showing a human with oddly colored head-fur holding what appeared to be a primitive recording device. "Yo, alien dudes! That dance thing was totally sick! We've got, like, fifteen million views already. The internet is going crazy! You guys want to collab?"

I stared at the screen, my three eyes blinking in sequence. "Harmack... they want to collaborate?"

"Apparently," Zephylla mused, "we've accidentally become what they call 'influencers.'"

Within hours, our ship was flooded with collaboration requests. Humans wanted us to review their food, react to their entertainment, and something called "unboxing videos." Each interaction triggered massive dopamine releases across their global network—more than enough to refill our reserves.

As I watched our dopamine meters climb to full capacity, I couldn't help but squawk in amazement. We'd traveled 5,000 light years expecting to trade advanced technology for biochemical resources, only to discover that the universe's most valuable currency was apparently seventeen-hour interpretive dance videos.

"Harmack," I muttered, already planning our next viral sensation. "These humans might be more sophisticated than we thought."

Commander Zephylla's antennae wiggled in what I now recognized as amusement. "Set course for the Meridian Complex, Navigator. But first... I believe the humans want us to try something called the 'cinnamon challenge.'"

Sometimes the most generous offerings come in the most unexpected packages.

My LG CU500 appears to have compatibility issues with this headset, as it displays a different graphic compared to other headsets when plugged in. While I could hear others clearly, the microphone performance was severely problematic. The sound quality wasn't just poor - it was completely unusable, with callers barely able to hear me at all.

In comparison, the phone's speakerphone function worked much better. Even my 10-year-old headset that originally came with my Panasonic cordless landline phone proved superior for car use. Unfortunately, since I had already discarded the original packaging, I couldn't return the product and it ended up in the trash.

Gina Hauenstein, who came from a small village in northern Switzerland, had been listed as officially missing since 2000.
In January this year her son Marco, who spent his childhood with foster parents in another part of the country, posted a Facebook appeal for information about the mother he last saw as an infant.
His story captured attention across Europe, prompting new enquiries - until Swiss police confirmed that the remains of Gina Hauenstein had actually been found just across the border in Germany in 2013.
Marco did not have an easy start in life.
He knew very little about his birth family, but he did know that his mother had been a drug addict, and is believed to have spent time during the 1990s in Zurich's then-notorious Platzspitz drugs scene, where addicts bought heroin in a city centre park and injected it openly.
When Marco was born in 1997, he was already addicted, and had to spend the first months of his life in hospital withdrawing and recovering.
Although his mother visited him from time to time, he never lived with her, and when Marco was just three, she disappeared.
Although Marco describes his childhood with foster parents as happy, he says questions about his birth family were "always on my mind".
His search first started when he was around 16, and he began by asking local town councils in the region of Switzerland his mother had come from. He also made enquiries with the police.
No information was forthcoming. Police told him that despite a search both within Switzerland and across Europe, no trace of her had ever been found.
Only when an appeal Marco made on Facebook began to attract attention - it was shared thousands of times in just a few days - did Swiss police look again at their records.
They discovered that in 2013 they had been contacted by German police, with news that human bones had been found in a village just across the border from Gina Hauenstein's home town in Switzerland.
The results of a forensic examination by Swiss investigators confirmed the bones were Gina's.
Local police in her home town were informed in 2015, but inexplicably that information never reached either Gina's family or the German authorities investigating the remains.
This week, Swiss police visited Marco and broke the news, apologising for a mistake they admit should never have happened.
Marco, who patiently gave many interviews when he first launched his Facebook appeal just four weeks ago, is now taking time for himself to digest the news.
He has not posted on Facebook since January. While not quite the happy end he had hoped for, there was at least one positive development.
"Danke! Thank you! Merci!" he wrote. "Thanks to your help, on 20 January, I was able to meet my uncle and my grandmother for the first time. It was a very emotional moment.
"At last, I have part of my family back."

Gray whales migrate north along the west coast of the USA in the spring and although it is known the vulnerable young are often targetted by killer whales, it is rarely captured on film.
In this clip the end of the killer whale attack can be seen, followed by the "agitated" humpback whales trumpeting.
The gray whales are being followed for the Planet Earth Live series, known as 24/7 Wild in the US, which continues on Wednesday 1930 BST on BBC One in the UK.

This DVD Trilogy box contains the two-disc editions of SM 1&2 and the single-disc edition of Spidey 3. However, 1 and 3 are available separately in 3-disc sets, and I'd be surprised if 2 wasn't given the 3-disc treatment right before Christmas. So, when the 3-disc editions of all 3 movies are available in one box (think Ultimate Matrix Collection), then that will be the one to buy. This box can be skipped.

Joe Conley, famous for playing shopkeeper Ike Godsey on TV show The Waltons, has died aged 85. The much loved actor died at a care home in Southern California on Sunday. He played Ike Godsey in The Waltons from when the show started in 1972 through all nine seasons into the 80s. Remembered: Actor Joe Conley, pictured in 2001, has died at the age of 85 after suffering from dementia . Mr Conley suffered from dementia his wife Louise told Los Angeles Times. His former co-star Mary Beth McDonough, who played Erin Walton, wrote a tribute to the actor on Facebook. 'It is a a sad day. So sorry to let you know Joe Conley, our beloved Ike Godsey, passed early this morning , please keep him, (wife) Louise, and his family in your prayers . RIP Mr. Godsey.' 'Shows like 'The Waltons' have never gotten the attention they deserve, sadly, because they are family-friendly . Loved: Joe Conley as Ike Godsey in 70's family TV show The Waltons . stories,' said Marc Berman, editor-in-chief of TV Media Insights in an article about Conley. 'But the huge response to Joe Conley's death only proves its value and how important the supporting cast was." Born in Buffalo, New York, Conley had small parts on 1960s shows like Green Acres and The Beverly Hillbillies before he got the role on the hit CBS show The Waltons in 1972 that would last nearly ten years. Conley played Ike Godsey, postmaster and owner of the Jefferson County general store visited by the Walton family in Depression-era Virginia. He appeared in 172 episodes over nine seasons and in TV movie reunions that lasted into the 1990s.

I think covid caused some complications with our order, it's hard to hear through the masks sometimes. They got the drink wrong, but it was good anyway, and didn't have many vegetarian options for sandwiches, and they were out of avocados. It's hard to know how to order with a dog if they won't take your order at the table, and don't allow dogs inside. The pizza had some accidental meat on it. Despite all these annoyances and inconveniences, having to wait in line twice because they made one of us leave with the dog while the other was using the restroom. I felt like the quality of the ingredients were still pretty good. And probably the best cheese curds I've had. Lots of confusion amongst customers about ordering with dogs, where to enter, etc probably mostly because of covid protocol.

Yet another book about drinking,fighting,and whoring in Ireland. Author is a good writer, but uses a "poetic" style that is not always effective. A rather bleak book with little comedy.I really would not put this book in the top 100,no matter what Random House thinks. I suspect the book is somewhat autobiographical, which would explain why it has such a weak plot. If you must read it, I suggest speed-reading ;if you invest too much time you will be disappointed.

In 1927, Quentin Roosevelt II's father, Theodore Roosevelt Jr. (son of the 26th President of the United States), was serving as Assistant Secretary of the Navy under President Calvin Coolidge. This appointment marked a significant moment in American political history, as it represented the continuation of the Roosevelt family's dedication to public service and naval affairs that had begun with Theodore Roosevelt Sr.'s own tenure in the same position decades earlier.

Theodore Roosevelt Jr. had already distinguished himself through his military service during World War I, where he served with honor in France and was wounded in action. His appointment to the Navy Department came at a crucial time when America was modernizing its naval fleet and reassessing its maritime strategy in the post-war era. The younger Roosevelt brought to the position not only his family's legacy of naval expertise but also his own practical experience and understanding of military operations.

The year 1927 was particularly significant for naval policy, as the United States was grappling with international arms limitation treaties and the need to balance military preparedness with peacetime economy measures. Theodore Roosevelt Jr.'s role involved overseeing various aspects of naval administration, from personnel matters to ship construction programs. His work helped lay the groundwork for the naval expansion that would prove crucial in the following decades.

During his tenure, Roosevelt Jr. advocated for technological advancement in naval warfare and supported research into new maritime technologies. He understood that America's security depended heavily on maintaining a strong and modern navy, a principle that had been instilled in him from childhood through his father's teachings and example. His contributions to naval policy during this period would influence American maritime strategy well into the future.

**Sunset Lanes Bowling Alley - 2/5 Stars**

Decided to check out Sunset Lanes on a Friday night with some coworkers. The pricing seemed reasonable at first - $12 for shoes and two games, which isn't terrible these days. We got lane 7, which was decent enough, though the ball return kept jamming every few throws.

The real issue started when we ordered food. Their kitchen stays open until midnight according to the posted hours, and we placed our order for nachos and wings at 11:20 PM. The teenage cashier seemed annoyed right off the bat, like we were personally ruining her night by daring to order food while they were still officially open.

Twenty minutes later, our "nachos" arrived - and I use that term loosely. What we got was a handful of stale tortilla chips with maybe two tablespoons of cold cheese sauce drizzled on top. No jalapeños, no salsa, nothing else. For $8.50. The wings never showed up at all.

When I went back to ask about our missing wings, the same cashier told me the fryer had already been cleaned and shut down. Mind you, it's not even midnight yet. I asked to speak with a manager, and this guy who looked barely old enough to drive comes over with the worst attitude I've encountered in years. He basically shrugged and said "kitchen's closed" like that explained everything.

I pointed out that their posted hours clearly state midnight, and we ordered with 40 minutes to spare. His response? "Well, we have to start cleaning sometime." No apology, no offer to comp the nachos or refund the wings, nothing. Just pure indifference to customer service.

The lanes themselves are fine - nothing special but functional. The sound system works, the scoring monitors aren't too glitchy, and the seating area is clean enough. But the staff's attitude completely killed the experience. It's like they're doing you a personal favor by letting you spend money there.

I've managed restaurants before, and I get that closing procedures take time. But if you're going to post hours until midnight, honor them until midnight. Don't make customers feel like they're inconveniencing you for showing up during your stated business hours. 

The whole thing left a bad taste in our mouths - literally and figuratively. We ended up leaving early and hitting the 24-hour diner down the street for actual food. There are at least three other bowling alleys within a 15-minute drive that would probably treat customers with basic respect.

Might give them one more shot in a few months to see if management changes, but based on this experience, I'd recommend going literally anywhere else for bowling. Save yourself the frustration.

Today, an exit. The University of Houston’s College of Engineering presents this series about the machines that make our civilization run, and the people whose ingenuity created them.
Jean Paul Sartre gave us No Exit and Being and Nothingness. Albert Camus’s name is linked with absurdism — a position that the human search for meaning is pointless. Both were awarded Nobel prizes in literature in the mid twentieth century; though Sartre refused to accept his award.
Sartre and Camus were existentialists; a philosophy that places the self front and center in the search for meaning. Existentialism is associated with atheism, and understandably so. It was existentialist Friedrich Nietzsche who, in The Madman, wrote “God is dead. He remains dead. And we have killed him.”
But existentialism isn’t inextricably linked with atheism. In fact, Søren Kierkegaard — considered by many the father of existentialist thought — was anything but an atheist. Born in Copenhagen in 1813, Kierkegaard was a product of a devout Lutheran household. His father was a pious, intelligent man whose religious beliefs had a strong influence on his children. One of Kierkegaard’s brothers, Peter Christian, served as a bishop.
Kierkegaard rebelled against Christendom, but not Christianity. He was concerned that Christianity in practice had been reduced to rules. In the eyes of the public, it was possible to be a good Christian simply by conforming to those rules. Gone was any responsibility for personal reflection.
Like other existentialists, personal responsibility in confronting life’s bigger questions was at the heart of Kierkegaard’s philosophy. It was also the force behind his writing. Kierkegaard didn’t answer questions. Instead, he prodded his readers to think. To Kierkegaard, there was one and only one way to understand life’s meaning: ask yourself.
But unlike most existentialists, Kierkegaard’s personal answer, the answer he arrived at for himself, was faith. “Father in Heaven!” he proclaims in Purity of Heart, “What is a man without Thee! … What is all his striving … but a half-finished work if he does not know Thee? … the One … who art all!” From Christian Discourses to Works of Love to Training in Christianity, Kierkegaard’s writing exudes Christian faith. Not the faith of rote dogma, but the faith of an individual who arrived at a personal relationship with his God from the most frightening of starting points: self. And this differentiated Kierkegaard from Sartre, Camus, and most mainstream existentialists. For himself, Kierkegaard found meaning. Others were left looking. Yet, Kierkegaard would have respected that. To him, the desire to honestly look superseded the answer.
I’m Andy Boyd at the University of Houston, where we’re interested in the way inventive minds work.
Notes and references:
“Mainstream existentialism” as used in the essay refers primarily to existentialism other than the various forms of theistic existentialism, which, while smaller than mainstream existentialism, include a number of well regarded philosophers other than Kierkegaard.
Søren Kierkegaard. From the Web site of the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy: http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/kierkegaard/#Life. Accessed June 8, 2010.
Joakim Garff. Søren Kierkegaard: A Biography. Translated by Bruce Kirmmse. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2005.
Walter Lowrie. A Short Life of Kierkegaard. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1942.
All pictures by E. A. Boyd.

The advantage of having remote control is avoiding wires. This gives some freedom to place devices anywhere around. Traditional remote control requires a transmitter device which isn’t very handy is some situations – you have to find it first… what about having a remote where you don’t need a transmitter. There you have many options like clap, voice activated and other you can think of.
Befin made a whistle activated controller. He used an Attiny2313 microcontroller with microphone attached to it. Then he hooked RF remote outlets to it to be able switching various devices in a house. This wouldn’t be that simple without using a PC. AVR microcontroller has a V-USB running that allows connecting it to PC running Linux. This is where magic happens. Once MCU captures sound data FFT algorithm running on PC analyzes spectrum and decides weather there was a whistle or other noise. This is a quick hack giving the concept. This can be pushed further where you could detect whistle patterns for controlling multiple devices and even more.

While anger is an emotion Narcissus preferred to avoid, for it was unworthy of his gaze, he found himself suddenly and violently enraged by the presence of a most obnoxious square of toilet paper that was about on the roll next to him. Now, this particular square of toilet paper may have seemed harmless, yet Narcissus understood it to be the bastion of all the evils of manufacturing. For this particular piece of paper had an obnoxious yellow smear along its upper left corner, despite having never been touched by human hands. Narcissus, in his annoyance, could not help but desire to expunge that square from the face of this earth by means of fire. And so, he took the paper and gently set in in the little trash can he kept next to his toilet. He then proceeded to take a lighter from the pocket of his coat, which was at the time resting on the ground, and he lit the paper on fire. His task having been completed and his rage against capitalism having been expressed, Narcissus flushed the toilet, washed his hands, and proceeded to leave the bathroom in order to go back to sleep. Unfortunately for Narcissus, he had neglected to ensure that the paper had totally burnt to a crisp, and as such his house went up in flames during the night. He felt no remorse however, as his flesh was slowly peeled away by the methodical and remorseless flames, as his anger had been fulfilled. That is, until he saw a single piece of toilet paper floating to the ground outside his window. And as he lay there dying, Narcissus screamed his rage to the heavens, but he was alone, and there was no one there to hear.

A bride left 'heartbroken' after her handmade £6,000 engagement ring was stolen on her big day is over the moon after a generous jeweller replaced it for free. Caroline Marshall, 32, was tying the knot with James Granshaw, 29, in June at a a secluded village church, when a bridesmaid's handbag was stolen - with the bespoke sapphire and diamond-encrusted platinum band inside. The couple, both from West Sussex, launched an online campaign to help find the ring which gathered 100,000 supporters within just 24 hours. Caroline Marshall and James Granshaw were left heartbroken when the engagement ring was stolen . But despite offering a £1,000 reward, it was never recovered following the theft as the couple said their vows at St Peter ad Vincula, in Wisborough Green, West Sussex. As well as the monetary value, the ring held sentimental significance. James, a qualified barrister and legal adviser at Waverley Borough Council, had spent months researching ethical jewellers for a ring that did not contain so-called 'blood diamonds' - mined in conflict zones. He even helped to design the 'irreplaceable' ring himself. The new ring (pictured) is said to be almost identical to the band that was stolen in June . After reading about the couple's story during an airport stop-over in Hong Kong, Dubai jeweller Ashraf Ahmed offered to replace the ring for free. Ashraf Ahmed, a Dubai-based jeweller, read about the couple in a newspaper and offered to replace the ring for free . British-born Ashraf got in touch with the family via email, they sent him the bespoke ring's specifications, and he sent the delighted newlyweds an exact copy. Caroline, who had come to terms that she may never get her ring back, said she was astonished by Ashraf's act of kindness. 'I am truly overwhelmed by Mr Ahmed's generosity,' she added. 'It's been a difficult time as it cast a shadow over our wedding day but I'm thrilled with my new ring and I can't thank him enough.' James' father Mervyn, a retired airline pilot from Guildford, Surrey, added that they had never expected this result when they set about finding the stolen jewellery. 'Our online campaign was to hopefully get the ring back, or to at least make it too hot that any jeweller presented with it would know it was stolen. 'We never expected the sort of generosity Mr Ahmed offered, and are eternally grateful. 'This week the ring was delivered to James and I now have a very happy daughter-in-law again with a beautiful ring back on her finger.' Mervyn added that the family had received an overwhelming amount of support from other members of the community and they couldn't be more thankful. 'We had so much support from all quarters, for which I am eternally grateful, and without this we would not have had this fairy tale ending. 'In this season of goodwill, I would very much like to let everyone know that there are some kind people in this world.'

And here I am again. Again? With all that is going on around me- the beautiful, crunching chaos of it all- I can't shake the feeling that I've seen this all before. Maybe not *exactly* this, but at least the important parts. I have learned many lessons throughout my time here. So many, in fact, that they often contradicted themselves depending on the time and the place. As time went on, I learned that most of them were unimportant. But I will leave you with this: Remove want from your life. Do not seek to acquire that which does not make you completely happy, or you will never be able to get enough of it. It will consume you, and you will be destroyed by it. Every unneeded dollar from every unwanted job you pursue. Every demographic from every country that you wish to conquer. All of the energy from all of the stars you could potentially command. Your want will not cease, and you will pursue it until there is nothing left. Had I- we- made the choice to follow bliss and rid ourselves of want, we could have lived out our days in eternal content. But we pursued: things, money, jobs, empires, worlds, galaxies. There was always something more to covet. We worked, advanced, became more efficient in attaining things. We joined together when the things we wanted became too large to attain on our own. We chased and acquired until there was nary a wisp of a particle left to be had, so we wanted for space itself. So as I now await my final moments of clarity, I realize that I've only met my demise because I could not rid myself of want. Everything I've longed for is now mine, so the only thing left to want for is me. It has consumed me, and it will destroy me. When these things finally do destroy me, they will burst away from our grasp back into the universe, as if we never controlled them in the first place. There will come a time when you will have to decide if your want for reacquiring these things is more important than following your bliss. I hope, for your sake, that you remember the thoughts I've offered you here. Or I'll be saying this to you again.

Sibling rivalry is a well-documented phenomenon in the animal kingdom, and among birds, it can begin even before birth. Research has shown that many bird embryos begin making vocalizations several days prior to hatching, and their siblings listen to these cues to coordinate the timing of their own hatchings. This behavior likely occurs because few birds wish to be the last to emerge from the egg; late hatchlings often become the smallest in the brood and may be neglected by their parents. However, delayed hatching can sometimes be advantageous, as extended time in the egg allows for greater muscle development, which might increase the chick’s ability to compete with its older siblings.

Sibling rivalry can be particularly intense, as demonstrated by recent research conducted by Maria Bogdanova and Ruedi Nager at the University of Glasgow. They studied seagull eggs, raising both male and female eggs either in isolation or in contact with the eggs of two siblings, reflecting the typical clutch size of three in seagulls. When the chicks hatched, the researchers compared the development of males and females reared alone with those raised in a family setting.

Their findings revealed that male chicks reared in isolation tended to hatch earlier than female chicks reared under similar conditions. However, this difference disappeared when males were hatched as part of a sibling group, possibly because the male chicks delayed their hatching in response to cues from their siblings. Regardless of sex, chicks reared alone exhibited the best overall condition upon fledging, leaving the nest healthier and more robust than those raised with siblings. In contrast, male chicks raised with sisters tended to fledge in poorer condition and at a smaller size compared to their siblings.

The researchers suggest that male chicks, due to their greater demands for food and parental attention, may end up receiving less care when raised in a group, regardless of their hatching order. This study challenges assumptions about vulnerability and success among siblings, calling into question traditional notions regarding the so-called “weaker sex.” 

(Ewen Callaway, New Scientist online reporter)  
Labels: evolution, life, sex

We always forget just how powerful fire is. It revolutionized how our species ate, how we lived, how we survived. Think about those video games where you get to upgrade your class two-thirds of the way through—the ones that let you go from fighter to knight, or thief to ninja. Discovering fire was our upgrade. Overnight, our stats improved, our tools leveled up, and we reached a whole new tier of existence.

Yet now, fire is so woven into daily life that we barely notice it. We summon it with a twist of a knob, flick a lighter for a cigarette, or drive cars powered by countless, perfectly-timed flashes of fire, miniature infernos marching in lockstep. Just consider that: millions of people, starting and stopping fires every single day, as effortlessly as breathing. We've more than figured fire out—we've mastered it.

I’m reminded of this mastery as I turn on the stove. Fire, anytime I want. The whole city, really, has that power flowing to every home: gas lines, pilot lights, appliances, all engineered so that with a gesture, I can conjure flame. Not only that, I can control its strength, coaxing or caging it as I please. I don’t simply access fire—I command it.

Today, I choose to turn the stove to high, calling forth the blue flames. There’s a showiness to the yellow, dancing and licking at the air like a child getting its bearings. But it’s the blue that holds the true magic—solid, focused, a pillar of heat. That blue flame answers to me.

I set a pan on the burner, and the fire pours heat into metal. This is part of the pact: unchecked, fire could devour the room and take the house with it. But I play the pied piper; the flames move to my tune, and my home stays safe. Now, the pan and its shimmer of oil join the ritual, absorbing the heat, becoming actors on my stage.

They, too, follow my command. As strips of meat sizzle and spit in the pan, I watch the flame closely. I am a strict master—demanding just the right temperature, never too much, never too little. After all, I don’t want to burn the stir fry.

Scientists are looking at what climate conditions were like 3.3 million years ago—a period often referred to as the mid-Pliocene Warm Period—in order to better understand how our planet might respond to rising levels of atmospheric carbon dioxide today. During that time, global temperatures are believed to have been 2-3 degrees Celsius warmer than pre-industrial levels, with carbon dioxide concentrations similar to those currently observed in the 21st century.

Through analyzing ancient sediments, fossilized pollen, and ice core proxies, researchers can reconstruct the temperature, precipitation, and atmospheric composition of the distant past. Sea levels during the mid-Pliocene, for example, were likely 10 to 20 meters higher than today, indicating significant melting of polar ice sheets and a substantially different coastline for continents worldwide. Fossil evidence suggests that forests extended much farther north in the Arctic, and subtropical environments reached well into present-day temperate zones.

These investigations are not simply academic—understanding past warm periods can help scientists anticipate future climate impacts. The similarities between today's greenhouse gas concentrations and those of the mid-Pliocene make it a valuable case study for predicting long-term trends in temperature, ecosystem shifts, and sea level rise.

The research also highlights the feedback loops that can accelerate warming, such as reduced ice cover leading to greater solar absorption, further increasing temperatures. By piecing together climate records from millions of years ago, scientists hope to provide policymakers with more accurate projections and to underscore the urgency of cutting global emissions to avoid the most severe consequences of climate change.

Credits: ESA/B. Bergaglio
A three-headed startracker on one of ESA’s three Swarm satellites currently being prepared for launch, swathed in insulating material to prevent the temperature extremes in orbit knocking them out of alignment.
Startrackers are commonplace on satellites for navigation and attitude control, but this case they are an integral part of the magnetic mapping mission’s instrument package.
As Swarm’s Vector Field Magnetic Magnetometer measures the strength and direction of Earth’s magnetic field lines, this startracker provides a reference orientation.
Both instrument and startracker sit on an ultra-stable 'optical bench', maintaining an alignment of a single arcsecond – the diameter of a 10 cent coin seen from 4 km away.
Both were developed for the Swarm mission by the Technical University of Denmark.

By . James Slack . Visitors to Britain from 'high risk' countries will have to put up a £3,000 bond under plans introduced by Home Secretary Theresa May . Tens of thousands of visitors from India, Pakistan, Bangladesh and Nigeria will be forced to pay a £3,000 bond to be allowed to enter Britain. They will only get the money back once they leave the country in a bid to end abuse of the visa system. Home Secretary Theresa May said the intention was to make the immigration system more ‘selective’ and deter people from ‘overstaying’ once their visitor visa has expired. In the long-term, she hopes to extend the Australian-style bond scheme to also include foreign workers and students. The idea will be welcomed by backbench Tory MPs, who have been urging the government to take a tougher line on immigration to combat the threat of UKIP. But it is likely to face legal challenges on the grounds that – because it targets only people from so-called ‘high risk’ countries – it is discriminatory. There are also fears it may lead to countries such as India making British tourists pay a similar bond. The scheme will be piloted from November, for people from India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nigeria and Ghana. They are being targeted because of the high volume of visitor visa applications and relatively high levels of abuse. Mrs May said: ‘In the long run we’re interested in a system of bonds that deters overstaying and recovers costs if a foreign national has used our public services.’ The £3,000 bonds are part of Mrs May's plans to bring annual net migration to under 100,000 by 2015 . Immigration bonds have been repeatedly considered by ministers over the years, but have never been successfully introduced. Labour abandoned its own plans for a £1,000 bond in 2008 amid an outcry from migrant rights groups. They claim the policy is . discriminatory as it does not apply to people from countries such as . Australia, New Zealand and Canada. Keith Vaz, chairman of the Home . Affairs Select Committee, said the idea was ‘unworkable’ and . ‘impractical’. Mr Vaz, an ex-Foreign Office minister, . claimed it would not deter people from trying to stay on after their . visas ended, and said the targeted countries would be ‘likely to . retaliate’. The policy has the backing of Lib Dem . leader Nick Clegg. He suggested the introduction of bonds in March, . though was proposing the level should be fixed at £1,000. The move will require a change in the immigration rules but not a vote by MPs.

Another dawn in the Hollywood Hills. For Dwayne 'the Rock' Johnson, it meant another pre-dawn workout, another meticulously blended protein shake, another day battling the slow, creeping fatigue beneath the granite physique. He wasn't old, but the endless demands of blockbuster productions had started to etch subtle lines around his eyes, a faint stiffness in his usually boundless energy. As the whir of his Magic Bullet filled the pristine, minimalist kitchen, a sound *unlike* any blender whir sliced through the morning calm. The panoramic glass of his viewing deck, moments ago offering a sprawling vista of Los Angeles, shimmered, then exploded inwards with a silent, impossible implosion. A wave of force, smelling faintly of ozone and something impossibly alien, slammed into him, throwing him back. Through the dissipating smoke and crystalline dust, a gravelly voice boomed, 'I smell what you're cooking!' A blinding flash, a supernova of energy, erupted from the epicentre, so pure and overwhelming, Johnson's last thought was that he was staring into the face of a furious deity. A phone rings. A rough hand with muscular forearms reaches for the receiver knocking over a table full of Bud Light cans in the process. Lifting the phone to his ear, Stone Cold Steve Austin burps a 'What?!' to the caller. 'Steve, this is Vince McMahon. The Rock was killed today. We believe he was killed by the champion of an Alien race, they don't get that our industry is entertainment! They have demanded the UN provide a champion. You're all we got Steve! 'What about the Undertaker?' replied Steve 'He's already dead...' 'We'll shit, guess it's time to drink beer and kickass. And you know what Vince?' 'You're all outta beer?' asks Vince 'Hell no! I'm coming for you after I open a can of whup ass on those alien sonsofbitches, what?!.' Steve slams the phone down. Downs his beer and exits his trailer. Earth's greatest champion walks towards his destiny.

I expected to see him. The thought of facing him made me question the worth of getting into heaven. Just open the door so I can't face my fear, fail and continue on my way to hell because logically that is where "they" are sending me anyways. "To get in heaven.." pfft. This is just a ploy. I'm already here devil, open the door and begin your mockery. It's all I got used to life on Earth, might as well gain a few perk points in this department. Fuck. I deserve this. Bring it. I was the one who wanted to die. The door creaked open and--- wha? Wait, who is that? For a split milisecond I didn't even recognize my own face. "What did you say? That you deserve this??" I just stared blankly at her. I always wondered how I looked to other people. My weird facial quirks and body language. Fuck I look way weird compared to in the mirror. My voice is unfamiliar too, just like in a recording. Fucking weird. "You know repeating "fuck" over and over just makes you sound unitelligent." I didn't know what to say, think or do. So I was scared. "Don't worry, relax. I'm here to show you heaven isn't an infinite field of clouds where you worship a narcissistic god or man like you were raised to believe. It's much bigger than that" She smiled that contagious giant smile I learned to mask and perfect. But she didn't seem to be faking "Heaven is the opportunity to continuously learn, grow and progress. People naturally want to keep living and progress their minds and survive. Well, that is what dying is. Surving. Death is an illusion, you will continue to evolve and do great things. You can even learn to love yourself. Because it looks like you forgot how to." I cried. Sobbed. Bawled. It took everything for my whole body not to collapse. Motherfucker. Get the fuck away from me I can't handle this right now. "I didn't deserve what you did to me. I hated you for the longest time and wanted nothing to do with you. Time is something youll learn more about, but just know you don't need as much of it as you think. You're wonderful, now work on creating your heaven and visit the ones you love. Forgive yourself like I did you." I cried and cried. She hugged me and absorbed all of the pain. I had hope and I couldn't remember the last time I felt true love. True peace. Comfort. This is true happiness. My arm turned hot and caught ablaze. I opened my eyes and felt the strength of the raging fire engulf our bodies in flames. I fell with a thump. "Well, looks like you fell for my evil ploy, girl!" Laughed an eerie laugh.

I tried everything but the doors won't open. The room is dimly lighted. The hazy shine of my laptop's screen provides for more brightness in the room than the bulb itself. I continue reading the comments on this thread. Not a single comment is related to the topic. One of them says... 'Don't worry, we are there for you' And some says 'I've brought your favourite cake' And many more comments like this saying they are there for the OP. I hardly saw this much sympathy on Reddit. But none of them says how the fuck should I get out of this cage. Wait a minute, is there somebody else imprisoned like me? It's been a while for me living in this house alone. I don't even remember how I got here. The last thing I remember before I came here is a hazy memory of a car ride. The room is getting dark. And I have stopped counting days. Roughly it would be more than a week, maybe. The windows of this house is dark. There is no way I can know what's happening outside. I then again stuck my eyes on the screen. Now I was reading the usernames in the thread. OP's name was caterpillar. I remember how my mom used to call me a caterpillar when I was small. Then looking at the other usernames in comment section, I felt like I know these names. God knows what they are. I don't remember when did I had a meal. I don't feel hungry. I don't feel my energy getting wasted even when I stroll for hours here and there in the house. I don't even remember when did I slept in these days. It's been around 2 months that I noticed any change. Today I saw a crack in the door. An extremely white light was glowing outside. I felt like the insect getting attracted towards that light. I saw the comments are now coming at a faster rate. People commenting really sorrowful things. As I move forward towards the door, the crack widens and more of that brilliant white light pours inside. I wonder what could be outside so bright. And suddenly there was no door. And it was all white light and it feels orgasmically satisfying to enter into it. ................ In a hospital, many people are waiting eagerly outside the ICU ward. Some are crying, some are marching right and left in front of the Ward's door. After a long wait the doctor comes out and says "I am sorry". People burst into tears. Doctor hands the medical report. One day later a local newspaper reads "The boy from the dreadful car accident died yesterday after 2 and half months in coma."

THE FINGER LAKES region of western New York is one of the most beautiful places on earth. The 11 lakes dangle like a necklace below Lake Ontario, surrounded by hills that are a breathtaking green in summer, red and orange for a flash in the autumn, then snowy white until the cycle repeats. It’s an area where the main tension has been of the resident-vs.-renter sort.
This summer, the tension, visibly staked out with lawn signs, was different. The topic: hydraulic fracturing, “fracking” for short. In this process, fluid—primarily water, with some sand and other chemicals—is injected deep underground to break apart shale rock, releasing natural gas and oil. Back at the surface, gas and oil are cleaned and sold; the water mixture is dumped into deep wells.
The procedure has only been made cost-effective in the last decade or so; awareness of retrievable shale oil and gas deposits isn’t a whole lot older. Combine the two, and you have an energy boom—one that led natural gas to nearly overtake coal for electricity production at one point last year.
A key question that has not been definitively answered: Does fracking, compared to the fuel it displaces, increase or decrease greenhouse gas production? Since natural gas, compared to coal, produces significantly less carbon dioxide when burned, cheaper natural gas is one reason why U.S. carbon dioxide emissions have gone down significantly of late. But natural gas is primarily methane—a gas that is more than 20 times as effective at trapping heat as carbon dioxide. During the fracking process, some of that methane escapes into the atmosphere; there is debate over how much.
Another critical, and contested, question is the extent to which fracking fluid pollutes groundwater. There have been isolated reports of chemicals contained in fracking fluid being found in the wild, including by the EPA in Wyoming last year. Back in 2004, an initial EPA report found that fracking with diesel fuel posed a risk to groundwater (though the overall verdict of the Bush administration-era agency was that fracking was safe). It’s difficult to determine the extent to which such pollution is a problem, because many fracking companies are allowed under state laws to keep the proprietary ingredients of their fluid secret.
At least the states have the ability to ask. In 2005, Congress permanently exempted fracking from regulation under the federal Safe Drinking Water Act—a move championed by then-Vice President Dick Cheney (and later opposed by former EPA officials).
ProPublica, an investigative journalism nonprofit, recently announced that one of the most likely sources of groundwater contamination comes not from the drilling or fracking, but from the disposal of fracking fluid. More than 30 trillion gallons of waste fracking fluid have been injected underground; disposal wells are regularly operated at unsafe pressures. And those wells are, in theory, permanent.
It is not in the fracking companies’ interest to answer pollution questions objectively—particularly while fracking wells erupt with money as much as natural gas. New York state recently decided to reset its entire decision-making process, beginning its scientific analysis again from square one, delaying any decision on whether the state will allow fracking.
In Penn Yan, in the Finger Lakes region, jobs are in short supply, and the local Keuka Lake offers both revenue and pride. This fall, I spoke with an employee of the local Chamber of Commerce to ask if it had taken a position on the roiling fracking debate. I expected an organization of local businesses to be staunchly supportive. The Chamber hadn’t taken a position, she explained. As many members opposed fracking as supported it.
With good reason.
Philip Bump covers environmental news for Grist magazine.
Image: Murky water, Sheli Jensen / Shutterstock.com

Septic tanks or sewage treatment systems are essential for any household in remote areas where residences are not attached to the central sewage system or pipelines. Houses not attached to the main sewage system should install self-sufficient septic tank systems that comply with the Groundwater Directive introduced for pan-Europe groundwater protection. Poorly built or badly maintained septic tank systems can cause serious environmental damage by polluting water supplies in addition to groundwater. Groundwater, once contaminated, becomes difficult and very expensive to treat for rendering it fit for human consumption. Therefore, strict EC and UK laws are enforced for septic tank installation and operation. Failing to comply with these standards may not only result in serious penalties but also result in long-term and irreversible contamination of limited water resources.

Household sewage treatment units that do not function properly are an obligation to the house owner as well. Failed systems may result in backflows, slow-moving drains, foul smells surrounding the area, overflowing tanks, and swampy soak fields. House owners can take a few precautions to keep their septic tank units working and efficient for long periods.

Pumping the tank regularly is important to empty and clean the effluent tank at recommended intervals. The time lag between pumpings is dependent on the size and usage of the system, as well as the number of members in the residence. Regular pumping keeps the tank free of solid buildup and bio-mat on the walls of the tank, which not only reduce the capacity of the tank to hold waste but can also prove corrosive to the tank material.

Watching what goes into the tank is also crucial. Do not flush personal hygiene products, napkins, and metal or plastic pieces down the toilet. These materials are not degraded easily and sit inside the tank for long, using the space meant for incoming effluent. Additionally, washing down excessive amounts of chemicals in the form of household cleaners into the system may kill favorable bacteria inside the tank. Aerobic bacteria help in breaking down the effluent into cleaner liquid that seeps into the soak field. Aerobic action decreases the amount of residual solids.

Installing a septic tank conversion unit can also be beneficial. Conversion units consist of aerator pumps, air diffusers, and sometimes outlet waste filters. Keeping the septic tank aerated eliminates and prevents rotten egg smell, which is due to toxic gas generation, including hydrogen sulfide. Additionally, the presence of air inside the tank boosts aerobic action. Aerobic bacteria are at least 20 times more efficient than anaerobic bacteria.

"Well?" I drummed my fingers against the desk nervously as Karen turned the last page. Her brow was furrowed, and as she finished reading she shook her head slowly. After years of watching her read my work I knew that look, and it wasn't good. "Chris, where did this come from?" she looked up at me, holding the manuscript out in front of her delicately, as if it might burst into flames at any moment. "It's only a draft," I said meekly. "But it's - it's sick!" she said, "this depiction of human existence, with its starvation, war, and abuse, it's horrendous. And, honestly, it just seems a bit too farfetched. Do you really expect me to believe that in one half of this world people ate so much that they died from obesity, while millions starved to death only a few thousand miles away?" I sighed. "You're right, I suppose I did let myself get a bit carried away there." "And the plot," she continued, "don't you think it's a bit repetitive? I mean, all these - what was it you called them? Oh yeah, ' wars ' - all these ' wars ' are just fought for the same selfish reasons, only they're disguised as religion or politics or whatever the trend is at the time. And then you've got the civil rights movements. I mean, it's the same every time, whether it's giving rights to women, racial minorities, or gay people, it's just the same drawn-out process. Surely after a few years people would just catch on to the fact that everyone should be treated equally and stop making progress so difficult?" I nodded slowly. It was difficult to hear her pick holes in the work I had laboured over for months, but if our twenty years of marriage had taught me anything it was that my wife's toughest criticisms were the ones I needed to hear most. "Plus, you keep killing all my favourite characters," she said. "I mean, I really liked that Martin Luther King guy, I don't see why he had to die so early on. It's a bit disturbing, too, the idea that one human being would kill another one. I know you're trying to be unflinchingly honest, but that just made me really uncomfortable," she said with a shiver. "You're right," I said, "it's too much." Karen reached over and squeezed my hand. "You're a great writer Chris," she said, "you don't need to rely on such brutal honesty. Just tone it down a little, okay?" I smiled at her and nodded. "How about we open a bottle of wine?" I said. She smiled. "I'll get the glasses." As she left the room, I looked down at the manuscript in my hands, and thought about the dark, twisted world those words had depicted—our own. I opened the bottom drawer of my desk, the one full of old half-baked ideas and rough drafts that never went anywhere, and threw it in. Karen was right - no one would ever believe in such a place, not if it were presented as fiction.

Purpose: This qualitative study aimed to explore how physical therapy students learn about disability through the Exploring Perspectives on Disability (EPOD) learning event, developed by the School of Rehabilitative Science at McMaster University. The EPOD includes two scheduled classes on disability, a shadowing experience with a community member living with a disability, and a reflective summary assignment. EPOD was designed to broaden students’ perspectives on disability, encourage positive attitudes toward disability, and support best practice in community-based care.

Method: Fifty-seven first-semester students in a Master’s entry-level physical therapy program participated in the EPOD event. In their reflective summaries, students described and reflected on their experiences and observations from shadowing an individual with a disability. These summaries were transcribed into NUD*IST, a qualitative data management software. Each summary was reviewed by two of four investigators, who independently read, coded, and categorized the entries before collaborating to identify key themes.

Results: Three major themes emerged: (1) students’ assumptions about disability were challenged; (2) their understanding of the supports and barriers faced by people with disabilities was deepened; and (3) they recognized their own professional growth and learning.

Conclusions: Participation in the EPOD learning event provided students with new knowledge about disability and influenced their attitudes toward people with disabilities. The EPOD was effective in broadening students’ perspectives and fostering more positive attitudes regarding disability.

Williams RM. Physical Therapy Students' Perspectives on Disability. The Internet Journal of Allied Health Sciences and Practice. 2007 Jul 01;5(3), Article 5.

Vitamin A, also known as beta-carotene or retinol,
is a very important vitamin. Preformed vitamin A, as is found in fish liver oil, was the first vitamin officially named and was thereby given the letter A to identify it. Retinol, another name for preformed vitamin A, was so named because of its importance in vision. The rods of the eye, which are located within the retina, contain rhodopsin, or visual purple, and need vitamin A for proper vision. Several carotene pigments found in foods, mainly yellow and orange vegetables and fruits, can be converted to vitamin A in our body and thus are termed provitamin A. Beta-carotene is the most available and also the one that yields the highest amount of A.
Vitamin A is absorbed primarily from the small intestine. Absorption of this fat-soluble vitamin is reduced with alcohol use, with vitamin E deficiency, with cortisone medication, and with excessive iron intake or the use of mineral oil, as well as with exercise. As a fat-soluble vitamin, vitamin A can be stored in the body and used when there is decreased intake. About 90 percent of the storable vitamin A is in the liver; it is also stored in the kidneys, lungs, eyes, and the fat tissue. In addition to reducing absorption, alcohol use also depletes liver stores. The storage of vitamin A is decreased during times of stress or illness unless intake is increased. The body needs the mineral zinc to help release stores of vitamin A for use.
Vitamin A is needed at a level of at least 5,000 IUs (international units) per day, though this may vary due to many factors. Deficiencies of vitamin A are still fairly common worldwide and cause many difficulties. Actually, analysis of the average American diet reveals that it provides only about 4,000 units of vitamin A daily, so the many problems of vitamin A deficiency, such as visual changes, skin dryness, and increased infections, are more common than most people realize.
