Winger Kazenga LuaLua is expected to be named among the substitutes once more after making his debut from the bench in the win over Reading last week.
Fulham striker Chris Martin is expected to feature again, after boss Slavisa Jokanvoic said the row with Derby about the on-loan player "is over".
Forwards Floyd Ayite and Neeskens Kebano are away at the Africa Cup of Nations in Gabon.
Rangers are looking to secure a fourth consecutive Championship win while Fulham have lost just one of their last seven league games.
The R's won the first west London derby of the season 2-1 at Loftus Road on 1 October, after the Whites missed two penalties.
QPR manager Ian Holloway told BBC Radio London:
"Losing so many games in a row forced my hand to be as brutal as I have been [with transfers].
"It is sometimes about clearing out things to get a freshness. The atmosphere here is one of optimism and the young players have helped with that.
"Our pitch is small and tight and Fulham are a fantastic test for us. We need to get around them and in their faces and try and carry on our momentum.
"Slavisa Jokanovic seemed to get no credit for what he did at Watford and no appreciation from them. Fulham's wage bill has gone backwards yet he has managed to hold it all together and play some wonderful football."
Fulham boss Slavisa Jokanovic told BBC Radio London:
"We played well against QPR last time, created many opportunities and missed two penalties.
"At this moment we play with more confidence and have improved ourselves. We know how important it is for our fans.
"I have to be happy because we have created an identity. We are more solid but in football it is important to win the game.
"We want to play these kinds of games and, although it is not my first derby around the world, I am excited like our supporters."

I recently came across the book "Toddler Activities for Every Room: Quick and Easy Fun at Home" and I must say it's been a game-changer for my little one. The book is filled with creative and engaging activities that can be done in every room of the house, from the kitchen to the backyard. What I love most about this book is that it provides a wide range of ideas that cater to different ages and abilities, making it perfect for toddlers of all stages. 

The activities are easy to set up and require minimal equipment, which is a huge plus for busy parents like myself. The book is also well-organized and easy to navigate, with clear instructions and beautiful illustrations that make it a joy to read. 

One of my favorite things about this book is that it encourages learning through play, which is essential for toddlers. The activities are designed to promote cognitive development, fine motor skills, and social skills, all while having fun. My toddler has loved every activity we've tried so far, and I've seen a significant improvement in their curiosity and creativity. 

Overall, I highly recommend "Toddler Activities for Every Room" to any parent or caregiver looking for fun and engaging ways to keep their toddler entertained and stimulated at home. It's a must-have resource that will provide hours of entertainment and learning for your little one.

With few exceptions, adult butterflies and moths eat only various liquids to maintain their water balance and energy stores. Most adults sip flower nectar, but other imbibe fluids from sap flowers on trees, rotting fruits, bird droppings, or animal dung. Many adult butterflies are found drinking fluids at wet sand or mud, especially along stream courses or the edges of dirt roads or trails.
Some exceptions are the adults of longwing butterflies such as the Zebra are able to collect pollen from certain flowers with their proboscis and to break it down and absorb amino acids (proteins) which contribute to the ability to survive, mate and lay eggs for long periods (6 months or so). With their short proboscis (tongue) the adults of Harvester butterflies can actually pierce the bodies of woolly aphids and drink their fluids, the only bugs that adult butterflies eat. The caterpillar of almost all butterflies and moths eat various parts of plants. Each species may specialize of only a few kinds of plants or plant parts. The caterpillars of the Harvester butterfly and its relatives are exceptions in that they feed solely on aphids.
|Members of the family Sphingidae are medium to large moths, with heavy bodies; wingspread reaches 5 inches or more in some species. Although a few are active in the daytime, most species in the group are active at dusk. Most, but not all, sphingids feed much like hummingbirds, hovering in front of a flower and sipping nectar through the extended proboscis. The proboscis rolls up like a party noisemaker when not in use, and may not be readily evident in a resting moth. Some species lack scales on large portions of their wings, and therefore have transparent or clear wings. These are commonly referred to as "clearwing hummingbird moths." (Note however that the scientifically accepted common name of "Hummingbird clearwing" refers specifically to Hemaris thysbe, at right.)||

This was my first visit to Flavors Fried Fish & Wings, and honestly, I was curious because I’d heard mixed things from friends. I decided to try the 3-piece catfish combo with seasoned fries and a side of coleslaw, along with a lemonade. The total came to just under $12, which seemed reasonable for the amount of food.

Let me start with the good: the catfish was hot and crispy, not greasy at all. The breading had some nice flavor, a little bit of a kick but not too spicy, and the fish inside was flaky and fresh. I’d definitely give the catfish a 4 out of 5 — it’s some of the better fried fish I’ve had from a take-out spot.

The fries were a bit less impressive. They were seasoned well, and the portion was generous, but they could have been crispier. By the time I got home (less than 10 minutes away), they were starting to get soggy. Not a dealbreaker, but maybe next time I’ll try eating in or ask for them extra crispy. I’d rate the fries a 2.5 out of 5.

The coleslaw was fresh, crunchy, and not drowning in mayo, which is a plus for me. It was a little on the sweet side, but I enjoyed it. I’d say a solid 3.5 out of 5 for the slaw. The lemonade was good, not too sweet, and tasted homemade.

The staff were friendly and got my order right, although it did take a little longer than I expected for a take-out spot (about 15 minutes). The place looked clean and there were a few tables if you want to eat in. Overall, I’d come back for the catfish and maybe try the wings next time. Flavors isn’t fancy, but it’s a good neighborhood spot when you want something fried and fresh!

The Invention of Teenagers: LIFE and the Triumph of Youth Culture
Historians and social critics differ on the specifics of the timeline, but most cultural observers agree that the strange and fascinating creature known as the American teenager — as we now understand the species — came into being sometime in the early 1940s. This is not to say that for millennia human beings had somehow passed from childhood to adulthood without enduring the squalls of adolescence. But the modern notion of the teen years as a recognized, quantifiable life stage, complete with its own fashions, behavior, vernacular and arcane rituals, simply did not exist until the post-Depression era.
Here, in the first of a series of galleries on the evolution of LIFE magazine’s — and, by extension, America’s — view of teenagers through the middle part of the 20th century, LIFE.com presents photos that the inimitable Nina Leen shot for a December 1944 article, “Teen-Age Girls: They Live in a Wonderful World of Their Own.”
Leen focused on a group of 12 girls, from 15 to 17 years old, living in Webster Groves, Mo., a suburb of St. Louis.
(Note the hyphenated phrase, “teen-age.” By all accounts, it would be several years before the word as we spell it today, “teenager,” would make an appearance in the pages of LIFE. The word “teen-agers” itself, meanwhile, likely made its debut in print in a 1941 issue of Popular Science Monthly.)
In its December 1944 feature, LIFE breathlessly discussed the “teen-age” phenomenon in language that, in 2013, somehow feels naive, chauvinistic, celebratory and insightful, all at once. That so many of the article’s impossibly broad, sweeping claims (“Some 6,000,000 U.S. teen-age girls live in a world all their own: a lovely, gay, enthusiastic, funny and blissful society. . . .”) clearly apply to a specific type of teenager — i.e., white, middle-class — tends to blunt some of the more incisive observations. But taken as a whole, the LIFE article and Leen’s photographs constitute a fascinating, early look at a segment of the American populace that, over the ensuing decades, for better and for worse, has assumed an increasingly central role in the shaping of Western culture.
As LIFE stated the case to its millions of readers in 1944:
There is a time in the life of every American girl when the most important ting in the world is to be one of a crowd of other girls and to act and speak and dress exactly as they do. This is the teen age.
Some 6,000,000 U.S. teen-age girls live in a world all their own — a lovely, gay, enthusiastic, funny and blissful society almost untouched by the war. It is a world of sweaters and skirts and bobby sox and loafers, of hair worn long, of eye-glass rims painted red with nail polish, of high school boys no yet gone to war. It is world still devoted to parents who are pals even if they use the telephone too much. It is a world of Vergil’s Aeneid, second-year French and plane geometry, of class plays, field hockey, “moron” jokes and put-on accents. It is a world of slumber parties and the Hit Parade, of peanut butter and popcorn and the endless collecting of menus and match covers and little stuffed animals.
American businessmen, many of whom have teen-age daughters, have only recently begun to realize that teen-agers make up a big and special market. . . . The movies and the theater make money by turning a sometimes superficial and sometimes social-minded eye on teen-agers.
Their new importance means little to teen-age millions. By their energy, originality and good looks they have brought public attention down from debutantes and college girls to themselves. Moving through the awkward age, the troubles of growing up, their welter of fads and taboos, they eventually become — in the judgment of almost every Western nation — the most attractive women in the world.
Then again, perhaps that particular discussion is best left to another series on LIFE.com — one looking at, say, LIFE’s portrayal of women through the years?

Looking at today's lottery list, I groan as I see that I've been assigned not one, but two cases. The money will be nice, but as I'm getting older I can't help but feel like the current pace—two cases a day—will kill me very quickly. It isn’t just my imagination; the data supports my fear. Studies show that surgeons are at a much higher risk of burnout and depression than the general population, with the American College of Surgeons reporting that nearly 40% of surgeons experience symptoms of burnout. Just last week, three more surgeons in our hospital system committed suicide, their names whispered in the break rooms and memorialized with somber emails. I have accepted some of their caseload, knowing it means longer hours, less sleep, and the ever-present pressure to be perfect in the operating room.

This is the sacrifice I chose to make, and I try to remind myself of that when the weight feels unbearable. Marsha may be gone, but that doesn't make me into a jaded, bitter excuse of a healthcare provider. I refuse to let tragedy or exhaustion transform me into the kind of doctor who stops caring. The alcohol, however, that does. It’s a well-documented pattern in our profession; according to a 2022 JAMA study, nearly 15% of surgeons admit to problematic alcohol use, often citing stress and trauma as the root causes. I see myself reflected in those statistics, caught in the same destructive cycle.

Going to my office, I set up the necessary supplies. Tightening the lightly powdered blue band across the antecubital region, I palpate a suitable vein. Finding one that is not too heavily scarred, I hardly wince as the twenty-two gauge needle pierces my skin. Looking at the ground, I see specks of crimson dotting my carpet from the days of my residency, when I wasn't as proficient at keeping the blood in the catheter. Why someone chose to give a surgeon an office with a carpet is beyond me in the first place—hospital administrators, so far removed from clinical realities, never seem to think of the practicalities.

IV established, I push a tiny bit of Ativan. Perhaps it's a tiny bit more than I needed yesterday, and that was a bit more than I needed the day before, in some twisted circle. But who's going to stop me? The extension set is prepared, and my assistant tapes the port to my back for the nurses to access intraoperatively. I tear my eyes away from hers as she tries to tell me, without speaking, that I need to stop, that I can't keep doing this to myself. Her silent plea stings more than any needle, but I trudge onwards down the elevator, ignoring the piteous looks my coworkers give me.

They will never understand why I do this, and they must never know. In the surgical world, showing vulnerability is often mistaken for weakness. The culture of silence is pervasive, with surveys indicating that over 60% of physicians would not seek help for mental health issues out of fear of professional repercussions. So I keep going, shouldering the burdens left behind by friends and colleagues, trying to convince myself that I am helping, even as I fall apart.

Green Day's 2000 album, Warning, is horrendous; it never should have been released. It shows Green Day running out of gas, churning out three-minute pop songs. I bought Warning and regretted it immediately. Sure, some of the songs like 'Church on Sunday' and 'Waiting' sound ok, but overall Warning is a waste of your time and money. Green Day's old school stuff was awesome, raw, and honest material. Dookie and Insomniac are both awesome, and Nimrod shows Green Day at their creative peak, but it seemed that Green Day were on the downside of their career when Warning came out in 2000. American Idiot is their first good album in 7 years, following 1997's Nimrod. So, once again, Warning is garbage and is not worth your money. If you are just checking out Green Day buy Dookie, Insomniac, Nimrod, and American Idiot.

I laid at the edge of the moat, the only survivor of the party sent to rescue the princess. My beard soaking in the fetid muck and blood of my compatriots.They had prepared for the usual sorts of monsters a princess may be stolen by. Whether it was a minotaur, dragon, undead, or just the creepy ex boyfriend who wouldn't give up, they knew how to banish these creatures. But not a goose, no, never a goose. Had the king known it was a goose he would have simply given up and done his best to forget he ever had a daughter. Those vile beasts, appearing ever so graceful yet to contain a beak of razors with the attitude to match. Luckily hunting these winged beasts and their brethren was the family business. It is what my entire clan worked towards. As soon as I had spotted the foul beast i was able to identify it and lay in wait, searching through my pack for the right tools for this particular hellspawn. Feeling the time was right I blew heartily into my horn. Forged solely for the purpose of calling forth such beast, it blew clear across the castle grounds, summoning it towards me. I law still as it soared, searching for the source. I pray it does not see me in time. I stare it down, my staff of destruction following until the moment of death calls loud across the water. I drag its lifeless corpse from the water, a fine trophy. I reach the princess who exclaims "MY HERO! However did you save me, and uhh...Who exactly are you? I was kind of expecting a young handsome prince....but...I MEAN THANK YOU. You rescued me, so the reward is yours!" Not a man of many words, I merely reply, "That's a fact jack!". We ride off into the sunset in my lifted truck. I started my day as a commander of ducks, but am now the commander of geese.

Dr. Jacobs marked another answer and lifted his pen. Another confirmation. He sighed heavily at the results, the sound echoing through the empty gymnasium. One table, one old desk chair with a broken wheel, and a floor covered in lost papers and scuff marks from the earlier testing. This was what his work had become. He tapped the pen on the paper, leaving marks that slowly soaked through to the test below it. It was always such a strange feeling to identify the mind that would turn violent before anyone else could see the signs. There was responsibility in it. He decided their fate. A lifetime of monitoring and treatment for something they couldn't control...for actions they had yet to take. There was weight in his decisions, in his evaluations. He set the paper aside and lifted his pen to find the ink had dried up. He bent over and dabbed the tip in the inkwell at his feet. A long, thin string of dark ink clung to the instrument as he lifted his arm back to the table. It was fortunate the test hadn't existed when he was their age. He wiped the pen clean and turned the page.

James Alexander Gordon, the legendary BBC voice who read Saturday football results for over 40 years, has died at 78 after a battle with cancer. Famous for his instantly recognisable delivery and subtle vocal cues, the Scot turned classified results into a national ritual. Gordon retired last year after surgery to remove his larynx.

Tributes poured in from across the football world. Gary Lineker called him “the voice of the football results.” BBC’s Richard Burgess praised him as “an iconic radio presence and a true gentleman.” Robbie Savage remembered his “fantastic voice," while Peter Reid recalled, “You always remember his voice. For football fans, it was the big moment of the week.”

Known as JAG, Gordon joined the BBC in 1972 after a stint in music publishing. He started reading the scores a year later, with his distinctive style copied but never matched. His delivery let listeners predict results before he even finished the scoreline—a tradition that united fans across generations.

Japan has one of, if not the, strictest emission control regimes in the world. Upon purchase, when a car is three years old, and every two years after that, each car must undergo a rigorous and sometimes costly Sha-ken inspection. These ensure that it meets tough emission standards and is in sound mechanical condition.
Sha-ken makes it expensive for Japanese car owners to keep an older car on the road. Yet these regular inspections, combined with other factors - congested roads that ensure most cars have very low mileage, a relatively temperate climate, underground parking - mean that a five or seven year old Japanese car is typically in excellent condition. The net result is that Japan exports used cars at low prices to Australia, New Zealand, India, South Africa, Botswana, Zambia, and just about every country where right-hand-drive (RHD) cars are used.
From a narrow, national perspective, Japan's emission control regime appears to protect the environment. Yet by encouraging Japanese car owners to abandon perfectly good vehicles, which are then dumped on other countries, effectively it subsidizes car exports. From a global perspective, therefore, the environmental impacts of Japan's emissions control regime are far less clear.
An increase in the supply of cars would be expected to increase the quantity of cars on the road. But does increased car ownership automatically increase greenhouse gas emissions? It depends upon the alternatives. Is the alternative to a "new reconditioned" Japanese car a 50 year-old Chevy with a V8 engine held together with wire and prayers? A methane belching team of oxen? A solar-powered monorail system? On balance, my guess is that the increased supply of cars leads to increased emissions, but I don't have solid empirical evidence.
Even if importing cheap Japanese used cars into, say, Zambia leads to increased emissions, is it a bad thing? Zambians love their "new reconditioned" Japanese cars, just as Canadians would, if we were able to tap the cheap used Japanese car market. [We drive on the right, Japan drives on the left, and although some high end specialty used Japanese cars can be found in Vancouver, they are rarely seen elsewhere in Canada.]
Cars are a key part of Zambia's transport system. "Traders", mostly women, drive around the country, and make money through arbitrage - buying goods that are relatively cheap in Lusaka, driving down to Livingstone and reselling them, then stocking up with whatever is relatively cheap in Livingstone, and driving back to Lusaka. Cars also play a key role in the tourism industry - every taxi is a Japanese import.
One could argue that cheap cars fuel economic development. Even if they lead to an increase in Zambian carbon emissions, so what - Zambians still produce relatively few carbon emissions on a per capita basis. From a global perspective, surely the responsibility for reductions in carbon emissions should lie with those, like globe-trotting academics, who produce high levels of emissions - concern for the environment shouldn't trap Africans in poverty.
In short, it's complicated. But like any economist, I'm a sucker for an example of a policy with unintended consequences.

A Manchester United supporter is appealing for help to find a fellow fan who saved his life after he suffered a heart attack outside Old Trafford earlier this week.

John Wainwright, 58, says he owes his life to the quick-thinking stranger who rushed to his aid as crowds dispersed following United's match against Arsenal on Sunday afternoon.

Wainwright, from Stockport, collapsed near the Sir Matt Busby Way exit shortly after the final whistle. Witnesses say he fell to the ground and appeared unresponsive as dozens of fans passed by. A man, believed to be in his thirties and wearing a United scarf, immediately sprang into action.

“He started shouting for an ambulance but didn’t wait – he checked for a pulse and started doing chest compressions right away,” said Wainwright, speaking from Manchester Royal Infirmary where he is now recovering. “If he hadn’t done that, I don’t think I’d be here. I want to thank him more than anything, but I never even got his name.”

Paramedics arrived within minutes and credited the bystander’s swift resuscitation efforts with saving Wainwright’s life. Doctors say early CPR was critical in his survival and have called the unknown fan a “real hero.”

Wainwright’s daughter Emily has taken to social media in search of the good Samaritan. “If anyone knows who helped my dad, please get in touch,” she wrote. “He means the world to us.”

Manchester United Football Club says it is working with local authorities to review CCTV footage and help identify the rescuer. “The unity and compassion shown among fans is at the heart of our club,” a spokesperson said.

Wainwright says he is hoping to meet his saviour in person once he is released from hospital. “He deserves my endless gratitude—and my season ticket for a game if he wants!”

**Innovating for Every Woman, Every Child: Transformative Solutions for Global Health Challenges**

In a world where every two minutes a woman dies from preventable causes related to pregnancy or childbirth, and millions of children lack access to essential health services, the quest for transformative global health solutions has never been more urgent. The ambition to secure a healthier future for every woman and every child lies at the heart of the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), especially SDG 3: Good Health and Well-Being. Achieving these goals requires more than commitments; it demands innovation—radical, tailored, and equitable.

**Bridging Gaps With Technology**

Digital health technologies are reshaping the landscape of maternal, newborn, and child health. Mobile health (mHealth) applications, such as MomConnect in South Africa and mMitra in India, provide expectant mothers with vital information on prenatal care, nutrition, and immunizations through SMS and voice messages. Artificial intelligence and telemedicine platforms are enabling healthcare providers to reach remote populations, ensuring early diagnosis and intervention for conditions like preeclampsia or neonatal sepsis. Such solutions not only overcome geographical barriers but also empower women to make informed choices about their health.

**Scaling Community-Led Innovations**

Community health workers (CHWs) have proven to be the backbone of healthcare delivery in resource-limited settings. Programs like Ethiopia’s Health Extension Program and Rwanda’s network of community health workers enable frontline delivery of essential maternal and child health services, from home-based newborn care to immunization campaigns and family planning counseling. Innovations that leverage locally sourced solutions—like reusable menstrual hygiene kits designed with community input—help address unique cultural and socioeconomic constraints.

**Advancing Access Through Innovative Financing**

Financial barriers remain one of the most formidable obstacles to care. Results-based financing models and microinsurance products are unlocking access to skilled birth attendance and emergency obstetric care. Social impact bonds and blended finance initiatives drive private investment into underserved areas, fostering sustainable health systems. By aligning incentives and accountability across sectors, these approaches channel resources where they are needed most.

**Fostering Cross-Sectoral Collaborations**

Transformative change often emerges at the intersection of disciplines. Collaborations between governments, non-profit organizations, pharmaceutical companies, and tech startups are generating affordable diagnostics, lifesaving medicines, and innovative delivery mechanisms—like the use of drones in Rwanda and Ghana to deliver blood products and vaccines to remote clinics.

**Championing Gender Equity and Agency**

Putting women and girls at the center of health innovation ensures that solutions are tailored to their specific needs. Programs integrating education, nutrition, and reproductive health empower women to break cycles of poor health and poverty. In Kenya, the Girl Power Microbank initiative couples financial literacy with reproductive health education, enabling adolescent girls to avoid early pregnancies and stay in school.

**The Road Ahead: Catalyzing Transformation**

While remarkable progress has been made, persistent inequalities and the impacts of global crises—including pandemics and climate change—continue to threaten hard-won gains. To truly innovate for every woman and every child, the global community must redouble its efforts to:

- Invest in robust data systems to drive evidence-based solutions
- Champion locally driven, culturally relevant innovations
- Ensure that health innovations are accessible, affordable, and sustainable
- Amplify women’s voices in decision-making at all levels

Only by embracing collaboration, ingenuity, and inclusivity can we meet the global health challenges of today and build a future where every woman and every child not only survives, but thrives. The journey is long, but with transformative innovation and unwavering commitment, a world of health and dignity for all is within reach.

Characterization in Othello
Lesson 9 of 15
Objective: SWBAT identify and use precise words and phrases, telling details, and sensory language to convey a vivid picture of a character from Othello.
Following completion of the first three Acts in Othello, I like to take a step back and look at the motivations and activities of the characters. As traditional with a Shakespearean tragedy, the connections among characters can become a tangled web of relationships. Accordingly, I have students create a map of all the characters and identify how they are connected. Students will then be able to make inferences into character motivations; this concept is the impetus behind the bipoem assignment.
Connections among Characters
To begin the lesson, I will ask the class to volunteer the names of all the characters in the play that we have been introduced so far. Once a list is comprised, students will then create a flow chart that indicates the relationships among all the characters. I have enclosed a completed sample and a blank copy.
Once the character map is established, students will choose one of the characters and write a biopoem on that character by completing the attached template. This activity is different from a traditional biopoem because it has been accommodated to meet the Common Core. The assignment requires students to use phrases and words from the text in completing the template. The purpose of this assignment is for students to have an ability to step into the shoes of one of the characters. In order to complete the template, students will have to make many inferences into what motivates the character. The attached template further explains how to structure the poem.
If technology is available, I have adapted the biopoem into a video poem by using iMovie. See attached.
As we read this scene in Othello, I illustrate to students how Shakespeare demonstrates his mastery of language. Othello is inquiring about the handkerchief because he is aware that Desdemona has "misplaced" it. While he questions her about its whereabouts, Desdemona is skillfully trying to change the subject.

After months of sleepless nights and desperate searches for relief, I finally decided to try this controversial sleep aid that exists in the shadowy realm of unregulated medications. What I discovered was both a blessing and a curse wrapped in a small, unassuming package that arrived promptly at my door.

450 mg. keeps me feeling exhausted and weak for the entire day following use. 225 mg. or less seems to work fine. Long term it causes painful gastrointestinal issues and is quite likely responsible for reducing my white blood cell count. Safety and dosage information on it is hard to find. It is not FDA approved, and obviously a powerful medication. At times I find it a necessary evil. For short term use, it did allow me to get some sleep. Insomniacs: please use with extreme caution!

The drone of the highway was a lullaby for the tired. Eleanor had been driving for hours, the world outside her window a blur of green and grey. Then, through the late afternoon haze, the green sign materialized: "Exit 36." A small town, a gas station, a diner. Nothing remarkable to the casual eye.

But Eleanor wasn't casual. A familiar knot tightened in her chest, a low thrumming that was more memory than heartbeat. She squeezed the steering wheel.

Thirty years. Thirty years since she’d last taken that turn. Back then, her beat-up sedan had sputtered, choked on bad gas just beyond the off-ramp, right by the overgrown field where the old oak stood. He’d been there, fixing a flat on his own jalopy, humming some blues tune she’d never heard before. That afternoon, beneath the dusty glare of the summer sun, a different life had unfurled. A life of shared sandwiches in that diner, of late-night talks under the ancient oak, of promises whispered into the humid air. Promises made, and promises broken. Eventually, by the same exit, they had said goodbye. Not with shouts or tears, but with a quiet, mutual understanding that their paths, once intertwined by a broken-down car, had diverged irrevocably. He had turned left, towards the town, towards the life he was building. She had turned right, back onto the highway, towards… whatever lay ahead.

The current Eleanor, with her sensible car and her sensible life, glanced at the speedometer. She was going too fast to turn off now, even if she wanted to. A part of her did. A smaller, more foolish part, wanted to see if the diner still served pie that tasted like regret and sugar. Wanted to see if the oak still stood, scarred but defiant. Wanted to know if, by some cosmic joke, he might be there, fixing another flat, humming the same blues tune.

The sign receded in her rearview mirror, shrinking until it was just another green rectangle in the vast, indifferent landscape. The knot in her chest slowly loosened, replaced by the familiar ache of roads not taken. The highway stretched ahead, empty and endless, just like the future she had chosen, one exit at a time.

…poured out of the newly formed cavity, a crimson tide against the dull grey of the rock.

“What in the blazes?!” Chuck bellowed, scrambling back from the drill. Maria swore, a string of colourful expletives in three different languages. Leo, ever the pragmatist, was already reaching for his emergency flare gun.

I, however, found myself mesmerized. The red dots weren’t chaotic. They flowed with an almost liquid precision, coalescing and reforming, like iron filings drawn by an invisible magnet. And as they poured, a faint, high-pitched hum began to emanate from the opening, a sound that vibrated not just in my ears, but in my very bones.

“They’re… they’re moving *together*,” I whispered, more to myself than to the others.

The dots, now a river of crimson, began to sculpt themselves. First, a shimmering, opaque skin formed over their collective mass. Then, limbs extended, impossibly long and slender, yet with an underlying strength that hinted at immense power. A head emerged, smooth and featureless, save for two large, black, unblinking eyes that seemed to absorb all light.

It was no creature as we understood it. It was a construction, a living, breathing, humming entity made of thousands of tiny, individual units. And it was *huge*. Taller than our drilling rig, it unfurled itself from the cavity, a crimson titan stepping onto the cavern floor.

The hum intensified, and then, a voice. Not spoken, not heard, but felt. It resonated directly in our minds, a symphony of thoughts rather than words.

*“You have awoken us.”*

Chuck dropped his flare gun. Maria’s jaw hung open. Leo, for the first time since I’d known him, looked utterly lost.

*“We have slept for eons. Your… curiosity… has stirred us.”*

The red titan extended one of its impossibly long arms, a silent invitation. The air around it shimmered, and a faint, sweet scent, like ozone mixed with ancient earth, filled the cavern.

“What… what do you want?” I managed to stammer, my voice a thin reed against the overwhelming presence.

The titan tilted its featureless head. *“To observe. To learn. To remember.”*

And as it spoke, the thousands of red dots that formed its surface began to shift, to swirl, to project images directly into our minds: star-filled nebulae, ancient civilizations we’d never known, alien landscapes bathed in twin suns. It was a history of the cosmos, playing out before our terrified, awe-struck eyes, narrated by a being made of a million tiny, living fragments.

We had been drilling for oil, for resources. We had found something far, far more profound. We had found a library, and a librarian, made of light and consciousness, buried deep beneath the earth. And it was ready to share its stories.

Originally published on The Human Trafficking Project:
Today marks the culmination of National Slavery and Human Trafficking Prevention Month. President Obama proclaimed January, 2010 to be dedicated to promoting anti-trafficking work. On February first, the United States honors the signing of the resolution that formally abolished the legality of slavery in the United States on February 1st, 1865.
The 13th Amendment to the Constitution proclaimed that "Neither slavery nor involuntary servitude. . . shall exist within the United States." Though 145 years have passed, slavery continues to flourish and more people are enslaved today than ever before. Nevertheless, Freedom Day honors an important milestone for eradicating slavery and acknowledging the fundamental aberration of the crime. The day also serves as an opportunity for anti-trafficking organizations and activists to revitalize their work.
As President Obama stated in his proclamation "The United States was founded on the principle that all people are born with an unalienable right to freedom -- an ideal that has driven the engine of American progress throughout our history. As a Nation, we have known moments of great darkness and greater light; and dim years of chattel slavery illuminated and brought to an end by President Lincoln's actions and a painful Civil War. Yet even today, the darkness and inhumanity of enslavement exists. Millions of people worldwide are held in compelled service, as well as thousands within the United States. . . [W]e acknowledge that forms of slavery still exist in the modern era, and we recommit ourselves to stopping the human traffickers who ply this horrific trade."
National Freedom Day is an opportunity to honor and remember the important work to eradicate slavery that has been done and to recommit efforts to deliver on the 13th Ammendment's declaration that slavery shall not exist in the United States, while expanding that promise so that slavery does not exist anywhere in the world.
By Jennifer Kimball

The man, whose name I already knew from the whispers of the wind that carried his scent, finally heard it. Not the creak of a floorboard, or the rustle of a curtain, but the soft, almost imperceptible click of the front door locking itself from the inside. He paused, his cigarette halfway to his lips, a plume of smoke momentarily obscuring his sharp features. He hadn't heard me, not really. I was too swift, too subtle, a shadow among shadows.

He turned, a flicker of annoyance crossing his face, and tried the knob. It was solid, unyielding. He tried again, with more force this time, a low growl escaping his throat. I felt a thrill, a delicious shiver, as his frustration began to bloom. Such a delicate flower, human irritation, so easily cultivated.

He moved to the windows, testing each one. They were old, sturdy, and now, thanks to my silent ministrations, firmly sealed. He didn't smash them, not yet. He was a man of calculated moves, I knew that. A man who preferred to dissect his problems, not bludgeon them. That's why he was here, after all. To dissect me, if he only knew.

He surveyed the room again, his gaze lingering on the ornate grandfather clock, its pendulum swinging with a rhythmic, mocking tick. He looked at the peeling wallpaper, the faded floral patterns, the very air thick with the dust of forgotten lives. He was searching for me, I knew, though he didn't know what he was looking for. A draft? A loose floorboard? A glint of something unnatural?

I was everywhere and nowhere. I was the chill that suddenly permeated the room, the faint scent of decay that wasn't there a moment ago. I was the almost imperceptible hum in the electrical wiring, the subtle shift in the house's very weight.

He sat down on a dusty armchair, his suit a stark contrast to the faded fabric. He lit another cigarette, the orange glow illuminating the tension in his jaw. He pulled out his phone, but there was no signal. Of course there wasn't.

"Hello?" he called out, his voice sharp, devoid of any genuine fear, only a growing impatience. "Anyone here?"

I didn't answer. I didn't need to. I was here. And now, so was he. And the game, my dear victim, had only just begun. The corruption he carried, the darkness he nurtured, it was a feast I had been anticipating for centuries. And he, unwittingly, had brought it right to my doorstep.

i just finished watching the series, and it was such an incredible journey from start to finish. The character development throughout the seasons was phenomenal, especially watching the protagonist evolve from a naive newcomer to someone who truly understands the complexities of their world. The writing struck the perfect balance between humor and drama, never feeling forced or overly sentimental. Each episode built naturally on the last, creating momentum that made it nearly impossible to stop watching. The supporting cast brought depth and authenticity to every scene, making even minor characters feel essential to the story. The cinematography was consistently beautiful, with each location feeling like a character in its own right. While there were a few slower episodes in the middle seasons, they ultimately served the larger narrative arc perfectly. The finale was both satisfying and bittersweet, providing closure while leaving just enough open to interpretation. This series will definitely stick with me for a long time, and I'm already planning to rewatch it to catch details I surely missed the first time around.

The ocean's advance upon the beach was characterized by a gentle, finger-like undulation, subtly displacing the sand. As the tide receded, it ensnared a solitary black bowtie, drawing it into the depths. Adjacent to the bowtie lay a starched white shirt, conspicuously lacking a button near its collar. It reposed on the sand, exhibiting a placid motion in response to the breeze. Further inland, on the sidewalk, a pair of shoes, polished yet exhibiting signs of wear, were positioned meticulously side-by-side, their laces untied, oriented towards the ocean. Beyond the shoes, on the asphalt, rested a pair of black slacks. These garments, neatly folded with a belt coiled atop them, also displayed evidence of considerable use. The discernible outline of a wallet was present within a rear pocket.

Across the thoroughfare stood a church of modest dimensions, its interior vacant, its parking facility accommodating only a single vehicle. At the entrance of the church, a jacket was draped over the railing of the steps leading inward. Traversing the corridor and the foyer revealed a small chamber, adorned with floral arrangements. Within this chamber, a solitary chair, upholstered in soft red felt, displayed the effects of prolonged use. This chair was situated before a dark mahogany coffin, flanked by floral tributes bearing notes from associates and family members. Upon the chair rested a pair of rings: one of white gold, featuring a small, unadorned princess-cut diamond, exhibiting wear; the other, a simple silver band, substantial in weight and marked by scratches. An inscription was discernible within the rings: "Not even through death will we part."

**Spiritual Interrogations: Culture, Gender, and Community in Early African American Women's Writing**

*Available within 2 weeks*

**Description**

The late eighteenth century marked a significant period when many Black women arrived at the slave-trading ports of the American Northeast. These women's powerful voices played a crucial role in forming early African American communities, united by shared experiences and spiritual values. Their writings would profoundly shape African Americans' collective sense of identity as a people.

Katherine Clay Bassard's groundbreaking book offers the first comprehensive examination of pre-Emancipation writings from 1760 to 1863, exploring how African American religious culture developed alongside emerging free Black communities. Her insightful study examines the interconnections among race, culture, and community through the works of four remarkable women: poets Phillis Wheatley and Ann Plato, both Congregationalists; itinerant preacher Jarena Lee; and Shaker eldress Rebecca Cox Jackson, both of whom had ties to African Methodism.

These writers drew upon what Bassard terms a "spirituals matrix," creatively transforming established literary genres to incorporate the spiritual music and sacred rituals of the African diaspora. Bassard's illuminating scholarship brings these pioneering writers back into focus, recognizing them as co-creators—alongside all Black women who followed—of African American intellectual life.

**Table of Contents**
Ch. 1: The Daughters' Arrival: Histories, Theories, Vernaculars (p. 10)
Ch. 2: Diaspora Subjectivity and Transatlantic Crossings: Phillis Wheatley's Poetics of Recovery (p. 28)
Ch. 3: "The Too Advent'rous Strain": Slavery, Conversion, and Poetic Empowerment in Phillis Wheatley's Elegies (p. 58)
Ch. 4: "Social Piety" in Ann Plato's Essays (p. 71)
Ch. 5: "I Took a Text": Itinerancy, Community, and Intertextuality in Jarena Lee's Spiritual Narratives (p. 87)
Ch. 6: Rituals of Desire: Spirit, Culture, and Sexuality in the Writings of Rebecca Cox Jackson (p. 108)
Ch. 7: Performing Community: Culture, Community, and African American Subjectivity before Emancipation (p. 127)
Afterword: The Sacred Subject (p. 140)

**Critical Praise**
"Spiritual Interrogations marks a transformative shift in the academic disciplines that converge in Black women's studies." —Joycelyn Moody, Hamilton College, *Signs*

**Publication Details**
Series: Princeton Studies in Culture
Language: English
Publisher: Princeton University Press
Publication Date: February 1999
Pages: 192

A distinction been made between Balancers (the mentally strong who make progress) and Jugglers (those who only talk about making progress). The Thinker's Scale (a positive and a negative ruler separated by zero) is a useful tool for forming rough ideas about Jugglers, regardless of their position on the Black American social ladder. The labels for the four subdivisions of each ruler are: Mild (1to 24); Slight (25-49); Moderate (5074); and Extreme or Severe (75-99). It is the Juggler's philosophy of life; degree of organized or disorganized mindset and practices; presence or absence of self-defeating habits; and degree of reacting to the bad that ultimately determine which category is applicable and on which scale. Each category has its structural base, foundation, and above ground competency--and their props. The juggling portions of an individual's life may be occasional/ repeated/continuous; temporary/chronic; or be a portion of ones life or represent a way of life. The mentally strong (usually Doers) do good juggling and achieve goals. Observing how people cook often gives a hint as to who are or who are likely to be Jugglers.
Mild Jugglers take a long time to cook a simple meal. Slight Jugglers heat up and place on the table a salmon patty (out of a package) and corn and beans (out of separate cans). However, on the kitchen counter they use 10 pots and pans, 8 plates and saucers, 12 pieces of silverware, etc.--thereby creating as much work to do in getting things back in order as it took to cook the meal. ModerateChronic Jugglers fail to put these things back on the shelves. Extreme Chronic Jugglers allow the food on each of these to dry which, of course, requires more time, energy, and effort to get rid of that dried food. All of these Jugglers have out of control organizational skills; waste time in all they do; allow needless problems to happen, and then get bigger before taking action, if at all. When there is no attempt tohandle each problem as it arises (e.g. like washing each plate as it is used and putting it back on the shelf), then they quickly get overwhelmed with problems--and those problems create more problems. Every problem is like removing a Prop off of an organizational structure and the more props removed, the greater the magnitude of what needs to be juggled. If this pattern is present outside the kitchen and in other areas of ones life, the accumulated problems generate ripples of disorganizing mental processes and, as a result, there are fashioned various types of self-defeating patterns of thinking, feeling, and behaving present in all activities of daily living. The mental structure of Extreme Jugglers lack props; much soundness in mental structure; and a clear foundational base of Love.
By contrast, successful "Doers" have a well- structured mindset situated on a foundational Love base, an organized mind, and efficient and effective habits. Together these make for successful "Balancing." Usually Do-ers problems are caused by Be-ers. Example: A Doer (who makes well- thought out decisions and sticks with them) says to a Be-er (whose decisions change on a whim): "when you come over bring food to cook while we are waiting for the repairman" and to the Doer this is final. But the morning the repairman is to arrive, the Be-er decides on going out to eat after the repairman comes and therefore does not bring food. Now, because repairmen do not keep their word, the Doer cannot go out early to get food because the repairman might come early. Either the repairman does not show up at all or may do so hours later than he said. Meanwhile, there is no food in the house and, while waiting, both the Doer and Be-er have to juggle things in order to eat. The point is that not paying attention to detail; not handling little things; not taking care of problems when they arise; not cleaning up; not putting things back where they belong after completing a job; leaving a job for others to do; and not sticking with a decision are each bad habits that generate vicious cycles of juggling and failures.

I woke up this morning and I could instantly tell something was wrong. I looked around and all I saw was a grass field. Even though I usually Love the Outdoors this was too much for me. I called for a cab to take me home. When the cab came I walked to it, but the next thing I remember was being inside the cab. It was like I teleported in, because I have no memory of entering the cab. When I got home the same thing happened, but this time I left the cab. I shook it off assuming that I was just taking micronaps as a result of sleeping outdoors. Feeling hungry I went inside my house to my fridge. Usually I Love to Cook, but this morning I found myself craving cereal. I am usually a very Frugal person, but I never craved cereal. Who the fuck craves for bland cheeiros? I got my cereal an walked to my dinner table in another room. When I got there I couldn't find my goddamn table. Where the fuck was my fucking table. What the fuck was going on today. I walked to the only available seat, my toilet, and ate the bland cereal which I craved so much. While sitting on my toilet I heard a honking outside. "Shit"I thought"gotta go to work". I work as a police officer so I generally try to keep my fitness level up but due to a recent promotion I was going to have to make friends with my partner. The workday passed in a blur which it usually does, but this blur was much blanker. I can't remember a single thing I did that day, but I could feel I was closer to a promotion. Knowing I would have to make nice with my partner I invited him to my house. When he arrived I greeted him at the door and there we chatted. But we chatted for hours. Right there in front of my house. I knew that something was weird but, all I wanted to do was to chat with my partner. At around midnight my partner became a bit uncomfortable. I could tell that it was because of my Flirty personality. Just as I was about to go in for our first kiss. Right as I heard that music I heard every single time I kissed someone for the first time, I froze. Everything froze, me, him a car going down the street, everything. I heard a loud voice boom "Goddammit! I'm gonna lose all this progress". Then I looked up. That's when I saw it. The thing that had caused me to behave so weirdly all day. A giant floating green diamond.

This umbrella looks AWESOME out of the box. But as you will soon find out the connection between the top pole and bottom pole of the umbrella is VERY weak. It looks nice but is TRASH and very poorly made (in China)!

By John Stevens. PUBLISHED: 08:25 EST, 4 March 2014. UPDATED: 04:44 EST, 5 March 2014. A teacher has been awarded £230,000 in compensation after slipping on a ketchup sachet while walking down a hallway. He suffered severe injuries in the fall, which occurred as he was walking out of the staff room at a secondary school in Essex. The personal injury claim was settled outside court by council chiefs, who believed they may have been forced to pay up to £500,000, including legal fees, if the case had gone ahead. The payout was one of 34 handed to injured teachers in the county between 2011 and 2013, with a total of £1.05 million paid out. Critics have complained of a 'compensation culture' in schools, citing cases where staff claimed for slipping on wet floors or being injured by a cupboard door. The teacher in the ketchup incident was hurt on March 18, 2008, but did not file a claim until March 10, 2011, just eight days before the three-year legal time limit for making personal injury claims. A Freedom of Information request revealed that the council agreed to a final settlement of £230,000 in April last year, including £90,000 in damages for pain, suffering, and loss of amenity, as well as damages for losses such as those relating to earnings and pension. The payout also included £120,000 for the teacher's legal fees and £20,000 in other costs. Other claims include a £110,000 settlement to a staff member who suffered a spinal injury after slipping on a wet floor on two separate occasions on the same day, and a £39,450 payout to an employee who suffered a wrist injury when a faulty metal door swung open. Jonathan Isaby of the TaxPayers' Alliance said, 'The compensation culture appears to be more common in the school yard than a grazed knee. Wasting taxpayers' money on unnecessary payouts leaves less money available for teaching and higher tax bills for everyone else.' He added, 'Legal costs on top of the actual payouts only add to the overall bill, and the whole process undermines the ability of teachers to actually teach. It's time schools resisted unscrupulous claims before teachers are unable to leave the staff room for fear of the school being sued.' However, Jerry Glazier, the National Union of Teachers representative for Essex, said there is no compensation culture in schools. He stated, 'We need to see these claims in context. As a union, we will support members who pursue claims for personal injury when we feel they have been poorly treated by management in schools. Schools are generally very safe environments, but employers have a duty of care to protect both teachers and pupils.' Essex County Council said it takes 'health, safety, and wellbeing' seriously, adding that all compensation claims are investigated and damages paid if the authority is liable.

"let me go!" cried jesus. "please, let me go!"  
The crowd paused, stunned by the sound. Where he knelt on the dust, his wrists caught in the tangled branches of an old olive tree, he strained toward an errant, silvery ray of morning sunlight.

The soldiers hesitated. In the garden's trembling shadows, their hands faltered on rough rope.

"No," one muttered, voice empty, "we can’t..." But even in his refusal, his eyes avoided the desperation in Jesus’ own. The wind rustled, scattering petals and dust alike.

Jesus twisted toward them—not with anger, but with the wild, childish terror of any living thing who has glimpsed annihilation. "Let me go," he whispered again, and now it was not a demand but a plea, broken as the dawn.

One soldier shivered. Another, quiet and afraid, stepped forward. His hand shook as he reached for the knot—yet the others closed ranks, turning away, muttering their duty to the dark.

The garden held its breath. Somewhere, a bird started and took flight.

"You must," said the leader, voice thick with shame. "We have been told. Please... forgive us."

His sob cut the quiet. But they did not let him go. The light crept further through the branches, and the world, helpless, turned inevitably onward.

## Review: The AeroPress Go Portable Coffee Maker

Oh, the AeroPress Go. You tiny, unassuming cylinder of a device. You promise the world in a compact package, a beacon of expertly brewed coffee for the weary traveler, the desperate camper, the perpetually on-the-go caffeine fiend. And you deliver, in a way that’s almost… unsettling in its efficiency.

My usual morning ritual involves a monstrous, gleaming espresso machine that I lovingly polish and coax into producing liquid gold. It's a performance, a full-blown production. Then I met the AeroPress Go, and it felt like a betrayal. Like trading in a grand symphony orchestra for a lone, incredibly talented busker on a street corner. But damn, that busker can play.

The sheer, unadulterated *ease* of this thing is almost offensive. You don't plug it in. You don't need a PhD in barista science. You scoop, you pour, you press. And out comes a cup of coffee that punches far, far above its weight class. It’s not just good, it’s *consistently* good. Whether I’m camping in the wilderness, crammed into a hotel room, or just can’t be bothered with the elaborate dance of my bigger machine, the Go delivers. There's no bitterness, no sediment, just a smooth, rich, undeniably satisfying brew. It's a minimalist’s dream, a utilitarian masterpiece.

But let's not pretend this simplicity doesn't come with its own stark reality. There's no romance here, no ritualistic pleasure of the brewing process. It's pure, unadulterated, efficient extraction. It's the equivalent of a highly trained, emotionless efficiency expert designing your morning coffee. It strips away all the ornamentation, all the frills, and leaves you with just the essential, delicious outcome. It’s almost a little cold in its perfection.

The "Go" aspect is truly remarkable. Everything fits inside itself, a Russian doll of coffee paraphernalia. The plunger becomes a mug, the stirrer slots neatly into the filter cap. It’s so self-contained, so self-sufficient, it’s almost arrogant. It practically sneers at your bulky, cumbersome coffee solutions. It judges your elaborate grinders and your delicate pour-over apparatuses. It simply *works*. And it works *well*.

For anyone who demands quality coffee without the fuss, without the ceremony, without the slightest impediment to their forward momentum, the AeroPress Go is a revelation. It’s a stark reminder that sometimes the most impressive solutions are the ones that are stripped down to their bare, functional essence. It might not inspire poetic musings or romantic sighs, but it will deliver a damn good cup of coffee, every single time. And in its own quiet, efficient way, that’s a powerful statement.

The girl at reception wore a crisp navy suit and glasses that glinted under fluorescent lights. She smiled as she waved Arjun through the reinforced glass doors of the Summit Directorate. Somewhere in that smile was a trace of challenge—as if she knew a great deal more than she let on.

Security scanned his retina. The corridor led Arjun past surveillance screens buzzing with garbled audio. On level nine, the mission control doors opened at his approach, because Arjun had the right badge and, more importantly, the right face. He paused in front of the operations board, scanning the digital map, heartbeat calm despite the flicker of nerves. His handler should be here.

But he was not Arjun Singh, and today he would not meet his handler. He was Gabriel—the real Arjun incapacitated, somewhere far from here, a fate he preferred not to imagine. Gabriel had thirty minutes to retrieve the codes and exit before his luck ran out.

A man swept in, eyes flinty. "Singh, we have a problem. The asset in Riyadh went dark. You authorized new comms protocols?"

"Yes," Gabriel lied, voice steady.

"You'll brief the Director."

He nodded, chest tight.

On level twelve, review screens flickered. The Director—impressive, remote, unforgiving—studied him. "The traitor used your access," she said. "Did you leak protocol?"

Gabriel's mouth went dry. "No, ma'am. My credentials are secure." His voice shook, just enough to sound nervous—like Arjun would sound.

A warning bleated on the console. "Unauthorized code access," someone muttered, and suddenly, pistols glinted from holsters as guards poured in.

"On the floor!"

Gabriel froze, every muscle at attention.

From behind, a familiar voice: "Director, that's not Arjun Singh."

The receptionist, her glasses now off, producing a badge marked Internal Security. She leveled her gun at Gabriel. "Our friend here failed the silent distress code. The real Singh never steps with his left foot first."

The standoff crumpled into chaos—alarms, shouts, the Director shouting orders. As Gabriel was cuffed, he caught the tiniest quirk at the corner of the receptionist’s mouth. Professional pride, or relief—he couldn’t tell.

In espionage, he supposed, no identity was ever truly secure.

It is the latest in a series of US military actions seen as a response to China's increasingly assertive claims over territory in the South China Sea.
The US says it will also base a military reconnaissance plane at Singapore's Paya Lebar air base.
US P-8s already operate from Japan and the Philippines, and surveillance flights have taken off from Malaysia.
The P-8 was deployed on Monday, and will remain in Singapore until 14 December.
In addition to the P-8 deployment, the US says it will operate a military plane, either a P-8 Poseidon or a P-3 Orion, from Singapore for the foreseeable future, rotating planes on a quarterly basis.
The US-Singapore agreement, announced after a meeting in Washington on Monday between US Defense Secretary Ash Carter and Singapore Defence Minister Ng Eng Hen, also covers co-operation on counter-terrorism, fighting piracy, and disaster relief.
Speaking at a daily news briefing, Chinese foreign ministry spokeswoman Hua Chunying said the US was "pushing regional militarisation", which did "not accord with the joint long-term interests" of the region.
Despite rising tension with China over its South China Sea territorial claims, a US navy spokesman based in Singapore insisted that the deployments were "not solely focussed on the South China Sea".
"It's not about the South China Sea, it's about partnership with Singapore and other partners in the region," said Lt Commander Arlo Abrahamson, speaking to the BBC.
He pointed to regional co-operation in the search for the missing Malaysian Airlines flight MH370 and the Air Asia plane that crashed on 28 December 2014, as examples of the kinds of operations that would be helped by a base in Singapore.
A US navy statement also said that the deployment was "in support of shared maritime security and HA-DR (High Availability and Disaster Recovery) initiatives in the Indo-Asia-Pacific region."
China claims almost the entire South China Sea, putting it in territorial dispute with several countries, including Vietnam, the Philippines and Malaysia, among others. It has been building islands, equipped with runways, in the area.
China also has a separate dispute with Japan over islands in the East China Sea.

"The person you are trying to call is unavailable," the automated voice said. "Please hang up and try your call again." "Oh what the fuck," Bob sighed. The last three packages sent to him by Amazon were empty boxes. Bob ordered Xbox 360 games, taking advantage of lowered prices since the release of the Xbox One. He'd been waiting for *Fallout: New Vegas* for what seemed to him a long, long time--three or four days, anyway. Bob'd only called Amazon five or six times; significantly less than the last time this happened. Earlier that week the tap water'd turned a brownish colour--now he couldn't even take that shower he'd been planning. In terms of other necessities Bob hadn't worried in quite some time. He lived on dry foods stored for the better part of the past year. His mother sent him boxes and boxes with food when he first moved out, but hadn't sent anything in a while. Crates of Redbull and Mountain Dew lined his walls. *Well, I guess I could go over there and see what's going on*, he thought. *It's only a fifteen minute walk*. Bob saved his game, tried smelling himself, and stepped outside into the fresh white sunlight. The light's whiteness was unmatched in comparison to the whiteness of his skin--a skin that hadn't met that light in months. The walk over had been quiet and empty--not unlike childhood sick days spent in the quiet workweek of suburbia. He walked through the front door of the Amazon headquarters. No one answered his mumbled calls. Nauseous and uncomfortable, he decided to go back home. _______________________________________________________________________________ "The person you are trying to call is unavailable," the automated voice said. "Please hang up and try your call again." --Bob heard this over and over. His mother wouldn't answer, his friend wouldn't answer, the Gamestop down the road wouldn't answer. Bob spent much of the next couple weeks looking out the window. In this time he saw three cars pass along what used to be a traffic jammed street during rush hour. Had the rapture his mother went on and on about finally come? He roamed the world of *Fallout 3*, picturing himself in a similarly emptied wasteland. And then, a couple weeks later, he awoke not to the one-PM sunlight shining into his eyes, but to the triple-knocking delivery man. "UPS," the voice called. "Hello?" Bob jumped to his feet and answered the door in one motion. "Hi," Bob said. "Hi, hi." "Mr. Smith?" "Yes, that's me." "Few packages here for you." "Thanks," he said. "Thanks so much." Bob looked past the man at four great boxes stacked one upon the other. "All this?" "Seems like it," he said. "Sent from Missouri." "Oh." Bob closed the door with tears in his eyes. Inside the packages were bags of dried foods. The note read, "LOVE YOU. COME HOME IF FEELING LONELY. ALWAYS AVAILABLE. -- MOM" Bob keeled over and wept. After a few minutes he got up, packed a knapsack and headed into the ever-white light.

A 47-year-old woman has been charged with sexually molesting a 12-year-old boy by sneaking into his bedroom to perform oral sex. The alleged victim told investigators that Keturah Zillah Gibson would wake him after 2am and carry out the sex acts. He said she would kiss him and perform oral sex at the house they shared in Pembroke Pines, Florida. Keturah Zillah Gibson, 47, has been charged with multiple counts of lewd and lascivious battery and molestation of a 12-year-old who lived in the same house in Pembroke Pines, Florida . Gibson was charged on Monday with multiple counts of lewd and lascivious battery and molestation of a 12-year-old boy and with tampering with a victim. According to police Gibson sent instant . messages to the boy telling him if their encounters were ever discovered . he was to say they 'never ever' had sex. Gibson, who doesn't have a police record, has denied the accusations and said she never performed sexual acts on the boy. During two police interviews Gibson told detectives the boy had kissed her, attempted to place her hand on his buttocks on one occasion, and that the attention made her feel good. The victim told a different story and alleged between June 2012 and December 2013 they had between five and ten sexual encounters. The boy said Gibson sent him a message . with instructions that he should not tell anyone, that she had spoken . with an attorney and that as long as there was no sexual activity she . could not be arrested. Gibson was ordered to surrender her passport and has been detained in Broward County Jail on $110,000 bond . Gibson was arrested on Sunday after the boy's mum said she wanted to bring charges. When Gibson appeared before Broward County Judge John Hurley on multiple lewd and lascivious charges she was ordered to stay away from her alleged victim. Gibson was also charged with witness tampering and molestation. She was also ordered to surrender her passport and remained in a Broward jail Tuesday on $110,000 bond, records show. Hurley appointed a Guardian ad Litem for the boy and asked a prosecutor to notify the Florida Department of Children and Families. Video: woman charged with molesting a 12-year-old .

Back in 1986, the British Broadcasting Company embarked on one of the most ambitious digital publishing efforts of all time: The Domesday Project. Its goal was to build a modern equivalent of the Domesday Book, the massive record commissioned in 1086 by William the Conqueror to document all of England’s property and subjects. The BBC assembled a huge archive—two videodiscs filled with photographs, video, and more than 300 megabytes of text. In total, more than a million people contributed to the project.

However, in March of this year, The Observer newspaper reported a troubling development: just 16 years after its creation, the $4.3 million BBC Domesday Project was no longer accessible. The special computers required to play the 12-inch videodiscs had become obsolete, and as a result, no one could access the wealth of information collected about Britain in 1986. In stark contrast, the original Domesday Book from 1086 is still in good condition and can be read by anyone with the right credentials.

This situation has alarmed computer scientists and archivists, who warn that we are in danger of losing our cultural heritage—at least the parts stored electronically—due to rapidly outdated technology. They argue that digital information is more fragile than its physical counterparts.

But this interpretation is misleading. The challenge of accessing old digital data is real, but not insurmountable. At a conference, David Stork, chief scientist at Ricoh Innovations, discussed the need for systems that guarantee long-term retrieval of digital information. He pointed out that documents stored using outdated software or formats are often difficult to access even a few years later, and advocated for dedicated efforts and resources to ensure digital preservation.

Looking at the Domesday Project, it’s true that the original discs required specialized hardware and became quickly obsolete. However, determined engineers and preservationists managed to copy the data from the original discs onto modern systems and created software to emulate the BBC Micro computer, allowing today’s PCs to access the material. Although the process was costly and time-consuming, it demonstrated that digital recovery is possible when the information is deemed valuable.

The main lesson of the Domesday Project is not that electronic information is inherently ephemeral, but that using nonstandard or proprietary file formats creates significant challenges and hidden long-term costs. The BBC initially saved money by using a videodisc format, but these savings have been offset by the resources needed to transfer and preserve the data for the future. The true problem is not digital storage itself, but failing to plan for the inevitable need to migrate data as technology changes.

**The Animated Movie Guide: A Comprehensive Journey Through Animation History**

★★★★☆

Jerry Beck's "The Animated Movie Guide" stands as an impressive compendium that tackles the ambitious task of cataloging and reviewing animated films from around the world. This hefty reference book succeeds in being both encyclopedic and accessible, making it valuable for animation enthusiasts, film students, and casual viewers alike.

The book's greatest strength lies in its remarkable scope. Beck doesn't limit himself to mainstream Disney features or American productions, instead casting a wide net that includes international films, independent works, and lesser-known gems that might otherwise be forgotten. The entries are well-organized chronologically, allowing readers to trace the evolution of animation techniques and storytelling approaches over decades.

Each film entry provides essential information including plot summaries, technical details, and critical assessment. Beck's writing is clear and engaging, avoiding overly academic jargon while still providing substantive analysis. His obvious passion for the medium shines through, particularly when discussing groundbreaking works or defending unfairly maligned films.

The inclusion of both theatrical releases and made-for-TV movies broadens the book's utility, though this occasionally leads to entries that feel less essential. Some readers might question the inclusion of certain obscure titles while more significant works receive relatively brief treatment.

The book's production values are solid, with a clean layout that makes information easy to locate. However, the limited number of illustrations feels like a missed opportunity, especially for a visual medium like animation.

While "The Animated Movie Guide" may not be the final word on every film it covers, it serves as an excellent starting point for exploration and a reliable reference for settling debates about animated cinema's rich history.
