Third-grade students from across Maury County spent a day down on the farm learning how to keep bees, saddle horses, churn butter and milk cows.
The University of Tennessee’s Middle Tennessee Education and Research Center in Spring Hill opened up its gates to students, teachers and parents to teach them the importance of agriculture. Children were able to get hands-on experience with animals as well as learn the processes used to bring milk, eggs, butter and beef from farm to table.
Students were able to see a wide variety of animals up close, such as goats, cows and horses. Lauryn Francis, a student from McDowell Elementary, said she has been to farms before but Tuesday’s field trip was a new experience.
“I’ve never been on a farm like this before,” she said. “I liked seeing the horses and petting the animals. I got to pet a sheep and a goat.”
Ashley Graham, another student from McDowell, said she also enjoyed the petting zoo, especially getting to pet the rabbits brought in by local farmers.
“I have been on my Pappaw Jack’s farm before, but this is different,” she said. “I learned about all the tools they use to put out fires on the farm.”
David Hemsley of Columbia is a goat farmer and member of the Middle Tennessee Goat Association who volunteered his time to teach students about the animals. Hemsley said being able to touch animals is important for local students, many of whom have little or no experience with livestock.
“We want to educate children about the value of goats and the value of farming,” Hemsley said. “We want to increase their understanding ... so maybe one day if they want to get a little piece of land they might consider a goat as a farm animal.”
Bugs and insects were among the other farm creatures students learned about. Marcus White, a student at Spring Hill Elementary School, said the field trip was his first time on a farm and he got to learn about how insects from bees to spiders help with agriculture.
“We got to see bees,” White said. “I didn’t know you could keep them in your back yard. I also learned that hornets live in the ground.”
Brayden Ciphers, another student from Spring Hill Elementary, said he enjoyed spending time with his friends while learning about the different farm animals.
“I’ve never been to a place like this before,” he said. “I learned all about different kinds of spiders. I liked looking at all the different types of bugs and learning about them.”
Students also got to learn how the milk from a cow can be used for drinking or turned into butter. Ann Russell is a member of Tennessee Farm Bureau Women and spent the day demonstrating how butter is made to students. Many of the churns and molds Russell brought for her demonstration were from her own family collection.
“There are very few children now who are raised on farms,” Russell said. “They think everything comes from a store. We want to let them know where it all really comes from.”
Culleoka Unit School student Annsley Morrow said her favorite part of the day was seeing the dairy cows and where her milk comes from.
“Seeing them getting milked was fun, but I didn’t know you had to wake up so early in the morning to do it,” she said. “I live on a farm, and I want to help out more now.”
Cindy Garner, a teacher at Culleoka, said experiencing farm life gives students a broader experience.
“They get to see and do things they ordinarily don’t,” she said. “They’ve loved seeing the milk barn, the baby calves and seeing the cows getting milked.”
Parents and grandparents also were able to learn a little from the process. Randy Davidson, grandfather of a student at Culleoka Unit School, said he felt it was important for his grandson to learn the importance of animals on the farm and the wide variety of animals farmer use.
“This has helped him learn the purpose of each animal,” he said. “It’s a great educational process for them to see and touch these animals.”
Suzanne Sumerel, a mother of a student at Spring Hill Elementary, said she wants her daughter to see food doesn’t just come from a store.
“As a society, we’ve gotten away from agriculture, so it’s good they are getting exposed to this at such a young age,” Sumerel said. “I’ve learned a lot about bugs today myself.”

The architectural concept for the design of the theatre is an archetypal scenic street. The design is based on the most direct forms of theatre of the past, such as the Italian Commedia del Arte, and the Elizabethan theatres with galleried courtyards, such as Shakespeare’s Globe Theatre. It gives rise to theatrical activity by encouraging both actors and audience to see and be seen.
The theatre is a spatial progression starting from the pavement of the Mile End Road in the East End of London, through the gateway into an open air scenic space, through the steel curtain wall to the covered scenic street. Large doors lead into a small garden with a pavilion, the Young Peoples' Theatre. This assortment of theatrical spaces constitutes an a small world of theatres.
When the Greater London Council was abolished by the government led by Mrs. Thatcher in the mid 1980's the Theatre company no longer recieved public funding and were forced to sell the building. The Half Moon Theatre has since been converted into a pub.

**Exploring Weather Patterns with Young Scientists: A Third Grade Journey**

By Ms. Taylor Johnson, 3rd Grade, Green Valley Elementary  
Springfield, Illinois

Last year, I launched a weather observation project with my third graders, and it transformed the way my students understood science. The idea was simple: empower students to become weather detectives, collecting daily data and comparing their local results with partner classrooms across the country. The experience was engaging, educational, and full of surprises.

**Getting Started: Building Our Weather Station**

We began by assembling a basic classroom weather station. Using simple tools—a thermometer, a homemade rain gauge (a clear plastic jar marked with measurements), and a wind sock crafted in art class—we were ready to start collecting weather data. Each student was assigned a week to act as the official weather reporter, responsible for recording temperature, precipitation, wind direction, and cloud cover.

**Daily Data Collection and Discussion**

Every morning, our weather reporter presented their findings to the class. We kept a large weather chart on the wall to track trends. Students quickly noticed patterns: how temperatures dipped after rain, or how gusty days often brought changes in cloud types. These observations sparked lively discussions and questions: Why does it rain more in spring? What causes wind to change direction?

**Connecting with Partner Classrooms**

To broaden our understanding, we partnered with two other third grade classes—one in Arizona and one in Maine. Each class shared their weather data weekly through a secure online platform. This comparison was eye-opening for my students. In January, while we bundled up against icy winds, our friends in Arizona reported sunny, warm days. Conversely, our Maine partners tracked snow accumulation and freezing temperatures.

**Building Scientific Skills**

Throughout the project, students learned more than just facts about weather. They practiced careful observation, accurate recording, and respectful collaboration. They also developed mapping skills as we used U.S. maps to locate our partner schools and discuss how geography affects climate.

**Reflecting on the Experience**

By the end of the year, my students could explain not just what the weather was like, but *why* it changed. They learned to connect local observations to broader patterns, and they discovered the joy of sharing science with peers far away. The project turned abstract science concepts into real-world learning and fostered curiosity that extended beyond the classroom.

Next year, we plan to add a new twist: creating simple weather forecasts based on our data and sharing them in video messages with our partner classes. I can’t wait to see where our scientific adventures take us next!

---

This project is a wonderful way for elementary students to practice observation, collaboration, and critical thinking—all while having fun exploring the world around them. Why not try it with your class?

Their ability to travel, multiply, and spread disease has always made rats one of mankind's greatest pests. New Zealand says it's time to wipe them out—every last one of them.

Prime Minister John Key on Monday announced an ambitious plan to completely rid the South Pacific nation of rats and some other nuisance animals, including possums and stoats, by the year 2050. The government is hoping a rat-free countryside will give a boost to native birds, including the iconic kiwi. Many bird species are threatened with extinction because rats and other pests feast on their eggs and compete with them for food. New Zealand is hoping to build on its success in eradicating rats from several of its smaller islands.

However, some scientists caution that the goal, while laudable, will be extremely difficult to achieve in a nation that's similar in size to the United Kingdom. Speaking from a wildlife sanctuary in Wellington, Key said the goal would require the help of everyone from philanthropists to indigenous Maori tribes.

"This is the most ambitious conservation project attempted anywhere in the world, but we believe if we all work together as a country we can achieve it," he said.

He said the government would initially contribute 28 million New Zealand dollars ($20 million) over four years toward setting up a company to run the program, and would consider partially matching money contributed by local councils and businesses. Key acknowledged the goal would only be achieved with scientific advances.

New Zealand's Department of Conservation has eradicated rats from several small islands using traps, poisons, and baits. It has also intensively managed some areas on the main islands to make them safer for native birds. But it would require a massive escalation of those efforts to completely wipe out the pests.

Ecologist James Russell, from the University of Auckland, has written about the idea before. "I really do think it's possible," he said. "It will require people working in every nook and corner of the country."

He said getting rid of the pests would make a huge difference to the health of native flora and fauna. New Zealand is unusual in that its native animals are mainly birds, some of which became flightless over time. When humans arrived and brought rats, the rodents had few predators.

Jacqueline Beggs, another ecologist from the university, said eliminating pests from small, uninhabited islands was one thing, but getting everybody from farmers to anti-government types to agree on the idea would prove much more difficult, if not impossible.

"It's definitely a fantastic challenge," she said. "It will really stretch the boundaries."

Beggs said she also worries the goal could distract from other important environmental issues and could even create new problems, such as an explosion in the population of mice.

It was dark here. Even at noon, so little sunlight filtered down through the thick treetops of the Amazon that it the whole land was plunged into an eternal twilight. As the sun sank below the horizon, what little light there was evaporated, leaving only the heat. Gabriel Freitas had been walking for days. At least, he thought it was days. It was hard to tell in this place. Every step felt the same as the last, and there was no way of knowing how far he had walked, or how many nights had slipped away. But he was still alive, and that was something. He knew all the tricks. Find a river, or at least a stream, and follow it. Eventually, they all led to civilization. Never sleep on the bare ground, and always keep your weapon ready to scare away the larger predators. Gabriel didn't really worry about any of that. It was the little monsters that were the worst in this alien environment. But by far the hardest part was staying alert. It was so easy to drift...to find yourself mindlessly marching, one foot in front of the other. But here, the vegetation was so dense you could be marching five feet from a highway that would take you home and never even notice it. You had to stay alert. So perhaps it wasn't a surprise when Gabriel noticed the familiar, bubbling sound of water behind the hum of jungle life. His mind started to race as he turned his head, tracking the sound, then set off through the underbrush, alternating between thanking god and cursing the jungle that He had created. The clearing came suddenly, and without warning, as all things in the Amazon do. In an instant, the ground underfoot turned from wet, mossy soil to hard, slick stone, and the darkened jungle gave way to a small clearing, illuminated by the silvery light of the full moon. In the center, shallow pool reflected perfectly the thousand million stars of the Milky Way, and the smiling face of the moon. Gabriel doubled over, every ragged breath catching in his lungs as the surroundings hit him. A long, uncomfortable silence fell over the clearing, the only sound, the beating of Gabriel Frietas's heart. After what seemed like an eternity, he finally breathed out the breath he hadn't realized he had been holding. His first steps towards the fountain were slow, faltering. He stopped at the very edge, peering down into the clear, shining depths of the water. Without thinking, he shed he clothes, his unsteady steps towards the water becoming more sure with every footfall. Then, once again holding his breath, he plunged downward, through the water, into the abyss, unsure of what to expect. _________________________________________________________ Gabriel Freitas opened his eyes to a room lit dimly by firelight, and he screamed. Above him, strange faces looked down with wonder and delight. The midwife took the hand of his mother, and, with a knowing smile, and said, "It's a boy, Mrs. Freitas. A beautiful baby boy."

In this ongoing series of making games on the classroom, I’ve been taking a look at a number of user-friendly tools for making interactive content, including:
Every tool I’ve examined has different strengths, and ultimately choosing the right tool for your own project or a class assignment can determine its success. I’ve been looking for ways to change up my digital narrative & culture class, so I’ve been auditioning a number of other free platforms to see what they might offer. One of my favorites so far is Ren’Py, a free, open source tool for making visual novels.
Visual novels are a form of playable narrative that typically emphasizes static graphics and a branching narrative. While many of the works using the form emphasize character relationships (and are often romances) and anime graphics, the fundamental structures are very flexible. Here are a few Ren’Py games for inspiration: Analogue: A Hate Story and Digital: A Love Story (both by designer Christine Love). Both are some of the most impressive Ren’Py works I’ve played, and include a range of interfaces and commentary on social and technological institutions. There’s also a huge list of Ren’Py games, with many of them available for free.
Unlike some of the tools I’ve discussed previously in the Games in the Classroom series, Ren’Py does require programming. However, the scripting for basic game-making is fairly accessible, and it can actually provide a gateway to learning Python. The easiest way to get started is to use the Quickstart tutorial: it takes you through making a basic conversation model. Dialogue is at the heart of Ren’Py, and building (and illustrating) choice-based conversations is very simple. For a class project, a simple one-encounter game based on the model would be very effective and could serve as a way to integrate simple coding in an engaging way.
I’d love to see someone make a textbook or learning experience with Ren’Py. It would certainly make a lot of sense for modeling language learning through conversations, moral dilemmas and choices, historical situations, and other types of experiences.
Have you tried Ren’Py? Share your tips in the comments!Return to Top

A U.S. drone strike that killed the Pakistan Taliban's leader last week won't derail government efforts at peace talks with the group, Pakistan's prime minister said Monday. "A drone strike under these circumstances has harmed the dialogue and peace efforts of the government," Prime Minister Nawaz Sharif's office said in a statement. "But we believe that we will not ... allow the dialogue and peace efforts to get derailed." At a Cabinet meeting Monday, ministers decried drone attacks as "unacceptable" and called them a violation of Pakistan's sovereignty. "Diplomatic efforts will be continued to stop these attacks," the statement said. "Given Pakistani peoples' resolve and sacrifices in this war against terrorism, it is incumbent upon the international community to support this endeavor of the government for the accomplishment of peace. The government of Pakistan will not allow any internal or external force to sabotage the dialogue process." The meeting came three days after a U.S. drone strike killed Pakistan Taliban leader Hakimullah Mehsud. Mehsud, who had a $5 million U.S. bounty on his head for his alleged involvement in the 2009 attack on a U.S. base in Afghanistan, was killed in northwestern Pakistan Friday, senior U.S. and Pakistani officials told CNN. On Monday, Pakistani jihadists distributed a photograph purporting to show Mehsud before his burial and describing him as a martyr, the SITE Intelligence Group said. New leader elected . An official with the Pakistan Taliban said Sunday that the group had voted to elect Asmatullah Shaheen, who is on Pakistan's most-wanted list, as its interim head. The group, also known as Tehrik-e-Taliban Pakistan, will not be making an announcement on a new permanent leader for several days, according to Azam Tariq, a member of the Pakistan Taliban's Shura Council. Meanwhile, the U.S. ambassador to Islamabad was summoned to Pakistan's Foreign Ministry on Saturday. Foreign Ministry spokesman Aizaz Ahmed Chaudhry did not explain why the envoy was summoned. A U.S. State Department official confirmed the meeting but would not disclose details about it. Afghan President criticizes U.S. drone strike that killed Pakistani Taliban leader . Pakistan Taliban claimed Times Square plot . The Pakistan Taliban, who have long been conducting an insurgency against the Pakistani government, claimed responsibility for the December 2009 suicide bombing at the United States' Forward Operating Base Chapman in Khost, Afghanistan. Five CIA officers were among the seven U.S. citizens killed, along with a member of Jordanian intelligence. The group also claimed responsibility for a failed May 2010 attempt to detonate a car bomb in New York's Times Square. The following September, the U.S. State Department designated the Pakistan Taliban as a foreign terrorist organization. Pakistan has stepped up security, and officials have said they're bracing for reprisals after Friday's drone strike. Pakistan summons U.S. envoy after death of Pakistan Taliban leader . Prospects for peace . Mehsud's death could make progress in proposed peace talks between the Pakistani government and Pakistan Taliban more difficult. A relatively young and charismatic leader, he held together a disparate group with different tribal and other allegiances. His killing may upset some elements within the Pakistan Taliban if they believe that the Pakistani government was involved. The Pakistan Taliban could also pose an increased security threat if they splinter into smaller groups, which could be harder for security forces to detect. CNN's Shaista Aziz, David Simpson and Susanna Capelouto contributed to this report.

**Mis-Teeq – "Scandalous" ★★★★☆**

Mis-Teeq delivers another infectious slice of UK garage-pop perfection with "Scandalous," proving once again why they're one of Britain's most compelling girl groups. The track opens with a hypnotic blend of shuffling beats and sultry vocals that immediately draws you into its web of attitude and swagger.

Alesha Dixon, Sabrina Washington, and Su-Elise Nash trade verses with effortless chemistry, their harmonies weaving together over a production that's both polished and gritty. The song's backbone lies in its irresistible bassline and crisp percussion, creating a groove that's impossible to resist. Lyrically, "Scandalous" embraces its provocative title with tongue-in-cheek confidence, celebrating feminine power and allure without crossing into exploitation.

What sets this track apart is its sophisticated production – the layered vocals, subtle string arrangements, and perfectly timed breakdowns showcase a group that understands both commercial appeal and artistic integrity. The chorus is undeniably catchy, built for both radio play and club floors, while maintaining the distinctive UK garage influence that defines Mis-Teeq's sound.

While "Scandalous" doesn't quite reach the heights of their breakthrough hit "All I Want," it solidifies their position as masters of their craft. This is smart, sexy pop music that respects its audience's intelligence while delivering pure entertainment. A worthy addition to any early 2000s playlist.

The dust motes danced in the late Texas sun, illuminating the haze rising from the asphalt. Stone Cold Steve Austin leaned back in his beat-up pickup, a cold beer sweating in his calloused hand, the radio spitting out classic rock. It was a damn good day. Until it wasn’t.

A shadow, not of a cloud, but something vast and obsidian, fell over his ranch. A hum vibrated through the ground, rattling the beer can in his hand. A colossal, silent craft descended, metallic tendrils unfolding from its underside like grotesque petals. It landed with a soft *thud* that belied its size, kicking up a plume of red dirt.

From its gaping maw, three figures emerged. Tall, slender, their skin an oily grey, eyes like black, bottomless pools. They wore no clothes, only an aura of alien superiority. The lead figure, slightly larger, extended a multi-jointed digit.

*“Earthling. We have come to claim this quadrant. Resistance is… illogical.”* The voice wasn’t in his ears, but directly in his skull, cold and clinical.

Stone Cold took a long, slow sip of his beer. He eyed the aliens, then his truck, then the beer again. He crushed the empty can in his fist, the metallic *ping* shockingly loud in the sudden silence.

“Logical, huh?” he drawled, his voice a gravelly rumble. He pushed himself up, his denim vest catching the light. “Lemme tell you sumthin’ about logic, pal. Logic ain’t got a damn thing to do with a good ol’ fashioned can of whoop-ass.”

The aliens exchanged a telepathic flicker of what might have been confusion. The leader gestured. Energy lances, thin and vibrant blue, shot from the hands of the other two, tearing up the ground just inches from Austin’s feet.

Stone Cold didn’t flinch. He just grinned, a dangerous glint in his eyes. “You ain’t from around here, are ya? You think you can just show up on *my* ranch, tell *me* what’s logical, and shoot at the Texas Rattlesnake?” He pointed a finger at the lead alien. “You just opened a whole can of worms you ain’t ready for.”

He reached behind him, grabbed two fresh beers from a cooler. He slammed them together with a mighty *CLINK*, froth exploding. “Give me a ‘Hell Yeah!’”

Silence. The aliens stared.

“I said, GIMME A ‘HELL YEAH!’” he roared, and the sound echoed across the plains, rattling the very craft.

The lead alien’s black eyes narrowed. *“Your aggression is primitive. Futile.”* It took a step forward.

Stone Cold lunged. He ducked under a phantom energy blast, kicked the lead alien square in its impossibly thin gut, bending it double with a sickening *squelch*. Then, faster than their alien optic nerves could process, he hooked an arm around its neck, spun, and dropped, delivering the Stone Cold Stunner.

The alien hit the dirt with a sound like a wet sack of marbles, its limbs flailing unnaturally. A high-pitched, wailing shriek tore through the mental silence, as its two subordinates recoiled in horror, their advanced brains unable to compute such raw, unpredictable violence. Their leader, the conqueror, was twitching uncontrollably on the ground.

Stone Cold stood over it, dusting off his hands. He popped open a beer, took a long, satisfying swig. He looked at the other two aliens, who were now backing away towards their ship, terrified.

“That’s what happens when you mess with Texas,” he stated, then paused, looking directly at the retreating aliens. “And that’s the bottom line, ‘cause Stone Cold said so!”

With a final, terrified shriek, the two remaining aliens scrambled back into their ship. The metallic petals retracted, the hum returned, intensified, and then, with a silent, impossible rush of air, the colossal craft shot straight up into the sky, disappearing beyond the horizon in a blink.

Stone Cold took another long swig of beer. The dust settled. The sun still shone. He looked at the spot where the ship had been. He looked at the twitching alien on the ground.

“Well, alright then.” He finished his beer, tossed the can, and climbed back into his pickup. He still had some chores to do. And maybe, just maybe, he’d earned another beer.

The old woman, Elara, hummed a tuneless melody as she stirred her evening tea. The scent of chamomile and dried lavender filled the small cottage, a stark contrast to the acrid tang of ozone that had, for weeks now, seeped in through the cracks in her ancient walls. Tonight, however, it was different. Tonight, the ozone was laced with something else – a sweetness, like burnt sugar, but with an underlying metallic bite.

She set down her spoon, her gaze drifting to the window. The setting sun, usually a riot of oranges and purples, was a bruised, sickly yellow. The sky was the color of old parchment. Elara had seen the sky turn many shades in her ninety-three years, but never this.

"No words could describe what I have on my mind…" she murmured, the words echoing a half-forgotten lullaby from her childhood. She hadn't thought of that song in decades.

A faint scratching sound came from the front door, not the familiar scritch of a squirrel, but something heavier, more deliberate. Elara’s heart, usually a steady, slow drum, began to tap a faster rhythm. She knew, with the chilling certainty of old age, what awaited her. She’d felt its approach for days, a cold breath on the back of her neck, a prickle of unease that no amount of tea or warm blankets could soothe.

The scratching intensified, a rhythmic rasp that seemed to vibrate through the very floorboards. A tendril of shadow, blacker than any night, snaked under the door, unfurling like a velvet ribbon. It wasn't the kind of shadow cast by a physical object; this was a shadow that *was* the object, a living absence.

"…Seeing death as it stands right in front of me," Elara finished the lullaby, her voice surprisingly steady. She watched the shadow, mesmerized, as it grew, thickening into a form that was undeniably human, yet utterly alien. It didn't have eyes, or a mouth, but she felt its gaze, a profound emptiness that promised oblivion. The sweet, metallic scent grew stronger, almost cloying.

From the shadow's feet, thin, black vines began to sprout, not from the earth, but from the very air. They were sleek and glistening, like polished obsidian, and they snaked across her worn rug, reaching for the legs of her tea table, for the hem of her woolen skirt. They weren't aggressive, not yet, but their presence was an undeniable claim.

Elara smiled, a small, tired smile. She’d lived a long life, full of joy and sorrow, love and loss. She had no regrets. As the first vine brushed her ankle, cool and smooth, she simply closed her eyes, the last notes of her lullaby fading into the silence of the encroaching darkness.

Every day, HIV continues to shape millions of lives around the world. Despite decades of research, more than 33 million people are currently living with HIV, and 2.7 million new infections occur each year—a testament to the virus’s relentless grip on global health. HIV, a member of the lentivirus family, is the causative agent of acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS), a devastating condition in which the immune system is gradually dismantled, leaving individuals vulnerable to life-threatening infections and cancers.

While there is still no definitive cure for HIV, remarkable advances in treatment—such as combination antiretroviral therapies—enable many people living with HIV to lead long and productive lives. However, the high cost of these treatments places them out of reach for countless individuals in low-income countries, perpetuating cycles of illness and loss. The ultimate hope lies in developing a vaccine that could prevent HIV transmission altogether. Yet, the virus’s ability to rapidly mutate, particularly its envelope glycoprotein, has so far thwarted traditional vaccine approaches.

The GeoVax Approach

GeoVax, a pioneering biotech company based in Atlanta, GA, is taking an innovative path toward an HIV vaccine. Their strategy centers on virus-like particles displaying the trimeric, membrane-bound form of the HIV-1 envelope glycoprotein, aiming to trigger both robust antibody and cellular immune responses. This dual approach—stimulating high frequencies of T-cells alongside antibody production—has shown in preclinical primate models to offer stronger protection against HIV-1 than either response alone. GeoVax’s vaccine candidate uses a DNA priming immunization followed by a recombinant live virus MVA (Modified Vaccinia Ankara) boost, a combination that has elicited promising protective immune responses and could represent a turning point in HIV prevention.

How VGXI Helped

Before partnering with VGXI, GeoVax had produced their plasmid with several other contract manufacturing organizations (CMOs). However, VGXI’s proprietary AIRMIX™ technology delivered higher yields and faster turnaround times, significantly reducing GeoVax’s cost per patient.

“VGXI took our first plasmid in its existing cell bank, plugged it into their standard process, and immediately increased the yield while reducing processing time. It was this experience that drew us to VGXI for our latest production. The new project involves a plasmid that is bigger than our first one. With the latest project, VGXI was able to take our plasmid from process development through fill-finish with excellent purity, quick turnaround, and smooth project execution,” said Michael Hellerstein, Director of Quality Control.

To achieve certainty when diagnosing Multiple Sclerosis, it is important that the doctor does not rely solely on reported symptoms. In fact, doctors perform various tests and then compile a detailed medical history, which includes information on any possible past trauma or injuries, as well as medications taken now and in the past, and any relevant family medical history. Although it is not proven that MS is genetic in nature, doctors will ask because some believe a link exists.

As symptoms that manifest for this disease can be shared with other illnesses, the doctor will also perform blood tests to exclude pathogens such as HIV or Lyme disease. Along with this, a lumbar puncture will be performed to take samples of the cerebrospinal fluid and check them for the number of antibodies. A high number of these antibodies is a sign of an active immune system. MS is one of the few autoimmune diseases extant, and therefore, abnormally high levels of antibodies present as a symptom.

Doctors also use MRI scans to determine whether there are any lesions on the spinal cord, the brain, and possibly optic nerves. The MRI scan will pinpoint any old dormant lesions as well as active ones. To see these clearly, doctors will inject gadolinium, which is a special kind of dye that will make these lesions glow when an MRI is performed. Eliminating one of these many tests can result in an improper diagnosis, so doctors generally use a combination of all tests to diagnose an individual with MS.

**Criteria Doctors Use in Diagnosing MS**

In addition to tests, when diagnosing MS, doctors will also run the symptoms and the demographics of the patient against a list of criteria. Women are twice as likely to be diagnosed with MS than men are. This disease also usually strikes those who are between 20 and 50 years of age, as well as have had at least two episodes which occurred a minimum of a month apart and lasted 24 hours or more. Such symptoms then indicate damage done to the brain and the spinal cord, and if the MRI scan shows 2 lesions or more, then the doctor will be able to make a determination diagnosing Multiple Sclerosis. It is important to remember that the more information the doctor can be provided with, the better and more definitive the diagnosis will be. Early diagnosis enables individuals to receive treatment for MS as soon as possible.

Julie Bishop's concern follows the arrest of several Nauru opposition MPs and the seizure of their passports.
Australia has one of its offshore asylum seeker processing centre on Nauru and relies on the nation's justice and legal system to operate it.
New Zealand and other Pacific nations also raised concerns at a Pacific Islands Forum in Sydney this week.
The summit primarily discussed the response to a potential natural disaster in the region, but the political situation in the tiny island nation was also discussed.
Nauru's government has denied there is a "breakdown in democracy".
In recent weeks it has suspended opposition MPs from parliament without pay for giving interviews critical of the government to foreign media.
In June, a protest outside the parliament building led to the arrest of three opposition MPs.
Two are still in custody and another has been prevented from going to New Zealand to see his family.
The government said the MPs were participating in a violent riot and would be entitled to a fair trial.
The country has also blocked social media sites and set visa fees for journalists as high as A$8,000 ($5,979; Â£3,883).
Ms Bishop on Friday urged Nauru to give its citizens "natural justice", Australian Broadcasting Corp (ABC) reported.
"We are concerned, that's why I'm raising it," she told the ABC on Friday.
"I know my colleague, Murray McCully, the foreign minister of New Zealand, has raised it because New Zealand funds the Nauru justice system," she said.
"We urge there to be an adherence to the rule of law, that their justice system operates properly, that people are not denied natural justice, that they're given an opportunity to present their case.
"I've had one confidential discussion with the president [Baron Waqa] and I'll be having another one today."
In a statement reporting by Australian media, Nauru Justice Minister David Adeang said: "Contrary to reports in some sections of the media, there is certainly no breakdown in democracy or any other turmoil in Nauru."
"We are merely upholding the rule of law and those who break the rules will be arrested."
Australia sends about A$26m in aid a year to Nauru on top of funds for running the detention facility, which was opened in 2001.
Australia has a policy of mandatory detention for any asylum seekers caught trying to reach its shores.
Those found to be genuine refugees are resettled in other countries such as Cambodia.

By . Eleanor Gower . His world famous half sister has just been featured on the cover of Time Magazine's Most Influential issue. But sadly for little Nixon Knowles, life is a far cry from Beyonce's charmed existence. The four-year-old youngster - a product of an affair between Beyonce's father Mathew Knowles and Canadian-born actress Alexsandra Wright - has now moved into a trailer park just outside Los Angeles. Scroll down for video... Sad story: Beyone's four-year-old half brother Nixon Knowles and his mother Alexsandra Wright, have moved in to a trailer park just outside Los Angeles . New footage from Inside Edition features mother and son moving into the park after a judge granted Knowles a substantial cut in his . child support payments from $12,000 to roughly $2,500 a month, due to his dwindling income. This meant Wright could no longer afford the Los Angeles home she lived in with . Nixon. Worse still, a judge has since agreed that Knowles had in fact 'overpaid Wright to the tune of $110,000', according to a Thursday TMZ report. Fortunately, a women’s support group . arranged for the pair to move into a trailer after Wright was forced to go on . public assistance. 'I'm in the middle of a fight for my son's rights,' Wright said when quizzed about her financial struggles. Bleak outlook: The actress spoke to Inside Edition about their move . Little brother: Nixon Knowles is the product of an affair between Alexandra and Beyone's father Matthew Knowles . Most influential: Beyonce is one of the cover stars of Time magazine's famous issue . Far . from expecting Beyonce to use some of her millions to help them, Wright . insists she wants Knowles to accept his parental responsibilities. 'Take accountability. Stand up, do the right thing,' she said. 'It’s not Beyonce’s fault. I don’t hold her accountable. I hold the father of my child accountable.' She claims a recent article suggesting she 'blamed the star singer for her . predicament' led to her receiving a thousand death threats in the last . week alone. 'Because this article said I blamed Beyonce,' said Wright. 'And again, let . me say for the record - it’s not her fault. I’ve never blamed her. I want . the best for her. Everyone's in pain.' Wright laid into Knowles during a TV interview last month, claiming he had never . even met his son. She told Inside Edition: 'You've seen more . of my son than he has.' It previously emerged she was using 'food stamps to . survive' after it was alleged that Knowles owed $32,135,90 in child support. I don't blame Beyonce: Alexandra used a televised interview to send a message to the singer after receiving death threats . But the judge's latest ruling has come down on the side of Knowles, suggesting he should in fact have been only paying Wright $2,485 all along. Knowles will even get credit for 'overpayment', meaning Wright will receive no cash from him for the next three and a half years. She has vowed to 'continue her fight.' However, with Wright on welfare assistance, TMZ suggest that Knowles may still have to make some sort of payment to his former paramour. New home: Alexsandra was helped by a homeless charity after being forced to go on public assistance . According to TMZ, Knowles previously claimed in court he pulled in $127K in 2013, a far cry from the $3M a year he earned as his daughter's manager. Wright conceived her son during an . affair with Knowles, who was married to Beyoncé's mother Tina at the . time - the revelation of the relationship ended their marriage. Beyonce, 32, and sister Solange, 27, are both estranged from their father and didn't attend his wedding when he re-married earlier this . year, tying the knot with former model Gena Charmaine Avery in Houston, . Texas in June. Before the split: Mathew with his superstar . daughter (he used to be her manager, too) her sister Solange and his . former wife of 31 years, Tina .

This robe is extremely short. So much so that it's not much of a cover-up. However, it is pretty, and the satin quilted hanger that it comes on is almost as exciting as the garment itself. I would have liked it to be longer. The chemise or babydoll fit me very strangely, and I will not be wearing it. I've worn the robe once or twice, but would get more use out of a longer one. However, it wasn't worth returning because it was so cheap that return shipping would eat up most of the refund.

Huge news, and hat tip to excellent food-policy writer Monica Eng at the Chicago Tribune: In a piece published Tuesday, she details that 300 hospitals in the Chicago area and nationwide have begun preferentially buying and serving meat that is raised without the use of antibiotics.
Using the ingredients is primarily a response to patient demand, said (Carolyn Lammersfeld, national director of nutrition at Cancer Treatment Centers of America) but the centers are also “watching the controversy over the nontherapeutic use of antibiotics and their potential to cause resistant strains of bacteria.”
The issue is of particular concern for cancer patients, who have compromised immune systems, she noted. “Many also might already being taking antibiotics, so they don’t want additional ones in food if they can avoid it,” Lammersfeld said.
(Diane Imrie, director of nutrition services at Fletcher Allen Health Care in Vermont) estimated that her food costs rose about $67,000 last year when she switched to antibiotic-free chicken from conventional. “But that’s also about the same cost as treating a single MRSA infection,” she said.
It’s interesting to see this story land just as a new paper in Foodborne Pathogens and Disease is making the rounds. The paper (Jiayi Zhang, Samantha K. Wall, Li Xu, Paul D. Ebner. “Contamination Rates and Antimicrobial Resistance in Bacteria Isolated from “Grass-Fed” Labeled Beef Products,” doi:10.1089/fpd.2010.0562) compares the bacterial burden in grass-fed and conventionally raised beef and finds no significant differences: equivalent amounts of both drug-sensitive and drug-resistant bacteria in both types of beef.
It concludes, “There are no clear food safety advantages to grass-fed beef products over conventional beef products” — an assertion that’s likely to be seized on by those who see no need to change current antibiotic use in agriculture. (For an example of that POV, here’s the testimony from last week’s House of Representatives hearing by Richard Carnevale, DVM of the Animal Health Institute.)
I suspect though that the paper’s analysis doesn’t look far enough. Here’s one example: the authors found that Enterococcus species in both conventional and grass-fed meat were resistant to chloramphenicol, erythromycin, flavomycin, penicillin, and tetracyline — drugs that are used in agriculture (and that could have been given to the grass-fed animals, which were not guaranteed to have been raised drug-free). But Enterococcus spp. isolates from conventional beef were more frequently resistant to daptomycin and linezolid — which are new-to-market drugs of last resort in human medicine that are not given to animals
That finding, right there — the migration of resistance to a human-only drug into an organism carried by an animal — signals one of the insoluble problems of overuse of antibiotics. Once created, resistance factors move horizontally among bacteria, from the farm to humans, and apparently in this case, from humans to the farm as well. We have almost no control over their movement, and on the agricultural side, almost no surveillance to detect it, either. That argues for reducing the overuse of antibiotics in human medicine and on the farm.
If this health care coalition’s refusal to purchase meat raised using antibiotics helps to enlarge the market for drug-free meat, then it may reduce ag antibiotic use, and therefore the selective pressure that encourages resistant organisms to emerge. That can only be a good thing.

Out of pure blind luck the cell phone rested on the asphalt in front of him. His blood trickled past his fingertips and off of the side of the road. "Oh." He murmured. He fumbled with the phone until he could get the number dialed. He knew who to call. Not a lover, no she was gone, but someone else entirely. *You have reached Anderson's phone! Leave a message and I might get back to ya!" *Beep! * "Anderson, you slimy little shit. I promise you, this is not the end. You may have killed me but the boys are watching you. You're dead meat. You got it? When they're done with you, you'll be shoveling shit for the devil." He coughed, expelling a little clot of blood, before he continued. "I'll be sure to save you a spot in hell, you greedy little asshole. Oh and be sure to have fun finding the money, pal. I told you the wrong location. Now you'll never get it." His last breaths were filled with laughter in between gasps for air.

In the shadow of Yosemite’s granite cliffs, silent giants are vanishing. The park’s age-old trees—some predating the birth of nations—are disappearing at a pace that has shocked scientists. Once thought invincible, these towering sentinels are succumbing, leaving behind empty spaces where forest cathedrals once stood. Now, after gathering and analyzing 60 years of meticulous data, forest ecologists warn that even Yosemite, a crown jewel of preservation, is not immune. Their findings show a staggering 24% drop in the number of large-diameter trees since the 1930s, a decline linked to the relentless march of climate change. “It’s astonishing,” says James Lutz, one of the study’s authors. “These are survivors, trees that have weathered centuries of storms and droughts—yet something now is overwhelming them.” If Yosemite's iconic giants are at risk despite decades of protection, what hope is there for forests with fewer safeguards?

**Lonely elephant Mondy seeks specific new companion**

**GREENWOOD SANCTUARY** – Mondy, the beloved 52-year-old matriarch of Greenwood Sanctuary, is not just looking for a friend; she's searching for a soulmate. After a year of quiet solitude following the passing of her lifelong companion, sanctuary staff have launched an unprecedented global search for a very specific type of elephant to fill the void in her heart.

Mondy arrived at Greenwood a decade ago with her best friend, Thandi. Their bond was legendary, a testament to the deep emotional lives of elephants. Thandi's death last spring left Mondy visibly distraught, and while she tolerates other elephants from afar, she hasn't formed a new close bond. Keepers have noted Mondy spending more time alone, displaying less engagement during enrichment activities, and often standing vigil at the fence line where she and Thandi used to share their evening meals.

"It's not just about introducing any available elephant," explains Dr. Elara Vance, lead elephant behavioral specialist at Greenwood. "Mondy needs a companion who is gentle, patient, and mature – ideally an older female, perhaps in her late 40s or early 50s, who has herself experienced loss or is known for her calming influence. We've observed her rejecting more boisterous or younger elephants. She communicates subtle cues that only a deeply understanding companion would pick up on, almost like an unspoken language."

The sanctuary is collaborating with other facilities globally, reviewing profiles of elephants that fit Mondy's unique requirements. They're looking at temperament, age, social history, and even subtle behavioral patterns documented by other elephant experts. The process is meticulous, involving extensive video analysis and expert consultations.

"Mondy deserves a true companion for her golden years," Dr. Vance adds, her voice full of determination. "We won't rest until we find the perfect match for our wise old girl, someone who can share her quiet wisdom and provide the stable, nurturing companionship she so clearly craves." The hope is that by finding a precise match, Mondy can once again experience the profound emotional connection that elephants thrive on.

"This is *beyond* ridiculous," I choked out, a raw edge of desperation in my voice, unable to stomach another second of this degrading, futile charade. I *despised* myself for ever believing, for letting hope blind me. The memory of why I'd agreed, of the desperation that had driven me to this, was a bitter taste in my mouth. It had seemed like our *only* option. "C'mon, man, *please*. Just one more. He *swore* it worked for his friend. Think of it, *our way out*. No Clip! No more limits!" Dave pleaded, his voice ragged, eyes wide with a manic desperation that mirrored my own buried fear. I closed my eyes, a wave of weariness and a gut-wrenching sense of dread washing over me, but his words, hollow as they felt, still held a sliver of terrifying promise. He read the next combination, his voice trembling slightly. "Walk forward with the right foot. Crouch. Sniff. Walk backwards with the left foot. Place both arms forward. Jump twice in place, and run to the opposite location as quickly as possible." I followed his instructions to the letter, every movement a leaden act of faith. "Huh," Dave murmured, his voice deflating, the last vestiges of hope draining from his face. "Maybe he *was* full of shit..." A crushing wave of despair hit me. My heart sank, heavy as lead in my chest. Nothing. Absolutely nothing had changed. The crushing reality was a physical blow. "Let's just go. This was our last chance, and we just look stu—" My words died in my throat, swallowed by a sudden, terrifying roar. A sickening screech of tires ripped through the air, followed by a deafening crash as multiple cop cars, sirens wailing like banshees, materialized from the shadows and slammed into our makeshift garage hideout. Metal screamed, glass shattered, and the world dissolved into chaos. "SHIT!" I screamed, my voice raw with terror, my heart hammering against my ribs, a cold dread seizing me as five ominous, burning stars burned themselves into my vision, not just a game mechanic, but the terrifying symbol of our absolute, inescapable doom.

It was on a quite unremarkable Saturday morning that I found it. The sky was sullen and grey, and little but a sharp breeze broke the otherwise prevailing silence that hung heavy over the village in which I found myself. What exactly it wast that I had found I was not yet sure, but the quality of the workmanship on the small Mahoney box I was carrying, with fine brass hinges and extravagant carvings on all sides, assured me that what I had found was something quite remarkable, or at the very least valuable. Had I known then what I know now about the contents of that small mahogany box I would perhaps have never opened it. I might have discarded it, destroyed it even. But this is no use now. The box is open. What's done is done and all I can do now is SHIT 1% HOW DID THIS HAPPEN SO QUICKLY!

Cardiff University is currently exhibiting some of its collection as part of a three-day event to mark the centenary of Thomas' untimely death.
But Alan Hughes, head of special collections and archive, said some other items were in poor condition.
The National Manuscripts Conservation Trust has awarded them almost Â£3,000.
The university holds the largest archive of Thomas' letters, diaries, notebooks, poems, photographs and personal belongings.
Mr Hughes said the grant had allowed us it to "collaborate with Glamorgan Archives to undertake essential conservation work on many of Edward Thomas' notebooks".
He said nine were in particularly poor condition due to acidification, fragile bindings and general deterioration over time.
"Many are unable to be handled without a high risk of further damage and are therefore inaccessible to scrutiny, celebration, digitisation and study," he said.
Thomas, known for his poems written as World War One raged across Europe, was killed in the Battle of Arras at Easter 1917, at the age of 39.
While he was born in England, Prof Katie Gramich who organised this week's event, said his parents were Welsh and he loved Wales.
She said his work was influenced by the time he spent walking in Wales and he felt "cheated of a Welsh identity".
The exhibition, which runs until the autumn, features previously unheard archive recordings of family and friends, interviewed by the university's Prof R George Thomas in 1967.

Tuesday, Mike Simmons presented “A Global Education Program of Collaboration in Astronomical experiments”, the transit of Venus programme of Astronomers Without Borders, at the CAP 2011 meeting in Beijing. The transit of Venus gives the opportunity to replicate an entire chain of solar system measurements from the ancient Greeks’ determination of the Earth’s diameter to the Sun-Earth distance in kilometres. Classrooms worldwide will be engaged in these measurements and encouraged to collaborate with other classrooms in distant locations. The transit of Venus and the expanding use of online communicating technologies combine to provide a unique opportunity to engage classrooms around the world without regard to location or cultural differences. Says Simmons,
Students will gain not only scientific knowledge and an understanding of simple scientific methodology through inquiry-based learning, but also knowledge of the wider world, and the Universe, in which they live.
The yearlong programme provides a series of simple, hands-on projects, enabling students to make measurements of the size of the Earth and inner solar system using instrumentation created with common, easily obtained materials. Through a real-time, interactive GIS data system, students can collaborate to obtain the multiple, widely-separated observations needed for many of these measurements without the travel required earlier, and they can experience the Earth’s shape first-hand through shared observations.
The entire programme culminates with the transit of Venus in June 2012, when classrooms will use our phone app to time the exact moments of the apparent contacts between Venus and the Sun. This programme is a global, collaborative project open to institutions worldwide. For information on participation go to AWB’s website or contact Mike Simmons: email@example.com.
