**French Election Poll Shows Macron Leading Le Pen 61%-39% as Misinformation Campaigns Target Runoff Vote**

PARIS - President Emmanuel Macron holds a commanding 22-point lead over far-right challenger Marine Le Pen ahead of Sunday's presidential runoff election, according to a new Ipsos poll released Thursday, even as social media platforms struggle to combat a surge of false information targeting the vote.

The survey of 1,500 likely voters shows Macron at 61% compared to Le Pen's 39%, representing a slight widening from earlier polls this week that had shown the incumbent's lead narrowing to the high teens.

French authorities and tech companies have identified dozens of misleading posts and fabricated news stories circulating on Twitter, Facebook, and messaging apps in the final days before the election. The disinformation efforts include false claims about voting procedures, doctored images, and unsubstantiated allegations about both candidates.

"We're seeing a concentrated push of false narratives designed to suppress turnout and sow doubt about the electoral process," said Marie Dubois, a researcher at the French Institute for Strategic Analysis who tracks online disinformation.

The Interior Ministry reported blocking 47 fake websites mimicking official election information pages and working with social media companies to remove over 200 misleading posts since Monday.

Macron campaign officials expressed cautious optimism about the polling data while warning supporters against complacency. "Every vote matters," said campaign spokesperson Jean-Claude Moreau. "We're taking nothing for granted until the final ballot is counted."

Le Pen's team dismissed the polls, with spokesman David Rachline telling reporters the race remains "much closer than the establishment wants to admit."

Polling stations open at 8 a.m. local time on Sunday, with initial results expected shortly after polls close at 8 p.m.

Scientists have created a machine with the potential to read our dreams while we sleep, a silent, flickering window into the subconscious. Researchers in the US say it is so powerful that it can extract images from people’s brains and, with a faint hum and the soft glow of a screen, display them. The data from the brain scanner, a smooth, cool dome, has already been used to detect and reconstruct images of faces that people are thinking of, their features coalescing from a shimmering haze. Researchers believe the same technology, with its subtle whirring, could be used in the future to enable them to reconstruct images from people’s memories, imagination, and dreams – a tapestry woven from the mind's eye. Scientists say they have created a machine with the potential to read dreams during sleep (library image), its presence a faint, almost imperceptible thrum in the air. It could also possibly be used to collect images of criminals from the minds of witnesses, perhaps even retrieving the lingering scent of smoke or the coarse feel of a collar.

Alan Cowen, a neuroscientist at the University of California, Berkeley, said: ‘Our methods yield strikingly accurate neural reconstructions of faces. The subtle shifts in light and shadow, the unique textures of skin and hair, are beginning to emerge with remarkable clarity. ‘This represents a novel and promising approach for investigating face perception, but also suggests avenues for reconstructing ‘offline’ visual experiences — including dreams, memories and imagination.’

Six volunteers were shown 300 faces while they laid inside an MRI scanner, the confined space echoing with the rhythmic clatter and thumps of the machine. Scientists were then able to analyse how their brains responded to dozens of different facial features including blond hair and blue eyes to dark skin and beards, each detail leaving its distinct neural signature. When they had compiled a database of responses, they showed the volunteers a new set of faces, and measured their reaction to each image, the subtle shifts in their neural activity like whispers in the data. By comparing the second responses to the database, they were able to reconstruct the image they were looking at, a visual echo of their thoughts.

The research is based on a theory that all human processes have a ‘neural correlate’ and that thoughts and feelings are merely a complex pattern of chemical reactions, a silent, intricate dance within the brain. Some neuroscientists believe there is the potential to read such patterns if they can only build sensitive enough instruments, instruments that can detect the faintest electrical currents and the most subtle chemical fluctuations. Six volunteers were shown 300 faces while they laid inside an MRI scanner. They then compiled a database of responses used to create the machine, its sleek surfaces cool to the touch.

Mr Cowen and his fellow researchers, Brice Kuhl of New York University and Professor Marvin Chun of Yale, believe that extracting facial images is the first step towards eventually producing advanced mind-reading technology. Mr Kuhl said: ‘I study memory, and it’s hard not to be excited by the prospect of being able to reconstruct the images that we bring to mind when we remember something, to pull them from the swirling mists of the past and make them visible. ‘We are certainly heading in the direction of reconstructing dreams too. Something that looks like a high-definition movie of your dreams, complete with the lingering taste of a dream-meal or the phantom touch of a dream-lover, is not going to happen in the immediate future, but we have already seen improvements in the sensitivity of these methods.’

Mr Cowen assured the public that the technology did not enable them to forcibly extract information from subjects, however. The machine offered no scent of coercion, no harsh textures of force. He told Fox News: ‘This sort of technology can only read active parts of the brain. So you couldn’t read passive memories – you would have to get the person to imagine the memory to read it, to actively bring it to the forefront of their mind, perhaps even recalling the accompanying sounds or smells. ‘It’s a matter of time, and eventually – maybe 200 years from now – we’ll have some way of reading inactive parts of the brain. ‘But that’s a much harder problem, as it involves measuring very fine details of brain structure that we don’t even really understand, the intricate, delicate lacework of neurons that hold our deepest, most dormant secrets.’

By . Emily Crane . and Aap . A man has handed himself in almost 12 hours after allegedly running from the scene of a fatal car accident during a police chase northwest of Melbourne. Police were responding to reports that three men and a woman wearing balaclavas were allegedly trying to rob a ticket machine at Macedon railway station, about 70km from Melbourne, just before 5am on Saturday. The four people took off in a sedan and police gave chase but a short time later, officers found the car had rolled on a nearby bend. Scroll down for video . A man has handed himself in almost 12 hours after allegedly running from the scene of a fatal car accident during a police chase northwest of Melbourne . One man was killed after being ejected from the vehicle and police saw two men fleeing into dense bush, Nine News reports. Police arrested a 27-year-old Sunbury woman at the scene and she is under police guard at Royal Melbourne Hospital. 'The fact they left their friends, one dead and one injured, and fled off into the bush probably speaks volumes about their character,' Assistant Commissioner Jack Blayney said of the two men who fled. Police were responding to reports of an alleged robbery at Macedon railway station, about 70km from Melbourne, just before 5am on Saturday . The four people took off in a sedan and police gave chase but a short time later, officers found the car had rolled on a nearby bend . A Sunbury man, 30, was later arrested on a roadside at nearby Gisborne about 9.45am on Saturday. He had minor injuries and remains in police custody. But police were still looking for the second man until Saturday evening when a 32-year-old from Diggers Rest showed up at Gisborne Police Station. He was being held for questioning. Investigators believe the car lost control in wet conditions and clipped a tree. 'If the police are indicating to people that they should pull over, they should pull over. It's not worth risking your life, the life of your friends, the life of innocent members of the public and also the lives of police officers for such trivial matters,' Blayney said. One man was killed after being ejected from the vehicle and police saw two men fleeing into dense bush . Police arrested a 27-year-old Sunbury woman at the scene and a man, 30, was later arrested on a roadside at nearby Gisborne. Police were still looking for the second man when he showed up at Gisborne Police Station .

Like other reviewers, our Vinotemp unit failed due to a dead main circuit board in less than a year. It's truly outrageous that Vinotemp then demanded $45 for a replacement part for a product that prematurely failed. This forces us to question how they can justify such poor durability and makes me wonder if these units are deliberately designed to fail within a year, thereby forcing customers to buy new parts. Such planned obsolescence, cynical as it sounds, is a real concern. Shame on Vinotemp!

The Butterfly looked out of the window at the city below. The heavy orange sun was setting, and a warm breeze blew through his room. It was a small space, twelve feet by twelve feet, built a lofty half mile off the ground. The views of the city and the desert were spectacular in all directions. When he had first been brought to this place, as a boy, he'd found it intoxicating- to gaze down on all of the people below, living out their small lives, knowing the power and responsibility he held over them. But now, he was old and tired, and the weight of hundreds of thousands of lives rested heavily upon his shoulders. He had saved men from bullets, women from trains, babies from rivers- and yet, somehow, the world was ending. The population had skyrocketed, the water supply had plummeted, and what came next was a waiting game. He could not save humankind from itself. It was a simple, sad kind of end. 

A chime from behind him signaled the arrival of dinner. He turned to see a lavish plate of roasted duck and a huge jug of red wine materialize on his great stone table. As always, dinner was a solitary affair. They never spoke to him. It was feared that human contact would cloud his mind and interfere with his Gift. The only time a Butterfly spoke with another human was when he was ready to pass the Gift on to a successor. Instead, all of his gustatory desires were catered to by means of a computerized teleportation device built into the table. He would input his meal requests every night, and the next day they would arrive at precisely 9AM, 1PM, and 6PM. Sometimes, he experimented with bizarre combinations of foods, just to stave off the boredom. Salmon and strawberries. Chocolate dipped bratwursts. Tonight, however, he had gone traditional; even eccentricity for eccentricity's sake got boring, eventually. 

It was an odd relationship that he held with the citizens below. The great sense of respect and awe that they had for the Butterfly was juxtaposed with their complete and utter reliance on him. There were parades held in his name, songs sung on his greatness- yet there was no door to his tower. Day after day, he sat alone on a cushion on the floor, eyes closed, watching a thousand perilous lives unfold in his mind, all at once. The images rushed through his head like recalled music- there one second, gone the next. He would scribble details in shorthand on a sheet of paper, watch the words melt away as they were relayed to the world below. He'd been trained by his predecessor on how to filter them; how to watch for the big ones. The Priorities. The natural disasters, the important politicians. This morning, he'd stopped a plane crash in Dubai; just after lunch, he'd prevented a famous actress's drug overdose. 

He couldn't catch everything, though. That was the hardest part; the ones he had to ignore. They lingered in his peripheral vision long after they'd left the earth, begging him to save them. Tonight, though, one in particular had stuck with him. The wrinkled face of an old woman, reflected in a car window on a rainy freeway. She had looked like someone he had known, once. And he had seen the terror in her eyes, and he had felt something heavy and rusted move in his heart. And he had tried to save her, entirely against protocol, scrawling her coordinates and underlining them feverishly. But still, he knew that she would slip through the cracks. Her death was not a Priority. 

He finished his last mouthful of roasted duck, took one last swig of wine, and dabbed his face with a napkin. He pushed back his chair and returned to the window. The sky was nearly dark, now, and he could see lights twinkling in the city below. The Butterfly hoisted himself out of the window, perched on the ledge, and felt the wind in his hair. He breathed in and stepped off the ledge. And, closing his eyes, for the first time in forever, he saw absolute, glorious nothingness. For the first time in his life, the Butterfly flew.

This "updated cut" is an absolute travesty. I went in hoping for a fresh look at a beloved classic, perhaps some minor polish, but what I got was a complete demolition of everything I cherished. The new color grading sucks the life out of every frame, replacing the warm, familiar palette with something cold and sterile. Dialogue has been re-dubbed in places, stripping away the iconic delivery I remember. Worse still, some added scenes actively contradict the established lore or slow the pacing to a crawl. It doesn't enhance; it diminishes. My nostalgia isn't just ruined; it's been actively assaulted. They didn't update it; they fundamentally misunderstood it. A heartbreaking disappointment.

The information in this book can easily be found in seed catalogs—many of which actually provide more detail than what’s offered here. There’s not much information about each tomato variety. Each two-page spread features only half a page of text in large print, while the other page is just a large photo. There’s also no information about the history of heirloom tomatoes. Additionally, covering 100 heirloom tomatoes barely scratches the surface of the varieties available on the market. I’ll be returning this book.

I don’t always give a Five Star Review—in fact, I’ve been known to give just one star to some establishments. This wasn’t my first visit to The Vic Bar + Kitchen, and not every previous experience has been as stellar as this one, though none have been truly disappointing either. I organize events for people over 50 in the West Valley and thought it was time to introduce everyone to the glorious vista from The Vic’s terrace. Although we didn’t find a table outside, our indoor table was perfect for the group, and we were able to relax on the terrace afterwards.

Our server, a young man named Andrew, was professional, exceptionally efficient, and completely attentive to our needs whenever we required anything. Despite being busy, he managed his tables with aplomb. The Maitre d’ greeted us upon arrival, listened patiently as I explained our seating preferences and who we were, and quickly found us a table.

The service was wonderful and the wine deals were excellent, but I have to tell you about the most delicious sliders I’ve ever tasted—truly divine! Happy Hour runs from 3pm to 6pm, Monday through Saturday, and even if you live far away, it’s absolutely worth the drive. I loved it!

This amazing place features a cafe that serves chips, pretzels, ice cream, and smoothies - perfect healthy options if you're on a diet. The facility boasts tons of trampolines, rock walls, foam pits, a newly installed zip line, and an obstacle course. With so many activities to choose from, I had an incredible time and would definitely return if I had the budget for it. However, it is rather expensive - the required sky socks cost $2, and a 2-hour session runs around $20.

Saehae Bok Manhi Padeuseyo is the greeting that Koreans used on New Year's Day. Like Chinese, Koreans also celebrate New Years Day using the Solar Calendar so tomorrow is called "Seollal" in Korea which is the same as Chinese New Year. It is also a big event in Korea where most people go to their hometowns to celebrate Seollal so there's always heavy traffic during this day specially now that it happens on Sunday. Seollal always includes 3 consecutive days holidays. Before, the day, and after but this year, it happens on Sunday which means Monday is the only holiday that workers have, so vacation is too short.
On Seollal, children receive Saebae Don or "New Year Money". They bow down to their parents, grandparents, relatives or oldies and they receive some amount of money. Most kids put their money in their bank accounts like my nieces and nephews. Most parents don't use the money and most kids have their own bank accounts. Last week, I asked one of my students on what would he do with the money although I already know that it goes to his account. It was a free-talking class so even I knew that it goes to his bank account, I still asked in order to make the conversation longer. He said he would put it in his account, then I asked where he would use the money in his account. He said, he will use it when he goes to college and when he get married. Oh my goodness. He's just a 1st grade in middle school. How many years before he could use the money? Wow! Koreans are really good at saving money! Even the kids!
Oh well, back to the topic, I'm supposed to be in my in-laws' house to help preparing for the food to use for "Jesa" (ceremonial for the death). Jesa is done every Chuseok (Thanksgiving Day) and "Seollal" (Chinese New Year). A day when Koreans prepare lots of food and offer to the death. Sometimes they go to the tomb and offer another food like fruits, jeon (different kinds of fried food), fish, vegetables, korean snacks, soup, sul (korean alcohol) and even cigarettes if the death person smoked when he was still on earth. We were supposed to leave last night but we worked until evening so we called my in-laws and told them to go there today, instead. We planned to leave in the morning as soon as we get up (although morning is impossible because we usually get up at noon). We got up at 10:30 the earliest waking-up time but it's now 3p.m. and we are still at home. My hubby is too lazy to go. He said because the vacation is too short. So going back and forth to my in-laws already takes almost a day. Anyway, I'm already done with the chores. As usual, I did the general cleaning like I usually do every Saturday and I'm ready to leave now. I'm all set but my hubby is still playing soccer on his xbox.

The wind kicked up, smearing an empty plastic bag across the back window of a car that sat on three wheels no the curb, its fourth dangling uselessly a half-foot above the group. The air stank of smoke and decay, thick with the rich odor of flesh gone bad. Black clouds rolled on the horizon, beneath the red light of burning city blocks. A single, stereotypical car alarm was bleating somewhere in the distance, but no human hand would ever silence it. Miriam sat there, taking a long, shaky pull from her cigarette before nearly doubling over in a coughing fit. She had given up the habit nearly twenty years ago, when her first son had been born. Mike's car registration had been two weeks expired when it began and he was just a memory now. She half-sat, half-squatted against the wall with its sign declaring it a "DESIGNATED SMOKING AREA" and took another pull when the coughing had ceased. Her second sound had been in the car with his father, Michael, who had forgotten to use a turn signal. He was normally a conscientious driver, but he had been in a rush to get home to his hysterical wife who had been babbling about their oldest dying. Michael had been guilty of the little sin, but Reese's only crime had been staying late for band practice, from which his father had been picking him up. By the time the news of the accident reached Miriam, the world was already changed. She took a last pull, trying to pretend the tears were from the coughing, but she knew the truth: it'd been a week since the beginning and days since she had last seen another person, but still the pain was a raw wound in her chest and it could wake her in the middle of the night or come leaping from around the corner and leave her a weeping mess. She stared at the cigarette, chewing her lower lip. She brought it towards her lips and then stopped, lowering it instead. A light flick and she watched it flip, end over end, one side still stained with the lipstick she kept stupidly applying, the other a burning ember. She wondered if the litter would bounce when it hit the ground. She never got to see. After all, given enough time, everyone will be guilty of something.

## Beyond the Veil: Uncovering the Scientific Contributions of Enslaved African Women

The historical narrative of slavery often focuses on the physical and psychological toll it exacted, overlooking the intellectual resilience and unacknowledged scientific contributions of enslaved individuals. While the term "scientist" might seem anachronistic when applied to those denied formal education, a closer examination reveals a wealth of knowledge, particularly among enslaved African women, that profoundly impacted early American life. This article aims to pull back the veil of historical oversight and illuminate their often-invisible scientific prowess.

Arriving in the Americas, these women brought with them a rich tapestry of indigenous knowledge systems. Hailing from diverse regions of Africa, they possessed sophisticated understandings of botany, herbal medicine, agricultural practices, and animal husbandry, honed over generations. This wasn't merely folk wisdom; it was a deep, empirically-derived understanding of the natural world, passed down through oral traditions and practical application.

Consider their role in healing. Denied access to formal medical care, enslaved women became the primary caregivers for their communities, both black and white. They meticulously observed the medicinal properties of local flora, often identifying parallels with plants from their homelands. Their expertise in creating poultices, tinctures, and remedies for ailments ranging from fevers to snakebites was invaluable. This wasn't guesswork; it was a systematic approach to identifying, preparing, and administering treatments, a testament to their practical botanical and pharmacological knowledge.

Their agricultural acumen was equally significant. Many enslaved women arrived with extensive experience in cultivating diverse crops, managing soil fertility, and preserving harvests. They introduced new crops and techniques, such as intercropping and crop rotation, which significantly influenced the development of Southern agriculture. Their understanding of seed selection, pest control, and irrigation, often superior to that of their enslavers, directly contributed to the economic viability of many plantations.

Furthermore, their knowledge extended to materials science. From weaving intricate baskets and textiles to understanding the properties of clays for pottery, these women were skilled artisans and engineers. Their ability to transform raw materials into functional objects required an intimate understanding of their physical and chemical characteristics.

The "spiritual interrogations" of their time, as mentioned in the original prompt, were not limited to religious expression. They also involved a constant, often clandestine, interrogation of their environment for survival, for healing, and for maintaining a sense of cultural identity. This intellectual engagement, though rarely documented in traditional historical records, represents a powerful, unacknowledged scientific legacy. By recognizing the scientific contributions of enslaved African women, we not only correct a historical injustice but also gain a richer, more nuanced understanding of the foundations upon which early America was built. Their laboratories were the fields, their textbooks the forests, and their legacy a testament to the enduring power of human intellect in the face of unimaginable adversity.

## The Verdant Hearth: A Culinary Oasis in the Concrete Jungle

Stepping into The Verdant Hearth is like finding a secret garden in the heart of a bustling city. From the moment the heavy oak door swings inward, you’re enveloped in an atmosphere that is both sophisticated and deeply inviting. Forget the sterile, minimalist chic of so many modern eateries; The Verdant Hearth embraces a rustic elegance that feels genuinely lived-in and loved.

My first visit was on a whim, after a particularly grueling day at work had left me craving something more substantial and comforting than my usual takeout. What I found was a culinary balm for the soul. The aroma alone – a delightful blend of herbs, slow-roasted meats, and freshly baked bread – was enough to instantly lift my spirits.

The interior is a triumph of thoughtful design. Exposed brick walls are softened by cascading greenery, creating a sense of being in a lush conservatory. Warm, amber lighting casts a gentle glow, highlighting the antique wooden tables and mismatched, yet perfectly complementary, chairs. Even the soft murmur of conversation feels like an integral part of the ambiance, never intrusive but always present.

But the true star, of course, is the food. I started with their seasonal wild mushroom tart, a delicate pastry bursting with earthy flavors and a hint of truffle. It was a revelation. For my main, I opted for the slow-braised short ribs, which arrived meltingly tender, accompanied by a vibrant root vegetable medley that tasted like it had been plucked from a farmer’s market that very morning. Each bite was a symphony of flavors and textures, a testament to the chef's dedication to quality ingredients and masterful execution.

What truly sets The Verdant Hearth apart, however, is their commitment to sourcing. Their menu proudly lists local farms and producers, and you can taste the difference. The freshness of their produce, the succulence of their meats, it all speaks to a profound respect for the ingredients. It’s a stark contrast to the often-generic flavors you find elsewhere.

The service, too, is impeccable. Our server, a knowledgeable and genuinely friendly woman named Clara, guided us through the menu with grace and enthusiasm. She offered insightful recommendations and was attentive without ever being overbearing. It’s clear that the staff are not just employees, but passionate advocates for The Verdant Hearth's vision.

I’ve since returned several times, both for intimate dinners and lively brunch gatherings, and each experience has been consistently excellent. Their brunch menu, in particular, is a delightful indulgence, with inventive twists on classic dishes and an impressive selection of artisanal coffees.

The Verdant Hearth isn't just a restaurant; it's an experience. It’s a place where you can unwind, savor exceptional food, and feel truly cared for. It’s a culinary oasis that reminds you of the simple joy of a well-prepared meal and the beauty of genuine hospitality. If you’re looking for a dining experience that nourishes both body and soul, look no further. This place is a gem, and I wholeheartedly recommend it.

My name is Grima, but everyone I know calls me the Reaper. I've brought more souls to Valhalla than any of my sisters. They started believing I was killing them myself. What they don't understand is I have a heightened sense for death. When there is a brave warrior in battle that will result in his death, I know. I was just in the middle of a game of Risk: Ragnarok Edition when I got a feeling all throughout my body. It was like a pulse of blinding white light, my entire body feeling weightless. A hero was about to die. I abruptly stood up, startling the other valkyries at the table, and ran, leaping over the edge of the colosseum. My flight took hold and I soared through the sky, traveling between the nine worlds to Midgard. I found my brave hero in an alleyway, protection a child, the mere age of 6. I watched my new hero, a young woman in her early 20s, standing between the child and an assailant. I was perched upon a nearby dumpster, invisible to them, just in time to watch the woman scissor kick the man. "Run!" She yelled to the child. He ran around the man and fled around the corner, calling for help. "You aren't going to hurt him," she said. "And you're not going anywhere, bitch." As the toxic words oozed from his mouth like venom, he retrieved a gun from his waistband. She went in for another kick, but it was too late. The gun fired, and her body fell, lifeless. Her spirit stood, confused. I walked to her and introduced myself. "My name is Grima, I'm here to take you to Valhalla." "Grima? Like the Grim Reaper?" I laughed. "Where do you think they got the name? What is your name, brave hero?" "Ch- Chloe, but I'm no hero." "But you are. You defended that child with no hesitation, and it resulted in your life." "I did what any person would do." "I wish that were true." I took her by the hand, and together we took flight. I brought her back to Valhalla, where she will learn of her Norse lineage and train for the day Ragnarok arrives. As for me, I return to my game of Risk with my sisters, and they add another tally under the Reaper score count. 104.

An eBay seller claims to be auctioning the jailhouse ID card of former New England Patriots player Aaron Hernandez. The Friday listing displayed a laminated badge with a photo of Hernandez, 25, and his identifying details: height, weight, and birth date. Hernandez is on trial for the June 2013 murder of Odin Lloyd, his fiancée's sister's boyfriend. Lloyd was shot six times in an industrial park near Hernandez's home. The seller claimed it was Hernandez's Suffolk County jail ID card, as reported by CBS Boston. The user posted, "I was in jail with Aaron Hernandez the for patriot super star.. I happen to com across his jail id." Starting at $500, the auction received no bids before its removal on Saturday. It's unclear if the user or eBay staff took it down. Jurors in Hernandez's trial deliberated for nearly 20 hours last week, returning Monday to consider murder and illegal firearm/ammunition charges. Friends Ernest Wallace and Carlos Ortiz also face separate murder trials. Prosecutors contend the three men picked up Lloyd from his Boston home in the early hours of June 17, 2013, driving him to the industrial park where his bullet-riddled body was later found. The trial, which began in January with over 130 witnesses, saw prosecutors present video footage of Hernandez's movements around his home near the time of Lloyd's death. Investigators never recovered the .45-caliber Glock pistol used to shoot Lloyd. Hernandez faces murder and two firearms charges. A first-degree conviction carries a life sentence without parole; second-degree offers parole after 15 years. A rising NFL star with a $41 million contract, Hernandez was arrested June 26, 2013, and cut by the Patriots within hours.

**Harriman Seals Wycombe Win**

Wycombe Wanderers secured a vital 2-1 victory over Gillingham today, thanks to a superb late strike from midfielder Josh Harriman. The decisive goal at Adams Park ensured all three points for the Chairboys in a hard-fought League One encounter.

The match saw Wycombe take an early lead through Brandon Hanlan in the 17th minute, delighting the home crowd. However, Gillingham refused to yield and found an equalizer midway through the second half when Olly Lee converted a penalty after a handball in the box.

With the game seemingly headed for a draw and tensions rising, Harriman stepped up in the 84th minute. Following a swift counter-attack down the right flank, the ball was cut back to Harriman on the edge of the area. He unleashed a powerful, low shot that arrowed past the outstretched dive of the Gills' goalkeeper and into the bottom corner, sparking jubilant celebrations among the Wycombe faithful.

Manager Matt Bloomfield praised his team's resilience, stating, "We showed incredible character to keep pushing. Josh's goal was a moment of real quality and a just reward for our effort." The win lifts Wycombe up the league table, boosting their confidence for the remainder of the season.

## Drone Captures Near-Miss as Glacier Calves, Scientists Scramble to Safety

**GREENLAND'S ILLULISSAT FJORD –** A routine research expedition to monitor the calving of the Jakobshavn Glacier turned into a heart-stopping dash for survival yesterday, as an unprecedented icefall sent towering waves crashing towards a team of climate scientists. While no injuries were reported, the incident served as a stark reminder of the raw power of nature and the inherent risks of studying our rapidly changing planet.

The team, comprised of researchers from the Arctic Climate Institute and a documentary crew from "Frozen Frontiers," was positioned on a rocky outcrop approximately 500 meters from the glacier's face. Their mission: to deploy advanced sonar equipment and capture high-definition drone footage of the calving process, a phenomenon vital for understanding global sea-level rise.

"We'd set up our observation post, as we always do, with what we believed was a safe buffer," explained Dr. Anya Sharma, lead glaciologist with the Arctic Climate Institute. "The drone was up, capturing some incredible shots of the ice, when we heard a rumble unlike anything I've ever experienced."

The rumble quickly escalated into a thunderous roar as a section of the glacier, estimated to be the size of a small skyscraper, detached with terrifying speed. The ensuing splash created a colossal wave, initially appearing as a distant ripple, but rapidly gaining momentum and height.

"It was like watching a tsunami in slow motion," recounted Mark Ellison, the documentary's director, still visibly shaken. "Our drone operator, Sarah, was still flying, and her live feed suddenly showed this wall of water heading straight for us. That's when Dr. Sharma yelled to evacuate."

The team had mere seconds to react. Abandoning their equipment, they scrambled up the treacherous, ice-slicked rocks, desperately seeking higher ground. The first wave, estimated at over 10 meters high, crashed against their previous position, sending spray and debris showering over their heads.

"We were literally running for our lives, feeling the ground shake beneath us," Dr. Sharma described, her voice still hoarse. "The force of the water was immense. If we'd been a minute slower, we wouldn't be here."

Miraculously, the drone, piloted by Sarah Chen, managed to capture the entire terrifying sequence. The footage, now being analyzed by safety experts, shows the team's frantic escape and the sheer scale of the calving event. "I just kept the drone up, hoping to get something useful, even as I was running," Chen admitted. "It was pure instinct."

While the immediate danger passed, the incident has prompted a re-evaluation of safety protocols for glacier observation. Dr. Sharma, who has dedicated two decades to studying Arctic ice, called it "the most unpredictable and dangerous event" she'd witnessed in her career.

The dramatic footage is expected to feature prominently in the "Frozen Frontiers" documentary, offering a chilling, first-hand account of the accelerating changes in our polar regions and the risks scientists undertake to understand them. The team, though shaken, remains committed to their vital research, even as they acknowledge the increasing unpredictability of the environment they study.

Certainly! Here’s an educational text on a related topic, focusing on how to use reflective listening to enhance conversations in a way that promotes comfort and connection:

---

**Enhancing Conversations with Reflective Listening**

Building meaningful conversations, especially with new acquaintances or in sensitive situations, can feel challenging. Sometimes, even well-intended questions or enthusiastic storytelling can unintentionally make others feel on the spot or unheard. One powerful, often underused technique to create ease in your interactions is *reflective listening*.

Reflective listening is the practice of carefully listening to what someone says, then responding by summarizing or paraphrasing their words and emotions. Instead of jumping in with advice, judgments, or more questions, you help the speaker feel understood and validated.

This method does not require you to agree with every point, nor is it about offering solutions. Instead, it places the focus on the speaker’s experience. For example, if someone says, “I’m really nervous about my presentation tomorrow,” an instinct might be to ask, “What are you nervous about?” or “Have you done many presentations?” While not harmful, these questions shift focus or add pressure to explain. With reflective listening, you could respond: “It sounds like you’re feeling anxious about how the presentation will go.” This statement acknowledges and affirms their feeling, inviting them to share more if they wish—without demanding a response.

Reflective listening can take many forms:
- **Simple Reflection:** Restate what you heard.  
  *“So, you’re worried about tomorrow.”*
- **Clarifying Reflection:** Check if you’ve understood correctly.  
  *“Are you saying the preparation is what’s making you most anxious?”*
- **Empathic Reflection:** Address the emotions involved.  
  *“It must feel overwhelming to have so much riding on one event.”*

Why does this matter? When people feel truly heard, their anxiety often decreases. This builds trust and opens the door for deeper, more honest dialogue. It also prevents conversational pitfalls like interrupting, hijacking the topic, or unintentionally making someone defend their position.

You can practice reflective listening in everyday situations—at work, with family, or with friends. At your next opportunity, try setting aside advice or further questions. Instead, mirror what the other person is expressing. Notice how the conversation becomes more relaxed and meaningful.

In summary, switching from an interrogative to a reflective style creates a conversation climate where people feel supported rather than interrogated. Reflective listening encourages genuine connection by validating and respecting each other’s feelings and thoughts, allowing conversations to flourish naturally and comfortably.

by helen lawson published: 08:18 est, 10 june 2013 | UK retail sales unexpectedly dipped last month, casting a fresh shadow over the nation's fragile economic recovery and prompting renewed concerns for the struggling high street. Official figures released this morning by the Office for National Statistics (ONS) showed that retail sales volumes fell by 0.1% in May, defying analyst predictions of a modest increase.

The slight decline follows a stronger performance in April, which many hoped signalled a turning point for consumer spending. Economists had broadly anticipated a continuation of that upward trend, fuelled by warmer weather and the long bank holiday weekend. However, the data reveals a more cautious consumer, with spending on non-food items, particularly clothing and footwear, seeing the steepest fall.

"This is undoubtedly a setback for retailers hoping to capitalise on improving consumer confidence," said Sarah Jones, a senior economist at Capital Markets. "While one month's data doesn't make a trend, it highlights the persistent challenges facing the high street as households grapple with stagnant wage growth and ongoing austerity measures."

The ONS report indicated that despite a small uptick in online sales, this was not enough to offset the overall decline in brick-and-mortar purchases. Supermarkets and food retailers, however, saw a marginal increase, suggesting that essential spending remains more resilient than discretionary purchases.

Business leaders are now urging the government to consider further measures to stimulate consumer spending and support struggling independent shops. "The high street is the heart of our communities, and these figures are a stark reminder of the pressure it's under," commented Richard Smith, CEO of the British Retail Consortium. "We need policies that put more money back into people's pockets and encourage investment in our town centres."

The Bank of England will be closely scrutinising these figures as it weighs its options for monetary policy, with some analysts now suggesting that the prospect of further quantitative easing might be back on the table if economic data continues to disappoint. The FTSE 100 saw a slight dip in early trading following the announcement.

BBC Wales reported that five Welsh Conservative MPs have called on George Osborne to overturn a decision announced in November. They argued the policy shift was "disrespectful" and violated the Conservatives' 2015 election promises.

However, Cardiff North MP Craig Williams insisted there was no major dispute, adding that Assembly Members should demonstrate "some fiscal responsibility." Welsh Secretary Stephen Crabb has argued that granting income tax powers to Wales would lead to "better government for Wales."

Some of the dissenting MPs aim to discuss the matter with Mr Osborne in a meeting on Wednesday. The five—Byron Davies, Chris Davies, David Davies, James Davies, and David Jones—have sent him a letter about the issue.

"For many of us, this is a line in the sand," one MP stated. Another suggested it would be "very embarrassing" for the Conservative government if nearly half of its 11 Welsh MPs were to oppose the measure.

The letter claimed that during the general election, Tory candidates were able to assure voters that the Welsh assembly would not receive tax-raising powers without the people's consent. "Abandoning the commitment to a referendum would mean breaking a clear manifesto pledge," the letter added.

The Welsh Conservatives' 2015 manifesto said there was an "expectation that the Welsh government will hold a referendum on income tax powers." The five MPs argued that Welsh voters should not be "treated differently from the people of Scotland," who had a separate vote on tax powers during the 1997 devolution referendum.

"We believe imposing such powers without a referendum would be disrespectful to the Welsh people," they said.

Responding, Mr Williams commented: "You have situations where business rates are being devolved to places like Manchester, so Manchester City Council could have more fiscal control than all of Wales." He continued, "It's time the assembly was responsible for raising money as well as spending it on its own projects."

In December, Welsh Secretary Stephen Crabb said public opinion on the matter was "fluid," but there were "very, very strong reasons why we must move forward" to help strengthen Wales and its economy.

Disagreements over income tax devolution previously caused Welsh Conservative leader Andrew RT Davies to dismiss four shadow front benchers in February 2014, though they were reinstated in the summer. Mr Davies later stated he would cut 5p from the higher rate and 1p from the basic rate of income tax in Wales if elected first minister.

A Welsh Conservatives spokesperson said: "The UK government and Chancellor's position on this is very clear. We have consistently urged the Welsh government to take more responsibility for the money it raises and spends."

A Plaid Cymru spokesperson responded: "This shows the Tories' real stance on empowering Wales. They back giving significant powers, including policing, to English cities without a referendum, but are trying to prevent Wales from gaining even limited control over income tax, which could benefit the Welsh economy."

So at this store you can spend a couple hundred dollars on shirts and other stuff, but no way in hell can your daughter use the bathroom here. The air inside is heavy with the sharp scent of new fabric and cologne, the constant beep of scanners and low murmur of shoppers filling your ears, but apparently, basic humanity isn’t for sale. One of the cashiers, her voice clipped and distracted, said, "I know Circle K is open, they have a bathroom there," so I took my daughter down the block, the rough sidewalk scraping under our hurried steps, only to be smacked in the face by the stale, chemical smell of Circle K, where a handwritten "Out of Order" sign flapped on the bathroom door. My daughter shifted from foot to foot, hands clenched, and I felt a sick twist in my stomach as I scrambled to find another place for her before there was an issue. All of that could have been avoided with the simple, warm reassurance of "Yeah, no problem," but instead we were left out in the cold, desperate and uncomfortable.

Ganymede Impact Crater Morphology as Revealed by Galileo
C. M. Weitz, J. W. Head, R. Pappalardo (Brown University), C. Chapman (Southwest Research Inst.), R. Greeley (Arizona State University), P. Helfenstein (Cornell University), G. Neukum (DLR), Galileo SSI Team
We have used the Galileo G1, G2, G7, and G8 images to study the morpholo- gy and degradation of impact craters on Ganymede. Results from the G1 and G2 data showed three types of degradation states: pristine, partially degraded, and heavily degraded. With the more recent G7 and G8 images, there are now several other distinct crater morphologies that we have identified. Enki Catena is about 120 km in length and consists of 13 attached impact craters. The six craters in the chain that impacted onto the bright terrain have visible bright ejecta while those that impacted onto the dark terrain have barely visible ejecta. Kittu crater is about 15 km in diameter and it has a bright central peak surrounded by a bright floor and hummocky wall material. The crater rim in the north is linear in appearance at the location that corresponds to the boundary between the groove terrain and the adjacent dark terrain, indicating structural control by the underlying topography. The dark rays that are easily seen in the Voyager images are barely visible in the Galileo image. Neith crater has a central fractured dome surrounded by a jagged central ring, smoother outer ejecta facies, and less prominent outer rings. Achelous crater and its neighbor, which were imaged at low sun angle to show topography, have smooth floors and subdued pedestal ejecta. Nicholson Regio has tectonically disrupted craters on the groove and fractured terrains while the surrounding smoother dark terrain has numerous degrad- ed craters that may indicate burial by resurfacing or by regolith development.

A few days before he graduated, one of my students asked me how one keeps up with public affairs (as I was advocating) when juggling a demanding full-time job, a cute but fussy baby, an elderly parent who needs care, and something of a social life. 

I told him it isn’t easy, but suggested a few strategies: listen to NPR, watch CNN, and, from time to time, choose a particular story to follow closely. For instance, I mentioned the ongoing issue of what happens when major American corporations break the law. Recently, The New York Times investigated how the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) handles companies that violate anti-fraud laws. Frequently, the settlements that follow simply require the companies to promise not to break the law again—an odd arrangement, as the companies are already obligated by law not to commit fraud. The Times found that, over the past 15 years, 19 Wall Street firms—including Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan Chase, and Bank of America—broke anti-fraud laws they had already promised not to violate, sometimes repeatedly. Instead of bringing contempt charges, the SEC typically settles with another promise and penalty, without requiring the companies to admit wrongdoing; the ubiquitous "neither admit nor deny" clause protects them from investor lawsuits.

Shortly after the Times article appeared, The Washington Post reported that the SEC agreed to collect $285 million as a penalty for a $700 million loss to investors due to Citigroup’s fraud. Once again, Citigroup was allowed to "neither admit nor deny" any wrongdoing. This time, however, U.S. District Judge Jed S. Rakoff rejected the settlement as too weak, criticizing the SEC for being toothless and allowing executives to evade real accountability. Meanwhile, the director of the documentary "Inside Job," Charles Ferguson, highlighted the lack of criminal prosecutions after widespread mortgage fraud and signature forgeries during the financial crisis—a fact unchanged even under the Obama administration.

What happened next is telling. Despite new revelations, not much else did. The national media gave more attention to the latest accusations against Herman Cain than to systemic corporate crime. The White House was silent, and the Department of Justice took no evident action. Democratic leaders in Congress raised no outcry and called for no new investigation. While a federal judge had rejected a weak settlement, it remained unclear what the final outcome would be.

In the end, I told my student, such news stories say a lot about the current state of our society: a powerful Wall Street, a passive political establishment, and an exhausted public. Some days, it’s enough to make one wonder whether to join Occupy Wall Street, the Tea Party, or both. 

The opinions expressed in this commentary are solely those of Amitai Etzioni.

Spiders are amazing architects of silk, and now they can add a new skill to their resumes: electrical engineering. The garden cross spider, Araneus diadematus, tailors a web containing carefully crafted patterns of water and silk that efficiently conducts electricity. The web threads are made of silk laced with special silk “droplets” swollen with water and a special chemical cocktail that functions as a highly adhesive and water-attractant glue—a sticky trap that also works as a water magnet, sucking humidity from all around. This concoction electrically charges the web and helps spiders catch their prey, according to a study in Naturwissenschaften. The study is the first to report the electric twist on a spider’s arsenal to capture prey, along with detailed explanations on how this mechanism works. The researchers show that the webs cause tiny distortions in Earth’s electric field within a few millimeters of their location. This allows the web to literally spring toward prey as well as small charged particles like pollen and pollutants. According to the authors, such webs could also be used to monitor environmental pollution, as they are as efficient as industrial sensors at detecting and capturing airborne pollutants, such as pesticides.

Russia is Turkey's second most important foreign trading partner after Germany.
As a result, experts say economic retaliation would be keenly felt by Ankara - but the Kremlin would not be unharmed either.
Here the BBC's Russian Service looks at the main trade links between Russia and Turkey that risk being hit hardest by the downturn in relations.
Turkey is the top foreign holiday destination for Russian tourists. Some 3.2 million Russian holidaymakers travelled to Turkey last year, according to Russia's Federal Tourism Agency. In comparison, 2.5 million went on holiday to Egypt.
Until the past year, Russians made up the largest group of tourists in Turkey. But they lost the top spot to Germans because of the fall of the rouble.
Bookings to Turkey went back up after Russia banned flights to Egypt following the Sinai plane crash on 31 October. However, the downing of the Russian SU-24 warplane by Turkey means it is unlikely this trend will continue.
Turkey bought 57% of its gas from Russia in 2013.
Last year it became the second largest consumer of Russian gas after Germany: Germany imported 36bn cubic metres (bcm) and Turkey imported 27.3bcm.
Turkey gets its Russian gas via two routes - the Blue Stream pipeline across the Black Sea, and the gas transportation corridor through Romania, Ukraine and Moldova.
Plans for a "Turkish Stream" to transport 63bcm of Russian gas through Turkey and on to Europe - bypassing Ukraine - have stalled since they were announced by the Russian and Turkish presidents in December 2014.
Turkey has to import nearly all the energy it consumes.
In 2012, Moscow and Ankara agreed to build Turkey's first nuclear power plant, the Akkuyu plant. It was designed to save Turkey about $14bn (Â£9bn) annually on energy imports.
The nuclear power plant is now under construction in the province of Mersin on the Mediterranean coast. Russia has already invested $3bn in it.
In October, the Turkish authorities said Russia's military activity in Syria could threaten its future involvement in the project.
President Recep Tayyip Erdogan said in October: "If the Russians don't build Akkuyu, someone else will come along and build it."
There are about 100 Turkish construction companies working in Russia.
Turkish firms have built more than 800 projects in Russia since the end of the 1980s, according to official Russian data.
Turkish investors also have stakes in a number of brands sold in the Russian market, such as Tevolina shoes and the Gloria Jeans clothes brand, as well as the food and chemical industries.
On Wednesday the Russian state-owned news agency RIA Novosti quoted Gennadiy Onishchenko, an aide to the Russian prime minister, as saying that people who bought Turkish goods financially supported Turkish servicemen.
"Everyone understands that each Turkish tomato bought... is a contribution to yet another missile which will be shooting at our guys," he was quoted as saying.
Compiled by Dmitry Bulin

A phone's light glows on the dark road - the only light for miles. On the screen is a news article about the "Devil passenger of Johor." Eleven days ago, a young Chinese woman who picked up a hitchhiker was found strangled in her car. This is the fourth similar murder in six months.

"11 days, 7 hours and 22 minutes," mutters the hooded man holding the phone. The urge to kill feels like an itch he can't scratch. He's tried therapy with Dr. Sirivani and medication, but nothing helps. His therapist says the victims deserve to live, but doesn't he deserve happiness too?

A car rumbles in the distance. His heart pounds as he sticks out his thumb, hoping for another victim.

In the approaching car, the driver sees the hooded man and wonders if he's the "devil passenger." Three years ago, she held her murdered sister's body. When the police failed to help, she became a vigilante. She's killed over a dozen criminals - rapists, robbers, thieves - using different methods to avoid detection.

This is her first serial killer target. After three days of searching, she's finally found him. She slows down, lowers her window, and asks cheerfully in Malaysian: "Do you need a ride?"

According to ancient records, the patriarchs and founders of early civilizations in Egypt, India, China, Peru, Mesopotamia, Britain, and the Americas were established by the Serpents of Wisdom—spiritual masters associated with the serpent who arrived in these lands after leaving their beloved homelands and crossing great seas. These Serpents of Wisdom bore names denoting snake or dragon (such as Naga, Lung, Djedhi, Amaru, Quetzalcoatl, Adder, etc.) and oversaw the construction of magnificent civilizations where they and their descendants served as priest kings and enlightened heads of mystery school traditions. The Return of the Serpents of Wisdom recounts the history of these "Serpents"—where they came from, why they came, the secret wisdom they disseminated, and why they are returning now.

This beautifully written book is an idyllic tale of raising perfect children in a perfect world, where mom has all the patience and inventiveness of Mary Poppins. Tian Dayton's examples of raising her two children (who apparently never caused any problems at all) are so picture perfect that it actually made me depressed thinking about our daily stuggles with our very strong-willed 4-year old. Although this book portrays mothering as an unrealistic fairy tale, it does also serve as a goal, showing what we can all strive for in our quest to be the perfect mom. It is calming to read and explains the importance of touch, the role of the limbic system, and the importance of self-regulation. I'd recommend the book, but if you're looking for some real examples of how to manage difficult behaviours, I would highly recommend "Children are from Heaven" or "1-2-3 Magic" instead.

oprah winfrey is visiting india for the first time to participate in a series of cultural, philanthropic, and business events across several cities. The renowned American talk show host and media mogul landed in Mumbai on Monday, greeted by fans and members of the press at Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport.

Winfrey’s itinerary includes attending the Jaipur Literature Festival as a special guest speaker, visiting the headquarters of multiple non-profit organizations, and meeting with prominent Indian personalities from the fields of entertainment, literature, and business. According to sources close to her team, Oprah will also tour historic sites in Delhi and Agra, including the Taj Mahal, as part of a documentary segment highlighting India’s rich history and traditions.

Speaking briefly to reporters upon her arrival, Winfrey expressed excitement about her first visit to the country. “India has always fascinated me with its vibrant culture, spiritual traditions, and remarkable stories of resilience. I’m looking forward to learning, connecting, and sharing these experiences with my audience back home,” she said.

A highlight of her trip will be a planned interview with Indian women leaders and social reformers for an upcoming special on the Oprah Winfrey Network. Organizers say her presence is expected to spotlight pressing issues such as women’s empowerment and education in India, creating a platform for greater international dialogue and support.

Winfrey’s visit marks a significant cultural exchange as she connects with local communities and brings global attention to India’s evolving social landscape. Her journey is scheduled to last ten days, during which she will travel to Mumbai, Delhi, Jaipur, and Agra before returning to the United States.

the challenge is to develop sustainable water resource programs with a holistic understanding of ecological integrity, socio-economic equity, and technological innovation. This is not merely an engineering problem but a complex adaptive challenge requiring interdisciplinary collaboration and flexible policy frameworks. As global populations grow and climate change intensifies the frequency and severity of droughts and floods, traditional 'supply-side' management approaches are proving insufficient. We must shift towards integrated water resource management (IWRM) that considers the entire water cycle, from source to tap and back to the environment.

Key components of such programs include robust data collection and monitoring to inform decision-making, investment in water-saving technologies such as precision agriculture and advanced wastewater treatment for reuse, and the protection and restoration of natural water infrastructure like wetlands and forests. Equally crucial is public education and participation, fostering a culture of water conservation and responsible stewardship. Empowering local communities to manage their resources, alongside strong governance and regulatory frameworks, ensures that solutions are both context-specific and sustainable in the long term. Financing these initiatives requires innovative mechanisms, blending public investment with private sector engagement and international aid. Ultimately, success hinges on a commitment to long-term planning, adaptability, and the recognition that water is a shared, finite resource vital for all life.

**League Cup Second Round Delivers Stunning Upsets as Lower Division Sides Shine**

The Scottish League Cup's second round produced a weekend of dramatic results, with several lower-league teams causing major upsets against their higher-division opponents.

The most shocking result came at Stark's Park, where League One side Raith Rovers stunned Championship favorites Dundee United 2-1 in a thrilling Group C encounter. Goals from Jamie Gullan and Lewis Vaughan in the final twenty minutes overturned United's early lead, sending the home crowd into raptures.

"The boys showed incredible character tonight," said Rovers manager Ian Murray after the match. "We knew we had to be at our absolute best, and they delivered when it mattered most."

Meanwhile, League Two outfit Stirling Albion held their nerve to defeat Greenock Morton 3-2 on penalties following a 1-1 draw in Group A. The Binos, who had never progressed beyond this stage in recent memory, celebrated wildly as goalkeeper Blair Currie saved the decisive spot-kick.

Not all lower-league sides enjoyed success, however. Forfar Athletic's dreams were crushed by a clinical Queen of the South performance, with the Doonhamers running out comfortable 4-0 winners at Station Park. Striker Josh Todd grabbed a hat-trick to put his side firmly in control of Group F.

In Group B, Ross County maintained their perfect start to the competition with a hard-fought 2-0 victory over Airdrieonians. The Highlanders, who reached the quarter-finals last season, look well-placed to advance once again after goals from Jordan White and Alex Iacovitti sealed the win.

The weekend's action also saw Arbroath continue their impressive cup form with a narrow 1-0 triumph over Alloa Athletic. Dick Campbell's side, who have built a reputation for giant-killing in recent years, showed their experience in tight matches as they ground out another crucial victory.

Partick Thistle bounced back from their opening group defeat with an emphatic 3-1 win against East Fife, while Cove Rangers maintained their momentum from last season's promotion with a professional 2-0 success over Stranraer.

The results have significantly shaken up the group standings, with several pre-tournament favorites now facing must-win scenarios in their remaining fixtures. Dundee United, in particular, will need to regroup quickly after their surprise defeat left them bottom of their section.

"Cup football is unpredictable – that's what makes it special," commented SFA Cup Committee chairman Rod Petrie. "These results show the strength in depth across Scottish football."

The third round of group matches takes place next weekend, with qualification spots still up for grabs across all eight groups. Based on this weekend's evidence, more surprises could be in store as Scotland's smaller clubs continue to punch above their weight.

With prize money and prestige at stake, the remaining group fixtures promise to deliver more compelling drama as teams battle for their place in the knockout stages.

**A Leap of Hope: Captive-Bred Mountain Yellow-Legged Frogs Return to the Wild**

In a significant stride for biodiversity and amphibian conservation, a new generation of mountain yellow-legged frogs ( _Rana muscosa_ ) has been released into their native Sierra Nevada habitat. This meticulously planned reintroduction marks a critical phase in a multi-agency effort to save this iconic species from the brink of extinction, offering a beacon of hope for one of California's most imperiled amphibians.

Once a common sight and sound in the high-elevation streams and lakes of the Sierra Nevada, mountain yellow-legged frog populations have plummeted by over 90% in recent decades. The primary culprits are a devastating combination of chytrid fungus ( _Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis_ ), a highly virulent amphibian disease, and historical predation by non-native fish introduced for recreational angling. The remaining wild populations are fragmented and vulnerable, making active intervention essential for their survival.

This ambitious conservation program relies heavily on a sophisticated captive breeding effort, a testament to the dedication of wildlife biologists and herpetologists. For years, carefully selected adult frogs were brought into specialized facilities, mimicking their natural environment to encourage breeding. The resulting tadpoles and metamorphs (juvenile frogs) were then raised in a protected, disease-free environment, shielding them from the threats that decimate their wild counterparts. This controlled setting allows the frogs to grow larger and stronger, significantly increasing their chances of survival once released.

The release itself is a carefully choreographed event. Biologists transport the young frogs, often numbering in the hundreds, to pre-selected sites within their historical range. These locations are chosen based on several factors: the absence of non-native fish, the presence of suitable breeding and foraging habitat, and—crucially—evidence that the chytrid fungus either doesn't persist or that the local frog populations show some level of resistance. Each frog is often microchipped for future identification, allowing researchers to monitor their progress, dispersal, and long-term survival rates.

The goal of these releases is twofold: to bolster existing, dwindling populations and to establish new, healthy colonies in areas where the frogs have long since vanished. By introducing genetically diverse individuals, conservationists aim to increase the species' overall resilience and adaptability, particularly in the face of ongoing environmental challenges like climate change. The hope is that these released frogs will not only survive but thrive, reproduce, and eventually contribute to a self-sustaining wild population.

While the road to recovery for the mountain yellow-legged frog is long and fraught with challenges, these releases represent a vital step forward. They underscore the power of collaborative conservation, combining cutting-edge scientific research with dedicated field work. Each small frog taking its first hop into the wild carries with it the hopes of a species and a powerful message about our collective responsibility to protect the planet's precious biodiversity. The reintroduction of the mountain yellow-legged frog is more than just a conservation effort; it is an act of restoration, aiming to mend a crucial thread in the intricate tapestry of the Sierra Nevada ecosystem.

America was built on the back of the railroads, probably more than any other country or land in the world. The great rail companies and their tycoons distributed wealth and opportunity from one side of the continent to the other and up and down its breadth. The railroads relied on the backbreaking labor of newly arrived migrants to the "land of the free." Their sweat and lives went into laying the lines that would carry the great iron and steel engines and their carriages all over America. The train in the United States is a symbol of the success of the free enterprise capitalist economic system.

During the US Civil War (1861-1865), the railroads played a vital part in the North's success over the South. They transported essential items throughout the North, while the shorter rail lines in the South were starved of the replacement parts they needed during the war. The South's hoarding of the cotton crop economically depressed the Southern railways even further. After the Confederate defeat in 1865, the Southern railroads were rebuilt and extended through investment from the North.

New York financier JP Morgan was a powerful force in the railroad industry toward the end of the nineteenth century, not only investing heavily but also driving change in how the industry operated. He encouraged consolidation and pushed for integration. Governments then followed suit with greater regulation of the industry, ensuring its interstate functionality. The economic disaster within the industry during the Panic of 1893 saw around 25% of private railroads fail in the USA, leading to further consolidation within the industry.

Today, the American railroad is a sleek industry that has been pared down over decades through competition with airlines for passengers and trucks for freight. There have been periods of nationalization during wartime and in the 1970s, as well as numerous bankruptcies over the years, thinning out the players. After many years of being unprofitable, the railroads have made a comeback in the freight business and are now hauling a wide range of goods across the country. Technological innovations have made the railroads competitive again in the freight business, and promotional products and services have helped market this fact to US businesses across the continent. Fast trains, or very fast trains, could be the next development on the agenda in the US railroad industry, as the future is fast, and trains need to be able to keep up with the pace.

We've been stationed here for days, drilling away at the earth's crust. Every day for the past week we've drilled non-stop. The sensation of the drill's friction became so familiar to all four of us, until we hit yet another open cavity. Chuck noticed the drill stop. "You think we'll find something this time?" "Don't know, might be a dud like the last six times." Then I felt the ground shake—and the shaking grew stronger, no, closer. Thousands of red dots, each the size of a coin, poured out of the hole. They swarmed up my leg, biting all the way. I grabbed the hose we use for lubricating the drill and sprayed them off, escaping with only a few bites. "We found it!" I shouted. I ran to the shed and got the melted aluminum. I poured it in and waited for it to cool. Once it solidified, I pulled it out—a remarkable decoration, which I later sold for a large sum at auction. The doctor said the bites gave me a strange illness, but that didn't bother me much.

Heartburn and Your Heart
Heartburn didn’t get its name by accident. Although the problem stems from excess stomach acid rather than heart-related issues, it provokes searing pain focused directly behind the breastbone. Of the over eight million emergency room visits for chest pain each year, severe heartburn, also known as gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), accounts for over half the cases in which actual heart problems are ruled out.
The advent of a class of drugs called proton-pump inhibitors (PPIs) has been a godsend for people plagued by acid reflux. According to the FDA, about one in 14 people in the United States currently takes or has taken a PPI drug.
“PPIs are far more effective for suppressing stomach acid production than earlier classes of drugs. For people who are subject to bleeding in their intestinal tract, they can also lower that risk,” says Dr. Michelle O’Donoghue, cardiovascular specialist at Harvard-affiliated Brigham and Women’s Hospital.
Perils of PPIs?
But no drug is completely safe. Proton-pump inhibitors may cause allergic reactions (such as a rash) or diarrhea. Although the data are not conclusive, some studies have linked their use to osteoporosis (weakening of the bones) and an increased risk of pneumonia among people admitted to the hospital. Some reports have suggested that PPIs may interfere with the effectiveness of the heart drug clopidogrel (Plavix).
Now, there’s a new concern. Research published in the online journal PLOS ONE in June reports that the PPIs may increase the risk of a heart attack. However, Dr. O’Donoghue says these results should be viewed with caution. Observational studies like this one can’t account for every possible factor that might skew the results, she says. For example, some of the people in the study who were taking PPIs to treat acid reflux may have been doing so incorrectly because they were actually experiencing symptoms of heart disease. Also, people who were being treated with PPIs may have been suffering from other illnesses that may have increased their risk of heart attack. “At this point, there is no clear evidence that PPIs increase a person’s risk of heart disease, and I wouldn’t advise people to stop taking PPIs because of worries about heart attack risk alone,” says Dr. O’Donoghue. “Nonetheless, it’s a good idea to periodically reevaluate with your doctor any medication you use.”
If you have mild GERD with no other complicating medical conditions, you may be able to cut back on your PPI use. But check with your doctor first. You may need to continue on the drug if you regularly take aspirin, blood thinners, or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (see above story) or if you have a condition such as stomach ulcers or Barrett’s esophagus. Lifestyle changes such as losing weight, sleeping with the head of your bed slightly elevated, and avoiding eating before bed can all help calm stomach acid.

It is believed to be the first alleged breach of the Terrorism Prevention and Investigation Measures (TPim).
The suspect appeared at the Old Bailey accused of failing to contact a monitoring company on 16 January and failing to report to a police station on 27 January.
The Home Office has refused to comment on the case.
TPims replaced control orders in January 2012.
The suspect - who can only be referred to as BM - was one of nine British citizens subject to a TPim when the figures were last disclosed in March 2012.
The BBC is not aware of anyone else who has appeared in court charged with a breach of a TPim.
The Home Office said: "We do not comment on individual cases. The police investigate all breaches of TPims and will prosecute where that is viable."
Those subject to a TPim can be ordered to stay overnight at a specified address and report to a police station every day.
Other measures include a ban on contacting particular individuals, going to certain areas and places and travelling abroad.
Labour has argued that TPims have "weakened" public protection against terrorism.
In March 2012, the independent reviewer of terror laws, David Anderson, said TPims could prove less effective than control orders because they can be imposed for a maximum period of two years only.
The court heard that BM is charged with two counts of breaching Section 23 of a TPim order.
The allegations are that on 16 January he failed to contact a monitoring company, and on 27 January failed to report to a police station.
BM, who has a grey beard and was dressed for the hearing in a white shirt, cannot be named nor have his address published for legal reasons.
The prosecutor Louise Gray asked for a provisional date of 2 July 2012 to be set for a plea and case management hearing.
The defendant was granted unconditional bail although he remains subject to the restrictions imposed by his TPim.
A trial date was not set.

Detective Miller surveyed the scene, a crisp November chill biting through the thin air of the isolated cabin. Inside, the opulence of the deceased, Mr. Arthur Harrington, clashed starkly with the rustic log walls. Harrington, a city developer, lay sprawled beside his polished mahogany desk, a single bullet wound marring his expensive suit. No forced entry, no struggle. Just a silent, deadly efficiency.

Miller’s gaze swept over the pristine furnishings, the untouched crystal decanter, the unlit fireplace. Everything spoke of quiet, undisturbed wealth, save for the man bleeding out on the Persian rug. Then, tucked neatly beside the hearth, Miller saw it: a single, well-worn moccasin.

It was crafted from soft, pliable buckskin, hand-stitched with intricate, faded beadwork around the cuff. It looked ancient, almost reverent, completely out of place in Harrington’s modern hunting retreat. Miller knelt, gloved hand hovering.

“Belong to the victim?” he asked Deputy O’Malley, a quiet local whose eyes held the wisdom of the surrounding woods.

O’Malley shook his head. “Mr. Harrington wore hiking boots that cost more than my first truck. Never saw him in anything like that.”

“Killer’s, then?” Miller mused, picturing a stealthy figure, moving silently through the forest.

O’Malley’s brow furrowed. He pointed a calloused finger at the sole of the moccasin, where a faint, dark smear glistened. “Pine pitch. Fresh. And this kind of stitching… it’s a craft. Not something you just wear to sneak.”

Miller picked up the moccasin, turning it over. The beading was exquisite, a story in itself. “A message?”

O’Malley nodded slowly. “Harrington was buying up old land, Deputy. Planning his new ‘Wilderness Luxury Resort.’ Lots of folks here have long memories. And a deep respect for what’s ours.” He paused, looking out the cabin’s window towards the dense, whispering trees. “Sometimes, a man forgets what’s beneath his feet. And sometimes, the land reminds him.”

Miller looked at the silent moccasin in his hand, then at the dead developer. The murder was brutal, but the discarded footwear felt almost like a lament. A warning. The wilderness, it seemed, had finally spoken.

Credit: SPI Collaboration; ESA
Chemical elements are formed in a variety of ways. Some, like 26Al, an isotope of aluminum, are only produced during stellar explosions. Thus the amount of this isotope acts as a tracer of how many stars have exploded. Because this isotope has a very short half-life (only a million years, the blink of an astronomical eye) it can help astronomers understand how many stars have exploded in the very recent past. For example, the surprisingly large amount of 26Al in interplanetary space may indicate that the solar system was rather recently bathed by the ejecta of a nearby stellar explosion (which would have have rather dramatic effects for life on earth). Now astronomers have a new tool to probe the amount of 26Al in the Galaxy. The INTEGRAL gamma-ray observatory is able to measure gamma-ray emission from the decay of 26Al using the SPI spectrometer. The image above shows a Gamma-ray map of the Galaxy (made by a predecessor of INTEGRAL, the COMPTEL detector on the Compton Gamma-Ray Observatory). Two bright regions of 26Al emission in the center of the Galaxy and in the Cygnus region have now been observed by SPI, and SPI has clearly detected the 26Al emission line (which can be seen in the insets). Eventually SPI will observe the entire Galactic plane and will provide astronomers with the best measure of recent nucleosynthesis yet obtained.
