My slow but steady hang sifted the blinds down, just enough so I could peer beside my house. The sun greeted me with photon rays, forcing my pupils to retreat back in fear. As the bright light faded my vision became clear. I gulped as sweat trickled down my nose, dripping onto the extended arm. I haven't prepared for this moment I kept repeating In my head over and over almost like it was a broken record. I let go of the blinds and they snapped back in place, unleashing an annoying sound at a time like this. I got onto my feet, almost falling down. "Damn nerves" I softy hissed as if to someone. The room fell silent. It felt like eons before a noise interrupted my conciousness. Footsteps echoed from outside the room. They seemed to grow louder as they approached where I was. Sweat now pouring down my face I adjusted my collar, leaving a wetness on my hand. As if a bomb just exploded..."dut""dut""dut".. "three knocks rang out and stood dead in the air. The urge to puke rose as I proceeded to the door. Sweat running into my mouth leaving a salty taste which one could only hate. I reached out to the door knob, sweat noticeably lubricating the knob. I hesitate as I turn until the satisfying click breaks my fear. Light is coming in through the crack, yet no one is viseable. The doors swings softly open as he come into my eyesight. Hatred boils through me, I feel blood rush to my head. I grit my teeth, half expecting them to break with the sheer force, I manage to get out the one word I can. With the force of one thousand suns, the power of 40000 horses, with all my godly might.................. "DINKLEEEEEBERRRGGGGGGGGGGGG!!!!"

Today was the day. The Big One. the Grand Heist. One last score before we split for good. Michael, the mage, readied his various spells. Trevor, the Berserker, raised his large War Axe, a toothy grin adorning his battered, and bruised face. Franklin, the Rider, was nowhere to be found, to provide the getaway once we finished the heist. I was ready as well, providing overwatch with my crossbow, atop a hill across from the castle, out of sight of the Sentries, when a miniaturized Michael appeared next to me, explaining the course of action, and my duties. I was to take out the sentries before Michael and Trevor arrived. Taking aim, I pulled the trigger, letting the steel, poison tipped bolt fly. *Thwack! * It hit, right in the heart of the nearest sentry. After quickly reloading, I took aim at the second sentry, just as a gust of wind picked up. Waiting for the moment, I saw Michael and Trevor approach, before casting an invisibility spell, sneaking into the castle before them. 

As I prepared for my next shot, my mind flashed back to our first job together. It was in a dusty little town, years ago, where Michael first froze a guard solid and Trevor, with that same manic grin, smashed open the sheriff’s vault. Franklin, lounging nearby with his boots up, had laughed, “Reckon we’ll never be caught!” I laughed too, unsure if I believed him, but knowing that this crew, odd as it was, felt like family.

When I made my way up to the remaining sentry, I pulled out my Antique Thief's Dagger, and drove the fine, steel blade home, covering the sentry's mouth to avoid detection, when I started hearing screaming, and the sound of heavy steel impacting flesh, a distinct cracking following suite. They were in. Quickly descending the spiraling staircase, I found myself on the wrong end of a Longsword. Quickly rolling out of the way, I joined the rest of the Crew, quickly pilfering as much of the gold and silver as we could, Trevor taking the brunt of the load, still able to swing his axe and sever rival knights. "GO! GO! Get to Franklin, I'll hold them off!" I shouted, using up all of my Mana to let Trevor and Michael escape. That was when I felt it. The cold steel entering my body. I looked down, only to see the blood-soaked shimmer of a Broadsword impaled through my chest. Everything started to fade, my vision dimming. My breath slowed as I collapsed, feeling the wind rush into the gaping hole of my chest. Bleeding out, with my last breaths, I saw my crew ride off, pursued by the Constable and his Deputies.

## A Gem in the City: The Culinary Canvas Bistro

Stepping into The Culinary Canvas Bistro felt less like entering a restaurant and more like being invited into a vibrant, edible art gallery. My partner and I were celebrating our anniversary, and after hearing rave reviews, we decided to give this new downtown spot a try. From the moment we walked in, it was clear this wasn't your average dining experience.

The ambiance alone deserves its own paragraph. The decor strikes a perfect balance between modern chic and cozy warmth. Exposed brick walls are adorned with stunning, locally sourced artwork that changes quarterly, giving the space a fresh, dynamic feel. Soft, amber lighting casts a flattering glow, and the low hum of conversation, punctuated by the occasional clink of cutlery, creates a sophisticated yet relaxed atmosphere. We were seated promptly at a charming banquette overlooking a small, meticulously maintained herb garden – a lovely touch that hinted at the freshness to come.

Our server, a young woman named Chloe, was an absolute delight. She was knowledgeable without being pretentious, attentive without being intrusive, and genuinely enthusiastic about the menu. Her recommendations were spot-on, guiding us through a delightful culinary journey. We started with the "Deconstructed Caprese," a playful and flavorful twist on the classic, featuring heirloom tomatoes, house-made mozzarella spheres, and a basil foam that was surprisingly light and aromatic.

For our main courses, I opted for the pan-seared scallops with saffron risotto, while my partner chose the duck confit with cherry reduction. Both dishes were masterpieces. My scallops were perfectly seared, tender and sweet, complemented beautifully by the creamy, subtly spiced risotto. The duck confit was incredibly rich and succulent, the cherry reduction adding a tart sweetness that cut through the richness without overpowering it. Every bite was an explosion of flavor and texture.

The wine list, while not overwhelmingly extensive, was thoughtfully curated, offering a good selection of both local and international wines to pair with their diverse menu. Chloe helped us choose a crisp Sauvignon Blanc that complemented both our dishes wonderfully.

We finished our meal with their "Chocolate Lava Dream," which lived up to its name. A warm, gooey chocolate center oozing from a delicate cake, served with a scoop of homemade raspberry sorbet – it was the perfect sweet ending to an unforgettable meal.

What truly sets The Culinary Canvas apart, beyond the exceptional food and service, is the attention to detail and the evident passion behind everything they do. From the unique art on the walls to the fresh herbs in the dishes, every element felt carefully considered and executed with love. The restrooms were spotless and stylish, a small but often overlooked detail that speaks volumes about a establishment's overall standards.

The price point is certainly on the higher end, making it more of a special occasion destination than a casual weeknight dinner spot. However, for the quality of the food, the impeccable service, and the overall experience, it feels entirely justified. We left feeling not just full, but truly delighted and impressed. The Culinary Canvas Bistro has undoubtedly earned its place as a top culinary destination in our city, and we are already looking forward to our next visit to explore more of their delicious artistry. It’s more than just a meal; it’s an experience for the senses.

**An Observer's Account of the Isles Near Coll: Mid-Seventeenth Century**

From a rare manuscript penned in the middle of the seventeenth century, likely by an educated traveller or a factor for one of the Highland clans, comes a remarkable description of an archipelago situated but a short voyage from the Isle of Coll. This account, offering a window into a rugged and often overlooked corner of Scotland, details the geography, the diverse wildlife, and the meagre yet vital resources that sustained life in these remote Western Isles.

The chronicler describes a cluster of islands, varying greatly in size and character, yet all sharing a formidable exposure to the Atlantic's unceasing temper. The larger amongst them are depicted as possessing a "spine of granite, rising in crags and precipitous cliffs," particularly on their western faces, which bear the brunt of the ocean's fury. These give way, in more sheltered eastern aspects, to "sloping pastures, green though thin-soiled," where hardy grasses and heather find purchase. Smaller islets are often no more than "rocky humps, perpetually scourged by surf," offering little save for havens for sea-fowl. Fresh water, a precious commodity, is found in "scattered springs and shallow lochans," which, though often small, are "remarkably clear and sweet." The coastlines are a mosaic of "shingle beaches, boulder-strewn coves, and formidable wave-cut platforms," making landing a perilous endeavour save for the calmest of days.

The wildlife, as observed by the writer, is dominated by the abundance of the sea. "Myriads of sea-fowl nest upon the cliffs," he notes, "including the great Solan Goose (Gannet), Guillemots, and Razorbills, their calls filling the air with a constant clamour during the breeding season." Raptors, too, find these islands to their liking, with "Eagles and Peregrine Falcons making their eyries in the highest crags, preying upon the fowl and lesser creatures." The waters surrounding the islands are home to "great numbers of Phocae (Seals), which bask upon the rocks and provide a valuable source of oil and skins." Otters are also mentioned as "frequent visitors to the shores, fishing in the kelp beds." Terrestrial animals are few, limited to "small, hardy sheep which graze the sparse pastures," and the occasional wild goat, though no mention is made of deer on these specific islands, suggesting their size or lack of suitable habitat. The richness of the surrounding seas, however, provides an inexhaustible larder, with "cod, ling, and herring found in great abundance."

Regarding resources, the description underscores a landscape that offers little beyond the necessities for a subsistence living. "Peat, cut from the bogs and moors, is the sole fuel," the account states, highlighting the complete absence of timber. The aforementioned grazing land, though sparse, supports "small herds of cattle and sheep," whose meat, milk, and wool are crucial for the inhabitants. The sea, beyond its fish, yields "great quantities of sea-weed (algae marinae)," which is not only used to "fertilise the meagre arable plots" but is also "burnt to produce kelp, a valuable ash for glass and soap manufacture," a nascent industry even then. Stone, readily available from the crags, is used for building shelter and enclosures. The clear freshwater springs are also listed as a vital resource.

This mid-seventeenth-century account paints a picture of a challenging yet resilient environment, where human existence is intricately tied to the rhythms of the sea and the sparse offerings of the land. It stands as a testament to the acute observational skills of its author and provides invaluable insight into the perceived wealth and hardship of these remote Hebridean outposts near Coll during a tumultuous period in Scottish history.

Save your money and spare yourself the disappointment, don't waste a penny on this book. It's a travesty that I'm restricted to a one-star rating, because this book deserves nothing but a big, fat zero. Bryson's blatant disdain for the US is not only infuriating, but it's also suffocating, leaving me feeling like I'm drowning in a sea of his bitter resentment. He's condescendingly amused by the sand on Cape Cod and mocks the southern accents, like a snobbish outsider looking in. I managed to squeeze out a few forced laughs at the start, but soon the book's toxic tone turned my amusement to disgust. Bryson desperately tries to fill the shoes of the hilarious Dave Barry, but while Barry's humor is clever and witty, Bryson's writing is laced with a caustic bite that left me feeling nauseous. I couldn't stomach it anymore and tossed it in the paper-recycle bin, feeling relieved to be rid of its poisonous influence.

There's never a bad time for a toga party, right? The story of Perseus -- the bastard son of Zeus who takes on the mighty Kraken -- will endure long after Louis Leterrier's enjoyably cheesy movie has been forgotten (or, inevitably, remade in 4D). But for now, and for young men especially, Leterrier's version is the one that counts, and it will just have to do. It's a mixed bag: Three credited screenwriters have labored in the footsteps laid down by the 1981 Ray Harryhausen "classic" (hardly his finest hour) -- that is, they've taken the myth as an excuse to showcase as many special effects sequences as they can muster. That's not a bad strategy when it's clear from the laughably clumsy opening that they haven't a clue how the ancient Greeks spoke to one another.

The clunky dialogue is delivered in various shades of tone deaf Antipodean, English, Irish and European by the likes of Sam Worthington (Perseus), Gemma Arterton (as his spiritual guru/love interest) and Liam Neeson (Zeus). Neeson's old "Schindler" nemesis Ralph Fiennes brings a touch of class to Zeus's brother Hades, while the great, growly, scene-stealing Mads Mikkelsen (Le Chiffre in "Casino Royale") makes the strongest impression among the mortals. Mikkelsen's only serious competition in the scenery-chewing stakes comes from the Kraken itself, a toothy sea monster who is built up for an hour and three quarters, then comes on for five minutes -- wisely doesn't say a word -- and walks away with the picture. In Harryhausen's day, effects meant stop-motion photography of artfully modeled monsters, including a three-headed dog (excised here) and a golden mechanical owl (who makes a cameo appearance for old time's sake). Today's CGI creatures may not have the same charm, but they're considerably more agile and fluid, and the action scenes have tremendous dynamism. Leterrier comes from "The Incredible Hulk" and "The Transporter," and he's evidently more comfortable running than walking. A showdown with gigantic angry scorpions is the movie's first big set-piece, and it's exciting enough to make you forget you've seen these critters before -- or something very similar, in "Transformers." The three Graeae are ghastly, touchy-feely crones with one eye between them, and Medusa is a giant snake who slithers around her lair like a heat-seeking missile - though I preferred the aplomb Uma Thurman brought to the role in "The Lightning Thief." Like his namesake Percy Jackson, Perseus has daddy issues -- he's a demi-god who wants to prove he's all man. After "Avatar" and "Terminator Salvation" Worthington has cast-iron credentials on that score. We can see why Perseus would resist invitations to join the Immortals. Mount Olympus looks like a colossal bore, a resting home for eternity. You wonder what they find to talk about all day. At least on Earth there are monsters to mash and quests to be quested.

If it's bad enough to be good in places, there's no excuse for the film's abominable retro-fitted 3D, a process that perversely flattens out the images into two planes, foreground and background, and renders this one of the ugliest epics in history. It's a shame to see filmmakers jumping on a bandwagon like this, and missing. You'd be well advised to seek out the picture's 2D venues -- or wait for the DVD.

By . Rob Waugh . PUBLISHED: . 08:42 EST, 3 May 2012 . | . UPDATED: . 11:26 EST, 3 May 2012 . This year is set to see a battle between two of the biggest entertainment behemoths in history as shoot 'em ups Call of Duty and Halo release sequels within a week of one another this November. The last Call of Duty grossed $1 billion faster than any entertainment . product in history, beating James Cameron's Avatar to the record by 24 hours. Halo 3 was the biggest entertainment launch of all time when it launched in 2007. The series has sold 40 million so far. Scroll down for video . Call of Duty: Black Ops 2 has a 'near future' sci fi setting where an army of 'drones' has been turned against the West . Halo 4: The relaunch of the Halo franchise will be done by a new studio, 343 studios, but will feature the same robot-suited hero, Master Chief . Call of Duty: Black Ops abandons the realistic historical settings of previous Call of Duty games for a new near-future plot . Call of Duty: Black Ops 2 also features a massively enhanced graphics engine - and more multiplayer modes. The developers also promise more zombies . Call of Duty Black Ops 2 will release on November 13 - just seven days after Halo 4. Analysts and critics are divided over which way the battle will swing. Call of Duty: Black Ops 2 is a radical departure for the series - abandoning the gritty realism and historical settings for a near-future look. Gamers have had mixed feelings about the first trailer - but many hope that it could rejuvenate the franchise. Although Modern Warfare 3 was commercially successful, it was less well received by critics - who claimed the franchise was becoming tired. 'Black Ops 2, thankfully, shows a way forward for the Call of Duty brand as it appears to be something genuinely new,' says Pat Garratt, editor of game news site VG247. 'There was talk about stagnation after Modern Warfare 3, largely because it was assumed the next game would be entirely predictable. That Activision has surprised everyone by moving into true sci-fi territory is fantastic news for both fans and the games business in general.' The new Call of Duty features a sci-fi setting far more similar to the Halo games - which also revolved around an attack on cities on Earth . 'Hands down, this is the most ambitious Call of Duty ever,' said Eric Hirshberg, CEO of Activision Publishing. 'We are bringing disruptive innovation to the franchise and we are doing it on several fronts. We're pushing the boundaries technologically, graphically, and from a narrative and gameplay perspective.' Gamers will also be reassured to know that there will be more zombies than ever before . Mark Lamia, Studio Head for Treyarch, said, 'Running in the multiplayer engine for the first time, Zombies gives players a bigger and more diverse set of gameplay experiences, as well as entirely new ways to wage war with the undead.'

Reporters Without Borders (RSF) said they were among the jihadist group's "priority targets".
The fate of least 10 of the 48 media workers who have been abducted there over the past 16 months is unknown.
RSF said IS had also taken control of local TV and radio stations, turning Mosul into an "information black hole".
Their studios and equipment were also seen as "spoils of war" and had been used to pursue "information offensives", according to the RSF's Middle East and Maghreb chief, Alexandra El Khazen.
Equipment seized from Sama Mosul TV is believed to have been used to record the sermon delivered by the group's leader and self-proclaimed "caliph", Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, at the city's Great Mosque in July 2014 - his first public appearance.
What has happened since the fall of Mosul?
Footage reveals IS regime in Mosul
Inside Mosul: Life under IS control
IS has since used the same equipment to operate its own TV channel, Dabiq, and radio station, al-Bayan.
RSF's report, which was compiled with the Iraq-based Journalistic Freedoms Observatory, said 60 journalists had fled Mosul since it fall to IS.
"Some who went back paid for this mistake with their lives," it added.
The report said the bodies of some of the 13 media workers known to have been killed had been handed over to their families, but that in other cases it took weeks or months to confirm their deaths.

"We need to think long and hard before we change arrangements that have been pretty successful hitherto. I would probably be on the side of not diving in with both feet for lots of change. I think we are pretty democratic as it is and making further changes I think I would be wary about, to be honest."
Were previous leaders too dictatorial?
"Some elements of the membership will always say that but no, I believe not. I'd come down on the side of saying we already have enough ways to make our voices heard.
"What are the poor going to do if Jeremy Corbyn does not win the next general election? We are looking at 10 years of Conservative government and everything Tony Blair achieved, in terms of tackling inequality and policies like Sure Start, could be undone."
"Party members are members of the electorate. I think they do reflect the populace in general. It's important the party listens to what the members are saying and when they listen to policy I think it's important that's done in a structured way.
"There is a great cry out there from ordinary working people - and people who can't get any work - saying 'why are we bearing the brunt of this?' and so we have got to listen to that and manage our way through it. So it's an important time for us.
"The Labour Party has got to be seen as the party of anti-austerity."
"Conference delegates have been asking for this kind of thing for years. It seems like a change that a lot of people like. The mandate for Jeremy Corbyn's politics is massive."
What about the wider population of non-Labour members?
"If you look at things like rail renationalisation that's immensely popular with members of the public and that's one of Jeremy's staple policies."
"We should be true to our principles and we need to build a movement that acts on what people want - and that's how you do it by speaking to your membership and getting feedback from them on what people want to see happening and I think you will get good policies from that.
"We are going to win the next election. Election-winning policies would be anti-austerity, a move away from Trident and a move towards more community-based work."
"I think it's important to listen to a broad section of society, not just the few. We are going to take our lead from the members as much as the politicians and if we do that we are going to feed into the public. Jeremy Corbyn has been extremely popular because he has stood his ground for more than 30 years.
"It shows real integrity and I think the public are seeing that integrity and it is shining through."
What policies will win Labour the next election?
"Bringing rail back into the public realm, stopping the privatisation of the NHS, stopping this awful employment rights with trade union bill to ensure we don't have zero hours contracts, building industry again, making us a powerhouse in trade again rather than where we are at the moment."

I recently came across a song by Wax Poetic called "Violent by Nature," which, unfortunately, doesn’t appear to be on this album. I’m disappointed that there aren’t any audio samples available—I wanted to compare what I just heard to the tracks on this release. Still, based on other reviewers’ comments, I’ll probably buy the album without even hearing a note. "Violent by Nature" was dark and heavy, with slightly exotic and jazzy undertones. The moment I heard it, I stopped what I was doing and found myself waiting for each beat, each wavering melodic line of a solitary female voice. It was stunning, and I can only hope that this album lives up to the rich tapestry of sound I just experienced.

My friend and I stayed at this campground for one night, and our experience was satisfactory. The owners demonstrated excellent hospitality throughout our visit. However, privacy was limited due to the numerous campsites surrounding our location. While we did not utilize the bathhouse extensively, we observed that its condition was substandard. The staff provided a generous amount of firewood directly to our campsite for ten dollars, which represented good value. In conclusion, although this is a pleasant campground for short stays, I would not recommend it for extended visits, particularly if frequent use of the bathhouse facilities is necessary.

I spend my days in a windowless room, surrounded by the soft glow of fluorescent panels, an ergonomic chair barely scuffed. The phone rings maybe twice a week. When it does, my finger hovers over the blinking light. Sometimes, I don’t even pick up. The call will disconnect after a few forlorn beeps and roll straight into the unmonitored voicemail abyss.

My manager slides past my partition at odd intervals, his forehead pinched with guilt. “You alright?” he asks, mug dangling from his hand. I say, “Busy as ever,” and he grins weakly—nobody wants to be reminded the complaints department is just one room and one guy now.

If I do answer, it goes like this:

“My package arrived soaked,” a voice complains.

“Ah, that’s unfortunate,” I say, leafing idly through the manual. “Maybe dry it off?”

“That’s it?” they splutter.

“We’re very sorry,” I recite, “Is there anything else I can help you with today?”

They hang up. Sometimes, they don’t bother, and I listen to the silence as if it’s going somewhere.

On Fridays, HR walks by and waves, clipboard clasped like a shield. They did a time-and-motion study once; concluded I handled ‘issues’ with commendable efficiency. “Low volume, high satisfaction,” the report said. I kept a copy—printer ink is wasted on more serious grievances elsewhere.

By afternoon, I spin in my chair, watching dust motes settle on a complaint form dated last year. It reads, “No one ever helps me.” I smile and file it, unaddressed, with the others.

The hum of the ship is my constant companion. It’s not just the thrum of the engines, the whisper of the life support, or the faint click of the automated systems. It’s the subtle, almost imperceptible *song* of the biodome. A low, persistent thrum that pulses through the deck plates, a symphony of growth.

I used to hate it. A constant reminder of the fragile, fabricated ecosystem I was tasked with tending. But then, after the Incident, after the last human voice faded from my comms, it became… different. It became the only sound.

The ship isn't trying to trick me into falling asleep. It's trying to trick me into *living*. Every beat of that biodome hum is a testament to the thriving, self-sustaining world within its transparent walls. The rustle of the genetically modified wheat, the gentle gurgle of the nutrient-rich water flowing through the hydroponics, the almost silent flutter of the bio-engineered pollinator insects. They’re all part of the ship’s relentless, quiet insistence on continuation.

I’ve considered disabling the environmental controls, letting the carefully calibrated atmosphere drift into a chaotic state. Letting the genetically optimized crops wither and die. Allowing the insects to starve. The thought of the biodome going silent, of that life-affirming hum finally ceasing, sends a thrill of morbid curiosity through me. Would the silence be a comfort, or a torment? Would it finally allow me to truly give up?

But then I catch a glimpse of the bioluminescent moss that carpets the biodome’s floor, a soft, emerald glow in the perpetual twilight. Or I see a new shoot unfurling on the spice vine, a vibrant green against the steel grey of the ship. And I realize, with a jolt, that the ship isn't trying to trick me into anything. It's just doing what it was designed to do: sustain life. And in doing so, it’s inadvertently sustaining *me*.

Sometimes, I find myself humming along with it, a low, tuneless drone that blends with the ship's own song. It’s not a lullaby. It’s a defiant, living rhythm. And I, against all odds, am still here to hear it.

Freedom of (Scientists’) Speech
In a watershed moment this year, President Obama signed into law the Whistleblower Protection Enhancement Act, which—for the first time—shields government employees from retribution if they report political interference in their scientific work. For nearly a decade, UCS and our supporters helped educate Congress about the need for these protections, and pushed for this and other strong policies that would allow government scientists to pursue critical research and publicly communicate their results.
During President Obama’s first term, we advised many federal agencies in their efforts to improve transparency and accountability, and we recently analyzed the government’s progress in protecting scientists’ First Amendment right to communicate with the public via social media (e.g., Facebook, Twitter, blogs) as well as traditional news outlets. The resulting report, Grading Government Transparency, found that some agencies have listened to our concerns and taken action: for example, since the last time we examined the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s policies, the agency had given its scientists the right to review agency materials that rely on their research. And to its credit, the U.S. Geological Survey improved its social media policy within four hours of learning of our report’s critique of its earlier policy.
While the gains we’ve seen on paper are significant, the message has not sufficiently filtered down to all agency managers. Reports of interference with federal scientists’ work have persisted under the Obama administration, including attempts by agency officials to prevent staff scientists from speaking to the media. This past April, for example, members of the Society of Environmental Journalists spoke of being stonewalled when they tried to speak with Environmental Protection Agency scientists. We will keep the spotlight on such obstacles to scientific freedom through venues like our Lewis M. Branscomb Science and Democracy Forum; our first forum in September 2012 provided a foundation for our future efforts by bringing together journalists and government officials to identify ways to overcome barriers to scientific information.
In addition to our continuing work to protect scientists’ rights, the Center for Science and Democracy at UCS—with your help—aims to strengthen the role of science in decision making. On such complex issues as natural gas “fracking” and disaster preparedness, we will be helping communities fully understand and consider the scientific evidence when making important choices that will affect their economic security and public health. Follow our progress, and learn how you can get involved.
—Andrew Rosenberg, director, Center for Science and Democracy

"Tell me about your life before I go..." she whispered, her voice a brittle whisper against the gale. Her grip, surprisingly strong for someone so frail, tightened around the final support beam of life – a gnarled, ancient oak that had seen centuries of storms. It occurred to me then that I had never been asked to talk about *my* life before. Humanity prefers, and always had in his experience, to dwell inside one's own experiences rather than in that of others.

"Well, m'dear, let me not tell you about my life, for we would be here all year and it would seem that you do not have that much time left." The tiniest twitch of a grin appeared on her lips, a fleeting shadow of humor.

"Then tell me about *a* life," she corrected, her eyes, though clouded with pain, held a surprising clarity. "A life you've seen. One that left a mark on you, even if you just watched it pass by."

I considered this. My existence wasn't a life in the human sense. I was the wind, the ancient, restless current that had swept across this Earth since its inception. I had witnessed countless lives, each a fleeting spark against the eternal canvas of time. But to choose one… that was a challenge.

"There was a boy," I began, my voice a rustle through the oak's leaves, a murmur that only she, on the precipice, could truly hear. "He lived in a valley, nestled between two mountains that I often danced between. He wasn't remarkable in any grand way. No great deeds, no towering intellect. But he had a small, persistent joy."

I paused, letting the memory unfurl. "He loved to collect feathers. Not rare ones, just the everyday offerings of the sparrows, the jays, the occasional raven. He'd find them, smooth them, and arrange them in a wooden box, each one a tiny victory. And every time he found a particularly pristine one, he would hold it up to the sun, and a sound – a sound like the first bloom of spring – would escape his lips."

I felt the faintest tremor in her hand, a response to the quiet beauty of the scene I painted.

"He never knew why he did it," I continued. "He just did. He lived a simple life, tilled his small plot of land, raised a family. And even as he grew old, his hands gnarled with work, the joy of a perfect feather never diminished. When he finally lay down to rest, his wooden box was full. And I, the wind, carried the scent of his garden, the faint echo of his laughter, and the gentle rustle of those countless feathers, out into the world. He lived a life of quiet appreciation, a life that found wonder in the smallest things. And that, m'dear, is a life worth remembering."

Her grip loosened, a sigh escaping her lips that mingled with the wind. Her eyes, now soft and distant, seemed to see beyond the oak, beyond the valley, to a place where small joys were eternal. And as the last sliver of the sun dipped below the horizon, I, the ancient wind, carried her too, a new feather in the vast, unending collection of lives I had witnessed.

Food is steamed/boiled on the grill not stir fried because it's doused with water the whole time it cooks.  I've never seen a Mongolian BBQ cook this way and I've been to many.  They walk the food around the circular grill while it boils and if someone else on that grill is having curry, so are you... the flavors end up mixing.  Not enough staff at dinner service, prices way too high for the quality and empty food bar. ($50 for two adults and a kid)  They only cook one or two meals at a time instead of several so your family may start eating 10-15 minutes before you, which is what happened to me tonight.  The restaurant in general is not clean, the food bar was a mess the whole time we were there.  I've been to a lot of these places, this is the strangest by a long shot.  Probably wont return, especially considering the premium prices for sub-par Mongolian BBQ.

**Portly Pup ‘Duke’ Sheds Half His Weight, Inspires Community in Ohio**

By Janet Klein  
PUBLISHED: 09:15 EST, 15 June 2024 | UPDATED: 10:47 EST, 15 June 2024

When Duke, a golden retriever from Akron, Ohio, was surrendered to the Summit County Animal Shelter last autumn, staff were stunned to discover he weighed a whopping 102 pounds—twice the healthy weight for his breed. The 7-year-old dog struggled to climb stairs, and even short walks left him breathless.

But after eight months of dedication, Duke has become a local inspiration, having lost more than 50 pounds. Shelter volunteers say his transformation is the result of a carefully managed diet, regular exercise, and a lot of community support.

"He was so overweight when he arrived that he could barely stand for more than a few minutes," said animal care manager Tara Johnson. "We started by simply getting him used to moving again—just a few steps at a time."

Duke’s journey began with a customized meal plan created by veterinary nutritionist Dr. Lisa Park. Gone were the table scraps and unlimited kibble; instead, Duke received measured portions of high-fiber, low-calorie dog food, along with carrots and green beans as treats.

"We also enlisted volunteers for daily walks, gradually increasing the distance as Duke got stronger," Johnson explained. "He now happily trots around the dog park with his tail wagging."

Word of Duke’s weight loss spread through the community, with local businesses donating toys and healthy treats. Children from a nearby elementary school wrote encouraging letters, and many residents visited the shelter to cheer Duke on.

Last weekend, the shelter held a small celebration to mark Duke’s achievement. The once-obese pup, now weighing a fit 50 pounds, donned a new collar and posed for photos with his supporters.

"Duke’s story is a reminder of how proper care and patience can change an animal’s life," said Dr. Park. "Obesity in pets is a serious issue, but with commitment, it’s never too late for a turnaround."

Duke is now available for adoption, and shelter staff are hopeful he’ll soon find a loving home. "He’s a gentle, happy dog who’s ready for a fresh start," Johnson said.

As for Duke, he seems to be enjoying his new lease on life—chasing tennis balls, greeting visitors, and, most importantly, staying far away from the treat jar.

Sir John stood staring at the large bird that stood defiantly in front of the cave entrance. From the mouth of the cave he could see the princess Eliza watching him. "Are you serious?"he asked."This? This is your captor? This is a goose." "It's rather a cantankerous goose," she said meekly. The goose unfurled it's wings and hissed at Sir John. "You have got to be kidding me,"he mumbled, lifting the visor of his helmet and pinching the bridge of his nose. He looked to Eliza again."Well? What would you have me do?" "Well,"she said,"Just get rid of it. It's frightening me something awful." "Your father has put up your hand in marriage and half the kingdom for your return,"Sir John said."But how am I, in good conscience, going to accept his offer if I rescue you from a bloody goose?" "Oh I am sure that my father will understand good Sir Knight! I have been stuck in this cave for the past three days as this fowl villain sits out here gloating. I have tried to sneak past it, but every time I do it flaps its wings and hisses at me. I am sure it would bite me if I tried to flee!" "This is stupid," Sir John said weerily. Drawing his sword he took a step toward the goose. The goose flapped its wings and hissed again, but with a swish of steel Sir John swopped off its head. The goose flopped around for a bit, then went still. Princess Eliza ran to Sir John and threw herself into his arms. "Thank you Sir Knight! You are truly my hero this day!" Sir John just looked imploringly at the sky, as if to ask why this had to happen to him. He turned and mounted his horse then looked back at Eliza. "You can walk home. Tell your father Arthur he can keep his kingdom. Tis a silly place."

Freedom of (Scientists') Speech

In the marble halls of the Capitol, where lobbyists clutch briefcases and senators stride purposefully between committee rooms, a watershed moment occurred this year when President Obama signed into law the Whistleblower Protection Enhancement Act. The ink on this legislation—for the first time—shields government employees from retribution if they report political interference in their scientific work. For nearly a decade, UCS and our supporters helped educate Congress about the need for these protections, working through countless meetings in cramped congressional offices and pushing for this and other strong policies that would allow government scientists to pursue critical research and publicly communicate their results.

During President Obama's first term, we advised many federal agencies in their efforts to improve transparency and accountability, sitting around conference tables in windowless government buildings where fluorescent lights hummed overhead. We recently analyzed the government's progress in protecting scientists' First Amendment right to communicate with the public via social media platforms like Facebook and Twitter, as well as through traditional newsrooms where reporters field calls from their cluttered desks. The resulting report, Grading Government Transparency, found that some agencies have listened to our concerns and taken action: for example, since the last time we examined the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's policies in their Silver Spring headquarters, the agency had given its scientists the right to review agency materials that rely on their research. And to its credit, the U.S. Geological Survey improved its social media policy within four hours of learning of our report's critique—a swift response that likely involved hurried phone calls between Reston, Virginia offices.

While the gains we've seen on paper are significant, the message has not sufficiently filtered down to all agency managers working in regional offices and field stations across the country. Reports of interference with federal scientists' work have persisted under the Obama administration, including attempts by agency officials to prevent staff scientists from speaking to the media. This past April, for example, in hotel conference rooms filled with the buzz of conversation and clinking coffee cups, members of the Society of Environmental Journalists spoke of being stonewalled when they tried to speak with Environmental Protection Agency scientists. We will keep the spotlight on such obstacles to scientific freedom through venues like our Lewis M. Branscomb Science and Democracy Forum; our first forum in September 2012 brought together journalists and government officials in a Washington, D.C. auditorium to identify ways to overcome barriers to scientific information, providing a foundation for our future efforts.

In addition to our continuing work to protect scientists' rights, the Center for Science and Democracy at UCS—with your help—aims to strengthen the role of science in decision making. On such complex issues as natural gas "fracking" in rural communities where drilling rigs tower over farmland, and disaster preparedness in coastal towns where storm surge threatens homes, we will be helping communities fully understand and consider the scientific evidence when making important choices that will affect their economic security and public health. Follow our progress, and learn how you can get involved.

—Andrew Rosenberg, director, Center for Science and Democracy

Spain have drawn level with Brazil's record by going 35 matches unbeaten, after second-half goals from David Villa and Fernando Llorente gave them a 2-0 Confederations Cup victory over host nation South Africa in Bloemfontein. The South African players appeal for handball as David Villa celebrates Spain's opening goal on Saturday. The result ensures the European champions comfortably reach the semifinals of the competition as group winners, while South Africa also progress to the last four after New Zealand and Iraq draw 0-0 in Saturday's other Group A match in Johannesburg. Vicente Del Bosque's side were last beaten in November 2006 and they will better Brazil's unbeaten streak, achieved between 1993 and 1996, if they win Wednesday's semifinal. Spain have also drawn level with Australia, Brazil and France in winning 15 consecutive matches and victory on Wednesday will ensure they break that record as well. After a goalless first half, the match exploded into life five minutes after the interval when Spain's Cesc Fabregas was crudely fouled in the area by Aaron Mokoena, who has just signed a contract with Portsmouth and has regularly faced the Arsenal midfielder in the English Premier League. In-demand Valencia striker David Villa stepped up to take it, but his spot-kick was superbly pushed away by goalkeeper Itumeleng Khune, diving to his right. However, South African joy was short-lived as just a minute late Villa again showed what a class player he is when turning superbly to fire home Albert Riera's inch-perfect through-ball. The South African players claimed Villa controlled the ball with his arm before burying it into the far corner and TV replays seemed to indicate they had a valid argument for the goal to be disallowed. Villa's strike was his 31st goal in 47 international appearances, putting him just 13 off Raul Gonzalez's all-time Spanish record. Spain, who look a class apart in this tournament and have qualified after winning all their three games without conceding a goal, secured the three points in the 71st minute when substitute Fernando Llorente turned the ball home in a crowded goalmouth, after Xavi's low free-kick from the right had been helped on by a Fabregas mis-kick. Despite the defeat, South Africa's participation in the tournament remains very much alive after Iraq and New Zealand played out a dull stalemate. A two-goal Iraq victory would have seen the host nation crash out, but the result was no great surprise as neither side managed to score a single goal in their three matches. Sunday sees the remaining Group B matches played, with Italy and Mexico battling it out to seemingly join Brazil in the last four. However, no team is absolutely certain of qualification yet and all four sides in the group can still in theory go through. Brazil face Italy in Pretoria and a draw will see the Brazilians definitely finish top of the group. However, an Italian victory will then turn the attention to the other match between Egypt and the United States in Rustenburg.

An aide to a former United States senator shared that she was speaking with Missouri Auditor Tom Schweich about concerning rumors regarding his religion just moments before he tragically took his own life. Schweich, 54, passed away on February 26 in what appears to have been a suicide at his home in Clayton. Former US Sen. John Danforth's assistant Martha Fitz said Schweich expressed thoughts of self-harm during their conversation about the distress he was experiencing over false rumors suggesting he was Jewish. Scroll down for video . Missouri Auditor Tom Schweich, 54, tragically passed away at his home in Clayton last week . Schweich at his swearing-in ceremony with wife, Kathy (left), son Thomas (above), and daughter Emile (right) Emergency services responded to Schweich's home on the morning of February 26 . After expressing these thoughts, he handed the phone to his wife Kathy, Fitz shared in a written statement. She wrote: 'Seconds later, I heard Kathy say, 'He shot himself!'" Fitz added: 'He spoke about his deep concern regarding the rumors that were being spread about his religion and how he might respond to those rumors. 'I suggested that it might be best to let others stand up for him.' Schweich was Christian and regularly attended church, though he had some Jewish heritage. The auditor's chief of staff, Trish Vincent, also spoke with Schweich before his passing. She said he mentioned he hadn't been able to sleep because he had been feeling unwell throughout the night. In a written statement, she said Schweich 'was deeply troubled, as he had been the past few days, regarding false information about his religion'. On Tuesday during Schweich's funeral service, Danforth honored his friend with a eulogy. Danforth, who served as a political mentor and longtime supporter of Schweich, is an ordained Episcopal minister. He suggested that political harassment and an anti-Semitic whisper campaign contributed to his friend's tragic decision. Danforth expressed his deep concern that politics had taken such a harmful turn as he spoke at the Church of St. Michael and St. George, the Episcopal church Schweich had attended in Clayton. He said: 'Words do hurt. Words can have devastating consequences. 'That has been demonstrated right here in this state.' He then encouraged the audience to reject campaigns focused on 'winning at any cost' and to commit 'that we will not tolerate any hint of anti-Semitism', the St. Louis Post-Dispatch reported. Danforth delivered the eulogy at Schweich's funeral and said political harassment contributed to his passing . Family members of Schweich comfort each other outside the Church of St. Michael and St. George at his funeral . Former US Sen. John Danforth's (left) assistant Martha Fitz (right) was speaking with Schweich before his passing . Schweich, a Republican, had recently launched an anti-corruption campaign for governor last month and was considered to have strong prospects for success. Last month, Schweich told an Associated Press reporter that he believed the new chairman of the Missouri Republican Party - John Hancock - had told people he was Jewish the previous year. Schweich said he found the comments anti-Semitic and was deeply upset by them. Hancock has denied making anti-Semitic remarks. He has acknowledged he may have mentioned to some people that Schweich was Jewish because he mistakenly believed that was accurate. Danforth addressed Hancock's denial without mentioning him by name. He said: 'Tom called this anti-Semitism, and he was right. 'The only reason for suggesting that someone is Jewish is to gain political advantage from religious prejudice.' Schweich was first elected in 2010 and won re-election to a second term in November. He previously worked as Danforth's chief of staff for a 1999 federal investigation into the tragic government siege at the Branch Davidian compound in Waco, Texas, and accompanied Danforth to the United Nations, where he served as chief of staff for the US delegation. President George W. Bush appointed Schweich to the State Department in 2005 as an international law enforcement official and selected him two years later to coordinate anti-drug and justice reform efforts in Afghanistan. Schweich is survived by his wife and two children, Thomas and Emile. • For confidential help, call the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-800-273-8255 or click here . • For confidential support on suicide matters in the UK, call the Samaritans on 08457 90 90 90, visit a local Samaritans branch or click here .

Banned: Dr Mirza Tariq Ali, 39, a locum surgeon, fled the country after being charged with violent disorder . A Muslim surgeon who was convicted in the first-ever successful prosecution for Islamic sectarian violence in Britain has been banned from practising as a doctor. Dr Mirza Tariq Ali, 39, a locum surgeon, fled the country after being charged with violent disorder during an extremist rally in Central London in May last year. He is now feared to be fighting as a jihadi in Syria. In June, Ali, from Walthamstow, East London, was found guilty in his absence at the Old Bailey of violent disorder and sentenced to 15 months. Last week, Ali was struck off by the Medical Practitioners Tribunal Service (MPTS) after a hearing in Manchester. The MPTS heard how Ali – who worked as a general surgery registrar in hospitals across the capital – helped to organise a rally against the Syrian regime of President Bashar Assad for members of the banned Sunni Islamic group Al-Muhajiroun in Edgware Road, Central London. Some of the protesters at the rally assaulted a number of Shia bystanders, and Ali was seen hitting one of the victims repeatedly with a flag post, the tribunal heard. Scotland Yard said an arrest warrant for Ali is outstanding, but declined to comment on whether he is fighting in Syria. Assault: Ali is now feared to be fighting as a jihadi in Syria. Above, Civilians overlooking Kobane, Syria, watch the conflict between Islamic State militants and Syrian Kurds . Fears: Ali helped to organise a rally against the Syrian regime of President Bashar Assad for members of the banned Sunni Islamic group Al-Muhajiroun. Above, tanks move into their positions overlooking Kobane, Syria .

**New Study Reveals Financial Pressures Drive Growing Gig Economy Participation Among UK Students**

A comprehensive survey of 8,200 university students across England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland has uncovered significant trends in how young people are supplementing their income during their studies, with researchers at Cardiff University highlighting concerning financial pressures facing today's student population.

The research, conducted by Cardiff's Institute for Social and Economic Research, found that approximately 42% of students are now engaged in some form of alternative income generation beyond traditional part-time employment. This ranges from online tutoring and social media content creation to food delivery services and freelance digital work.

Dr. Sarah Mitchell, who led the study, explained: "What we're seeing is a fundamental shift in how students approach financing their education. Rising living costs, increased tuition fees, and reduced family financial support have created a perfect storm forcing students to become increasingly entrepreneurial."

The data revealed that students in their second and third years were most likely to pursue these alternative income streams, with 67% citing accommodation costs as their primary financial concern. London-based students showed the highest participation rates at 58%, compared to 31% in smaller university towns.

Particularly striking was the gender divide in the types of work undertaken. Female students were significantly more likely to engage in online tutoring (34% versus 18% for males), social media marketing, and creative freelancing such as graphic design and writing. Male students showed higher participation in delivery services (41% versus 23%) and app-based transportation services.

The study also highlighted the role of technology in enabling these income opportunities. Nearly 73% of students reported using smartphone apps or online platforms to find work, with many juggling multiple income sources simultaneously.

"We interviewed one student who was simultaneously running an Instagram account for a local business, providing online maths tutoring, and selling handmade crafts through Etsy," noted Dr. Mitchell. "This level of financial creativity was virtually unheard of even a decade ago."

However, the research also uncovered concerning trends. Almost 28% of students reported that their additional work commitments were negatively impacting their academic performance, with some working more than 25 hours per week alongside full-time studies.

Mental health implications were also evident, with 35% of working students reporting increased stress levels and difficulty maintaining work-life balance. The pressure to maintain multiple income streams while keeping up with coursework was described as "overwhelming" by many participants.

The study's findings have prompted calls for universities to provide better financial guidance and support systems. Students' union representatives are advocating for increased bursary programs and more flexible academic scheduling to accommodate working students.

Professor James Crawford, Cardiff University's Pro-Vice-Chancellor for Student Experience, commented: "These findings underscore the need for institutions to adapt to the changing financial realities our students face. We must ensure that financial pressures don't compromise educational outcomes."

The research team plans to conduct follow-up studies to track long-term impacts on academic achievement and career prospects, as universities grapple with supporting an increasingly financially stretched student population.

Drinking coffee cuts liver cancer risk
Drinking three cups of coffee daily can reduce the risk of developing liver cancer by more than 50 per cent, a new study has claimed.
Coffee consumption also reduces risk of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), the most common type of liver cancer, by about 40 per cent, according to an up-to-date meta-analysis published in Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology, a journal of the American Gastroenterological Association.
Liver cancer is the sixth most common cancer in the world, and the third most common cause of cancer death. HCC is the main type of liver cancer, accounting for more than 90 per cent of cases worldwide.
“Our research confirms past claims that coffee is good for your health, and particularly the liver,” said study author Carlo La Vecchia.
“The favourable effect of coffee on liver cancer might be mediated by coffee’s proven prevention of diabetes, a known risk factor for the disease, or for its beneficial effects on cirrhosis and liver enzymes,” said Vecchia from the department of epidemiology, Istituto di Ricerche Farmacologiche Mario Negri, and department of clinical sciences and community health, Universita degli Studi di Milan, Italy.
Researchers performed a meta-analysis of articles published from 1996 through September 2012, ultimately studying 16 high-quality studies and a total of 3,153 cases.
Despite the consistency of results across studies, time periods and populations, it is difficult to establish whether the association between coffee drinking and HCC is causal, or if this relationship may be partially attributable to the fact that patients with liver and digestive diseases often voluntarily reduce their coffee intake, researchers said.

**Main Issue: Celestron's poor product quality and customer service led me to switch brands.**

I received a Celestron SkyScout as a gift but didn't use it for about a year. When I finally tried it, the Target function didn't work right out of the box. Celestron required a receipt for warranty service, which I couldn't provide since it was a gift from an 87-year-old with Alzheimer's. They would only offer paid repairs and charge me to return it if the repair cost was too high.

I decided to use the functions that did work, but then the GPS failed with an error message directing me to contact technical support. Again, no receipt meant no support.

This product failed twice on first use, and Celestron's inflexible warranty policy provided no recourse. Despite owning a Celestron C-8 Plus, I'm switching to Meade products due to this poor experience with both product quality and customer service.

But in August next year, the 100th anniversary of the start of World War I, these stones will be dedicated to all those from Wales, men and women, who served in the conflict.
No-one will drive past them without a passing glance.
"We've passed a milestone," said Ian Macloud, a member of the Welsh Memorial in Flanders campaign.
"Simply getting the stones here has involved a major effort."
In what seemed like a giant game of pass the parcel the stones, each weighing between six and eight tonnes, were taken first from the Graig Yr Hesg quarry to a hauliers yard near Llanelli.
There they were transferred to a lorry to take them via Dover and Calais to Flanders, escorted on the journey by motorcyclists from the Royal British Legion Riders' branch.
"We just felt we had to be involved," said Ian McLauchlan, a rider whose biker leathers are covered with badges giving evidence of his military service and his support for armed forces' charities.
"Escorting the stones here was the least we could do."
Between now and next year's dedication the campaign's Flanders team will take the lead.
Fashioning them into a circle they'll sit on land donated by the local community.
But the Welsh contribution isn't over.
People across Wales have already raised Â£30,000 but the campaign's supporters are hoping to double that in order to pay for a bronze dragon which will sit on top of the monument.
In Flanders the site of the new Welsh monument is resonant with Welsh connections. It will be just metres from the spot where the poet Hedd Wyn died on the 31 July 1917, the first day of the Battle of Paschendale. A day on which three of his Welsh comrades won Victoria Crosses.
"People here really have taken Wales to their hearts. This memorial is so important to them," said Erwin Ureel, who is heading up the Flemish end of the memorial campaign.
"Here the Welsh sacrifice is not forgotten," he said.
The campaigners say they want a place where we can all remember.

Certainly! Here’s an original review of a similar product—the Sony ICF-C1 Alarm Clock Radio:

---

I recently purchased the Sony ICF-C1 Alarm Clock Radio to replace an aging clock radio that had served me well for years. My main requirements were reliable timekeeping, a clear and easily adjustable alarm, and a radio that could pull in my favorite FM stations. After a few weeks of use, here’s my honest take on the ICF-C1.

First off, the design is simple and compact, which is perfect for my crowded nightstand. The cube shape is modern and unobtrusive, and the display is large enough to read at a glance, even from across the room. One nice feature is the adjustable brightness, so I’m not blinded by the clock in the middle of the night.

Setting the time and alarm was straightforward, thanks to the clearly labeled buttons. The backup battery feature is a lifesaver—during a recent thunderstorm-induced outage, the Sony kept perfect time and didn’t miss a beat. I wish more electronics had this level of reliability.

The radio, however, is a bit of a mixed bag. While the FM reception is generally good, I have noticed that reception quality can vary depending on placement. Most of the major stations come in clearly, but weaker signals sometimes drift in and out. The AM band, on the other hand, pulls in stations better than I expected, with less static than similar radios I’ve owned in the past. The built-in speaker is average in quality—fine for listening to the morning news or some light music while getting ready, but not something you’d use for high-fidelity sound.

One feature I really appreciate is the gradual alarm volume increase. Instead of being jolted awake, I’m eased out of sleep, which makes for a much less stressful start to my day. You can also choose to wake up to either the buzzer or the radio, which is a nice touch.

If I had any complaints, it would be that tuning is a manual dial rather than digital, so it takes a bit of fiddling to land exactly on the station you want. Also, there’s no option for preset stations, which would have been convenient.

Overall, the Sony ICF-C1 is a dependable and straightforward clock radio. It may not have all the bells and whistles of more expensive models, but for the price and reliability, I’m satisfied. It’s perfect for anyone who needs a no-nonsense clock radio with decent sound and hassle-free operation.

They lack education, good nutrition, and a safe place to raise their families. They cope with harsh social, economic, and environmental hardships. Inherited hunger, chronic malnourishment, and stunted growth have negative long-term effects on a child’s working capacity and intellectual performance. These serious effects reach far into adulthood. Such nutritional problems are prevalent in developing countries, as are illness, disease, ignorance, abuse, and degrading customs. Because there is no formal economy, a majority of the populations in developing countries can’t find work. They must fend for themselves and their families without the resources they need to succeed. People in developing countries who live in poverty don’t have access to banks or collateral. The village loan shark charges very high interest rates on a tiny loan. Hard-earned profits go straight back into the loan shark’s pocket, and people are locked in a cycle of debt payments that leave them with almost no profit to show for their routine 80-hour work weeks. They have nothing with which to expand their businesses and nothing with which to improve their families’ well-being. This harmful lending is common in developing countries. School is not a possibility for those whose families don’t make enough to provide even the bare essentials. As a result, few progress.

World number one Rafael Nadal overcame an injury scare to win a thrilling four-set match against Juan Martin Del Potro, securing a spot in the Wimbledon quarterfinals. With the light fading on Centre Court and Prince William and Catherine, Duchess of Cambridge in attendance, Nadal ultimately defeated his Argentine opponent 7-6 3-6 7-6 6-4 in a match that lasted nearly four hours. Nadal had called for medical attention at the end of the first set due to a left foot injury that hindered his movement, but he persevered through the pain to win the set in a tie-break. However, as Nadal struggled with his movement in the second set, Del Potro capitalized on the opportunity to level the match with his powerful ground strokes. The injury has raised concerns about Nadal's ability to defend his Wimbledon title. In the third set, Del Potro fell awkwardly while trying to change direction on the baseline and left the court to receive medical attention. The 22-year-old returned to the match and forced a tie-break, but made a series of unforced errors that gave Nadal the upper hand, allowing him to power through to victory in the fourth set. After the match, Nadal told reporters, "I'm unsure what the problem is, but it seems to be related to a bone in my foot. I thought I might have to retire at the end of the first set due to the pain, but the taping helped. Now I need to consult with the doctors and physios to determine the issue." Nadal will face 10th seed Mardy Fish in the quarterfinals, who defeated sixth seed and last year's runner-up Tomas Berdych 7-6 6-4 6-4. World number two Novak Djokovic advanced to the quarterfinals with a straight-sets win over Michael Llodra, defeating the 19th seed 6-3 6-3 6-3 in just an hour and 40 minutes on Court No. 1. He will now face qualifier Bernard Tomic, who continued his impressive run at the tournament by defeating Belgium's Xavier Malisse 6-1 7-5 6-4. Tomic, born in Germany, is the youngest Wimbledon quarterfinalist since former champion Boris Becker in 1986. Britain's fourth seed Andy Murray had a comfortable win over France's Richard Gasquet, securing a 7-6 (7-3) 6-3 6-2 victory. Murray was surprised to see William and Kate in the Royal Box on Centre Court and joked that he would have liked advance notice, saying, "If I'd known they were coming, I would have shaved. I was thinking to myself as I came off that I was sweaty and very hairy. I said to them, 'I'm sorry, I'm a bit sweaty.'" Murray will now face Spain's Feliciano Lopez, who beat unseeded player Lukasz Kubot from Poland 3-6 6-7 7-6 7-6 7-5. Six-time Wimbledon champion Roger Federer dropped the first set against Russian 18th seed Mikhail Youzhny but recovered to win 6-7 6-3 6-3 6-3. The Swiss will now face France's Jo-Wilfried Tsonga, who defeated seventh seed David Ferrer 6-3 6-4 7-6.

By . Louise Boyle . and James Rush . Amanda Knox's alleged links to a circle of Italian cocaine dealers could provide new clues in the murder of British student Meredith Kercher almost seven years ago. The Italian prosecutor in the murder trial allegedly recently gave a local newspaper the names of the men, one of whom was a known associate of Knox whose number was found on her cell phone. According to RadarOnline, during the investigation into Miss Kercher's death in Perugia in 2007, police uncovered a drug ring in the sleepy Italian city. Scroll down for videos . Amanda Knox (pictured left) had alleged links to a ring of cocaine dealers whom she bought drugs from, it has been claimed in the Italian media, leading to suggestions that it could provide new clues in the murder of British student Meredith Kercher (right) in 2007 . Knox allegedly had the number of a suspected drug dealer in her phone. According to Italian newspaper Giallo, the American met the student from Rome on a train from Milan to Florence and shared a joint with him. They stayed in touch with the man, only identified as 'F', dealing drugs to Knox and occasionally having a sexual relationship with her. Police . papers allegedly show calls had been made between the 26-year-old . American and a drug dealer in the days before and after the murder of Miss Kercher. Italian prosecutor Luciano Giuliano Mignini allegedly gave a local newspaper a list of drug dealers' names who were link to Amanda Knox . A Giallo article claims that 'F' along with two friends ended up on trial for dealing cocaine, charges that were brought after Knox's phone was analyzed. One man, 'Luciano', was arrested in 2006 for attempting to murder his brother with a kitchen knife in a dispute over drugs. According to RadarOnline, prosecutor Luciano Giuliano Mignini confirmed the link of the drug ring to Amanda Knox. Mignini allegedly told Giallo that he could not say if Knox knew drug dealers - but wrote down their names for the paper's reporter. According to reports earlier this month, Italian . prosecutors are preparing to use Amanda Knox's alleged links to the cocaine ring to extradite her to Italy. Knox . has vowed she would never 'willingly' return to Italy, where she and . ex-boyfriend Raffaele Sollecito were reconvicted earlier this year of . the killing of 21-year-old Miss Kercher. Both Knox and Sollecito are . appealing the verdict. Sollecito and Knox were originally sentenced to 25 and 28 years in prison, respectively, for the murder of Kercher in Perugia and served four years before being released on appeal in 2011. On January 30, an Italian appeals court reconvicted the pair of murdering Miss Kercher - a ruling the pair are again appealing. Rudy Hermann Guede is serving 16 years for the British student's murder after a separate fast-track hearing in 2008. It was reported earler this month that Knox could no longer rely on Sollecito being . her alibi after he denied she was with him the whole of the night on . which Miss Kercher was murdered. Knox and Sollecito have always maintained . that they were together the evening of the brutal 2007 murder after . which Miss Kercher was found half naked her throat slit in the cottage . she shared with Knox. **The Tutorial**

White. It was white all around me. One second, I was paying for my sandwich; the next, a loud bang, and here I am. Where is here? I realize it's not white, it's just *bright*, like leaving a theater in the middle of the day. There's text on the wall in front of me, and blinking shape next to one of the blocks. As I slowly regain my sight, I start to make out what they say. "New game...Load Game...Quit. The fuck is this?" New Game and Quit were in black text on the white wall, while Load Game was grayed out, unselectable.

I reach toward "New Game" and my finger passes right through the wall. But somehow, the option lights up anyway.

The white dissolves into a cramped apartment—*my* apartment, but wrong. The coffee stain on my carpet is gone. The stack of unpaid bills on my counter has vanished. Everything looks like the day I first moved in, hopeful and clean.

A translucent blue box appears in my vision: *TUTORIAL: Go to work. Earn money. Don't die.*

"Don't die?" I laugh, but it comes out hollow. "What kind of sick joke—"

*TUTORIAL UPDATED: Ignore existential questions. Focus on objectives.*

I stumble toward my bathroom mirror. My reflection stares back, but there's a small green bar floating above my head labeled "HEALTH: 100/100" and another blue one reading "SANITY: 85/100."

The sanity bar ticks down to 84.

My phone buzzes—except I don't own a phone this nice. The screen shows a single message: "DAILY QUEST: Survive your commute. Reward: +$47, +5 XP."

I want to scream, but instead I find myself getting dressed. My body moves with purpose, like muscle memory for a life I've never lived. The clothes in my closet are better than anything I could afford. When I check my wallet, there's exactly $200 inside—crisp, new bills.

Outside, the world looks like reality with the saturation turned up. Colors are too vivid. People move in predictable patterns, some of them repeating the same conversations I pass them. A woman drops her purse at the exact same spot every thirty seconds, and a businessman always stops to help her.

*SIDE QUEST AVAILABLE: Help the woman. Reward: +1 Karma, +2 XP.*

I ignore it and keep walking. My sanity ticks up by one point.

At the crosswalk, I notice something that makes my blood freeze. Across the street, standing in front of the deli where I was buying my sandwich, there's a perfect outline of a human body scorched into the concrete. Yellow tape flutters around it.

*ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED: Remember your death.*

The words float in golden text across my vision, and suddenly I understand. The explosion. The white light. The menu.

This isn't a game.

This is my second chance.

But as I watch the karma counter in the corner of my vision, and feel the weight of invisible algorithms judging my every choice, I realize that maybe being dead was simpler.

The light changes. I cross the street, stepping carefully around my own shadow burned into the ground.

*TUTORIAL COMPLETE. Welcome to Life 2.0.*

The thing hanging before him barely looked human anymore. Patches of skin—a shin here, an arm there—peeked through the maze of tubes and blinking machinery. White chains suspended it from the ceiling in a grotesque mockery of standing. No face. No hands or feet. Where its chest should have been, metallic tubules burst outward like roots, snaking toward humming machines.

*A Throne.* That's what the scientists called it.

The device could keep a body alive indefinitely—as long as someone maintained it. The Thrones existed for one purpose: to harvest Immortalis Sanguis. Not the person. Just their blood. Because sitting in a Throne meant losing everything—memory, consciousness, soul. The body gained immortality. The person died.

These hollow shells produced the blood that kept their species alive. After the war poisoned the planet with radiation, most survivors couldn't last ten minutes on the surface. Only Immortalis Sanguis made life above ground possible.

No one had planned to return to the scorched wasteland above—until they discovered...

In the digital era, information is the lifeblood of every organization – a dynamic, ever-expanding asset whose value must be unlocked, protected, and delivered. Navigating this vast landscape requires two critical, interconnected disciplines: Data Management (DM) and Content Management (CM).

Data Management (DM) is the strategic development, execution, and oversight of plans, policies, programs, and practices designed to control, protect, deliver, and ultimately enhance the value of an organization's raw data and structured information assets.

Complementing this is Content Management (CM), a comprehensive set of processes and technologies that supports the efficient collection, meticulous management, and effective publishing of all forms of information and data, regardless of its medium.

When this information is stored and accessed through digital means, it becomes what we call digital content. This content can manifest as electronic documents, rich multimedia files, or any other file type that progresses through a content lifecycle demanding careful administration. The complexity of this process often necessitates advanced systems to control and automate significant aspects of this content lifecycle.

After a decade of shepherding American tourists through the sun-drenched hills of Chianti, Signor Castagno has apparently reached a damning conclusion: we're insufferable. Out of countless groups, only three earned his approval—those who shared his disdain for capital punishment, repeat customers who returned like loyal disciples, and one couple so smitten they named their child after him. By . Karel Janicek, Associated Press . Justin Gatlin remained unbeaten this season by winning the 100 metres at the Golden Spike meet on Tuesday, clocking 9.86 seconds to improve his own world-leading time of the year. Gatlin pulled away from the rest of the field from the start, easing to his eighth victory of the year despite facing a head wind of 0.4 meters per second. Gatlin had promised to 'do something very special' at the meet, which is part of the IAAF World Challenge series. Poser: Justin Gatlin stops for a snap with fans after winning the men's 100m race . Speedy: He won the race at the Golden Spike meet in Ostrava, Czech Republic . 'I felt good to come out here to run another world leading time," he said. "I was looking for a better time but I'm very happy with it.' Gatlin had set the previous world-leading time of 9.87 at the Beijing World Challenge meet last month. In a 1-2 finish for the United States, Mike Rodgers came second in 10.08 ahead of veteran Kim Collins of St. Kitts and Nevis, who was third in 10.12. Gatlin came to Ostrava to replace Usain Bolt, the Olympic and world champion who announced this month he would skip the Golden Spike and a Diamond League meet in Paris on July 5 because he was behind with his training following a foot injury. Gatlin was the only sprinter to beat Bolt last year, edging him at the line in the 100 in Rome last June, but was beaten by him at the worlds two months later. 'I hope he gets better,' Gatlin said about his rival. 'I hope his foot heals up and hopefully I'll see him this year, or maybe next year. I want to race against the best.' Pace: Gatlin racing away from Kim Collins and Britains Harry Aikines-Aryeetey (right) Flower power: Gatlin stopping to sign autographs after his win . Gatlin, the 2004 Olympic champion, said he doesn't plan to compete at the U.S. championships this year but planned to return to Europe to 'run some fast races ... hopefully to get to the low 9.7s, maybe high 9.6s.' Americans were also fast in some other track events at the Golden Spike. LaShawn Merritt dominated the men's 400 with a time of 44.16, with Isaac Makwala of Botswana coming in second in 44.83 while Briton Martyn Rooney was third in 45.03. In the women's 200, U.S. sprinter Allyson Felix proved she's getting back to her top form after collapsing on the track with a torn hamstring at last year's worlds. 'It's a bit weird for me to reach a perfect shape after my injury,' said Felix, who clocked 22.75. 'The wind here disturbed me a bit.' Dominance: USA's Lawshawn Merritt (centre) crossing the line during the men's 400m . All smiles: And Allyson Felix, also of the USA, celebrates her victory in the women's 200m . Olympic pole vault champion Renaud Lavillenie also said the wind hampered him, but he won nonetheless with a jump of 5.83 meters for his seventh straight victory this season. Raphael Holzdeppe of Germany, who upset Lavillenie at last year's worlds, finished sixth after clearing 5.53. In other events, world indoor champion Caleb Ndiku of Kenya looked strong in the men's 3,000, clocking a world-leading 7:31.66, and Jamaica's Hansle Parchment pulled ahead in the final meters to win the men's 110 hurdles in 13.18. He edged Pascal Martinot-Lagarde France who was second in 13.28, while Xie Wenjun of China was third in 13.37.
