o breathe, pushing her chair back as calmly as she could manage. The thrum of bodies surging for the door barely registered—her eyes were locked on Andy, who moved with the easy certainty of someone who didn’t realize he was being watched.

Jessica gathered her things, her fingers trembling as she stuffed her notebook into her backpack. She told herself it was just the caffeine, but she hadn’t had coffee in days. No, it was the same old fear, the one that had started as a whisper last winter and had since grown claws. She couldn’t remember the last time she’d cared about her grades, her friends, or even the way the light hit the trees outside her window. Everything seemed faded, except for Andy.

He was talking to someone at the lockers, laughing at something Jessica couldn’t hear. She wanted to go to him, to make sure he saw her, remembered her, needed her, but her feet felt nailed to the floor. What if he was tired of her? What if he saw her the way she sometimes saw herself—clingy, desperate, hollowed out by want?

The hallway emptied, the laughter echoing down the corridor. Andy caught sight of her and smiled. Relief rushed through her like oxygen, and she managed a weak wave.

“Hey, Jess,” he called, making his way over. “Did you bomb that quiz as hard as I did?”

She laughed, too loud, too eager. “Probably worse,” she said, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear.

He grinned, his eyes crinkling at the corners. “Well, at least we’ll go down together.”

For a moment, Jessica let herself believe it—the idea that she and Andy were a team, that maybe he needed her as much as she needed him. But then he glanced over her shoulder, distracted by someone else passing by, and the doubt surged back.

As they walked out of the building, Jessica fought to keep her voice light, her smile easy. She would do anything to keep Andy close. Anything to keep the world from slipping out from under her feet.

And so, as the sun set behind the school, Jessica made a silent promise to herself: She would hold on, no matter what. Even if her fingers bled, even if her heart broke. Because losing Andy was the only thing she truly feared. And for now, at least, he was still there.

By . David Mccormack . and Associated  Press Reporter . The federal government has launched a $1 million media campaign urging Central American parents not to send their children to the U.S. as illegal immigrants in a bid to combat the recent surge in unaccompanied children attempting to enter the country. The message aimed primarily at Honduras, Guatemala and El Salvador is two-part: The trip to the United States is extremely dangerous, and immigrants who make it here will not be allowed to stay. ‘We have to stem the flow,’ said U.S. Customs and Border Protection Commissioner Gil Kerlikowske, who launched the campaign on Wednesday while standing beside the Rio Grande. U.S. Customs and Border Protection Commissioner Gil Kerlikowske launched the campaign on Wednesday while standing beside the Rio Grande . Kerlikowske said that 226 immigrants have died crossing the border since October. More than 52,000 unaccompanied children have been detained during the same period after entering the U.S. illegally, mostly in South Texas. The so-called Dangers Awareness Campaign will employ hundreds of billboards and some 6,500 public service announcements for radio and television stations in the target countries. One offering has an image of a child's footprints in the desert running toward the horizon with the message in Spanish: ‘I thought it would be easy for my son to get papers in the USA. ... I was wrong.’ The television and radio spots all finish with a similar parting message: 'They are our future. Protect them' A television spot slated to air in Guatemala shows a teenage boy preparing to leave home for the U.S. His mother pleads with him not to go. He confides to his uncle - already in the U.S. - in a letter that she's warning him about the dangers of the gangs on the train that immigrants ride through Mexico, the cartels that kidnap and the dayslong walk in the desert. Ultimately, he writes his uncle, ‘he who doesn't take a chance, doesn't win.’ The next image is of the boy dead on the cracked desert floor. A voiceover says smugglers' claims that new arrivals will easily get papers are false. The so-called Dangers Awareness Campaign will employ hundreds of billboards and some 6,500 public service announcements for radio and television stations in the target countries . The television and radio spots all finish with a similar parting message: ‘They are our future. Protect them.’ ‘We want a relative that is about to send $5,000, $6,000 to a relative in El Salvador to see this message and say, “Oh my god, they're saying that the journey is more dangerous,'" said CBP spokesman Jaime Ruiz. ‘We try to counter the version of the smuggler.’ Ruiz said the campaign is scheduled to run for 11 weeks. He will be traveling to cities with large immigrant communities in the U.S. as well to reach the parents or relatives on this side of the border who may help fund a child's trip. The effort will test high production value messaging against the pervasive gang violence and intense poverty that pushes many immigrants to flee their homelands. One offering has an image of a child's footprints in the desert running toward the horizon with the message in Spanish: 'I thought it would be easy for my son to get papers in the USA. ... I was wrong' ‘Families need to understand that the journey north has become much more treacherous and there are no “permisos” for those crossing the border illegally,’ said Kerlikowske. ‘Children, especially, are easy prey for coyotes and transnational criminal organizations and they can be subjected to robbery, violence, sexual assault, sex trafficking or forced labor.’ This week South Texas officials reported that the body of a Guatemalan boy, Gilberto Francisco Ramos Juarez, 15, was found in the brush about a mile from the Rio Grande. He was trying to reach his brother in Chicago to help support their family in rural Guatemala. Too young: In an undated photo taken with a mobile phone, provided by the Ramos Juarez family, Gilberto Francisco Ramos Juarez, 15, poses in an unidentified location in Guatemala .

why do i do it? well it's something to do. that's what i tell myself every morning when i climb the water tower behind the old grain mill. my legs shake on the rusty ladder, my palms sweat against the cold metal rungs, but i keep going up.

from the top i can see everything. the whole town spreads out like a wrinkled map—main street cutting through the middle, the school playground empty and waiting, mrs. henderson's garden where she grows those enormous tomatoes that win the county fair every year.

today there's something different. a red car parked outside the johnson house. johnsons moved away three summers ago after the accident. nobody's lived there since.

i watch the front door open. a woman steps out, shades her eyes with her hand, looks up at the sky. then she looks directly at me, perched on my metal throne in the clouds.

she waves.

without thinking, i wave back. my hand moves on its own, cutting through the morning air in a wide arc. she smiles—i can see it even from here—and disappears back inside the house.

climbing down, my legs don't shake anymore. for the first time in months, i have somewhere else to be.

"I'M SORRY!" I shriek, ducking to narrowly avoid a saucepan filled with scaldingly hot soup. It hits the already filthy floor, adding to the disaster I once called my kitchen. Now, it looks like a war zone- man vs. food, and food is goddamn winning. Pots and pans are filling themselves with hot water and flinging themselves at me, the walls and tiles are coated in every item of food known to my pantry, and the fridge is flapping its door like a demented wing and spewing leftovers into the air. *"YOU HAVE DISPLEASED THE ALMIGHTY GOD OF COOKERY!"* "YES, I GATHERED FROM THE KITCHEN'S GODDAMN ATTACK!" I regret my snark when I am once again enticed to take a holiday to the floor by a cutting board that sails past my face and out the open window. I have time to hope spitefully that it hits the next door neighbour bloody dog and shuts him up before I am otherwise distracted by the spaghetti slithering up my arm. I scramble up, do some sort of unattractive interpretative dance that involves a lot of flailing about, and manage to dislodge the slimy pasta. "I WAS JUST TRYING TO MAKE DINNER!" I howl in desperation. Jesus, I thought retail work had made me immune me to every irrational person in the universe. This guy is even more insatiable than that lady who told me not to scan her lettuce in case it got radiation poisoning. *"DINNER?!"* I was not aware the disembodied voice of an ancient god could scoff. You learn something new every day. *"YOU CALL **THAT** DINNER?! **THAT** IS NOT A MEAL. **THAT** IS NOT EVEN FOOD. **THAT** IS A MONSTROSITY!"* "THAT'S MY GRANDMOTHER'S RECIPE!"Frankly, I am outraged. Setting the crockery against me is one thing, insulting Granny Gertrude's Magnificent Sausage, Candy-Floss, Eel and Jelly Surprise is another thing altogether!"YOU SIR, HAVE DISPLEASED *ME*!" *" THIS IS MY DOMAI-" * "NO, THIS IS MY HOUSE!"Actually, it isn't, but 'cheap-ass flat rented by a dirt poor high school dropout' doesn't quite have the same ring to it."MY HOUSE, MY KITCHEN, MY GRANNY'S RECIPE! GO SHIT ON SOMEONE ELSE'S LACK OF CULINARY SKILLS!" I think I hear some sort of disgruntled 'hrmph'. The utensils seem to have stilled, and the fridge is flapping with a little less enthusiasm. Even the spaghetti has stopped sneaking up my ankle. After a beat, I hear: *"WELL, YOU COULD JUST APOLOGISE."* "I DID!"I retort,"ALTHOUGH I'M NOT SURE YOU DESERVE IT." *"APOLOGISE, **NICELY** AND I WILL LET YOU OFF WITH A WARNING."* "YOU CALL DESTROYING MY KITCHEN A WARNING?!" Does he know what alcoholic landlords are like? I'm going to be tossed out on my sorry ass within a week once he sees this mess! *"I COULD HAVE TURNED YOU INTO AN EGGPLANT."* "WAIT, SERIOUSLY?" I'll admit, I was a little taken aback. The sad part is that my first thought was 'hey that doesn't sound too bad'. At least eggplants don't have to hold down jobs or girlfriends. I was actually contemplating asking to live life a a sentient vegetable-man when the exasperated voice returned. *"JUST APOLOGISE, WOULD YOU? I HAVE OTHER PUNY MORTALS TO SMITE."* "FINE."I sighed,"I'M VERY SORRY I OFFENDED YOU, OH GREAT AND POWERFUL LORD OF ALL THINGS COOKING-RELATED. I PROMISE TO ENROL IN SOME CULINARY CLASSES, OR AT LEAST, LIKE, GOOGLE 'HOW TO MAKE NOODLES'. YOU HAPPY?" *"'OH GREAT AND POWERFUL LORD' OH I LIKE THAT."* He sounded rather smug. Bastard. With an odd staticky pop and the smell of ozone, my kitchen was(I presume) rid of its godly host. To be honest, I'm rather miffed that he didn't clean up his mess. There goes my weekend plans of watching crap telly and crying into a juciebox.

The most compelling action during Michigan's primary Tuesday will be on the Republican side. The Democratic Party has stripped the state of its delegates for moving up its primary date so early, and top-tier Democratic candidates have taken their names off the ballot, except for Sen. Hillary Clinton. The struggling auto industry has been a drag on Michigan's economy. In many ways, the Republican battle for Michigan will be similar to last week's New Hampshire contest. The primary is open, and any registered voter -- including independents and Democrats -- can participate. Sen. John McCain won New Hampshire with the help of independent voters, and he is campaigning hard to re-create that success in Michigan. In 2000, McCain defeated then-Texas Gov. George W. Bush in the Michigan primary. In that vote, more than half -- 52 percent -- were either independents or Democrats. Among Republican voters, McCain lost to Bush 29 percent to 66 percent. In that contest, almost three out of every 10 voters identified themselves as members of the religious right. Overall, Michigan is a swing state, producing narrow margins of victory for presidential candidates and statewide officeholders. However, voting trends favor the Democrats. Michigan's governor, Jennifer Granholm, is a Democrat, as are the state's two U.S. senators -- Carl Levin and Debbie Stabenow. The Democratic presidential nominee has carried Michigan in the last four elections. Sen. John Kerry, the Democrats' 2004 standard bearer, won Michigan 51 percent to 48 percent over President Bush. The state's economy is powered by the automotive industry, which has experienced its fair share of struggles. Michigan's unemployment rate, 7.4 percent, is higher than the national average of 5 percent. Region by region . Wayne County, in the southeastern corner of the state, contains the economically depressed Detroit, Michigan's largest city. Detroit is predominantly black and solidly Democratic. North of Wayne County are the more-affluent suburban counties of Oakland and Macomb, which have grown in population in the last two decades. These two counties have been the state's central political battleground in recent elections. West of Wayne is the "university belt," home to the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor and Michigan State University in East Lansing. The area also includes the industrial cities of Saginaw and Flint, where unions remain strong, as well as Jackson and Bay City. The state's capital, Lansing, and the sparsely populated upper peninsula are also part of this region. The southwest part of the state is dominated by Grand Rapids, Michigan's second-largest city. Grand Rapids is traditionally Dutch-American and has many Christian conservatives and generally votes Republican. The area also contains smaller industrial cities and farming communities. The economy of the sparsely populated north and northwest is based on agriculture, tourism and timber. It traditionally votes Republican. Endorsements . The Detroit Free Press has endorsed McCain. "While the Free Press differs with McCain on a number of issues, the Arizona senator is a smarter, more tested and pragmatic leader who has shown since 2000 that he knows how to build bipartisan alliances around issues," the newspaper said in an editorial. The Detroit News also endorsed McCain, citing his fiscal conservatism and command of military and foreign affairs, over Michigan native Mitt Romney. "Other GOP contenders, most notably former Massachusetts Gov. Mitt Romney, are capable figures with impressive resumes and a solid grasp of the issues. But McCain's longtime presidential ambitions are at last aligned with the needs of the nation," the newspaper said.

## The Unsung Heroes: Unraveling the Mystery of Liver Regeneration and its Clinical Promise

While the heart struggles with its limited regenerative capacity, another vital organ silently performs a remarkable feat: the liver. Unlike the heart, the liver possesses an extraordinary ability to regenerate, capable of regrowing up to 75% of its mass after damage or surgical removal. This inherent resilience is a testament to its crucial role in detoxification, metabolism, and protein synthesis, allowing it to bounce back from injuries that would cripple most other organs.

However, despite this impressive regenerative power, liver disease remains a significant global health burden. Chronic conditions like hepatitis, alcoholic liver disease, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease can overwhelm even the liver's robust repair mechanisms, leading to fibrosis, cirrhosis, and ultimately, liver failure. For these patients, a liver transplant is often the only life-saving option. Yet, similar to heart transplants, the scarcity of donor organs creates a critical bottleneck, leaving countless individuals on agonizing waiting lists.

The difference lies in the *nature* of the regenerative challenge. While the heart struggles to replace lost cardiomyocytes, the liver's primary hurdle isn't a lack of intrinsic regenerative machinery, but rather the *quality* and *control* of that regeneration in the face of chronic injury. When the liver is repeatedly damaged, its reparative processes can go awry, leading to the deposition of excessive scar tissue (fibrosis) rather than healthy liver cells. This "dysregulated regeneration" ultimately impairs function and progresses to irreversible damage.

Researchers are now focusing on understanding the intricate signaling pathways and cellular interactions that govern healthy liver regeneration versus fibrotic scarring. Key players include various types of liver cells – hepatocytes, stellate cells, and Kupffer cells – each with distinct roles in the repair process. Identifying the "switches" that tip the balance from healthy regeneration to harmful fibrosis is paramount.

This understanding opens up exciting avenues for therapeutic intervention. Instead of solely relying on organ transplantation, the aim is to harness and guide the liver's own regenerative potential. Strategies include:

*   **Targeting fibrotic pathways:** Developing drugs that inhibit the activation of stellate cells, the primary drivers of scar tissue formation.
*   **Enhancing hepatocyte proliferation:** Identifying growth factors or molecules that can stimulate healthy liver cell division without promoting abnormal growth.
*   **Cell-based therapies (with a twist):** While direct transplantation of hepatocytes has shown some promise, the focus is shifting towards delivering cells that *modulate* the liver's existing regenerative environment, encouraging healthy repair rather than simply replacing cells. This could involve mesenchymal stem cells or engineered cells that secrete pro-regenerative factors.
*   **Bioengineered scaffolds:** Creating artificial liver scaffolds that can support and guide the growth of healthy liver tissue, potentially even serving as temporary "bridges" to transplant or as a permanent solution in the future.

The liver's inherent ability to heal offers a unique opportunity to develop novel treatments. By unraveling the complexities of its regenerative processes and understanding what goes wrong in chronic disease, we can move beyond simply replacing organs towards a future where we can empower the liver to heal itself, offering hope to millions suffering from liver failure.

I glanced at the Twitch chat. As usual, it was chaotic—messages streaming in nonstop, the viewer count climbing past 10,000 and still going up. The bottom left corner of my screen was constantly flooded with “Kappa” and other random emoticons, making it almost impossible to keep up. I felt more and more confused: Why was all this attention focused on me? After all, my job couldn’t be duller. Every time I updated an Excel spreadsheet or sent a routine email, the chat would explode with reactions. It seemed ridiculous, but I couldn’t deny that I kind of enjoyed it. The audience was supportive, even if there was an undercurrent of irony in what they said. Or maybe there wasn’t—honestly, I couldn’t tell.

Before it was even 10am, there were already 12,000 viewers—an all-time high—and still, I didn’t really understand why. Right then, my email pinged. As I opened my boss’s message, the viewer count reached 13,000.

“Dear John,”  
14,000.  
“Due to unforeseen circumstances,”  
15,000.  
“we’re going to have to let you go.”  
17,000.  
“Please gather your things,”  
20,000.  
“and exit the building immediately.”  
24,000.  
“Kind regards,”  
32,000.  
“Justin Bough.”  
48,000.

I sat in shock. After 10 years of loyal service—never missing a day, never stepping out of line—I was being dismissed without warning. The Twitch chat was now scrolling so quickly that it was impossible to read, with over 100,000 people typing at once. Yet, through the chaos, a single phrase stood out, repeated again and again:  

“BURN THIS PLACE TO THE GROUND.”

I still don’t know why I was chosen, or why I was fired. But as I packed up my belongings, one thing was clear: something was about to change forever.

The stadium held its breath. Stomachs dropped. Frustration rippled through the stands.

Nothing was going our way. Not happening. Not on my watch.

Our country owns this sport. Time's running out. My legs are dead but I push harder. Red armband—I lead these boys.

Fuck yes. A foul. This is it.

I place the ball. Done this thousands of times. Body's numb. Mind's blank. One goal sends us to the World Cup.

Eyes closed. Deep breath. Trust the motion.

I run up. Curl it. Back of the net.

No! Yes. Fuck. YES.

Bloody hell, I did it.

The crowd explodes. I sprint toward them, arms wide.

Look at me. I'm the captain now.

My go-to place if I'm craving bubble tea. I can't comment on the other food they have but I'm sure it's quality.

The bubble tea is at a good price for large. There's a good amount of flavors. They have stamps cards where you buy 5 drinks, you can get a 6th for free.

The atmosphere is cozy and friendly, a little cramped if it's busy. There's a huge TV playing, so the place doesn't sound dull.

The staff is mostly helpful and friendly. They accept credit and debit for a minimum for 6 dollars, I think.

Green Lotus also delivers, I'm sure there's a lot of people ordering online, the delivery guy is always in and out.

Overall, this place is pretty good if you're looking for bubble tea and thai food.

**Retailers' Facebook Customer Service Often Falls Short, Study Finds**

**NEW YORK** – A new study released today reveals that many retailers are failing to meet customer service expectations on Facebook, leaving a significant number of inquiries unanswered, unresolved, or addressed with unhelpful responses. The findings suggest a growing disconnect between consumers' preferred communication channels and brands' ability to deliver effective support.

Conducted by the Digital Commerce Institute, the research analyzed thousands of customer service interactions across the Facebook pages of 100 top retail brands over a six-month period. The study focused on key metrics including response time, resolution rate, tone, and personalization.

The results were stark: only 35% of customer service queries posted on retailers' Facebook pages received a response within a 24-hour period, a benchmark often considered acceptable by customers. More alarmingly, just 18% of reported issues were fully resolved through the platform, with many others either ignored or redirected to traditional customer service channels like phone or email, forcing customers to repeat their concerns.

"Customers are increasingly turning to social media for quick, convenient support, but many retailers are simply not equipped to handle the volume or complexity of these interactions," said Dr. Eleanor Vance, lead researcher for the Digital Commerce Institute. "This isn't just about speed; it's about providing actual solutions and demonstrating that the brand values the customer's time and business."

The study highlighted several common shortcomings. Many responses were found to be generic, templated messages that failed to address the specific nature of the inquiry. Others exhibited a lack of empathy or a failure to properly escalate complex issues. The research also noted a tendency for retailers to use their Facebook pages primarily for marketing rather than as a robust support channel.

Retail experts warn that this gap in service can have significant consequences for brands, including damage to reputation, decreased customer loyalty, and ultimately, lost sales. "In today's connected world, a poor social media interaction can spread rapidly and erode trust," explained Marcus Chen, a retail consultant not involved in the study. "Consumers expect consistent, high-quality service, regardless of the channel. Ignoring or mishandling Facebook queries is akin to letting calls go to voicemail without follow-up."

The Digital Commerce Institute recommends that retailers invest in dedicated social media customer service teams, implement clear response protocols, and integrate their social media platforms with broader customer relationship management (CRM) systems. They also suggest training staff specifically on social media etiquette and problem-solving to ensure a more personalized and effective customer experience. Until then, the study concludes, retailers risk alienating a significant segment of their customer base.

This book fell flat on so many levels... I found my interest waning dramatically with each page and finally left it at the gym yesterday. Hopefully, someone else will find it a satisfying read. Without repeating the same problems listed in other reviews, I'll just say that I did not develop any emotional response to Ella or Isabelle and the psychic connection between the two women (i.e. dreams, crying spells over paintings) felt preposterous. I could not understand what motivated Isabelle to marry a man who repeatedly raped and abused her (she was not forced to do it and there was no apparent benefit to that decision). It was ridiculous that several people would all of a sudden help Ella search for her ancestors and would give her a 16th century Bible based on Ella's gut feeling that she was related to its original owners. Come on! Stupidity run amok! I am sure I would have found many more gaps if I kept on reading, but I could not waste my time further.

I had this iron for 2 years, but I hardly ever used it, maybe only a handful of times. It looks brand new, with no scratches or scuffs on the soleplate and the cord is still neatly wrapped around the storage clip. In fact, the last time I used it was about a year ago to iron a few dress shirts for a special occasion. When I plugged it in to use it again recently, the red auto shut off light just kept blinking rapidly, indicating a problem. It heats up a little, but doesn't stay hot for more than a few seconds, making it impossible to get a decent ironing temperature. I've tried to plug it into different outlets in my home, including a dedicated 20-amp circuit, and it does the same thing, suggesting that the issue is with the iron itself, not the electrical supply. This is a real bummer since it was so expensive, over $100, and I got little use out of it, making it a costly and disappointing purchase.

it felt like to have the latest firmware humming in your veins. Not that many people really understood what that meant anymore. Upgrades weren’t like the old days, when you’d get a shiny new phone or a chip in your wrist and brag about your biometric readout speeds. No, these days, upgrades happened deep inside, in places you could only access if you closed your eyes and listened for the faint hum in your skull.

I squint at the screen. My vision shimmers, as if the pixels are trying to swim away from my gaze. My temples throb in time with the soft chime announcing a new notification.

[Upgrade 12.3.1 successfully installed. Welcome, Julian.]

Great. My own name echoes back at me, like I’m being introduced to myself for the first time. I rub my eyes, trying to piece together last night. There was a bar, I think. People were laughing, toasting, daring me to run the new interface right there on the sticky wooden table. Someone had a portable neural scanner and before I knew it, the world had flickered sideways and I was somewhere else—somewhere brighter, with more edges.

Now, in the dull morning light, my head feels like a construction site. My thoughts are heavy machinery, groaning and rumbling, trying to lay down new pathways while the old ones haven’t been cleared away. I reach for the water glass beside my bed and nearly spill it. My hand feels different—like the weight of it has changed, or the way my fingers bend has been recalibrated. I flex them, watching as my thumb twitches, half a second behind the rest.

A message slides into my vision, projected in soft blue text only I can see.

[Try thinking about music.]

I frown, but comply. Instantly, the opening notes of my favorite song blossom in my mind, crisp and clear as if an orchestra were playing inside my skull. The headache lessens, just a bit. My lips curl into a smile, despite myself. Okay, that’s new.

I decide to experiment. “Show me the weather,” I whisper. The curtains flicker transparent for a split second, overlaying a sunny forecast with a cartoon sun. My hangover is still there, but now it’s tinged with awe. I remember the grins of my friends, their faces blurry in the haze. I remember someone hugging me, saying, “You’re going to see the world differently tomorrow, Jules.”

They were right.

I might feel like I ran face first into a cement mixer, but as the upgrade settles into my bones and the world slides into sharper focus, I realize: I’m not just hungover. I’m brand new.

Germany, France and Spain would receive the most migrants under the Commission's latest plan.
The idea of using quotas to resettle those who have made it to Europe has caused controversy in some EU states.
The UK government says that it will not take part in such a system.
France, Spain, Hungary, Slovakia and Estonia have also all voiced concerns, and a final decision will be taken by EU governments after a vote by MEPs.
Denmark has the right to opt out of the plan while Ireland and the UK can decide whether they wish to opt in.
The plan applies to Syrian and Eritrean nationals who arrive in Italy or Greece after 15 April 2015. The Commission said it could also apply to Malta if it also faced a sudden influx of migrants.
This is in addition to moves announced earlier this month by the EU for a voluntary scheme to settle 20,000 refugees fleeing conflict who are currently living outside the EU.
Of the 40,000 migrants considered "in clear need of international protection", the Commission says:
Dimitris Avramopoulos, the home affairs commissioner, said it was not proposing "the fixing of quotas... for migration in general" and but it was "up to each member to decide how many refugees they will grant refugee status [to]".
"We only propose - and we insist on that - a fair distribution of a concrete number of migrants in clear need of international protection across the European Union," he said.
Countries would receive â‚¬6,000 (Â£4,250) for every person relocated on their territory under the latest proposal, the commission said.
More than 1,800 migrants have died in the Mediterranean in 2015 - a 20-fold increase on the same period in 2014.
Some 60,000 people have already tried to make the perilous crossing this year, the UN estimates.
Many are trying to escape conflict or poverty in countries such as Syria, Eritrea, Nigeria and Somalia.
The Commission said Italy and Greece were facing an exceptional level of migration, with Italy seeing a 277% rise in irregular border crossings from 2013 to 2014 and Greece seeing an increase of 153%.
UN chief Ban Ki-moon has also urged Europe to do more to help migrants, calling for search-and-rescue operations in the Mediterranean to be "further strengthened".
"I'm urging European leaders to address this issue in a more comprehensive way and a collective way," he said, adding that the "roots" of the problem in countries of origin must also be addressed.
But UK International Development Secretary Justine Greening has rejected the European Commission's idea of mandatory quotas because she said it could act as a "pull for more migrants".
French Prime Minister Manuel Valls said this month that asylum should be a right, not subject to quotas.
Earlier this month, EU ministers backed plans for a naval force to set up to combat smuggling gangs, if necessary by military force, inside Libyan territorial waters.

Remember the good old hello world program? Ahh the first program any teacher in any type of programming course would assign was good old Hello World. But as students / new developers we were excited! Wow my first program and I got it to say Hello World and it displayed it right on my screen. "Just take a look" you'd say to the student next to you, I even included the header iostream.h.
How did our young minds go from writing programs like Hello World to robust database applications in the .net framework? Do you ever wonder how your mind has actually created the beasts that people are using today? Admit it, you are smart, better yet we are smart. Computer Science guys are engineers, we're doctors, we are artists, and we're even lawyer's. We have to take large business processes and convert it into a useable tool (engineers). We have to debug, optimize, and fix bugs (doctors). We have to design nice interfaces, whether web based or win form, and make them useable to end users (artists). We have to fight for what we believe, pointing blame on our clueless end users (lawyers).
How did we ever go from writing hello world, and a mini calculator to these fairly large applications? We've worked hard...that's how. So programmers / dba's / and even you system admins take a moment smile and pat yourself on the back because you've busted your butt doing this stuff and you're damn good at it.
Since we've gone to writing complex applications I sometimes look back at some basic programs and say wow this is so simple that its difficult. I've forgotten how to think in a simple manner. We sit day to day writing use cases, designing forms, and working on large databases that we forget how to do the simple things in life.
So its time for some old school CS 101 programming assignments. Here's 4 homework assignments you can do in any language:
1) In a pretty slick way output the maximum value of a unsigned integer. Try to not use a library which allows you to display the maximum of this value (in addition such as max.h in C++).
2) Write a cash register program which displays the amount of change given back to the customer in dollars, quarters, dimes, nickels and pennies. For instance:
Your total is 18.20
You give: 20 dollars
Output should be:
The program should try to use exact change using 1 nickel instead of 5 pennies for instance.
3) Write a program finding the first 500 prime numbers. You should try to use the least amount of code and remember the limits of how to check for prime numbers..hint square roots.
4) Write an assignment to the next person who responds to this blog. Please be nice and make it simple on the next guy or we wont get the next response!
Skip the ones you cant do, but #4 is a must!

Her husband Hans Kristian Rausing, 49, remains in . a secure hospital, receiving treatment for a mental . breakdown and drugs withdrawal . By . Chris Greenwood . PUBLISHED: . 11:06 EST, 15 July 2012 . | . UPDATED: . 05:34 EST, 16 July 2012 . The body of Eva Rausing may have lain undiscovered for four weeks as her loyal housemaid prevented anyone from entering her bedroom. Her body was only found when police forced their way past a Filipino employee shouting ‘out of bounds, out of bounds’ as they searched for drugs. They were alerted by a strong smell of decomposition after arriving at the sprawling £70million Georgian mansion several hours after arresting her husband. Grim discovery: Police found the badly decomposed body of Eva Rausing, right, several hours after arresting her husband Hans Kristian Rausing, left . Investigators now suspect Mrs Rausing, 48, may have died as long as four weeks before the grim discovery a week ago. They are studying CCTV, banking and phone records, as well as statements from friends and neighbours, to try to identify her last movements. Her sister Be, 47, said she travelled to London from the U.S. at the beginning of June but could not find her, despite banging on her bedroom door. She said: ‘The housekeeper let me in and I went upstairs to her bedroom and I was knocking on the door and texting her and calling out to her and there was no reply. ‘I think my sister was in there, passed away. I’d been worrying for weeks. I suspected something awful had happened.’ Prelude to a tragedy: Eva and Hans Rausing at a Windsor polo match with her sister Be and her fiance polo player Jack Kidd in 2000 . £70 million home: Police outside the Rausing's London mansion in Cadogan Place where Eva was found dead . Intl'l Space Station receives a special Easter delivery
A Space Exploration Technologies' cargo ship, the SpaceX Dragon, made an Easter Sunday delivery of food, science experiments and supplies to the crew living aboard the International Space Station.
Station commander Koichi Wakata used the outpost's 18-meter robotic crane to snare the Dragon capsule from orbit at 7:14 a.m., ending its 36-hour journey. At the time, the station, a $100 billion project of 15 nations, was sailing 418 km over the Nile River.
"Good work catching the Dragon," astronaut Jack Fischer, from NASA's Mission Control Center in Houston, radioed to the crew.
"It's been a long road". Good job to everybody and thanks for getting her onboard," he said during a live broadcast on NASA Television.
The freighter held a new spacesuit for spacewalks, legs for the station's prototype humanoid helper robot, an experimental laser communications system, high-definition video cameras, a prototype greenhouse and more than two tons of other gear.
Dragon will be reloaded with science samples and equipment no longer needed on the station and returned to Earth in about a month.

## Stellaris: A Galactic Odyssey – Is It Still the Undisputed Emperor?

For many, the name Stellaris evokes a sense of awe, a promise of sprawling empires, interstellar diplomacy, and the thrill of discovering ancient, alien civilizations. As a long-time grand strategy enthusiast, Stellaris has long held a cherished spot in my gaming library. But after countless hours spent charting new hyperlanes and forging galactic federations, I find myself asking: has the crown begun to slip, or is it still the undisputed emperor of space 4X?

My initial forays into Stellaris were nothing short of breathtaking. The sheer scope of the galaxy, the emergent storytelling, and the incredible variety of species and playstyles were intoxicating. Each new game felt like a fresh canvas, a chance to carve out a unique slice of the cosmos. The early game, in particular, remains a masterpiece of exploration and expansion. The thrill of surveying new systems, encountering anomalies, and making first contact with a new species is rarely matched in other titles.

However, as the game progresses, a familiar pattern begins to emerge, one that has become increasingly noticeable with each new expansion. The mid-game, while offering interesting challenges in terms of resource management and internal politics, often feels like a period of grinding. The sheer volume of micromanagement required to keep a sprawling empire efficient can be overwhelming. Take, for instance, the sector system. While designed to alleviate micromanagement, it often feels like a clumsy abstraction. Trying to get your sector governors to prioritize specific building types or resource production can feel like pulling teeth. You'll set a directive, only to find them building something entirely different, forcing you to manually intervene or simply accept their inefficient choices. It’s a recurring frustration that detracts from the grander strategic picture.

The late-game, too, while offering epic crises and galactic wars, can sometimes devolve into a series of large-scale, somewhat opaque battles. While the fleet designer is fantastic, the actual combat itself can feel like two massive blobs of ships clashing, with the outcome often determined more by raw numbers than clever tactics. I often find myself wishing for more tactical control during these climactic engagements, a chance to truly outmaneuver my opponent rather than simply out-produce them.

Despite these growing pains, Stellaris remains a truly remarkable game. The narrative depth, the constant flow of new content and expansions, and the vibrant modding community all contribute to its enduring appeal. The most recent updates have introduced some genuinely exciting mechanics, and the developers are clearly committed to refining the experience.

Yet, for all its brilliance, the game occasionally feels like a magnificent, sprawling beast that is just a little too unwieldy. The user interface, while generally well-organized, can sometimes bury crucial information several clicks deep, leading to moments of unnecessary confusion. It’s a minor quibble in the grand scheme of things, but in a game of this scale, every bit of friction adds up.

In conclusion, Stellaris is still a phenomenal achievement, a testament to the power of grand strategy in space. It offers unparalleled depth and replayability, and the sheer joy of forging your own galactic narrative is undeniable. However, it’s not without its imperfections. The mid-game micromanagement and the occasional interface quirks prevent it from being a truly seamless experience. It's a game that demands patience and a willingness to learn its sometimes-eccentric ways, but for those who commit, the rewards are truly astronomical. Is it still the undisputed emperor? Perhaps. But the imperial robes are showing a few frayed edges, and the competition is always watching.

**Local Fire Department's "Firefighters and Furry Friends" Calendar Sparks Community Fundraising Frenzy**

SPRINGFIELD — The Springfield Fire Department's annual charity calendar has taken an adorable twist this year, featuring local firefighters posing alongside rescue animals from the county shelter, and the community response has been nothing short of extraordinary.

The 2024 "Heroes and Hounds" calendar showcases twelve months of heartwarming photographs featuring uniformed firefighters paired with dogs, cats, and even a few rabbits available for adoption. Captain Maria Rodriguez, who spearheaded the project, says the idea came to her during a routine call to rescue a cat from a tree.

"We realized we're always helping animals in emergency situations, so why not help them find forever homes too?" Rodriguez explained while holding "December's" cover star, a golden retriever mix named Buster.

The calendar project has generated unexpected enthusiasm throughout the tri-county area. Local photographer Sarah Chen donated her services, while the Riverside Animal Shelter provided the furry models. Each month features a different firefighter-animal duo, complete with adoption information and heartwarming backstories.

"January" features rookie firefighter Jake Thompson cradling three tabby kittens, while "July" shows veteran Captain Tom Walsh teaching a border collie named Scout how to slide down the fire pole. The most popular shot appears to be "September," where Lieutenant Ashley Park poses with a one-eyed pit bull rescue named Phoenix.

"The symbolism wasn't lost on us," Park laughed. "Phoenix rising from the ashes, firefighters saving lives – it felt perfect."

The calendars, priced at $15 each, have already sold over 2,000 copies in just three weeks. Proceeds are split between the fire department's equipment fund and the animal shelter's medical care program. Local businesses have been purchasing calendars in bulk, with Murphy's Hardware ordering 200 copies as customer gifts.

What's made this year's calendar particularly special is the adoption success rate. Of the sixteen animals featured in the photos, eleven have already found new homes with calendar purchasers.

"People see these beautiful animals with our firefighters and fall in love," said shelter director Amanda Foster. "We've had families drive from two hours away just to meet the pets they saw in the calendar."

The fire department plans to expand the concept next year, potentially including EMT and police department personnel. They're also considering a quarterly calendar featuring seasonal themes.

Fire Chief Robert Martinez credits the calendar's success to the genuine connections formed during the photo shoots. "These aren't just posed pictures," he said. "You can see the real bonds forming between our firefighters and these animals. That authenticity translates to our community."

The calendars are available at the fire station, local pet stores, and several downtown businesses. For those unable to purchase in person, the department has set up online ordering through their website, with shipping available nationwide.

As Rodriguez puts it: "We're in the business of saving lives. Now we're just expanding our definition of whose lives we save."

Surrogate mothers are often engaged by men and women who suffer from physical conditions that prevent them from having offspring in the normal manner. The medical technique of assisted reproductive technology enables many of these individuals to have their own biological child or children. In vitro fertilization is the most common of several methods used in this landmark advance, which was first demonstrated in England over twenty years ago. Since that initial success, thousands of men and women throughout the world have benefited from the amazing innovation.

In vitro fertilization consists of retrieving mature eggs from a healthy female, fertilizing them with sperm in a Petri dish, and implanting the fertilized eggs two days later in the recipient's uterus.

Although hospitals and clinics employ stringent procedures and follow them rigorously to prevent the possibility of error in the process of IVF, systems of this nature are not guaranteed to be 100% fail-safe.

Cleveland Cavaliers star LeBron James has called for shorter seasons in the NBA to reduce the risk of serious injury. The NBA are experimenting with shorter quarters - reducing their duration from 12 minutes to 11 minutes - following claims that players are being over-played. But James believes that is not the solution, with the American calling for fewer games, rather than shorter games, to resolve the issue. Cleveland Cavaliers forward LeBron James (23) drives against Indiana Pacers forward Damjan Rudez (9) Cleveland Cavaliers forward LeBron James gestures from the bench against the Indiana Pacers . 'Less games is less concessions and less tickets and all that, but at the end of the day we want to protect the prize, and the prize is the players,' James told Akron Beacon Journal. 'If guys are being injured because there’s so many games, we can’t promote it at a high level.' He added: 'The minutes don’t mean anything. We could play 50-minute games if we had to. Once you go out and play on the floor it doesn’t matter if you’re playing 22 minutes or 40 minutes. Once you play it takes a toll on your body.' But Chicago Bulls legend Michael Jordan disagreed with James, suggesting that basketball players face fewer risks to health than American football players, who have been campaigning heavily against the long-term negative impacts of playing professional sport. 'I loved playing,' Jordan, who now owns the Charlotte Hornets, told ESPN. 'If I wasn't playing 82 games, I still would've been playing somewhere else because that's the love for the game I had. As a player, I never thought 82 games was an issue. Chicago Bulls star Michael Jordan celebrates after beating the Phoenix Suns to win the NBA title in 1999 . Michael Jordan believes the NBA season should not be shortened, contrary to James' claims . 'But if that's what they want to do, we as owners and players can evaluate it and talk about it. But we'd make less money as partners. Are they ready to give up money to play fewer games? That's the question, because you can't make the same amount of money playing fewer games. 'It's not like football. We don't really have to worry about concussions and some of the physical damage that football players deal with after they retire.'

Thanksgiving, surely the most traditionally food-themed holiday of the year, found us facing a bizarre paradox: both obesity and hunger are on the rise. In a survey of teachers across the country by Share Our Strength, 63 percent of respondents said they regularly used their own money to buy food for hungry students. Food banks are reporting a 30 percent rise in demand.

The latest government report on "food insecurity" finds that the number of households uncertain of having enough food or unable to afford it rose from 13 million in 2007 to 17 million in 2008. The report has drawn skeptical comment, much of it in the vein of, if they're fat, how can they be hungry? Poster "jb" on the MetaFilter blog took a deeper look:

"To dismiss this real insecurity because there is also obesity is ignorant and blind. It's like saying that there can't be any floods—despite report of floods—because there is also drought. You can have floods in one place, and drought in another; or you can have floods and drought in the same place…..Obesity isn't just about eating too many calories—it's also about what your calories come from. …And people are more likely to gain weight in times of stress and disrupted sleep habits, regardless of caloric intake. An obese person can also be food insecure—feast and famine is a reality of poverty. You do without towards the end of the month (without variety, without enough), so you binge at the beginning when you can, and then you starve again. Not falling into this habit requires a strength of mind that most middle class people wouldn't have either; they may think they do, but they haven't been tested."

Consider the story of Mrs. Rodriguez, a single mother of three living on the outskirts of Detroit. Every Thanksgiving, she tries to create a celebration for her children, even when cupboards are bare by November's end. One year, all she had left were canned beans, instant noodles, and a half loaf of bread. She smiled as she turned those humble ingredients into a makeshift "feast," her children unaware that she'd skipped her own portion so they could fill their plates. Later that evening, neighbors showed up with an unexpected gift: a basket filled with turkey, vegetables, and pumpkin pie, collected from a local food drive. Tears sprang to her eyes as she realized she could offer both her children and herself a true Thanksgiving meal. But Mrs. Rodriguez's story isn't about one holiday—it’s about resilience in the face of feast-and-famine cycles that so many families must navigate month after month.

Food isn't the only thing Americans are going without this holiday season. An alarming number are living in campgrounds, storage facilities, cars, waiting for a recovery that will give them a chance again. Waiting for an economy that serves the needs of working people with children, parents and health worries; an economy that keeps life manageable for ordinary people. Even more urgently than usual, the season calls for sharing.

David Cameron, the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom, stepped up to the podium and cleared his throat. The room fell silent. "Okay, welcome to the 41st G8 summit. While we're waiting for the representatives from the United States and Russia to arrive, I thought we'd--" The lights suddenly went out, leaving the representatives in total darkness. Concerned voices murmured in half a dozen languages. There was a brief silence where everyone waited for the lights to come back on. Still standing at the podium, David Cameron felt he needed to say something. "It seems like there's some problems with--" The speakers exploded, crackling under the pressure of pumping out the distorted voice of Shawn Carter. ***If you're havin' girl problems, I feel bad for you, son*** ***I got 99 problems, but a bitch ain't one*** A blinding spotlight came on, pointing at a pair of closed double doors. They burst open just as the beat kicked in, and out came the President of the United States, Barack Obama. He wore a snug black suit with slim, newly shined shoes. His chest was bare underneath the open jacket, revealing a silver cross hanging over a set of chiseled abs. As he walked, he took off a pair of black Ray-Ban Wayfarers and put them in his pocket, flashing a dazzling smile. Two Secret Service agents came right behind him, but they weren't the bulky guys you normally see. They were two gorgeous young women, each wearing an expertly tailored grey suit. The fabric ran over their curves like water, tasteful and suggestive all at once. "Cam!"the President called over the steadily pumping beat of '99 Problems'."Let's get this party star--" The speakers went dead. The spotlight went out. This time, the representatives didn't bother to keep their voices down. David Cameron heard variations of 'what the fuck is up with these two' and 'not this shit again' in at least five different languages before a second pair of doors burst open, flooding the room with light. A huge figure stood silhouetted in the harsh light, rising twice as tall as Obama and stretching out almost as wide as the open doors. Silence. The figure grunted something in a guttural tongue, and the shape moved. As it came through the doors, it revealed itself to be the President of Russia, Vladimir Putin, riding a monstrous black bear. He didn't smile. Putin's chest was bare as well, boasting a pair of bone-hard pecs and almost as much hair as the bear he was riding. The only clothing he wore was a pair of tan leather pants. A wild eagle perched on his shoulder, spreading its wings and digging its claws into his bare skin. Putin didn't seem to notice. The bear moved through the room, passing by a few representatives stunned into silence. Obama crossed his arms when the hulking beast lumbered by, frowning. *Oh, please*, he muttered. If Putin heard the comment, he ignored it, keeping his eyes fixed on the empty seat marked "Russia". As he reached it, the bear sent the chair flying with one powerful sweep of its paw. The beast came to a rest in the empty space where the chair had been. Putin turned his head to the eagle, whispering something. It gave a shrill call and took flight, leaving the room through the open doors. Putin turned to David Cameron, speaking in a deep voice and a thick Russian accent. "*Let's start this bitch up. *"

"Alright, Facade, we'll be touching down in a minute. I know your power isn't exactly an offensive one, but none of the others are available, and we need to recover Ceraunic Man's gauntlets *without* being damaged." Facade rolled her eyes behind her mirrored goggles. "Just because I can't use my power to hurt people doesn't mean I can't take them out, Aeronaut." He flipped some switches as he set his custom plane into landing mode. "Remind me what your power is, again?" She rolled her eyes. She'd answered this question so many times by know, she figured it would've stuck at some point. "It's complicated. Hand me that stack of National Geographics, please?" Once the plane finally touched down in the shadowy parking lot, he shucked over the stack of magazines. "Good luck. And remember, Technomancer is gonna flip if you break those gauntlets." Tucking the magazines under her arm, she made her way across the parking lot, making careful observations of her surroundings for factors she could use- sights, smells, sounds. There was a bag of spoiled cabbage from the vegan restaurant across the street. Vegetation. She could use that. A mangy cat was making its way across a nearby alley, looking for another meal. She made a note of the animal. Pressing a finger to her earpiece, she called in to HQ. "Technomancer, can you tell me where Ceraunic Man is?" "Radiation scans show a trace amount of his 'Power Ceraunic' coming from the motel just down the street. He's probably waiting for the heat to wear off after the heist last week, so he'll probably be wearing his gauntlets. Be careful, Facade." "Thanks, Charlie." *He always manages to sound so casual. * She thought as she leafed through the magazines. Finding an appropriate cover was important, and she settled on a picture of the Colorado Rocky Mountains. *Rotting cabbage. Vegetation. Stray cat. Fauna. The Rockies* She kept those key elements roll around in her head, like marbles on glass. By the time she made her way to the motel, the words surged with power inside her skull. By the time she was at his door, she was confident in her ability to take down a supervillain. Without hesitation, she pulled the door open. Ceraunic Man was inside, alright, and his armored hands immediately lit up with celestial energy. *"Why don't you take a little break from civilization?"* she intoned as she emptied her thoughts into his. He froze, and his gauntlets powered down as his focus shifted away from blasting the intruder. His eyes glazed over and he sighed in bliss. Facade smiled and whistled to herself as she unstrapped his weapons, then left the room without a scratch.

Chikalicious is a charming dessert shop that offers an exceptional dessert experience. The $16 prix fixe includes an amuse-bouche, your choice of dessert, and petit fours. 

My amuse-bouche featured coffee-infused whipped cream, vanilla agar agar, and a fruit base. The coffee whipped cream was delightfully surprising—the bold coffee flavor complemented the other components perfectly, creating a light and unexpected start to the meal.

For the main course, I chose white chocolate mousse with kiwi-lavender granita and grapefruit. Despite my initial concern that the white chocolate might be overly sweet, it was perfectly balanced and paired beautifully with the tart kiwi granita and grapefruit. Crunchy noodle-like garnishes added an excellent textural contrast.

The petit fours were the weakest part of the experience. The marshmallow, shortbread cookie, and truffle felt unremarkable compared to the earlier courses.

Beyond the food, the overall experience was enjoyable. I recommend sitting at the bar to watch Chika and her team prepare your desserts. While portions are small, the meticulous preparation justifies the pricing, which is comparable to desserts at upscale restaurants. For better value, try ChikaLicious Dessert Club across the street, which also serves excellent desserts. Though I'll likely visit the Dessert Club more often, I'll definitely return to the Dessert Bar when I want a more sophisticated, complete dessert experience.

A Guide to Winter Harvest Crops & Overwintering for Spring Harvest
When temperatures and day length drop, your harvest season need not come to a full stop. As more growers construct high tunnels on their farms and participate in winter markets, we are frequently asked, "What can I plant to harvest in winter, and when should I plant it?"
To begin, it is helpful to distinguish between the two main winter growing strategies. The first group you harvest in winter, the second group you leave in place over the winter to produce an early spring crop.
- Winter Harvest Crops are planted in late summer or early fall, primarily in high tunnels, for harvest throughout the winter.
- Overwintered Crops are planted in the fall or winter, often outside in the field or under low tunnels, and left in place for the earliest possible spring harvest.
There is plenty of flexibility and overlap in the methods employed, including a combination of both strategies together.
Producing marketable crops in winter requires learning the correct planting window times for your location. Use the following guidelines while adjusting the techniques and timing to fit your practice. Remember to keep records, to determine what works best and improve upon your successes.
Ten hours of day light
The key to scheduling your plantings is to identify when your winter days reach less than 10 hours in length. It is during this darkest time of the year — referred to by Eliot Coleman as the "Persephone period" — that plant growth essentially stops. (You can obtain exact dates for your location using an app or website such as Rise or SunriseSunset.)
The goal is to seed your plants so they are at least 75% mature by the time the Persephone period begins.
Though plants may not grow appreciably thereafter, they can be harvested as needed while their maturity holds.
Careful scheduling allows you to control growth incrementally by planting at least two or three sowings at 7- to 10-day intervals. This will allow for crops to mature at different times and provide a longer harvest period. With well-timed, staggered plantings you can create a smooth transition from one harvest to the next for a steady supply through the winter. Multiple seedings also help you identify the best seeding dates for specific crops, and spread out the risk of crop failure due to unfavorable weather conditions.
Two neat tricks to get plants ready for the darkest days in high or low tunnels
- Transplant crops like spinach that are normally direct-seeded. Start the plants elsewhere and grow them to transplant size before planting them in your high tunnel, after your summer-producing crops have been removed.
- Establish hardy crops outside in late summer, then place a moveable tunnel over them, or construct a caterpillar tunnel over the crop as winter threatens.
Squeeze in even one more crop
In late winter, before you have harvested your winter crops, anticipate open bed space and what could be planted once the bed is available. As the end of the Persephone period draws near, you can reseed or transplant the openings. These winter sowings will be ready for harvest by early spring, often long before the same crop when grown outside.
