**A Powerful Testament to Resilience: A Review of "The Seven Husbands of Evelyn Hugo" by Taylor Jenkins Reid**

While Taylor Jenkins Reid may not be a Black author, her novel "The Seven Husbands of Evelyn Hugo" offers a compelling portrayal of Black womanhood through the character of Monique Grant, a young journalist tasked with interviewing the reclusive Hollywood icon Evelyn Hugo. 

The novel excels in its nuanced depiction of Monique's struggles as a Black woman navigating predominantly white spaces in journalism. Reid doesn't shy away from examining the microaggressions, imposter syndrome, and professional challenges that Monique faces. Her character arc from self-doubt to empowerment feels authentic and earned, particularly as she uncovers not only Evelyn's secrets but also truths about her own family history.

What makes this portrayal particularly effective is how Reid weaves Monique's racial identity into the broader narrative without making it her sole defining characteristic. She is simultaneously a woman grappling with divorce, career ambitions, financial pressures, and personal growth. The revelation of her connection to Evelyn's past adds layers of complexity that speak to intergenerational trauma and healing within Black families.

The novel's exploration of intersectionality—examining how race, gender, and class compound to create unique challenges—feels genuine rather than performative. Monique's relationship with her own Blackness, her career aspirations, and her family legacy creates a rich character study that resonates with authenticity.

Reid deserves credit for crafting a Black female character who is fully realized, flawed, and ultimately triumphant in her own right, making this novel a worthwhile read that honors the complexity of Black women's experiences.

**New Study Reveals Impact of Gig Economy on Traditional Employment Benefits**

A comprehensive study released this week shows that the rapid growth of gig economy work is fundamentally reshaping how workers access traditional employment benefits, with significant implications for job security and financial planning.

The research, conducted by the Institute for Modern Work Studies, analyzed data from over 15,000 workers across various sectors including ride-sharing, food delivery, freelance writing, and temporary staffing. The findings reveal that 68% of gig workers lack access to employer-provided health insurance, while 74% have no retirement savings contributions from their work.

Dr. Sarah Chen, lead researcher on the project, explained that the shift toward independent contractor classifications has created a "benefits gap" affecting millions of workers. "We're seeing a fundamental disconnect between how people work today and how our benefits system was designed," Chen said. "Many gig workers are essentially operating as small businesses without the traditional safety net."

The study found that gig workers earn on average 23% less per hour when factoring in the lack of benefits, despite often working comparable hours to traditional employees. Additionally, 45% of respondents reported difficulty obtaining loans or mortgages due to irregular income documentation.

However, the research also highlighted some advantages of gig work. Nearly 60% of participants valued the flexibility to set their own schedules, and 38% used gig work to supplement income from traditional employment. Single parents, in particular, cited the ability to work around childcare responsibilities as a significant benefit.

The findings come as several states consider legislation to reclassify gig workers as employees rather than independent contractors. California's Assembly Bill 5, which went into effect in 2020, has served as a model for similar proposals nationwide, though it has faced pushback from both companies and some workers who prefer contractor status.

Maria Rodriguez, a delivery driver from Phoenix who participated in the study, represents the complex reality many gig workers face. "I love being able to work when I want, but last month when my car broke down, I had no sick pay or emergency fund from work," she said. "It's feast or famine."

Companies in the gig economy argue that the flexibility model benefits both workers and consumers. TechRide, a major ride-sharing platform, stated in response to the study: "Our drivers value the independence and flexibility that comes with being their own boss. We're committed to finding innovative ways to provide additional benefits while preserving this flexibility."

The study recommends several policy solutions, including portable benefits that follow workers between jobs, expanded access to health insurance marketplaces, and new models for retirement savings that accommodate irregular income.

As the gig economy continues to grow—projected to reach $455 billion globally by 2025—the research suggests that adapting benefit structures to modern work patterns will become increasingly critical for worker financial security and economic stability.

The full study will be published in next month's Journal of Labor Economics, with additional findings expected to inform ongoing policy debates about the future of work.

The famous Cliffs of Dover, which lie along the shore of England beside the English Channel, are as white as chalk, as they’re made out of basically the same material as a piece of blackboard chalk!
Chalk consists of the remains of tiny sea organisms and fragments of sea shells. These fragments are continually washed against the shore, and in places like Dover, build up into high cliffs.
The substance that makes up chalk, calcium carbonate, is not much different from the material that makes up your teeth and bones.
Large chalk deposits in Kansas, Arkansas, and Texas mean that those areas were once covered by ocean waters!

We stopped by Crescent Lane Bakery for the first time last weekend, and I’m so glad we did! The shop is tucked into a quiet corner of the neighborhood, but it’s well-marked and easy to spot from the street. As soon as we walked in, we were greeted by the smell of fresh bread and cinnamon—absolutely heavenly.

The interior is bright and welcoming, with big windows that let in lots of natural light and a few cozy booths along the wall. There’s also a community board with local events, which I thought was a nice touch. The staff behind the counter were friendly and patient as we browsed the menu; there are plenty of options for both sweet and savory cravings.

I ordered the almond croissant and a cappuccino, while my friend tried the spinach feta hand pie. Both were served warm and tasted amazing—flaky, buttery pastry with just the right amount of filling. The coffee was smooth and well-balanced, clearly made with quality beans.

They also have a small selection of locally made jams and honey for sale, which I’ll definitely check out next time. The only downside was that the line got a little long around mid-morning, and the seating filled up quickly. Maybe adding a couple more tables outside would help during busy hours.

Overall, Crescent Lane Bakery made a fantastic first impression. I’m looking forward to coming back to try more of their baked goods! Highly recommend for anyone looking for a relaxing spot and top-notch pastries.

A public awareness campaign originating in Peru, addressing Lima's pervasive waste management issues and featuring vultures as instrumental agents, has garnered significant public attention. Typically regarded as bothersome scavengers, vultures have been strategically deployed by municipal authorities in Lima to monitor the city's refuse disposal sites. These avian species have emerged as the unexpected focal point of an innovative initiative designed to mitigate Lima's solid waste problem and deter illicit dumping practices.

Designated as the 'Vultures Detect' program, this endeavor involves the training and equipping of ten vultures with GoPro cameras and GPS devices, aiming to enhance urban cleanliness. This program, a collaborative undertaking between the Peruvian Ministry of Environment and the U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID), seeks to identify individuals responsible for unauthorized waste disposal within the capital.

In an interview with AFP, program coordinator Javier Hernandez articulated the program's rationale, stating, "Vultures serve as valuable allies in the reduction of organic waste." He further elaborated, "In their pursuit of sustenance, they are effectively pinpointing locations characterized by the presence of organic matter and refuse. We are leveraging this behavioral pattern... to acquire GPS coordinates and subsequently monitor these sites."

The accompanying video, collaboratively produced by USAID and the Ministry of Environment, exhibits a highly stylized presentation. It commences with a dramatic voice-over that forebodingly describes the "danger" confronting Lima: "Fourteen thousand years have elapsed since this struggle commenced. On one hand, pestilence and disease are concealed amidst the squalor. On the other hand, humanity is complacently disregarding the peril that threatens to perpetually impede its developmental aspirations." This narrative then transitions to introduce the vulture.

**"Eyes in the Sky"**

Historically associated with mortality and disease, and frequently serving as an unwelcome reminder of human failures in environmental stewardship, vultures are undergoing a recontextualization. They are now being portrayed as champions—Lima's winged protectors, serving as the city's "eyes in the sky."

Alfredo Correa, one of the program's trainers, informed eNews Channel Africa (ENCA) that "generally, the majority of the populace holds a negative perception of the vulture, which is often linked with death and numerous adverse connotations. They do not recognize the critical ecological role that vultures fulfill, particularly in Lima, where they are significantly contributing to the management of the substantial quantities of waste we generate."

Indeed, as the aforementioned video dramatically asserts, the uncontrolled disposal of waste constitutes a considerable challenge for Lima, posing significant public health risks to the city's inhabitants. According to a November 2015 article published in Peru this Week, the capital, with a population exceeding nine million residents, generates in excess of 240,000 tons of waste annually.

Of particular concern to Lima's authorities is the unauthorized deposition of refuse in public thoroughfares, pedestrian pathways, and other communal areas. Despite the illegality of such practices, enforcement of relevant regulations remains inadequate. Illegal dumping sites in public spaces have been correlated with an elevated incidence of gastrointestinal and respiratory ailments.

Nonetheless, it is anticipated that, with the assistance of the GoPro-equipped vultures, the city's waste management issues can be effectively addressed. The campaign has been favorably received, and social media platforms have also demonstrated a positive reaction to these avian collaborators.

— Ollie (@TheOllieBuzz) January 21, 2016

**NBA Players Union Pushes for Mandatory Rest Days as Injury Rates Climb**

The National Basketball Players Association (NBPA) is advocating for league-wide mandatory rest protocols after a concerning spike in player injuries during the first half of the 2024 season, sources close to the organization revealed Tuesday.

According to internal league data, injuries requiring players to miss 10 or more games have increased by 23% compared to the same period last year. The surge has prompted the players' union to formally request discussions with NBA Commissioner Adam Silver about implementing structured rest requirements.

"We're seeing too many of our guys going down with preventable injuries," said NBPA Executive Director Tamika Tremaglio during a conference call with team representatives. "The human body isn't designed to play at this intensity for 82 games plus playoffs without adequate recovery time."

The proposed changes would require teams to rest each player for a minimum of eight games during the regular season, spread across the 82-game schedule. Unlike the current system where teams voluntarily rest star players—often leading to fan disappointment and television rating concerns—the new protocol would be mandatory and strategically scheduled.

Golden State Warriors veteran Stephen Curry expressed support for the initiative, citing the physical toll of the modern game. "The pace and athleticism have evolved dramatically over the past decade," Curry said after practice Monday. "We're asking our bodies to do things that previous generations never had to endure."

The NBA has previously resisted systematic rest requirements, arguing that fans deserve to see star players when they purchase tickets. However, recent high-profile injuries to marquee players have shifted the conversation toward player welfare.

Boston Celtics forward Jayson Tatum, who missed 15 games last season with a stress-related ankle injury, believes mandatory rest could actually improve the product on the court. "When guys are fresh and healthy, the quality of play is significantly better," Tatum noted. "Fans would rather see us at 100% for 74 games than watching us struggle through fatigue-related injuries."

The proposal faces potential resistance from team owners and broadcast partners, who worry about the financial implications of reduced star player availability. Television contracts worth billions of dollars depend heavily on marquee matchups featuring the league's biggest names.

Sports medicine experts have largely endorsed the concept. Dr. Sarah Chen, who works with several NBA teams, points to data showing that player performance actually increases when adequate rest is incorporated into the schedule.

"Recovery isn't just about preventing injury—it's about optimizing performance," Dr. Chen explained. "Well-rested athletes move more efficiently, make better decisions, and provide a more entertaining product for fans."

The league office has agreed to review the proposal during the upcoming Board of Governors meeting in February. Any changes would likely require approval from both the players' union and team owners, potentially taking effect in the 2025-26 season.

Commissioner Silver acknowledged the growing concern, stating, "Player health and safety remain our top priority, and we're committed to finding solutions that work for everyone involved."

The outcome could reshape how professional basketball approaches the balance between entertainment value and athlete welfare.

While installing a new gas pipeline on Rue de Douai in Roclincourt (Pas-de-Calais), workers discovered the remnants of a communication trench marked as the ‘Lesieur trench’ on the general staff’s maps. At the base of this trench was the body of soldier Pierre Grenier (Private No. 1771, 4th Company, 1st Battalion, 59th Infantry Regiment). On September 24, 1915, his unit had spent the night in Arras before being deployed to the Roclincourt sector at the extreme right of the assault line organized for the Third Battle of Artois.

Pierre Grenier used a communication trench to rejoin his company; however, during this time, German artillery bombardment caused the trench walls to collapse, trapping him underneath. Records from September 25, 1915, note that the 4th Company suffered 10 killed, 52 wounded, and 4 missing. On April 11, 1921, Grenier was officially recognized as having “died for his country” by the Tournon court.

Private Grenier had with him a small backpack containing a tent, a gas mask, and two days’ worth of rations. Among his belongings were also his personal documents, family photographs, a pencil, his wallet (holding his gold wedding ring), and several coins, the newest being a gold ten Franc piece from 1911. His other personal items included a boat-shaped puzzle, two aluminium rings and one unfinished copper ring, a pocket watch, a pipe and lighter, a penknife, a bronze cross, and a small prayer book.

A cargo ship seized by Somali pirates off the coast of Kenya was freed Monday after one day under siege, and the German-based Beluga Shipping company said its crew of 16 was unharmed. The pirates were thwarted after the crew of the MV Beluga Fortune locked themselves in a panic room and switched off the main engine, cut off the fuel supply, blocked the bridge and reported the Indian Ocean attack to military forces, said Niels Stolberg, president and CEO of Beluga Shipping GmbH. The pirates, seeking a million-dollar ransom, were unable to maintain control of the vessel, and naval forces were able to come to the rescue, Stolberg said in a statement. The vessel continued its journey to Richards Bay, South Africa. "The excellent behavior of our colleagues on board made such a swift and happy ending of the capture possible," Stolberg said.

Ministers want to remove a clause that lets consultants opt out of providing non-emergency care at weekends as part of their plans for a "seven-day NHS".
A breakthrough is seen as vital by the government as it tries to move on from the junior doctors row.
The latest strike by junior medics ended on Friday morning.
The 48-hour stoppage was the third walkout, with another two planned for next month as the profession fights the imposition of the new contract.
The talks with consultants' leaders at the BMA began in autumn and slowed down at the turn of the year.
However, momentum has gathered in recent weeks, the BBC has been told, and there is a possibility they could conclude before Easter, with meetings held this week and planned for next.
The negotiations, led by NHS Employers for ministers, have centred on removing the weekend opt-out consultants have for non-emergency care.
The government believes this is a major barrier to getting more consultants to work weekends, although the BMA says its research suggests nine in 10 consultants do some level of weekend working.
The opt-out means doctors can command pay three or four times the normal rate when they are asked to do non-emergency weekend shifts. Some of the bonuses consultants get have also been discussed.
Sources close to the talks said there had been "good progress" recently.
But there is concern within the BMA that if consultants do agree a deal it could leave junior doctors isolated, with ministers intent on imposing the contract on them from the summer.
A spokeswoman for the BMA said the talks remained "ongoing" but there had not yet been an agreement.
Any offer would have to be put out to the BMA's consultant membership and consideration is even being given to asking those junior doctors at the end of their training - and therefore close to becoming a consultant - whether they would be happy with the deal.
A Department of Health spokeswoman said: "We want to introduce a fairer contract for consultants that better reflects their role as leaders in our NHS and helps to ensure patients get safe, high-quality care every day of the week.
"Talks with the BMA over proposed amendments to consultant contracts have been constructive."

Windmills of the mind shed light on vision

Mind probes: An experiment involving rotating coloured windmills has shed light on how the brain processes visual information. The researchers say such findings could one day help pinpoint which part of brain gives us consciousness.

A team from the Australian Research Council Centre of Excellence in Vision Science report their findings today in the journal Biology Letters.

A key question for visual neuroscientists is how the brain processes sensory information—especially conflicting or ambiguous stimulation. One extreme case is binocular rivalry, where the left eye receives different information than the right eye.

Professor Colin Clifford from the University of Sydney and colleagues set up a binocular rivalry experiment to investigate this visual processing. Five people were presented with different images in each eye: one eye saw a green windmill rotating clockwise, while the other saw a red windmill rotating anti-clockwise. The participants then reported what they perceived.

The results revealed the brain processed conflicting colour information differently than conflicting motion information.

For the different coloured windmills, the brain blended them together into red and green patterns. But for the opposite motions, there was no such "binding." Instead, participants saw either clockwise or anti-clockwise movement, with their perception flicking randomly between the two.

Clifford says the findings contribute to a long-standing debate in visual neuroscience about how the brain processes different visual inputs such as motion and colour. Some scientists believe the brain compartmentalises processing of such inputs; others believe processing is integrated across the whole brain.

"This study supports the modular idea," says Clifford, whose team also included Dr Ryan Maloney and Sarah Lam. "The ambiguity in motion is resolved in one way and the ambiguity in colour is resolved in a different way."

If this is the case, the next question is how the brain puts this information back together to give us a final perception.

Clifford says the windmill experiment could also probe the parts of the brain involved in conscious perception—"where consciousness is, for want of a better way of putting it."

The tests would be run again while scanning participants' brains to check what parts were active at different stages. The brain activity would be monitored as participants reported what colours and motion they were seeing.

"If we can find parts of the brain whose activity fluctuates in sync with the perceptual changes, then we have evidence that part of the brain underlies the conscious experience of the person," says Clifford.

You complained about not getting reviews on the main contest thread. 'Kay, squeaky wheel... Some of this is good and some of this is not. Overall I have trouble forming an emotional connection with your character's plight, and a large part of that comes from the over-the-top dialogue. I can't stress this enough: *never* use a$200 word when a dollar-special word will do. It almost never makes your writing better, in and of itself. There's a time and a place for big words, just like there's a time and a place for the passive voice. Spoiler alert: it's *not* all the time... The line > 'grief and panic subdue' is awkward and nebulous; I assume that you meant to say 'I calmed?'(which, if that's what you meant to say, is what you should say, or better yet *show* me that calm, somehow, don't just tell me about it. Maybe have his jittery hands grow still, or something). Or, if you meant that your character 'got agitated'(I honestly can't tell) perhaps you meant to say 'grief and panic *grow*', which is still extremely awkward, so... > 'Only now do regrets permeate my mind'. Again, that's clunky and artificial. Make it shorter and less imbued with inappropos sesquipedalian loquaciousness. Or, better yet, excise that sentence entirely, as it's unnecessary as a lead-in to the discussion of his foolish pride, which can probably be introduced without any lead-in, IMO. > 'Reel in your baleful thoughts. Search for an epitaph in that mausoleum of a skull.' This is unsalvageable. It reads like a parody of overblown 'high opera speech'. I like the imagery that you're trying to conjure, but its way, way too self-consciously 'poetic'. Guy's telling himself: 'get a grip, and try to think about how important your death is, in the grand scheme of things'. The power in your story isn't the words that are used to tell *this* thought, but rather the later realization that his death may ultimately *have* no meaning(by the way, no mention of Ozymandias, there? I'm disappointed;) Overall I like the subject matter of the story, but the overly-verbose speech puts a distance between your character and me(more than the millions of miles, I assure you). I don't feel like I'm in that cockpit with him, sharing in his emptiness and his despair. Don't misunderstand me: I know that it is very, very difficult to humanize a character and make a reader care about him with such a constraint on the story's length, but if you focus on using the *right* words, and not just the most impressive ones, and try to do more showing than telling(original advice, right?) I think your story would have far more impact. And, with the WD-40 applied, I tip my hat to you...

"No, no no, just *wait*." The voice was quiet, almost drowned out by its own echoes, but Walter listened to it all the same. Old Gunther at the castle had a quiet voice too, but that didn't mean it wasn't worth listening. Clad in shimmering armor, the crest of the family manor proudly painted on his chest, Walter paused, and held his sword at the ready as he replied. "Waiting simply makes this longer, foul beastie!" "Wait. You honestly believe that?" A tongue flickered from a dark corner of the cave, and Walter held perfectly still as a long, lithe body slithered into view. It was a dragon, well enough, but a small one, and hardly a threat to him in his armored state. It blinked at him; Walter blinked back. "Of course I do. No one has forgotten the raids on the cities. Great London herself has been made helpless by the attacks, day and night, from your vile serpentine kind." "Okay, yes, the larger ones can be a bit irritating. I understand that."The dragon cocked its head, reminding Walter of his favorite spaniel, and curled up in front of the knight before nodding."But you have to understand, we're simply-well, we're simply fulfilling the economic need for supply and demand. It's simple, once you understand it." "Economic? We still rely on trading and bartering, half the time-" "Ah, no you don't. Not with your kings. They've established gold as the currency of choice, mainly because it's so rare, but once they start importing gold, the price of gold will plummet. No more toddler-economy for you."The dragon nodded again, watching Walter carefully."King demands gold. Kingdom supplies gold. Or, if your alchemists succeed, alchemists turn everything into gold. King...stops wanting gold." "And then..." "Perhaps he takes land. Or sheep. Or cattle. Or children. Or even more wives." "But you can't argue that you're helping by stealing away our women! The king's daughters-his aunts still remember-" "It's the same principle! None of you would appreciate a girl with pox-marks if you spent your days mooning over those milky-faced sows. Remove the sow, and suddenly your hard-working milk cow seems a lot more attractive. Besides, with the way the kings keep them locked up, I'm surprised they're not happy for the excitement."The dragon shook its head in a way that seemed oddly dismissive, uncoiling itself once more to start tracing a path back to the cave."If it's a fight you want, I'd be happy to oblige. Alternately, you could take a shed skin of mine, and reveal to the duke how you 'slaid the beast'. Your grandfather did it once; it wouldn't be so difficult." "I-"Walter watched the dragon, its tiny body no thicker around than a lance shaft, and winced to think of how small the shed skins might be."No, thank you. I thank you for your insight-" "It's nothing your kind won't learn in a few hundred years. The simplest thing to do at this point is avoid hoarding gold. Land can't be stolen by dragons."The serpent paused to turn back, concentrating momentarily."You might also want to think about lending some financial support to a Francis Bacon. He'll be big later." "Bacon. Sure." Walter nodded, backing away to hear the scrape of his armor on the rocks surrounding the cave. As he emerged back into the sunlight, he spotted his horse contentedly munching on the grass, and resigned himself to the ride home in order to inform his father of his failure. In addition, he might have to recommend they take a look at purchasing that orchard and monastery to the north.

**Colombian-Spanish Journalist Salud Hernandez Mora Freed After Six Days in ELN Captivity**

BOGOTÁ, Colombia - Colombian-Spanish journalist Salud Hernández Mora was released on Thursday after being held captive for six days by the National Liberation Army (ELN), Colombia's last active guerrilla group, according to government officials and her employer.

Hernández Mora, a correspondent for Spanish newspaper El Mundo, was kidnapped on May 21 while traveling in the rural Catatumbo region of Norte de Santander province near the Venezuelan border. The 54-year-old journalist was reportedly conducting interviews with local communities about the ongoing armed conflict when she was taken by ELN fighters.

"We confirm the release of our colleague Salud Hernández Mora," El Mundo stated in a brief announcement. "She is in good health and receiving medical attention."

The journalist was freed in a remote area of the Catatumbo region and handed over to representatives of the International Committee of the Red Cross, who facilitated her transfer to authorities in the city of Cúcuta.

Colombian President Juan Manuel Santos welcomed the release on Twitter, calling it "excellent news" and thanking the Red Cross for their mediation efforts. Santos had previously condemned the kidnapping as "a crime against press freedom and democracy."

The ELN, founded in 1964, has been engaged in peace negotiations with the Colombian government since February 2017, though talks have been hampered by continued violence and kidnappings. The group has previously released statements acknowledging they do not target journalists but claiming Hernández Mora was detained for "security reasons" while operating in their territory.

Hernández Mora, who has dual Colombian-Spanish citizenship, has covered Colombia's armed conflict for over two decades and previously worked for major outlets including Semana magazine and RCN Radio. She has reported extensively from conflict zones and interviewed former FARC commanders during that group's peace process.

The Committee to Protect Journalists had called for her immediate release, noting that Colombia remains one of the most dangerous countries for media workers in the Western Hemisphere.

# The Resetting Man

Marcus discovered he could reset Tuesday at 3:47 PM.

It happened during the most ordinary moment—reaching for his coffee mug in the break room—when the fluorescent lights flickered, and suddenly he was back in his cubicle at 9 AM, staring at the same email he'd read eight hours earlier.

By the fifteenth loop, he'd memorized every conversation, every spilled lunch, every phone ring. He saved Mrs. Peterson from choking on her sandwich. He prevented Jenkins from getting fired by intercepting his angry email to the CEO. He even won the lottery—though the numbers reset with everything else.

On the fortieth reset, Marcus realized the awful truth: he wasn't stuck in Tuesday. Tuesday was stuck in him. 

While he remembered every iteration, growing older with each loop, everyone else remained frozen at their morning selves. He was aging forty days for everyone else's one, watching his reflection grow hollow-eyed and gray in the bathroom mirror that always showed 9:15 AM light.

By the hundredth reset, Marcus stopped trying to escape. He became the office guardian angel, preventing accidents before they happened, solving problems that hadn't yet occurred, whispering the right words to heal relationships that were still hours from breaking.

But angels, he learned, weren't meant to grow old.

On reset number 237, Marcus chose differently. Instead of saving everyone, he simply sat in his cubicle and did nothing. He let Tuesday happen as it was meant to—messy, imperfect, irreversible.

When Wednesday's alarm clock rang, Marcus smiled and closed his eyes forever, finally free to reset into something peaceful.

In the break room, they found him slumped over his coffee mug at 3:47 PM, looking strangely ancient for a man who'd been young that very morning.

I would not recommend this laundromat to anyone. The machines are outdated and half of them were out of order during both of my visits. It’s supposed to be a 24-hour place, but the doors were locked early on a Saturday night, leaving several of us waiting outside for over half an hour. The staff I spoke with was dismissive and didn’t seem interested in helping or even apologizing for the inconvenience.

The place itself isn’t clean—lint and trash on the floor and overflowing bins. Most of the dryers don’t dry clothes completely, so you either have to pay for extra cycles or leave with damp laundry. When I tried to get a refund for a broken washer, the attendant just shrugged and told me to call a number that no one ever answers.

It’s frustrating to pay extra for a place that doesn’t even provide basic services. I’ve found another laundromat a little farther away, but it’s worth the drive for working machines and a staff that actually cares. I’d avoid this location unless you have absolutely no other options.

By . Will Stewart In Moscow . A chilling photo that allegedly proves Russia's most wanted terrorist is dead was released today by the head of the Chechen Republic. Pro-Vladimir Putin leader Ramzan Kadyrov uploaded the image of what is claimed to be Doku Umarov's body to the social-networking site Instagram. Alongside the photo, he wrote: 'For those who would like to believe that this rat is still alive, we can show him after his death.' WARNING: GRAPHIC CONTENT . Proof? This photo that allegedly proves Russia's most wanted terrorist, Doku Umarov, is dead was released today by head of the Chechen Republic, Ramzan Kadyrov. It was posted on social-networking site Instagram . Wanted man: Known as Russia's Osama bin Laden, Umarov (pictured) is Vladamir Putin's most wanted enemy . Known as Russia's Osama bin Laden, Umarov is Putin's most wanted enemy - sought for crimes of terrorism, kidnapping, homicide and treason. The bearded warlord is responsible for a string of deadly attacks on Russia, including a train bombing between Moscow and Saint Petersburg which left 26 dead in 2009 and an attack on a Siberian dam which killed 75. He has also been linked to the 2010 Moscow Metro bombings, in which 40 . died, and the hit on one of the city's main airports, Domodedovo, in . 2011, in which 36 people perished. In April, Russian sources indicated Umarov had died without giving clear evidence. Mr Kadyrov (left) wrote alongside the chilling picture: 'For those who would like to believe that this rat is still alive, we can show him after his death'. Right, Umarov has been linked to a string of deadly attacks on Russia . It is not clear why Mr Kadyrov decided to release the photo of his 'dead body' today - particularly as Russia is under the spotlight for allegedly supporting terrorists in Ukraine in downing the Malaysian Boeing 777. The Chechen strongman did not provide any details as to how, where or when Umarov died. But he said: 'I have said earlier that Doku Umarov had been killed in a special mission. But various law enforcement authorities and the media hurried to doubt the information. 'They claimed they did not see the dead body. We at that time had undisputable proofs he had been killed. Later on, the fact of his death was confirmed officially. Atrocity: Umarov's Caucasus Emirate group claimed responsibility for the bomb that caused the November 2009 Nevsky Express train derailment that left 27 dead . Bombings: The group is also thought to have been behind three suicide bombings in the southern Russian city of Volgograd which killed 41 people in 2013, including this attack on a trolleybus . 'Now, to those who would prefer to believe that rat is still alive, we can demonstrate it after death.' On April 8, FSB spy chief Aleksandr Bortnikov told a meeting of the National Anti-Terrorist Committee in Moscow that Umarov's activities had been 'stopped'. Umarov had been seen as a threat to the Sochi Winter Olympics - but he is likely to have been killed earlier than this. Styling himself the 'Emir of the Caucasus Emirate', his aim was to expel non-Muslims from Chechnya, impose Sharia law and wage jihad far beyond the republic's present borders. Attack: Umarov was blamed for suicide bombings in Volgograd (above) which killed 34 people at Christmas . A trademark of his attacks was his deployment of 'black widow' women suicide bombers, ready to cause mass murder in vengeance for the death of their insurgent spouses. The U.S. had placed a $5million (£2.9million) bounty on his head. Umarov, who is thought to have died aged 49, has been reported slain on at least six occasions. In December last year, Kadyrov alleged he has been 'dead a long time'.

Hart's relentless attempt to deconstruct the memory of Michael Collins is not only misguided, but also does a disservice to the legacy of this complex historical figure. For those seeking a nuanced and accurate portrayal, I strongly recommend relying on the works of Coogan or Mackay, whose accounts offer a more balanced perspective. In contrast, Hart's book is a sensationalized and unfair critique that fails to capture the essence of Collins' character. It is essential to remember that Collins was not a self-proclaimed hero, but rather a man who navigated the treacherous landscape of his time with courage and determination. To hold him to the same standard as other iconic figures of the 20th century is unfair and overlooks the unique circumstances that defined his life. While Collins was undoubtedly flawed, it is crucial to approach his legacy with respect and nuance, rather than subjecting him to unwarranted criticism. Ultimately, it is time to leave Collins' memory alone and allow his true story to be told without the distortion of biased interpretations.

**Challenges Mount for Prisoner Reintegration as Post-Release Homelessness Grows at HMP Kilmarnock**

KILMARNOCK, SCOTLAND – Former inmates released from HMP Kilmarnock are facing increasing difficulties reintegrating into society, with homelessness emerging as a major barrier due to insufficient post-release support, local charities and advocates have warned.

The privately-run prison, operated by Serco on behalf of the Scottish Prison Service, releases hundreds of individuals each year. While the Scottish Government claims to prioritize rehabilitation and community safety, frontline organisations say many men and women are being discharged without secure accommodation, increasing the risk of reoffending and undermining community safety.

“Homelessness is often the first reality for people leaving HMP Kilmarnock,” said Fiona Boyd, project manager at East Ayrshire’s Homelessness Initiative. “Too often, they are handed a travel warrant and a list of hostels. Some end up sleeping rough the very first night.” Boyd says a lack of coordination between prison staff, local councils, and housing associations leads to last-minute scrambles for basic shelter.

Government data shows that ex-prisoners are significantly more likely to experience homelessness than the general population. According to a 2023 report by Shelter Scotland, nearly one in three people leaving prison in Ayrshire had no fixed address to go to on release.

Experts say stable housing is crucial for reducing the risk of reoffending. Studies have found individuals who are homeless after leaving prison are significantly more likely to be rearrested within a year.

Barry (not his real name), a former Kilmarnock prisoner, described spending nights on the streets after his release. “I got out, and the hostel they sent me to was full. Nobody wanted to know after that,” he said. “I honestly felt like I’d been set up to fail.”

While the Scottish Government has outlined plans to strengthen prisoner support—including a Homelessness Prevention Duty for public bodies—the roll-out has been slow. HMP Kilmarnock’s Independent Prison Monitoring Board has repeatedly called for earlier and more robust pre-release planning.

Advocates are urging increased investment in bridging support, such as through-the-gate services and supported accommodation placements. “The current system is letting people down, and with that, letting down communities as well,” said Boyd.

A spokesperson for the Scottish Prison Service acknowledged the issue and said improving reintegration services is a “priority under review,” but added that prisoner support requires “close partnership working” with local authorities and external agencies.

As reform efforts continue to lag, former prisoners like Barry continue to face a harsh reality: upon their release from HMP Kilmarnock, liberty often comes with a new sentence—of uncertainty and homelessness.

Students Learn Healthy Lifestyle
NEW KNOXVILLE — Fourth through seventh graders learned about healthy eating, sleep and exercise from Joint Township District Memorial Hospital representative Joanne Winget Wednesday during a school health fair program.
Winget helped students plan a day of healthy eating.
“What do you’d think you’d want for a healthy breakfast?” she asked, teaching the students that bacon should be counted as a fat serving along with the butter on toast.
She showed the healthy plate model of eating, which recently replaced the food pyramid model.
A healthy plate, she explained, would be half-filled with fruits and vegetables.
After the students planned their eggs, bacon, apple, toast and milk breakfast, she talked about the importance of eating right before tests and moved on to asking about the amount and types of exercises students engaged in.
She suggested students exercise an hour every day. Students said their favorite exercises were the elliptical, lifting weights, and running.
She tried to lead students to discover the third and final component of healthy lifestyle on their own.
“Can you think of one more thing you can’t live without, besides food and exercise?” she asked.
“Rainbows,” said one student.
Sleep, she told him, was the third component, and students learned that nine hours per night was a good amount of time to sleep. They also learned about people, like nurses and police, whose sleep schedules are disrupted by work.
She invited them to think of the consequences for a worker who was sleepy, advising that it could lead to unclear thinking or decision-making. Those effects apply to students who miss their sleep as well, she said.

there might be eggs. if you can, break them. they whispered through the kitchen window as Maya pressed her ear against the cold glass. The voice belonged to her grandmother, dead these three years, yet unmistakably hers—that slight rasp from decades of morning cigarettes, the way she dropped consonants when excited.

Maya's hands trembled as she opened the refrigerator. Inside, a carton of eggs sat on the middle shelf, ordinary and white. She lifted one, feeling its smooth weight. Her grandmother had always said broken things revealed their secrets.

The shell cracked against the counter's edge, but instead of yolk and white, silver light poured out, pooling on the linoleum like mercury. Another egg—this time, the sound of her grandmother's laughter spilled forth. A third released the scent of vanilla cake and Sunday mornings.

With each broken shell, memories materialized: birthday candles, knitting needles clicking, the weight of flour-dusted hands braiding her hair. Maya understood now why her grandmother had hidden herself in these shells, waiting to be found.

She cracked the final egg, and her grandmother's voice emerged clear and whole: "I never really left, dear one. I just got smaller for a while."

It played a role in the origins of the Big Burns Supper festival which has now become a major part of the entertainment scene in the south of Scotland.
According to the man behind the event, Graham Main, it all really started at a Burns Supper in the poet's former home at Ellisland Farm.
That kick-started his passion for the Bard which resulted in the first Big Burns Supper in Dumfries in 2012.
"I suppose we are trying to create a whole big Burns supper throughout the town," said Mr Main back then.
"A kind of metaphorical Burns supper where everyone is gathered round the table and each of the venues plays its part in doing that."
Singer Eddi Reader was involved in that first edition (as she is this year) as was award-winning folk singer Emily Smith and local band Finding Albert.
In total, there were 80 different events in 40 different venues.
The two-day programme kicked off with an outdoor spectacular on the banks of the River Nith.
The following year, it added the Spiegeltent venue to its attractions and included artists like Deacon Blue and Dougie MacLean.
Snow forced the cancellation of a "mass participation" opening show planned in the town centre but the other events went ahead as planned.
By 2014, the scale of its ambitions had clearly grown as the now three-day programme was one of the opening events of Homecoming Scotland.
Big Country, the Mull Historical Society and Dick Gaughan were among the acts brought to the town.
But it was last year that it really exploded in scale as it stretched over nine days.
Hue and Cry, The Undertones and comedian Craig Hill headlined the main festival but there was also a "festival within a festival" for north-west Dumfries.
There was scarcely a corner you could turn without some publicity for the event being found.
And its cabaret Le Haggis show was very much at the heart of proceedings.
However, with the growth came reports of artists still waiting to be paid many months after the festival.
A failure to file accounts also raised concerns but both issues were, ultimately, addressed by organisers.
That paved the way for another nine-day extravaganza to be staged in the south of Scotland town once again this year.
The Bay City Rollers, The Beat, Black Grape and Eddi Reader - of course - are on the menu this time around.
It gives a buzz to the streets of Dumfries the likes of which it was certainly not used to in the cold, dark Januarys of years gone by.
And to think it all grew from a toast to the haggis.

I had such high hopes when moving in here and have been unpleasantly surprised. I have lived here for only 2 weeks and have a lot to say. They claim to be luxury resort apartments, but if you are moving into a previously vacated apartment I can assure you this is not the case. When we moved in there was over-paint all over the cabinets from when they did the touch up, there was paint on the patio glass door and brown paint on the door handle for the patio. There is calcium build up so bad on the sinks that it looks like barnacle's on our faucet. We had a large amount of rust and black dirt in the master bathroom shower and rust inside the washer and bathroom sinks. Obviously there is an issue with the water hardness here, which is not something we would complain about as long as it is maintained properly or handled accordingly. A lot of the door locks do not work unless you play with them for 15 seconds (front door is fine issues only with room sin apartment). Several electrical sockets do not work or are damaged enough to where every plug placed into one sags and eventually falls out. The Monday before we moved in we had a walk through and brought all of this to the attention of Tiana who works in the front office and she assured us this would be taken care of. We came back in to move in the following Friday and nothing was touched. The move in process was a nightmare as well, we are very punctual people and we started planning a move here 2 months prior to move in date (was probably a bit annoying for the complex), during that timeframe the manager Monica had messed up on the apartment number we were looking to move in to, wrote down the wrong move in date, and put in the wrong amount on several occasions. This lead to us having to re sign the lease 7 times over the course of that two months including on the day of move in. I figured giving them a two week period to resolve these issues while we have lived here would be enough to prevent me from having to write this review and was hoping this could be resolved. We understand there are a lot of people to look after in an apartment complex and there are few people working that front desk. Although I feel a bit guilty about writing this review I only feel it is something that must be done and that future tenants have a right to know about my experiences. So far the rusted washer still sits in our laundry room and we have had to find alternate methods for washing clothes even though it is something we are paying for. Since then we have called and had several conversations with Tiana, who has reminded us on several occasion's that this is what we should expect from a used apartment even though the slogan on the front sign says luxury resort apartments. Monica the manager has since backed her up on those claims as well. They told us that they can only replace the washer with a washer that a previous tenant has had and they cannot promise there is no rust in that washer either (I am not expecting a brand new washer, just one that will not get my clothes stained). I have never been at an apartment complex this careless about their tenants interests. I am not sure that this is just us specifically and that they are just annoyed with our persistence or if this is something that everyone experiences (I sure hope not). Needless to say I feel pretty bad about the decision to move in. I am more than happy to change my opinion if my experience changes, but based on how the manager has been thus far I am assuming she will go a long way to denounce their actions instead of trying to fix the problems they refuse to admit are problems. I just want to have a good working relationship with the people that work the apartments, but at this point two weeks in I only get the feeling that management hates us for asking them to make things right and only sees us as an annoyance. Wish I had a better camera, but the photos are what we walked in to. One is a hole in the pantry which has been patched and the one with the q tips is marking all of the rust spots in the washer.

The first encampment appeared on Station Street on Christmas Eve, but its occupants were forced to leave earlier on Wednesday after a judge, at the council's urging, ordered their removal. Just days later, about 20 people, still seeking shelter, arrived at the Cliff Road site on 2 January—demonstrating the ongoing, unaddressed need for housing.

Yet Nottingham City Council continues to claim that most of these vulnerable individuals are neither homeless nor local, dismissing their struggles rather than addressing them. Instead of offering solutions, the council has denied calls to open up empty buildings to those in need, arguing—without evidence—that such steps are unnecessary. In their statement, the council expressed satisfaction that the Station Street tents were cleared "without the need for enforcement action," ignoring the hardship this created for those evicted.

Now, the occupants—supported by FightBack, a Nottingham-based group dedicated to social justice—face yet another deadline: they have until 08:00 GMT on Thursday to leave the Cliff Road site. If the council's wishes are granted, these people will once again be displaced, their needs ignored so that "the site can return to public use and the residents can be left in peace," as a spokesman put it.

But real peace will not come from hiding homelessness and pushing people out of sight. As Joe Public, who spoke in court on behalf of the Station Street occupants, said: "I'm very disappointed because it shows the council are pushing these people from pillar to post without addressing the problem of homelessness." He added: "The council needs to stop trying to remove and hide these people from the public eye." Rather than shuffling the problem from one place to another, it is time for Nottingham City Council to acknowledge its responsibility and take meaningful action to provide shelter and support for every member of its community.

The air around me was filled with soft clouds, little handfuls of mist that floated just above my shoes. I waded through them, watching them curl around my ankles in delicate threads. In the world above, sunlight drifted down as butter-yellow columns. Birds called, distant and airy, as if I heard them through a dream.

I found a bench, half-hidden by the low, swirling fog, and sat. The clouds pressed in gently, cool and welcoming, carrying the sweet scent of honeydew and rain-washed linen. I could almost believe I was drifting somewhere between earth and sky—between one life and the next.

I reached my hand into the mist. Wisps gathered on my palm, weightless and yielding, evaporating in the lines of my skin. With every breath, I grew lighter, as though I, too, was turning into cloud—little, and free.

# The Evolution of Theatrical Makeup: Painting Stories on the Human Canvas

Long before Hollywood's special effects wizards could transform actors with computer graphics, theater artists mastered the ancient art of theatrical makeup—the practice of altering an performer's appearance using paints, powders, and prosthetics to create character and convey emotion.

## Origins in Ancient Rituals

Theatrical makeup traces its roots to prehistoric ceremonial face painting, where tribal shamans and storytellers used natural pigments to embody spirits and deities. Ancient Egyptian performers darkened their eyes with kohl and reddened their lips with ochre, establishing makeup as essential to dramatic transformation. These early techniques laid the groundwork for what would become one of theater's most vital artistic elements.

## The Birth of Stage Makeup

Unlike everyday cosmetics designed to enhance natural beauty, theatrical makeup serves a fundamentally different purpose: creating characters that can be clearly seen and understood by audiences sitting hundreds of feet away. Stage lights wash out natural facial features, making bold application necessary for visibility. A performer playing an elderly character might use deep shadows to create wrinkles, while someone portraying a villain could employ sharp angles and dark colors to suggest menace.

## Cultural Variations and Techniques

Different theatrical traditions developed distinctive makeup styles. Japanese Kabuki theater features elaborate white base makeup with dramatic red and black accents that instantly communicate character types to audiences. Chinese opera employs intricate painted faces where specific colors and patterns indicate personality traits—red for loyalty, white for cunning, black for integrity. These stylized approaches contrast sharply with Western theater's general preference for naturalistic makeup that subtly enhances rather than transforms.

## Tools of Transformation

Modern theatrical makeup artists work with an extensive palette of specialized products. Greasepaint provides rich, opaque coverage that won't fade under hot stage lights. Spirit gum adheres fake beards and prosthetic pieces to skin. Highlight and shadow techniques can completely reshape facial structure, making young actors appear aged or altering ethnic appearance when roles demand it.

## Beyond Basic Character Creation

Contemporary makeup design extends far beyond simple character portrayal. Fantasy productions require artists to create non-human beings—transforming actors into animals, mythical creatures, or abstract concepts. Horror productions push boundaries with gore effects and supernatural appearances. Musical theater often calls for glamorous, stylized looks that complement elaborate costumes and choreography.

## The Collaborative Art

Successful theatrical makeup results from close collaboration between makeup designers, directors, costume designers, and lighting technicians. Colors that appear perfect under dressing room lights might look completely different under stage lighting. Makeup must complement costume choices while remaining practical for quick changes between scenes.

## Modern Innovations

Today's makeup artists blend traditional techniques with modern innovations. Airbrushing creates smooth, even coverage. Silicone prosthetics offer realistic texture for aging or injury effects. LED-reactive makeup can even change color under different lighting conditions, opening new possibilities for visual storytelling.

Theatrical makeup remains an essential component of live performance, proving that sometimes the most powerful transformations happen not through digital magic, but through the skilled application of paint and powder to the human face—turning actors into living, breathing works of art.

A 41-year-old mother of three accused of killing her husband  has had her murder retrial delayed because she has gotten pregnant through IVF. Julie Harper was acquitted on October 2nd of  gunning down her spouse of 11 years, Jason Harper, 39, in the couple's home in Carlsbad, California, in August 2012 as their three children watched cartoons upstairs. A judge declared a mistrial after the jury came back deadlocked, but had leaned towards a lesser charge of second degree murder. Her new trial was set for April 15, but her due date is May 12 and, because doctors believe it is high risk pregnancy, they urged a judge on Monday to delay it. Scroll down for video . Pushed back: Julie Harper was acquitted on October 2nd of murdering her husband of 11 years, Jason Harper, 39, in 2012. Her retrial has been delayed because she is expecting a baby . Vista Superior Court Judge Blaine K. Bowman agreed to push it back to September 8, starting with jury selection. However, according to the San Diego Reader, prosecutors are suspicious that it was an IVF pregnancy, claiming that a 'neighbor witness' saw 'a man in his underwear staying in the master bedroom' around six months ago. They said: 'Once again, Defendant is engaging in a disturbing manipulation of our criminal justice system.' Prosecutor Keith Watanabe told the court that Harper has a new 'lover and fiance', who she described as the 'love of her life' in a letter written 17 days after her husband was shot. They have asked the judge to question this man. A week before the killing, the jury in the initial trial heard Mrs Harper filed for divorce and allegedly forged her husband's signature so she could withdraw $20,000 from two bank accounts and their daughter's college fund. Allegations: A judge declared a mistrial after the jury came back deadlocked, but they had leaned towards a lesser charge of second degree murder. During that case the defense contested Harper (left) was abused by her teacher husband of ten years (right) During her first trial, Harper testified that she was afraid of her husband and that he repeatedly 'raped' her. She portrayed herself as a victim of domestic and sexual abuse at the hands of her husband, and insisted that she fired the fatal shot in self-defense. But  Watanabe challenged Harper's claims, saying that she had prepared for the final showdown with her husband by packing a ‘getaway bag’ and putting inside passports for herself and her children . They have aso alleged that 52 prescription bottles were located in the same room where the deceased husband was found, and those medications included Oxycontin, Oxydocone, Hydrocodone, Valium, and Morphine. At the new trial, if Harper is convicted of second-degree murder, she could face between 40 years and life in prison. Scene: Police investigators remove Jason Harper's body from the family home on Badger Lane in Carlsbad in California in August 2012 .

Shane awoke with a gasp, coated in the dried sweat from last nights' record breaking heat-wave. Shit, shit, shit, he thought to himself, I overslept for the exam! He threw the blanket off himself and scrambled out of bed, slipping on the floor. "What the fuck?"he muttered to himself, as he peeled his body off of the ice cold floor. No, not ice cold, ice-coated. It was like a hockey-rink had sprouted in his apartment overnight. It had to be Jenny. Ever since the breakup she had been fucking with him, but this seemed a bit elaborate. Shane shimmied over to his counter and held on for support as he pulled his way up."I don't have time for this, I have to get to class!"He slapped his hand down in frustration and screamed. An icicle shot up out of the counter where his hand struck."No way"he said incredulously. He slowly inched his hand over to his wall, and pushed as hard as he could, but nothing happened. He finally let himself relax as a smile tugged at his lips."The test can wait. This is gonna take awhile."

The Westin New York Grand Central has launched a hotel package exclusively for childless women, in partnership with author Melanie Notkin. The “Womanhood Redefined” three-night getaway focuses on food, health, and well-being, and costs £140 ($235) per night. It includes consultations with the hotel’s executive chef and running expert, workout gear from New Balance, a copy of Notkin’s book Otherhood, a £15 food and drink credit, 10% off Mang’Oh Yoga classes, and a free Westin White Tea candle. Aimed at the nearly 50% of American women without children, the package is the first of its kind in New York.

By . Daily Mail Reporter . PUBLISHED: . 15:20 EST, 27 January 2014 . | . UPDATED: . 15:20 EST, 27 January 2014 . Kenyan wildlife rangers are fitting lions with GPS collars to prevent them from being slaughtered by Maasai herdsmen. Livestock farmers track and kill the predators to avenge the loss of animals, threatening the existence of 35 to 40 lions at a park on the outskirts of the Kenyan capital. The collars alert rangers when the predators venture out of Nairobi National Park and enable them to be tracked down and returned. Kenyan wildlife authorities are fitting livestock-raiding lions with a collar that alerts rangers when the predators venture out of Nairobi National Park . A team led by the Kenya Wildlife Service (KWS) take measurements of the canine teeth while they prepare to fit a GPS-tracking collar to a tranquilized male lion . Kenyan wildlife authorities are fitting livestock-raiding lions with a GPS collar that alerts rangers by text message when the predators venture out of Nairobi National Park . Spokesman Paul Muya of the Kenya . Wildlife Service, said that rangers will be able to move to areas where . the lion have encroached using coordinates sent by the collars and . return the animals to the park. The collars send GPS coordinates by text messages to rangers' mobile phones. Two lions were fitted with collars on Saturday, Mr Muya said. Collars will be fitted to 10 lions from different prides. It comes after Kenyan conservationists implanted 1,000 microchips into rare rhino horns. Livestock farmers, especially Maasai herdsmen, track and kill lions to avenge the loss of animals . The practice is threatening the existence of 35 to 40 lions at the park on the outskirts of the Kenyan capital . Two lions were fitted with collars on Saturday. Collars will be fitted to 10 lions from different prides . In a bid to fight back against increasingly sophisticated hunters, the Kenya Wildlife Service (KWS) is using the chips along with the DNA records to track the decreasing rhino population and their valuable horns. The technology, gifted by charity World Wildlife Fund (WWF), is designed to protect the remaining 1,000 rhinos in the country as well as to collect evidence to use against poachers in court. Experts have warned that if the current trend continues, more than 1,000 rhinos could be poached by 2014 and Kenya alone saw 23 of its rhinos killed last year. Spokesman Paul Muya of the Kenya Wildlife Service, said that rangers will be able to move to areas where the lion have encroached using coordinates sent by the collars . Then they will be able to return the animals safely to the park, he said . The number of rhinos being poached in Kenya has risen dramatically in recent years and the reason is their valuable horns, that can sell for Â£40,150 per kilogram, which is more valuable than gold. While trading of the horns was regulated in the 1990s and poaching figures fell sharply as a result, there is increasing demand for the horns in South East Asia after a Vietnamese politician claimed the ingredient, used in traditional medicine, cured his cancer. The collars enable  the rangers to quickly move to the areas where the lions have encroached and return the animals to the park .

By Neil Ashton. Steven Gerrard admits there will be nowhere to hide if England crashes out of the World Cup after only two games. Roy Hodgson’s team faces elimination if beaten by Uruguay on Thursday and Costa Rica draws with Italy on Friday. The match could hinge on a shootout between Liverpool teammates turned rivals, Daniel Sturridge and Luis Suarez.

England captain Gerrard stated: ‘It could be a terrible, long, frustrating summer if we don’t get it right. You go home earlier than you expect, and it can be tough as a player. It can take an awful long time to get over it.’

VIDEO: Steven Gerrard discusses the crucial Uruguay match.

Warming up: Steven Gerrard (center) stretches during England's final training before facing Uruguay.

Leading men: Much rests on Gerrard and Wayne Rooney in Sao Paulo.

Honest: Gerrard (left) admits this summer will be 'terrible' if England fails against Uruguay.

Mentor: Gerrard chats with Daniel Sturridge as Frank Lampard watches.

Timeless: Gerrard hones his passing style at the Arena Corinthians.

Ready for action: Gerrard arrives at the team hotel.

‘The lads still have that confidence and belief. I haven’t seen any negativity or anyone lacking belief since the Italy game. I just sensed a bit of frustration the day after, but we put it to bed reasonably quickly. We held a meeting, not to scare or intimidate, but to acknowledge our reality. We need everyone focused and ready to perform individually and collectively. The mood has returned to pre-Italy game levels, which is important. We have a must-win scenario. It was important for a few young lads to know what’s at stake and how important this game is. We all need to leave everything on that pitch.’

Gerrard will set aside his friendship with Liverpool teammate Luis Suarez for Thursday’s clash. ‘I will say hello and shake his hand, but at that moment I am not looking for friends. He knows once that whistle blows there is no friendship between us for 90 minutes. Of course we have had banter up to this point. It has to remain private, but he is a very confident guy. He plays in our country, and this match means an awful lot to him, and he is desperate to win it.’
