Gerrard added: ‘They are in the same situation as us, maybe a worse situation. It is set up perfectly for a fantastic match. There is a lot at stake.’

Print version ISSN 0366-6913
VARELA, M. L. et al. Influence of kaolin waste addition on technological properties of a standard stoneware formulation produced in industrial scale. Cerâmica [online]. 2009, vol.55, n.334, pp. 209-215. ISSN 0366-6913. http://dx.doi.org/10.1590/S0366-69132009000200015.
There is a great concern by the industrial area on the disposal of waste generated during the production process. In the mineral sector the situation is the same, because large volumes of waste are disposed. The kaolin processing industry generates waste consisting mainly of kaolinite, mica and quartz muscovite, causing an environmental impact, justifying the research efforts to find a solution for this problem. The effect of the waste from the kaolin processing incorporation on the technological and physical properties of high quality tiles was here studied. This work aims to characterize and to study the processing of the kaolin waste from the industry of kaolin processing by adding of a "standard mass" supplied by an industry of ceramic tiles. The waste was characterized by X-ray diffraction, X-ray fluorescence and determination of particle size. Water absorption, apparent porosity, linear firing after sintering, apparent density and bending modulus of rupture analyses were carried out to determine the materials technological properties. Preliminary results show that the studied waste can be considered as a potential raw material for the industry of floor and ceramic tile.
Keywords : kaolin waste; stoneware; ceramic tile.

**Review: "C# Programming for Absolute Beginners" by RadTech Publications**

★★☆☆☆ (2/5 stars)

I picked up this C# programming guide hoping to finally make the transition from Java to C#, given that the book promised to cater to both complete newcomers and developers with experience in other languages. Unfortunately, after working through about 60% of the content, I can't recommend this book to either audience.

**The Good:**
The book does have some redeeming qualities. The code examples are generally well-formatted and easy to read, and the authors do a decent job explaining object-oriented concepts in the early chapters. I appreciated the inclusion of practical exercises at the end of each chapter, even though some of them felt disconnected from the main content. The coverage of basic syntax and data types is solid enough for someone who has never programmed before.

**The Problems:**
My main frustration is with the book's inconsistent pacing and poor organization. The authors spend three full chapters on basic console output and variables, then suddenly jump into advanced topics like delegates and events without adequate preparation. Important concepts like exception handling are crammed into a single chapter near the end, while less critical topics like formatting strings get excessive attention early on.

For experienced programmers, the book is painfully slow in some sections while being inadequately detailed in others. The authors assume no prior knowledge when explaining loops and conditionals, but then gloss over crucial differences between C# and other languages that would actually help someone making a transition.

The biggest oversight is the treatment of modern C# features. This book was published in 2022, yet it barely mentions nullable reference types, pattern matching, or async/await programming. These aren't advanced features anymore—they're essential parts of contemporary C# development that any learning resource should cover thoroughly.

**Technical Issues:**
I found several code examples that wouldn't compile without modifications, and the debugging section is woefully inadequate. In one particularly frustrating instance, a LINQ example in Chapter 12 uses syntax that wasn't properly introduced until Chapter 15. The index is also poorly constructed, making it difficult to find specific topics when you need to reference them later.

**Bottom Line:**
While RadTech Publications has produced some excellent programming books in the past, this one feels rushed and poorly planned. Beginners would be better served by online tutorials or interactive coding platforms that provide immediate feedback. Experienced developers looking to learn C# should consider more focused resources that respect their existing knowledge while highlighting what makes C# unique.

The book isn't completely without merit, but for the price point, there are simply better alternatives available. I'd suggest looking elsewhere unless you can find this heavily discounted and plan to supplement it with other learning materials.

t want to answer, but I couldn’t let it go to voicemail either. Not this time.

My fingers trembled as I pressed ‘accept.’ The hallway outside the exam room was eerily silent, my own ragged breath the only sound. For a moment, all I heard was static, then his voice—thin, exhausted, but undeniably his.

“Hey,” he said. It sounded like a word dragged out from the bottom of a well.

“Hey,” I replied, trying to keep my voice steady. “Are you okay?”

A pause. Then a shuddering sigh. “I didn’t know who else to call. I’m—” His voice cracked. “I’m sitting in the park. I don’t know what to do.”

I could picture him there, hunched on a splintered bench, hands shaking, hoodie pulled up. The image made my throat tight. I glanced through the window at the gray clouds gathering over the campus, and wondered if it was raining where he was.

“Do you want me to come get you?” I asked quietly. “Or just talk?”

He laughed, sharp and bitter. “I don’t want you to see me like this. I…I just needed to hear someone who remembers who I used to be.”

I sank onto the cold tile floor, my forgotten exam a distant memory. “I do. I remember you teaching me to ride a bike. I remember you sneaking extra birthday candles onto my cake every year. I remember you laughing so hard at that dumb cartoon, you nearly choked on your cereal.”

He was silent for a long time. Then a sniffle. “Do you think I can get back to that? Or am I just…lost?”

I closed my eyes, willing my own tears not to fall. “You’re not lost. You’re just…wandering. And I’ll keep answering, every time you call. I promise.”

He let out another breath, and something in it sounded like hope.

“Thanks,” he whispered.

When the call ended, I sat there for a while, clutching the phone like a lifeline. The exam was over. Maybe I’d fail. But some things were more important than grades. 

Sometimes, the right answer wasn’t on the test—it was picking up the call, no matter how scared you were of what you’d hear.

## Echoes of the Canvas: A Deep Dive into "Chromatic Rhapsody"

As someone who’s always appreciated the raw power and ephemeral beauty of street art, yet lacked the physical dexterity (and, let’s be honest, the guts) to truly participate, "Chromatic Rhapsody" has been a revelation. My partner, bless her insightful soul, surprised me with it last week, and I’ve been utterly consumed ever since. Forget those tired old "graffiti simulators" that simply let you spray predefined stencils; "Chromatic Rhapsody" is a masterclass in digital artistry, a vibrant, pulsating ode to the urban canvas.

The tagline on the box declares it "the digital atelier for the modern muralist," and it’s not an exaggeration. This isn't about tagging walls; it's about *composing* them. The depth of customization is simply staggering. You're not just choosing colors; you're mixing pigments, adjusting opacity, experimenting with different nozzle types that genuinely mimic the feel of various spray cans, from the broad, sweeping strokes of a fat cap to the intricate detail of a needle cap. The texture engine is mind-blowing – you can simulate the gritty brick of an old warehouse, the smooth, reflective surface of a train car, or the weathered concrete of a bridge underpass, and each surface reacts differently to your digital paint. The way the light catches a freshly "painted" surface, showing subtle drips and overspray, is a testament to the developers' dedication.

But "Chromatic Rhapsody" isn't just a technical marvel; it's an immersive experience. The "urban exploration" mode is where the magic truly unfolds. You're not just dropped into a generic city; you're navigating meticulously rendered, procedurally generated urban landscapes that feel alive. From overlooked alleyways brimming with forgotten textures to sprawling industrial complexes begging for a splash of color, each environment feels like a blank page waiting for your story. The satisfaction of discovering a perfectly angled wall, bathed in the golden hour light, and then spending an hour meticulously crafting a piece that feels *right* for that space, is incredibly rewarding.

And then there's the soundtrack. Oh, the soundtrack! It's a carefully curated tapestry of ambient electronica, soulful lo-fi beats, and experimental soundscapes that perfectly complement the creative process. It’s not just background noise; it's an integral part of the artistic flow. Tracks like "Neon Dreams" by Synaptic Shift gently guide your hand, while the more energetic "Urban Pulse" by Concrete Symphony pushes you to experiment with bolder strokes. It’s the kind of music that encourages introspection and inspiration, never distracting, always enhancing.

I've barely scratched the surface of what "Chromatic Rhapsody" offers. There's a thriving online community where artists share their creations, critique each other's work, and even participate in collaborative "murals." The ability to "layer" your art with others, creating dynamic, evolving pieces over time, is a truly innovative feature.

This isn't a game for those seeking instant gratification or competitive thrills. This is a meditative, artistic journey. It’s for anyone who's ever looked at a blank wall and seen a canvas, for anyone who appreciates the beauty of urban decay and the transformative power of art. "Chromatic Rhapsody" isn't just a product; it's an experience, a digital sanctuary for the creative spirit. A resounding five stars.

How to Calculate Parameters and Estimators
In econometrics, when you collect a random sample of data and calculate a statistic with that data, you're producing a point estimate, which is a single estimate of a population parameter.
Descriptive statistics are measurements that can be used to summarize your sample data and, subsequently, make predictions about your population of interest. When descriptive measures are calculated using population data, those values are called parameters. When you calculate descriptive measures using sample data, the values are called estimators (or statistics).
You could estimate many population parameters with sample data, but here you calculate the most popular statistics: mean, variance, standard deviation, covariance, and correlation. The following list indicates how each parameter and its corresponding estimator is calculated.
Mean (average): The mean is the simple average of the random variable, X. The population mean for X is
where Xi represents the individual measurements and N is the size of the population. The sample mean is
The difference between the sample and population mean is that that the sample mean uses the sample size n instead of the population size N.
Variance: The variance is the average of the squared differences from the mean. The population variance for a random variable X is
where Xi represents the individual measurements,
is the population mean, and N is the size of the population. The sample variance is
Note that the denominator for the sample variance not only uses the sample size n but also subtracts 1 from that number. This change is known as a degrees of freedom adjustment. Degrees of freedom adjustments are usually important in proving that estimators are unbiased.
Standard deviation: The standard deviation measures how spread out the random variable is, on average, from the mean. The standard deviation is the square root of the variance, so the population standard deviation for random variable X is
and the sample standard deviation is
Covariance: The covariance measures how much two random variables change together. The population covariance between two random variables X and Y is
where Xi represents the individual X values, Yi represents the individual Y values, and N is the total number of measurements in the population. The sample covariance is
is the sample mean of X,
is the sample mean of Y, and n is the sample size.
Correlation: The correlation refers to the relationship between two random variables or sets of data. The population correlation coefficient between two random variables X and Y is
is the population covariance,
is the population standard deviation of X, and
is the population standard deviation of Y. The sample correlation coefficient is
where sXY is the sample covariance, sX is the sample standard deviation of X, and sY is the sample standard deviation of Y.
Now, try working with some numbers. The table shows five observations of coffee sales and prices. Use the formulas to calculate the mean, variance, standard deviation, covariance, and correlation.
|Coffee Sales (in units), Y||Coffee Price (in local currency), X|
You can use computer software, such as R, Python, or STATA, to calculate descriptive statistics from the data. In R, you can use functions like mean(), var(), sd(), cov(), and cor(). In Python, you can use libraries like pandas or numpy. In STATA, by typing "sum" on the command line, you get the descriptive statistics for all the variables in your dataset. If you want the correlation between two variables, you can select Statistics→Summaries, tables, and tests→Summary and descriptive statistics→Correlations and covariances from the menu bar.
Or you can enter "corr variable1 variable2" on the command line. In your command, replace variable1 and variable2 with the actual names you've given the variables in your dataset. You can get covariance by adding an option to the correlation command; type "corr variable1 variable2, cov" on the command line.
You should verify that your manual calculations of these measures are consistent with your software's output.
Summarizing data with descriptive statistics is a relatively simple procedure, but make sure you examine the values carefully. You can use descriptive measures to ensure that your sample contains measurements that are realistic. For example, if your population of interest is university graduates, you wouldn't expect your random sample from that group to have an average age of 21.
Careful attention to these details provides more credibility in your data and the subsequent inferences you make.

Color-enhanced scanning electron micrograph showing Salmonella typhimurium (red) invading cultured human cells.
Salmonella is actually a group of bacteria that can cause diarrheal illness in humans. They are microscopic living creatures that pass from the feces of people or animals to other people or other animals. There are many different kinds of Salmonella bacteria. Salmonella serotype Typhimurium and Salmonella serotype Enteritidis are the most common in the United States. Salmonella germs have been known to cause illness for over 100 years. They were discovered by an American scientist named Salmon, for whom they are named.
Content Credit: Bette Jensen, CDC
Photo Credit: Rocky Mountain Laboratories, NIAID, NIH

Seasonal flu vaccine only 12 percent effective in adults – CDC

The CDC states in its weekly Morbidity and Mortality Report, released Thursday, that: "Overall, the estimate suggests that the 2014-15 influenza vaccine has low effectiveness against circulating influenza A (H3N2) viruses."

The report explained that the seasonal flu shot offered less protection because of the widely varied nature of the flu viruses in the US. Seventy percent of flu viruses were a different strain or had "drifted" from the virus used to produce the vaccination. "More than two-thirds of circulating A (H3N2) viruses are antigenically and genetically different (drifted) from the A (H3N2) vaccine component of 2014–15 Northern Hemisphere seasonal influenza vaccines," the report said. The CDC conceded that: "Flu viruses change constantly and the drifted H3N2 viruses did not appear until after the vaccine composition for the Northern Hemisphere had been chosen."

The news somewhat undermines the CDC drive to encourage the public to get vaccinated against the flu. However, in a release published on the CDC website, it still professed the importance of getting a flu vaccine, saying it "reduced a person's risk of having to go to the doctor because of flu by 23 percent among people of all ages." Those who benefited most strongly from receiving the vaccination were children below the age of 17. In this group it was 26 percent effective.

Given these limitations, on Thursday, the CDC recommended that additional flu treatment measures be prepared, particularly with regards to more vulnerable patients. "All hospitalized patients and all outpatients at high risk for serious complications should be treated as soon as possible with one of three available influenza antiviral medications if influenza is suspected, regardless of a patient's vaccination status," The Hill reported Joe Bresee, branch chief in the CDC's flu division, as saying.

Among high-risk groups are children younger than five years old, "but especially children younger than two years old," adults 65 years of age and older, pregnant women, American Indians and Alaskan Natives, and people who have preexisting medical conditions such as heart disease, asthma or a weakened immune system.

The old woman’s hands rested like two pale moths on the tabletop. Her voice carried the scent of dust and the hush of years uncounted. “If you answer yes, you will lose the thing—and so will the one you despise. We trade and give, until there is naught. Are you ready?” 

I nodded, feeling the storm inside my chest.

“Would you give up your home?”

“Yes,” I said, and the walls around me folded away like paper. Somewhere, far away, I thought I heard doors slamming closed forever.

“Would you surrender your name?”

“Yes.” With a shudder, the sound of my own voice became a stranger to me—a hollow echo with no owner. The world seemed less sharp, less sure.

“And your hopes?” She tapped a dry finger on the table, pity in her gaze.

I clenched empty fists. “Yes.” I felt a weight lifted from my heart, but the space left behind was chill and gaping, an endless night where nothing new could ever grow.

She smiled: not kindly, not cruelly, but as if understanding. “Your good memories?”

A tremor ran through me. I saw children’s laughter, soft embraces, birthdays, and lost them all to the void behind her eyes. I nodded, unable to say yes but knowing I had agreed. The person I hated—were they feeling these voids, too? Did they know where the emptiness came from?

She leaned in, shadows deepening over her wrinkled face. “Would you give up the last of your fears?”

I thought of the one I despised, all sharp angles and cutting words, shaped from years of pain and rivalry. I wondered if they sat now, wherever they were, emptied out by the same relentless bargain. Would we meet in whatever lay beyond, both hollowed out, all our hate and history bled into the ether?

“Yes,” I whispered, the ash of a word on my dry tongue. I felt a fierce, alien peace—no anxieties, no dread, not even hope. A true emptiness, silent and vast.

At last, the old woman—who might have been fate or memory or some secret part of myself—asked, “Will you give up your hate?”

My pulse fluttered, and I realized with a shock: I no longer remembered why I carried the grudge, or even the shape of the one I resented. My hate, drained by all that had come before, was no more than dust on the table between us.

“Yes,” I said, barely a shadow of sound.

We sat together in silence, two vessels emptied out by too many bargains, with nothing left to give—and nothing left to regret.

A family day at Wissahickon Creek in Roxborough, Pennsylvania, ended in tragedy on Friday afternoon when 41-year-old Pete Luciano and his 13-year-old son, Jordan, drowned.

Pete had taken his four children to the park. Just before 3 p.m., Jordan waded into the creek and began to struggle. His father immediately jumped in to save him. Luciano's three other children watched in horror from the creek banks as their father and brother struggled. They quickly ran to the nearby Valley Green Inn for help, WPVI reports.

Due to spotty cell phone service in the area, the inn used a landline to contact emergency services. By the time police arrived, it was too late. Both bodies were recovered from an area of the creek known as 'Devil's Pool' around 4 p.m., according to NBC Philadelphia.

Michelle Luciano, a mother of eight from Philadelphia's Hunting Park neighborhood, was separated from Pete but spoke movingly of him after the tragedy. 'I will always have the deepest love for him [and] respect to know that he risked his life to save our child,' she told WPVI. She added that it was her son, Justin, who, after attempting to save his family members, gathered his two siblings and ran for help. 'I know he's hurting but Justin has this tough interior,' Luciano said.

Warning signs, though often ignored, are posted near 'Devil's Pool' due to the immediate drop in shallow waters. Swimming in this particular area of Wissahickon Creek is illegal. As Sullivan told CBS Philadelphia, 'The depth of this particular waterway varies and changes very suddenly and you cannot really anticipate that.'

Jordan's older brother, Justin, now plans to petition the city to seal off dangerous creeks and rivers to prevent future deaths. Funeral arrangements have yet to be made for Pete and Jordan Luciano.

Striker Danny Hylton got his side up and running with a 50th-minute opener after Argyle failed to effectively deal with full-back Stephen O'Donnell's cross from the right.
When the ball bounced out to Hylton, he hammered it home on the rise from close range.
Luton doubled their lead on 70 minutes when Jack Marriott raced on to an incisive through ball from Cameron McGeehan, outpaced marker Yann Songo'o and side-footed past advancing keeper Luke McCormick.
In stoppage time Hylton teed up substitute Jonathan Smith to stroke home Luton's third goal.
Both Hylton and McGeehan had been denied by McCormick in the opening 45 minutes.
In the opening minute of the second half Argyle's new skipper denied Marriott with a full-length save as the forward cut in and let fly with a rising angled drive from the left.
Luton's Dan Potts - booked after 71 minutes - was dismissed in the final minute following a second caution.

John Leathem made no plea or declaration and was remanded in custody at a private hearing at Dumbarton Sheriff Court.
Paige's body was found in a wooded area just off Great Western Road in Clydebank at lunchtime on Monday.
The teenager was last seen near a deli a week ago after setting off for her part-time hairdressing job.
The case against Mr Leathem was continued for further examination and he is expected to appear in court again next week.
Meanwhile, a memorial event arranged on social media for the teenager was due to be held at Whitecrook Park in Clydebank on Saturday evening.
Police said it would be "fitting tribute to Paige", but urged people not to let off Chinese lanterns because the park is in the flight path of Glasgow Airport.
It comes after the landmark Titan Crane in Clydebank was lit up in pink on Thursday evening in memory of Paige.
Provost Douglas McAllister said: "Our community is in shock at this tragedy and our thoughts and prayers are with Paige's family and friends.
"This is a small gesture of support for everyone who knew Paige."
Two fund-raising pages to help her family meet funeral costs and other expenses collected more than Â£14,000 a day after they were set up.

I bought this as a "deal of the day." Years ago, I purchased Seasons 1 & 2 for $35, but I could never justify spending $30 per season for the rest. When I saw this complete set for just $99, I had to buy it! I ordered it on a Thursday morning, opting for standard two-day shipping with my Amazon Prime membership. To my surprise, my doorbell rang Friday evening—my package had arrived! It shipped from Kansas, and I live in Texas, so that was fast enough to be considered overnight shipping. Sweet!

*Man, these are all one depressing story after another. I need to find a happier prompt. Let's hope what I write stays in the realm of fiction! * They arranged the chairs. The audience were on the edge of their seats, as the last *batch* of unknowing volunteers slumped off of the stage. In their minds, the trick hadn't worked. For the audience, they had just viewed the best square dance in the world. Joe watched the show with wide eyes. He was always interested in these kinds of shows, and never thought to try them himself. When the hypnotist called out for volunteers, Joe's hand shot up. The hypnotist smirked. He hid it from the audience, but he knew what was coming. A boy that eager must be as easy to mould as clay. He watched the young child stumble onto the stage and stand before him, eyes sparkling with delight. He was up on the stage, the *big stage*. His mother watched from the audience, gripping her seat. She didn't know what to do. That visit to the hypnotist all those years ago was the best thing that ever happened to her dear child, but now she felt as though the knot was coming undone. But it was already too late. Joe sat in the middle chair on the stage, dazed and completely gone. The audience sent waves of *oohs* and *aahs* as the boy performed circus tricks, imitated animals and tried to be a car. His mother was all the more tense, however. The hypnotist was ecstatic. This was his best show yet- the boy didn't run out of energy. He knelt down to try something more adventurous. He held the microphone up to Joe's mouth, and asked a very simple question. "Joe, are your parents in the audience tonight?" Joe replied with childlike innocence. "Of course they are, there's Mum-" he pointed at his mother in the audience, flashing a darling smile which brought the audience's hearts with it. "And there's Da-" he stopped dead. His finger still pointed at his own seat, where his Dad *should* have been, he turned to the hypnotist and stared. The hypnotist knew that he'd done something wrong, and it was time for Plan B. He asked for new volunteers and gently guided Joe off the stage. Eager individuals shot their hands up, like Joe had done, and the show moved on. Joe did not sit down. Instead, he stared at his seat, next to his mother, and simply stared. His mother, shaking, put out her hand. "Come on, Joe dear. Watch the show with mummy" she beckoned. "Mum-" he began. "Y..yes dear?" "Why did Daddy shoot himself?"

It had been many years since I'd visited Chili's, largely due to a prior bad experience. However, my mom invited me to join her today, and I was genuinely impressed with the updated menu and reasonable portions. I ordered the Alex Santa Fe burger, my mom had the BBQ ribs and brisket meal, and our friend chose the steak. All our dishes were cooked perfectly and delicious.

My four-star rating stems from needing to repeatedly ask our waiter for refills and the complete absence of any sweet potato options (fries, baked, or mash). The service itself could have been five stars if he had made the effort to check on our table more proactively, rather than us constantly having to flag him down. Despite these points, the visit was otherwise great.

Again, he shot his finger toward the fridge door. It swung open, and a beer floated gently across the living room into his hands, popping itself open along the way—he made sure of that. He tapped the can, doubling the alcohol content as he considered the effort it would take to fetch the next one. Outside, a loud *bang* echoed through the neighborhood. *Those kids need something better to do.* They must have gotten their hands on their mothers’ cookbooks again. A few carefully arranged words from an innocent chocolate ganache recipe could be twisted into a deep, brooding *boom* that filled the streets and drifted into neighbors’ homes.

But that was nothing compared to what crooks and criminals were using these days. Unlocking safes with a pinch of the fingers, conjuring invisible barriers around buildings with just a wave of the hand. The only thing that gave him comfort was knowing the authorities had the superior abilities. They could crack through the spells used by crooks and shut them down— and they kept the details under tight wraps. Sure, a few outliers here and there: some crooked cops passing handy spells to hungry criminals. But never anything truly groundbreaking. Even the dirty cops knew secrecy was crucial when it came to government spells.

Overseas, though, people had gotten hold of some profoundly frightening magic—the kind only trained professionals should wield. Yet on TV, there were images of lightning bolts shooting from kids’ fingers and fireballs roaring from old men’s mouths. Rebellion was brewing, and the countries involved probably needed it, he supposed. Maybe it was just part of every country’s growing pains. He wasn’t much of a history buff, but he figured becoming a developed nation meant fighting a few wars along the way. He took a long drink, finished his beer, and—with a casual snap of his fingers—called for another from the fridge. This time, though, the can glided right past him and smacked into the television as another *BANG* from the sidewalk distracted him.

Jolting upright, he pried the beer can—stuck almost magnetically—to the television and headed for the front door. *I’m really gonna let them have it this time,* he thought, flicking open the door. “YOU LITTLE SHITS BETTER FIND SOMETHING QUIETER TO DO OUT HERE SOON, OR I’M CALLING THE POLICE.” The kids scattered down the street, giggling mischievously as sparks flew from the back of one of their pants. He couldn’t help but smile and mutter to himself, “Smart-ass.”

The ocean stretched out before me an endless topaz gem. Sitting by the bow of my old skiff, sail fluttering listlessly over my shoulder, I dropped my line one last time. I watched as the bait slid slowly beneath the surface turning deeper shades of blue as it sank until all that remained was a thin filament of wire pointing down toward the ink black depths. I sat back against the hull and let my gaze soften over the vast expanse of water. My left hand began to stroke my beard with the slow, meditative compulsion of accumulated boredom. Each breath took in the salt and sun and weariness of my toil. Closing my eyes I pulled the rim of my hat down over my face and imagined myself thirty years younger, a man of wealth and influence. I awoke some time later as the sun was reaching the peak of its mid day heat. The line sat still as ripples of water lapped gently against the side of the boat. As I was about to reel in the disappointment of my efforts I began to feel strangely. Placing a hand on the splintered white paint of the hull I became aware that the boat was humming, vibrating silently beneath me. Looking up the cloudless sky was empty of the usual gulls and cormorants that circled in wait of an easy meal. Suddenly the ocean began to froth and boil building heaps of green foam that popped spraying salty mist into my face. The sound that came next was like a titanic brass horn erupting from beneath the water, shaking my bones until I lost balance and fell over cradling my ears like a child. The water continued to crash against my small vessel and soak my clothes and skin. The skiff shook so violently I feared it would capsize at any moment. Struggling to regain my footing I pulled at the rope to hoist the sail and as I was tying off I glanced over the bow and laid eyes on death itself. It's head came first, a giant grey dome of slick shimmering scales that dwarfed that of any whale I'd ever known. Then it's eyes, deep yellow and flecked with gold like a tiger, pupils so wide I could have sailed right through them into oblivion. But it was the teeth that broke me. The monster opened it's gaping maw to reveal row after row of sickle sharp fangs tall as a man curving back toward the recesses of it's immense gullet. It could have swallowed ten men in boats without notice. I grabbed the handle of the rudder and prayed for wind, prayed that the waves would carry me away from this hellish beast, but like so many prayers these went unanswered. The sail sat limp on the mast and the water served only to fill the hull and wet my shoes. I looked back to see that the beast gaining on me. As it neared my vessel I could smell the acrid stench of rotting flesh on its breath. It's tiger eyes darted wildly with the pleasure of the hunt. I let go of the rudder and cursed god. I cursed the sea. This boat and the wind. I cursed myself and my empty stomach. And as I was about to close my eyes a long ruddy brown tentacle speared forth from the water and shot into the air. The boat rocked and swayed at the tentacle came crashing down not on me but across the face of the beast. The beast bellowed a deafening blast of hot, putrid air and I thought that even in the midst of my confused and fear I would find time to be sick. I closed my eyes tight and held on for dear life to the sides of the hull. The terrible trashing and wailing beyond my small vessel sounded like the end of days, a cacophony of screaming demons clawing their way into my skull. I dared one glance and caught the last of the beast, wrapped in the crushing hold of a net of tentacles, being pulled below the surface. A moment later the sea was calm. The frothy green foam clinging to the hull began to spread out and disappear. As I released my shaking hands from their hold on the railing and stared out at the still stretch of water where the beast had been, I felt something slither past my leg and I recoiled with a yelp. In the water pooled below me, back striped blue and black, swam a fat chub mackerel.

Have you ever taken a bite of a nice, shiny, juicy red apple, only to discover that you were not alone in enjoying your snack?
Hopefully, what you saw was a whole worm, because you know what it would mean if you found only half!
The worm nesting inside that apple did not have to crawl inside it to make the apple its home, for the worm was born there. Apples are not only worms’ homes, but their supermarkets as well. Here’s how it all happens.
During the summer, small fruit flies can be found buzzing around apple orchards. These are called apple maggot flies. Each female finds herself a sweet smelling apple that is ripening and lands on it. Using a small, sharp, hollow tube on the underside of her body, the fly stabs a small hole in the fruit. Then she releases her eggs, which slide down that hollow tube into the apple.
Soon afterwards, the eggs hatch into tiny white worms, in no way resembling their mother. These worms are called railroad worms.
All during the summer and into the fall, the always hungry worms nourish themselves by munching tunnels in the apple. When the apples are ripe in the fall and drop from the tree, the worms crawl out and burrow into the ground.
There, a hard outer skin develops on each worm’s body. This hard skin becomes a winter home for the worm in the ground. It is inside this winter home that the worm becomes an apple maggot fly.
The following summer, the fly breaks open the skin, emerges, and begins to fly about the orchard.
Then the entire process begins all over again.

The neon sign above "The Velvet Cage" flickered, casting a lurid glow on the rain-slicked alley. Inside, the air hummed with a different kind of electricity. Not the kind that powers the stage lights, but the silent, charged current of anticipation and judgment.

I ran The Velvet Cage. Not a brothel, not exactly. More like a sanctuary for stories. The girls here weren't for sale; their narratives were. Patrons paid handsomely to hear tales spun from the threads of their own desires, their own fears, their own secret shames. And I, Elara, was the gatekeeper of those stories.

My predecessors, as the prompt alluded, had a different philosophy. "Ugly johns," they called them. Men whose inner landscapes were as scarred as their outer ones, men who craved a specific kind of validation, or perhaps, a specific kind of degradation. They were the big spenders, the loyal customers. And they were, in their own way, the most vulnerable.

But I saw the vulnerability differently. It wasn't about what they would *do* to the girls, with bouncers and cameras as a flimsy shield. It was about what they *took* from the girls, even in the absence of physical harm. The way their eyes lingered, not with lust, but with a desperate, consuming hunger. The way their words, carefully chosen to elicit a specific reaction, chipped away at the girls' carefully constructed composure.

My girls, the storytellers of The Velvet Cage, were artists. They wove tapestries of emotion, painted portraits of longing, and sculpted fantasies from the clay of human experience. And like any artist, they needed to feel safe, respected, and seen. Not as objects, but as creators.

So, I had a different kind of bouncer at the door, one more attuned to the subtle shifts in a person's aura than to their physical stature. My bouncer was a woman named Wren, whose eyes held the wisdom of a thousand unspoken truths. She didn't look for physical ugliness, but for the ugliness of spirit. The men who radiated a predatory energy, a sense of entitlement that transcended their wallet size – those were the ones she politely, firmly, turned away.

My friends in the business scoffed. "You're leaving money on the table, Elara," they'd say, swirling their expensive brandies. "These men… they're the ones who *need* the fantasy. You're denying them that."

But I wasn't denying them the fantasy. I was protecting the integrity of the art. When a man walked into The Velvet Cage, he wasn't just buying a story; he was entering into a sacred space, a space where vulnerability was shared, not exploited. And the stories, when told from a place of genuine connection, were far more potent, far more transformative, than any coerced performance could ever be.

The Velvet Cage might not have been the most lucrative establishment in the city. But it was, without a doubt, the most beautiful. And for me, Elara, that was a currency far more valuable than gold.

Glimpses Into Baseball History: Louis Santop
From SABR member Brian McKenna at Glimpses Into Baseball History, December 18, 2011:

The baseball player commonly known as Santop was actually named Loftin. This was his family name and the name he consistently used throughout his life, except when traveling with his baseball clubs, where he appeared as Santop on passenger manifests, for instance.

His baseball career began in Texas under the name Loftin and continued this way when he subsequently moved to Oklahoma clubs. The Loftin name even appears during his early days in the East. The reason for the name change to Santop, however, remains unknown.

Even his first name presents inconsistencies. The 1900 Census lists him as “Luray,” and the 1910 Census as “Louria.” Despite these variations, he was known for most of his life as Louis, or possibly Lewis. He identified himself as Louis on his passport application, yet further complicated the matter with two separate WWI registration cards: on one, he signed as “Louis Santop Loftin,” and on the other, as “Lewis S. Loftin.” Additionally, his grave marker identifies him as Lewis.

Read the full article here: http://baseballhistoryblog.com/3629/louis-santop/
This page was last updated December 20, 2011 at 11:26 am MST.

I watch as the reflection in the mirror moves as I do. I look into the familiar eyes that seem to ask "What do you see?" Only to respond with the same answer. "An idiot." I quietly say to myself aloud, thinking about moving from the mirror, yet I stand my ground. *What do you really see? * an inner voice asks me, and I contemplate my own question. "I see someone who looks strong, but is forever breaking on the inside. I see someone who lives each pointless day, pushing thoughts of dying to the back of their head. It would be so easy..." I respond to myself, throwing myself into my self-made pool of depression and self-hate. I walk out of the washroom at my workplace and sit at my desk, with a view from ten storeys high. I had finished all the work that needed to be done, so I watch as life goes on outside my window. I carefully watch the sunset in the background of the city, having a bout of false happiness that ended as soon as it started. When the short-lived joy is over, I realise where I am, who I am, and death seemed even more tempting. Sometimes I trick myself into thinking I see the dawn, but it's just the fires burning.

## Echoes of Industry: Historic Mill Pond Drained for "Eco-Tourism" Hub

**BARNSLEY, YORKSHIRE –** A tranquil corner of Barnsley, cherished for generations, is now a desolate expanse of mud and exposed bedrock after the historic Mill Pond, a local landmark since the Industrial Revolution, was completely drained this week. The drastic measure, ordered by the newly formed “Dearne Valley Eco-Alliance,” aims to pave the way for a “state-of-the-art nature observation center” and a network of elevated walkways.

For over two centuries, the Mill Pond, fed by the River Dearne, has been a vibrant ecosystem and a silent witness to Barnsley's industrial past. Originally constructed to power the bustling textile mills that once lined its banks, it evolved into a beloved community space. Anglers cast their lines for pike and carp, families picnicked on its shores, and birdwatchers flocked to observe the diverse waterfowl that called its waters home. Locals fondly recall childhood memories of ice skating on its frozen surface during harsh winters and the rhythmic hum of the nearby mill, a sound that has long since faded but whose legacy the pond preserved.

However, the Eco-Alliance, a consortium of private investors and local council representatives, argues that the pond, despite its charm, was "underperforming" its ecological potential. "Our vision is to transform this area into a truly sustainable and educational eco-tourism destination," explained Dr. Eleanor Vance, CEO of the Eco-Alliance, at a press conference yesterday. "The existing pond, while picturesque, was a largely static body of water. By draining and re-landscaping, we can introduce a dynamic wetland system, featuring diverse aquatic flora and fauna, and create unparalleled opportunities for visitors to connect with nature."

The decision has been met with widespread dismay and anger from residents. "Underperforming?" scoffed 78-year-old Arthur Jenkins, who has lived beside the pond his entire life. "It performed perfectly well as a home for kingfishers and otters, and a place for people to find a bit of peace. Now it's just a muddy hole. It looks like a bomb's gone off."

Concerns have also been raised about the fate of the pond's existing wildlife. While the Eco-Alliance insists that a "comprehensive relocation strategy" was implemented for fish and larger animals, many residents report seeing stranded fish and distressed waterfowl in the days following the drainage. "They just scooped them out with nets and dumped them further downstream," claimed local conservationist, Sarah Davies. "It was a chaotic and poorly executed operation, and I fear many creatures have been lost."

The proposed "nature observation center," featuring a multi-story glass structure and a café, is slated for completion within two years. While the Eco-Alliance promises a "reimagined and enhanced" natural experience, many in Barnsley fear that what was once a genuine, organic connection to nature has been sacrificed for a sanitized, commercially driven interpretation. The once-serene Mill Pond, a living testament to Barnsley's heritage, now lies barren, a stark reminder of the often-contentious balance between progress and preservation.

Taiwan’s Microelectronics Research and Innovation: Pioneering Sustainable Solutions

Taiwan stands at the heart of the global microelectronics industry, home to world-leading semiconductor foundries and a vibrant ecosystem of chip design, packaging, and testing companies. As demand for electronic devices surges, Taiwan’s microelectronics sector is intensifying efforts to address environmental challenges linked to energy consumption, water use, chemical waste, and carbon emissions. Research initiatives, industry collaborations, and innovative technologies are forging a new path toward a greener future.

**Reducing Energy Consumption**

Leading Taiwanese companies and academic research centers are focused on increasing energy efficiency throughout the semiconductor manufacturing process. Firms like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) and United Microelectronics Corporation (UMC) are implementing state-of-the-art energy management systems, integrating artificial intelligence (AI) to optimize cleanroom operations and dynamically adjust power use. Research is advancing low-power process nodes, such as advanced 3nm, which enable more energy-efficient chips for data centers, mobile devices, and electric vehicles.

**Water Recycling and Conservation**

Semiconductor fabrication is water-intensive, but Taiwan’s chronic water shortages have accelerated innovation in water recycling. The Industrial Technology Research Institute (ITRI) and industry partners are advancing closed-loop water systems, reclaiming and purifying up to 90% of water used in fabrication. TSMC’s Fab 15, for example, has been recognized for reusing over 200,000 metric tons of water per day. Research efforts target even higher recycling rates and the development of non-drinking water sources for industrial processes.

**Green Chemistry and Waste Reduction**

Taiwanese researchers are working to minimize hazardous chemicals and improve waste treatment. Novel green chemistries are being developed for wafer cleaning and etching, using less toxic alternatives. In collaboration with academic institutions, industry leaders invest in solvent recovery, zero-liquid discharge, and advanced membrane filtration. Additionally, efforts are underway to valorize waste streams; spent acids and solvents are being recycled or repurposed, reducing landfill contributions and environmental risks.

**Renewable Energy Investments**

Taiwan’s microelectronics sector is transitioning toward renewable energy through long-term power purchase agreements, on-site solar installations, and participation in the island’s wind energy projects. TSMC has committed to sourcing 100% renewable energy for its global operations by 2050, while UMC and others have set ambitious interim goals. Research focuses on integrating green energy into sensitive manufacturing environments and energy storage systems for load balancing.

**Circular Economy and Eco-Design**

From design through end-of-life, circular economy principles are increasingly influential. Taiwanese researchers are pioneering design-for-recycling approaches, creating chips and modules that facilitate recovery of rare metals and materials after use. Collaborative projects emphasize minimizing package sizes and using recyclable substrates. Electronics component makers are adopting international sustainability certifications, reinforcing Taiwan’s role as an eco-conscious supplier.

**Collaboration and International Leadership**

Taiwan’s government, academia, and industry together promote sustainability through consortia and public-private partnerships. The Ministry of Economic Affairs funds green R&D, while science parks roll out environmental innovation pilots. Taiwanese companies participate in global initiatives such as the Responsible Business Alliance and RE100, sharing best practices and setting benchmarks for the region.

**Conclusion**

By harnessing innovation and collaboration, Taiwan’s microelectronics industry is tackling the sector’s environmental footprint head-on. Advances in energy and water management, green chemistry, and eco-design highlight the island’s commitment to sustainable development. As semiconductors remain indispensable to modern technology, Taiwan is not only providing the world with powerful microchips, but also shaping a cleaner, more sustainable future for the electronics industry.

Research published in the November 19 issue of The Lancet finds that MRI use for breast cancer screening in women at high risk for the disease – due to family history or a genetic mutation – detects more cancers than screening mammography.
Research published in the November 19 issue of The Lancet finds that MRI use for breast cancer screening in women at high risk for the disease – due to family history or a genetic mutation – detects more cancers than screening mammography. The review article also reports that cancers detected by MRI are smaller and less likely to have lymph node involvement than those detected by mammography. These findings provide strong evidence that MRI can benefit this high-risk group. [Read PubMed abstract.]
However, despite MRI being better than mammography at detecting tumors, MRI did not lead to improved outcomes for most women with breast cancer who underwent imaging prior to selecting a treatment.
Memorial Sloan Kettering Breast Surgical Service Chief Monica Morrow, lead author of the article, writes that MRI has become widely used to evaluate newly diagnosed breast cancer patients. The expectation had been that MRI’s higher sensitivity for detecting tumors would benefit patients by more accurately identifying the extent of cancer in the breast.
This would presumably lead to better surgical choices for removing the cancer, reducing the number of operations required, and possibly lowering the risk of the disease recurring. However, a review of patient outcome data from multiple studies found no evidence of such benefits.
“Our study differs from others in that we specifically asked whether MRI findings changed these outcomes for patients,” Dr. Morrow says. “There is no doubt that MRI finds some cancer that is undetected with other imaging methods, but in most patients this has not been shown to help when choosing a type of surgery.”
Evidence suggests MRI is still beneficial for specific groups of breast cancer patients, such as those receiving chemotherapy before surgery and those who have cancer in the lymph nodes without an identifiable breast tumor.
Dr. Morrow adds that the true value of MRI use in breast cancer patients may ultimately lie in its ability to assess how well a specific case of breast cancer is responding to a particular drug treatment, although this needs to be validated by large studies.

Children today are the next generation of leaders, workers, and community members. Unfortunately, 10% of preschoolers in the United States—children ages 2 to 5—are carrying enough extra pounds on their small bodies to be classified as obese, with an additional 10% being overweight. It is especially concerning that kids who are only just beginning their lives are already at risk for serious health problems and face a high likelihood of remaining obese into adulthood. Childhood obesity has been the focus of numerous studies, and researchers are increasingly identifying the root of the problem: the home environment and parental influence.

A recent publication from Penn State University’s Perelman School of Medicine concluded that in order for obesity prevention to be effective and sustainable, it needs to begin in infancy and early childhood, before unhealthy patterns become ingrained. This finding aligns with the understanding that children are profoundly shaped by the environments in which they are raised. Regardless of improvements in the nutritional value of school lunches or the introduction of new exercise programs at local recreation centers, the daily habits and routines children learn at home are the strongest predictors of their long-term health outcomes. To ensure children have the best chance at avoiding obesity and related health issues, their home environments must consistently model and reinforce attitudes and behaviors that promote healthy living.

These findings place significant responsibility on parents and caregivers to help their children develop healthy habits. However, before parents can effectively guide their children, they need to address their own health behaviors. Children closely observe and imitate their parents’ actions, forming lifelong habits based on what they see at home. The idea of “do as I say, not as I do” is ineffective; lasting change occurs when parents lead by example. If parents want their children to be active, eat nutritious foods, and maintain a healthy weight, they must first adopt these behaviors themselves.

There is a common concern that healthy living is an expensive privilege, accessible only to families with higher incomes. Many parents, especially those who are tired and juggling multiple jobs, may struggle to find time for physical activity or to prepare fresh, high-quality meals, which often cost more than highly processed and inexpensive alternatives. However, research suggests that personal initiative and a willingness to make incremental changes are just as important as financial resources. While it may seem like a heavy burden for parents, the reality is that no school program or community initiative can replace the critical, day-to-day choices made at home.

Parents should reflect on their aspirations for their children’s futures by asking: What kind of life do I want for my children? The next question should be: Is my own lifestyle setting the right example for my children to follow? The Penn State study underscores that early childhood is the most effective time to teach healthy lifestyle habits, and that the home environment is the foundation for a child’s lifelong health. Ultimately, the health of our nation begins with the choices made by families every day.

## The Whispers of Distant Worlds: Unveiling Exoplanets Through Transit Spectroscopy

The universe is a vast tapestry, and for centuries, humanity could only gaze upon its distant stars, wondering if they too harbored orbiting worlds. Today, thanks to ingenious technological advancements, we are not only detecting exoplanets but are beginning to unveil their very atmospheres. While the "wobble method" (radial velocity) has been instrumental in discovering countless worlds, a different technique, **transit spectroscopy**, is now offering a tantalizing glimpse into their chemical compositions.

Imagine a distant star, millions of miles away, and a planet passing directly in front of it from our perspective. This event, known as a **transit**, causes a slight dip in the star's observed brightness. While the initial discovery of such a dip primarily tells us about the planet's size relative to its star and its orbital period, a deeper analysis during this fleeting moment can reveal far more.

Here's where transit spectroscopy comes into play. As the exoplanet passes in front of its host star, a tiny fraction of the star's light filters through the planet's atmosphere before reaching our telescopes. Different chemical elements and molecules in that atmosphere absorb specific wavelengths of light. By carefully analyzing the stellar light during the transit and comparing it to the light when the planet is *not* transiting, scientists can identify these absorption features.

Think of it like this: if you shine a white light through a colored glass, the glass absorbs certain colors, and only the remaining colors pass through. Similarly, an exoplanet's atmosphere "colors" the starlight that passes through it, leaving a unique spectral fingerprint.

For example, the presence of certain absorption lines might indicate water vapor, methane, or even oxygen – all crucial ingredients for life as we know it. The strength and shape of these absorption lines can also provide clues about the atmospheric temperature and pressure.

One of the most exciting recent applications of transit spectroscopy has been in the study of "hot Jupiters" – gas giants orbiting incredibly close to their stars. These planets, while unlikely to host life, offer excellent targets for atmospheric analysis due to their large size and frequent transits. However, the technique is continually being refined to probe the atmospheres of smaller, potentially habitable worlds.

The challenges are immense. The atmospheric signal is incredibly faint, often requiring powerful space telescopes like the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) and sophisticated data analysis techniques. Yet, the rewards are immeasurable. Each spectral fingerprint we decipher brings us closer to answering one of humanity's most profound questions: are we alone in the universe? Transit spectroscopy, by whispering the secrets of distant atmospheres, is helping us piece together that cosmic puzzle, one absorption line at a time.

After starting to slowly run the Bissell Carpet Cleaner up and down the carpet, I instantly noticed large clumps of wet carpet lint mixed with pet hair that the vacuum was leaving behind. When I inspected the underside of the vacuum, I noticed that the carpet scrubbers were kicking up so much lint that the entire underside of the vacuum was covered in it. That was from running the vaccuum over only 12 square feet of carpet! Even though I kept manually removing the lint every time that I changed out the water in the vaccuum, I ran into another problem. The collecting lint was getting saturated with the dirty carpet water, which would then drip back onto the carpet, making it dirty once again. No matter how frequently I cleaned the bottom of the Bissell, the carpets just could not get clean. Unless you own carpets that amazingly don't produce lint, I would not recommend this carpet cleaner.

Denpasar airport will stay closed until at least 16:00 (08:00 GMT), the transport ministry said.
Mount Raung in East Java has spewed ash for nearly a week, threatening aircraft safety.
Bali, a top tourist hotspot, has left many stranded.
The airport briefly reopened Saturday as ash cleared, but shifting winds brought it back.
“We’re monitoring the situation and will decide on a further closure later,” said ministry spokesman JA Barata.
Mount Raung is 120km (75 miles) from Denpasar.
East Java’s Blimbingsari and Notohadinegoro airports were also hit.
Lombok’s international and domestic airports reopened earlier Friday.
Hundreds of flights are indefinitely delayed or canceled.
Analysis: Chris Davies, Volcanic Ash Advisory Centre, Darwin, Australia
Mount Raung has been erupting for a week, sending out a steady ash plume.
It’s not explosive, but the high summit means even light ash disrupts flights.
Modern jet engines draw in large amounts of air—ash melts inside, creating molten glass that coats the engine and disrupts fuel flow. This can lead to engine shutdowns, as seen with BA 009.
BBC’s Alice Budisatrijo in Jakarta reports the closures hit at the worst time: school holidays and Ramadan have packed airports with tourists and families.
Flight disruptions are affecting both holiday-makers and those heading home for the end of Ramadan.

It was hard on people, at first, when the news came. "The President's dead", they all cried. It was even harder when it was heard that no, he's alive back in the White House, asleep. How? We watched him die right in front of us! At first he was declared some sort of demon, or a messiah. Mass panic ensued, many people tried to kill themselves in anticipation of a rapture. Instead, they all found themselves like the President, waking in their own bed. It took time for everyone to calm down, and eventually things... changed. Laws were made laxer, hospitals and hotels became points of'respawn ', as it came to be known from the ever popular video games. Ironically, this reduced murder rates, as people would remember their killers and report them, or get revenge. Thankfully, people still had a "Final Death", when it was just their time to die. Death by old age, as we used to call it. Joseph was an ordinary person, who was but a kid when this event occurred. Now, he had become a successful man, who ran his own business and everything. He, like most others who grew up at his time, didn't fear death. It was just a thing that could happen, you'd get better. So after his latest fatal accident, and awakening from that, Joseph went to make himself dinner. He'd get to dealing with the incident later. As he walked down the stairs, he looked into the kitchen. The oven was on. Next thing he knew, Joseph was awakening again, everything on fire. Rubble blocked the doorway, something fell atop him. Squish. Moments later, Joseph awoke again, in pain. That thing, a support beam from the ceiling, was going through him. Flames engulfed the dying man, as he screamed in pain. Again and again, for what felt like an eternity, he died and lived and died again, remembering every single moment of agony. Even as the firefighters rescued him, there was such pain. Even in the ambulance, he was almost thoughtlessly hurting. Later that day, after getting some rest in the hospital bed, the doctor and nurse to either side, he let out a whimper to them. There was still a terrible wound in his torso, and horrible burns all over. "Look who's finally awake! Now, try not to yell." Joseph felt something press to his temple. Bang. When he came to, he was fine. Shocked, scared, but physically fine. Sitting up, the doctor who administered his treatment came in. "There you are!" He said. "Aren't you glad we can just *respawn*?"

Surrounded by a bunch of Indian restaurants along Lexington Avenue, you would not imagine that a quality Ethiopian restaurant like Meskerem would be nestled in here. This is my 2nd favorite Ethiopian place in New York, just after Queen of Sheba in Hell’s Kitchen. The staff is genuinely friendly—they always greet you with a smile and are quick to offer recommendations if you’re new to Ethiopian cuisine. The restaurant is much more spacious than others I’ve been to in the city, with plenty of room between tables so you don’t feel cramped, even during busy dinner hours. 

I’ve always opted for the large combination platters to share, which come with a generous variety of dishes. For example, the meat sampler includes tibs (sautéed beef), doro wat (spicy stewed chicken with a boiled egg), and kitfo (minced beef), while the vegetarian platter features favorites like misir wat (spicy lentils), gomen (collard greens), atkilt (cabbage and carrots), and shiro (spiced chickpea stew), all artfully arranged atop tangy, spongy injera bread. I’ve never been disappointed with either the food or the service on any of my visits—this place consistently delivers a great dining experience.

This week, I’m attending Mises University, a premier, rigorous summer course in the Austrian School of economics—what my friend Michael McKay calls “reality economics.”

Of the many lectures, one resonated deeply with the work of the Acton Institute. Peter Klein, an economics professor at the University of Missouri and a leading scholar on entrepreneurship, presented on the subject.

Dr. Klein approaches entrepreneurship from the Austrian, and more realistic, perspective. He rejects the notion of *homo economicus*—people as robotic, quantitatively-driven machines—instead viewing human beings as unique, free actors. We act under conditions of time and uncertainty. Every human action presupposes cause and effect, yet there's no guarantee our instincts are correct or our efforts will pay off. In this way, each of us, in choosing any action, becomes a kind of entrepreneur. We face uncertainty, with an intended—but never guaranteed—result.

This insight combines with the Austrian’s profoundly realist take on production. Production isn't some abstract graphical function. It's the concrete act of transforming natural resources—wood, stone, metal ore—through one's labor. It’s almost an investment of oneself, physically shaping the world.

Broadly, we all engage in this two-sided entrepreneurial action: actively and consciously transforming our environment, and doing so despite uncertainty and imperfect knowledge.

More specifically, this applies to those we typically call entrepreneurs. Ludwig von Mises dubbed them “entrepreneur-promoters.” These are the familiar figures: the small-business owner, the investment banker, the risk-taker. Their entrepreneurial spirit surpasses most; they are the ones willing to confront greater risk, navigate deeper uncertainty, and make more difficult decisions.

This realistic portrayal of entrepreneurship, I find, aligns exceptionally well with the theology in *The Call of the Entrepreneur*. The film teaches us that the entrepreneur is a “co-creator.” They transform raw materials into products for consumers, investing their time and energy into the process. Creativity and imagination, critically, play an indispensable role in this co-creation.

A profound sense of awe dawned on me during Dr. Klein’s lecture. Here, then, is another example of how the market process, when correctly understood and employed, is not merely a morally indifferent outcome of choice, but a morally positive force. Society and its consumers thrive. Both laborer and entrepreneur are reminded of their human dignity as they participate in God’s ongoing work of fashioning the world.

When purchasing a refrigerator or freezer for clinical, research, or laboratory use, many overlook a critical feature: the type of defrost cycle the unit offers. This oversight can be costly, as storing temperature-sensitive samples—especially vaccines—in an inappropriate defrost cycle can lead to damage, wasting both time and money.

While freezers inherently form frost and ice, refrigerators are often perceived as units that maintain temperatures above freezing. This raises the question: why is a defrost cycle important for a refrigerator? The answer lies in its cooling mechanism. Although the refrigerator's interior may not drop below freezing, the evaporator tubes, coils, or plates responsible for cooling typically do. Without an effective defrost mechanism, frost and ice will inevitably build up on these components, significantly impacting interior cabinet temperatures.

For refrigerators, two primary defrost methods are available: cycle defrost and auto-defrost. The first, **Cycle Defrost**, leverages the compressor's natural on/off cycles. As the name suggests, defrosting occurs routinely as part of the unit's normal operation. This method minimizes additional temperature fluctuations, beyond what naturally occurs, thereby helping to maintain sample integrity and proving to be an effective approach for refrigerators.

In contrast, **Auto-Defrost** (also known as frost-free) refrigerators utilize a more accelerated process for frost and ice removal. This method employs a heater and a timer, typically set to engage 2-3 times a day (often every 8 hours). During an auto-defrost cycle, the compressor temporarily turns off while the heater activates to melt the frost. However, this quicker process often leads to a significant, albeit short-lived, spike in interior temperature, which can be detrimental to temperature-sensitive samples.

Moving on to freezers, two distinct defrost methods are also prevalent: **Auto-Defrost** (frost-free) and **Manual Defrost**. Similar to their refrigerator counterparts, auto-defrost freezers incorporate a timer and usually a heater, typically cycling 2-3 times within a 24-hour period. However, the specific designs of these auto-defrost units can vary, affecting both cycle duration and interior temperature. Crucially, some of these cycles can raise the internal temperature above 0°C, potentially damaging sensitive samples stored within the unit.

In contrast, **Manual Defrost** freezers demand more active user involvement. This method requires physically turning off or unplugging the unit, necessitating a temporary transfer of stored items to another freezer while the ice melts and the unit is cleaned. Despite the added effort, the significant advantage of manual defrost is the complete absence of the temperature spikes inherent in auto-defrost freezers. This makes manual defrost ideal for protecting medical and scientific products, particularly delicate biological samples like enzymes, from potential damage.

Understanding the nuances of defrost cycles is vital for protecting valuable samples. To learn more about defrost cycles and the range of laboratory and clinical refrigeration units LABRepCo offers, please contact our specialists at 1-800-521-0754 or visit www.labrepco.com.

Nuclear energy accounts for approximately 14 percent of the world’s electric power production. There are 437 operating nuclear power reactors worldwide, most of which use enriched uranium for fuel, 100 of which generate approximately 20 percent of America’s electrical power. Operating nuclear power reactors have a generating capacity of more than 374,000 megawatts of electric power.
Thirty nations rely on nuclear energy for a portion of their electricity supply. More than 10 countries derive at least a third of their electricity from nuclear power, and France gets more than three-quarters. In Belgium, Hungary, Slovakia, and Ukraine, nuclear provides at least 40 percent of the electricity.
There are more than 70 reactors under construction in 14 countries around the world including the United States, China, Russia, India, France, Finland and Korea. In the United States, the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission recently issued combined construction and operating licenses for two new reactors in Georgia and two new reactors in South Carolina.
Unlike fossil fuels, nuclear energy does not create potentially harmful greenhouse gases. If all the nuclear plants operating worldwide were replaced with fossil-fuel fired plants, as much as 500 million more tons of carbon dioxide would be emitted into the atmosphere each year. Using nuclear is the equivalent of taking about 60 million cars off the road.
Rapidly increasing global demand for electricity will make nuclear an increasingly important source of energy in coming years. For example, China expects its need for electricity to quadruple in the next 20 years. Meeting this demand without causing extensive air pollution will require expanded use of nuclear power.

*How did this happen?* I toy with the small plastic bag in my hand. The rain feels calming against my bare skin, washing away the blood from my countless wounds. The crate I am resting behind is hard against my battered body. My time is running out. Although I am immune, the number of people like me continues to dwindle every day. I can't remember the last time I saw a normal person since Mark. Painstakingly, I tuck my Sig Sauer P238 pistol into the leather shoulder holster I took from my last kill. *How many kills?* It must be near the high fifties. Each one weighs heavier on my soul, if such a thing even exists. I can hear footsteps approaching. Slowly, I open the tiny ziplock bag and pop the last pill into my mouth. "Please come out!" calls out an overly ecstatic voice. A woman. I close my eyes, take a deep breath, and move away from my hiding spot. "Hi! How are you? Please, don't shoot." I keep my hands raised and away from my sides. The woman is heavyset. She will be slow, but the sawn-off shotgun in her possession would make up for that at this distance. Tilting her head slightly, the woman smiles widely. "Welcome to the Other Side." The shotgun dips as she rests her hands at her sides. With as much speed as I can muster, I draw my pistol and shoot her in the head. A clean shot between her eyes. Adrenaline pumping through my body, I can feel the heat of serotonin rapidly releasing from my central nervous system. It feels good. "That was the last pill we had," whispers a tired voice. "I told you to run, Mark." I turn to face a young boy. His clothes are as dishevelled as his unruly hair. "I did. I came back." He eyes the dead woman. "What do we do now?" "We need to get out of here, then find a new supplier." "How long can you keep taking it? It must be taking a toll on your body?" I sigh. In response to the "happiness virus," scientists quickly found a way to disguise immune people by converting methylenedioxyphenethylamine to a more potent, instant-acting form. Unfortunately, synaptic damage was amplified considerably. My piercing headaches are due to dipping levels of cerebral spinal fluid, and eventually, my brain will be sitting directly on my skull. There is no worse pain than your brain—which normally floats in CSF—sitting on your skull. "I don't have a lot of time left. We need to find you a safe place." Mark smiles at me oddly. "What?" He begins to laugh uncontrollably. "Mark?" I cock my gun, unable to look at him.

**Product Review: MealPlan Pro Nutrition App**

★★☆☆☆ (2/5 stars)

As a registered dietitian with over eight years of experience, I downloaded MealPlan Pro hoping to find a comprehensive tool to recommend to my clients who are managing Type 2 diabetes. Unfortunately, this app fell short of my professional expectations.

The app does cover basic nutritional principles and includes a decent database of common foods with their carbohydrate counts. The interface is clean and user-friendly, which I can appreciate. However, nothing in this app was particularly innovative or went beyond what I already know and teach my clients regularly. The meal suggestions are fairly generic – think grilled chicken with steamed vegetables and brown rice – without much creativity or consideration for different cultural food preferences.

If you're someone who is just beginning to learn about nutrition and carb counting, this app might serve you adequately. The basic tracking features work as advertised, and the educational content covers fundamental concepts like portion control and reading nutrition labels.

However, if you're a diabetic patient who needs more sophisticated meal planning tools or someone dealing with multiple dietary restrictions, I'd recommend looking elsewhere. The app lacks advanced features like insulin-to-carb ratio calculations, doesn't account for the glycemic index of foods, and offers no customization for medication timing or individual blood sugar patterns. Additionally, there's no integration with glucose monitoring devices, which would be incredibly valuable for people actively managing their condition.

The recipe database is limited and tends to focus on bland, "diet-friendly" options that many of my clients find uninspiring. For people trying to make sustainable lifestyle changes, enjoying your food is crucial for long-term success.

For the $12.99 monthly subscription fee, I expected more comprehensive features and evidence-based recommendations. There are free resources available that provide similar basic information, and more specialized apps that offer better functionality for serious diabetes management.

While MealPlan Pro isn't terrible, it's simply not robust enough for healthcare professionals to confidently recommend, nor sophisticated enough for patients who need more than elementary guidance.
