While this work doesn't quite reach the heights of Money and London Fields, or even Amis's accomplished shorter pieces like Night Train and Other People, it remains a complex and challenging read. The narrative structure feels somewhat scattered, though within this apparent chaos lie brilliant examples of Amis's extraordinary prose craftsmanship. The characters' actions push the boundaries of believability, and by the novel's conclusion, their behavior becomes genuinely exhausting. Perhaps, however, this reader fatigue is precisely what Amis intended...

In my honest opinion, Queen Latifah should definitely leave singing to professionals: aka... someone who has years of study in jazz vocals or musical theatre. No direct attack on her personally, but I see no reason why her celebrity status should outweigh the vast talent that other unknown singers have over her when it comes to publishing a CD. When I listened to her album, I heard some effort and a bit of smoky richness in her tone, and I could almost picture the dim glow of stage lights and the faint scent of old velvet curtains. But despite the occasional warmth in her voice, I missed the goosebumps that true jazz or Broadway vocalists provoke—the tingling of my skin from a powerful belt, the textured rasp or smooth silkiness that gives you chills. Her singing didn't fill the air with that electric crackle or leave the lingering perfume of deep emotion that lingers after a great performance. I thought that she had a lot of soul in her singing, but she just doesn't compare to hardcore jazz/broadway style vocalists, whose voices wrap around you like a velvet glove and leave you breathless, craving more.

sgt. litman gave his nexus 12 a meaningful thump so the cold, brittle screen flickered awake, outlines of the city’s evacuation orders spider-webbing beneath the glass like frost. His gloves left smudges against the decades-old synthpolymer, and he tapped until mimic lights traced his pulse down his index finger.

“Wake up, old girl,” he muttered.

She responded with a gravel-static voice, “Good morning, Sergeant. The time—”

“Skip the pleasantries. How far to the checkpoint?”

A digital map peeled itself into view, flashing crimson where safe routes flickered and died. Southbridge tunnels—the only passage under the cordon—strobed at half capacity. He’d be pushing his luck.

He glanced skyward. Ash mottled the bruised sunrise. The city’s exhale, holding poisons no filter could outlast.

“Sergeant Litman,” Nexus said, “advisory: thirty percent chance of breaching patrols in tunnel sector four.”

“Some odds are worth playing.”

He shouldered his pack, the synthbox still humming against his ribcage. Somewhere, Mara and the kid waited for the ghost of a promise he’d made.

Litman cracked his neck, and the Nexus whispered a pulse through his palm: the warmth of old circuits, the memory of hope. Then he started walking.

on the glass, and looked the gene-tech straight in the eye. “I want triple-reinforced ligaments, subdermal plating, and a pain response dialed down to zero. Throw in thermoreactive camouflage, if you can swing it.” I grinned, showing off my titanium-capped incisors. “No gills. No rainbow hair.”

The tech’s hands trembled as she keyed in my order, her nervousness radiating through the sterile clinic. She glanced at the line behind me—kids, mostly, with daydreams painted across their faces and not an ounce of real grit in their bodies.

“Are you sure?” she mumbled. “The last guy who asked for that… well, he didn’t last long.”

I leaned in, low and quiet. “That’s because he wasn’t me.”

I’d been at the top for three years—longer than anyone in the Genecage circuit’s history. In this sport, you didn’t win by looking pretty. You won by being unbreakable, unpredictable, and utterly ruthless. The upstarts thought a set of claws or a glowing tattoo would make them legends. They didn’t know what it took to survive a ten-minute round with a berserker on synth-adrenaline and boneblades jutting from her forearms.

The tech finished the order, the geneprinter humming to life behind her. She held up the vial, shimmering with blue and silver. “This’ll hurt,” she warned.

“Pain’s for people with nothing to fight for,” I replied, rolling up my sleeve.

The injection burned like hellfire, but I didn’t flinch. The upgrades rippled through my body, muscles tightening, bones humming with new strength. I flexed my hands and felt the subtle shift as the subdermal plates settled beneath my skin, like a second skeleton. My field of vision flickered, a new layer overlaying heat signatures and movement.

I caught my reflection in the chrome counter. No gills. No colors. Just eyes that had seen the inside of a hundred cages and hands that had broken a thousand dreams.

As I turned to leave, a girl with neon-blue hair and delicate fin-like ears caught my arm. “You’re Ragnarok,” she whispered, awe and fear mingling in her voice. “Can you give me advice? For my first fight?”

I looked her over, saw the hope and ignorance swimming in her gaze. I almost told her to run, to save herself while she still could, but I remembered what it felt like to be new. To be hungry.

“Forget the gills,” I told her. “Forget the hair. Ask for a fast-healing factor, and nerves like steel. You’ll need them.”

She nodded, swallowing hard. I watched her step up to the counter, determination straightening her spine.

Maybe she’d make it. Maybe not. But as I walked out, the crowd parted for me, whispers trailing like smoke. They still didn’t know what it meant to be a champion.

But they would learn.

The city of 200,000 people is the capital of Helmand province, parts of which have been seized back by the militant group.
The new assault is their most concerted push yet into the city, in the south of the country.
At least 14 people were killed by a suicide bomber in Lashkar Gah on Monday. including 10 police officers.
The city was the scene of heavy fighting between the Taliban and Nato-led forces before their withdrawal in 2014.
Militant forces are said to have taken Bolan, an area close to the city centre, and are also said to control Nawa, which is often called the gateway to Lashkar Gah.
A local politician told the BBC that Taliban militants came within two kilometres of the governor's compound.
However, a provincial government spokesman denied that areas close to the city centre were being contested. Officials said fighters have been pushed back to the outskirts of the city.
Thousands of people fled Helmand in August as Taliban fighters increased their activity, with many taking shelter in Lashkar Gah.
Authorities have closed the airport as fighting continues.
Helmand province is both a fertile farming region and a centre for opium production, bordering Pakistan on one end and covering major routes between cities.
Lashkar Gah is seen as the key to holding the entire province, and would represent a major victory for the Taliban.
The BBC's South Asia correspondent, Jill McGivering, said the fall of the city would be a "symbolic disaster" for government forces and the international community.
"For the past fifteen years, Lashkar Gah has been held up by the west as a safe, protected seat of government - a focus for international development, a weapon in the battle for hearts and minds," she said.
"In terms of its propaganda value too, the government and its backers simply can't afford to lose it."

Ruth Madoff revealed in an interview with CBS’s 60 Minutes that she and her husband, Bernie Madoff, attempted to end their lives on Christmas Eve in 2008. “I don’t know whose idea it was, but we decided to kill ourselves because it was so horrendous, what was happening,” she explained.

At the time, Bernie Madoff was facing the repercussions of his massive financial fraud, for which he was later convicted and sentenced to 150 years in prison. Ruth described how the couple made a suicide pact that night, a decision she said deepened their overwhelming sense of depression.

“We took a bunch of pills—what we had in the house. He took more, I took less,” Ruth recounted, noting that she avoided alcohol out of concern she might become sick. The medications included Ambien, a sedative, and possibly Klonopin, used to treat anxiety and panic disorders.

“We took the pills and woke up the next day... It was very impulsive, and I am glad we woke up,” she reflected.

The full interview, airing on CBS on October 30, is expected to reveal further previously undisclosed details about the Madoff family’s life after the collapse of Bernie’s decades-long Ponzi scheme, which devastated thousands of investors.

Out of all the SAT books I have used—Princeton, Barrons, Kaplan—this one I believe I have benefited the most from. Nothing can compare to practicing with REAL actual SAT tests. I used a timer and took all the tests in the book a month before test date, and was very happy with my improved score. Sure, the other books might give you weird hints and somewhat helpful techniques, but this is the REAL thing. The only downside? The tests didn't have explanations to the answers.

Had I only been aware... Why didn't the agency alert me that amnesia could result from temporal displacement? Were they even informed? Nevertheless, I find myself in circumstances I wouldn't inflict on my greatest adversary. Samantha Wright: my mark. The person I must eliminate. The purpose for which I was transported back through all these decades. And here I stand. A lovesick fool. In the times ahead, Samantha bears a merciless dictator. The identity burned into my mind, Jaxon. This despot succeeded in throwing the planet into atomic warfare through lies and manipulation. I, alongside a vast community, survived the conflict by taking refuge in an enormous subterranean bunker. Within these walls, we've spent numerous years strategizing to rebuild existence as it once was. Our scheme: eliminate the mother of the man who destroyed civilization. Uncertain of Jaxon's birth timing, we chose to err on the side of caution. Through endless hours of speculation and analyzing data, we determined that Jaxon's mother delivered him twenty-five to twenty-eight years following my departure date. I was transported twenty-nine years backward. "Rico, what troubles you?" The sweet voice rang in my ears. "Lost in thought again?" During all this contemplation, I overlooked that I was meant to prepare dinner. "Forgive me, darling," I replied. "Just... there's much weighing on my mind currently." "I see." The gorgeous woman settled beside me. "My mind is heavy too." "Really?" I questioned—things have been so uncomplicated for us, what could occupy her thoughts? "Indeed, sweetheart," she responded. "I must discuss it with you as well, but first, shall we have our meal?" "Certainly." Nervousness twisted in my gut. "Let's." ... We positioned ourselves at the table, quieter than normal. The sole noise came from utensils striking plates as we consumed our meal: steamed greens, roasted pork, and lumpy potato mash. "So," my beloved announced, "this might not be ideal timing to mention, but..." I swallowed nervously. "What's wrong?" She pushed her food around, worry evident across her features. "Well..." Nothing could have prepared me for her next words. "I'm expecting." She announced suddenly. My utensil clattered to the floor. What? "So, will you respond?" she questioned. The moment seemed suspended. Amusing, a temporal voyager experiencing frozen time in history; the paradox. "I, I..." I stammered, struggling with speech. "Are you even... pleased about this?" she asked, focusing on her plate. I am. I grinned. She grinned back. I completely abandoned my assignment then. ... Here we remain, twilight at a garden. The gentle wind flowing through my beloved's locks. "You realize, I never imagined it would occur," I mentioned. "What?" she beamed. "That you would accept." "Well," she radiated, "we wouldn't want our baby to be illegitimate, would we?" She gazed at the sparkling band on her hand. It shimmered in the light. "Considered any names?" I inquired. "Yes," she responded. "Jaxon."

As I tightened my grip around the choking orphan's throat, I smiled knowing Heaven was awaiting me. "You did it," said the cult leader, and the group began a slow clap like in the movies. I was given the ceremonial robe, with the seven concentric hoods. And I was given a tiny gold chair figurine on a necklace, a symbol of my guaranteed place in heaven. Next day at work, the precinct was none the wiser. "This looks like a grim one,"said the chief."Orphan. Kidnapped around midnight last night. A hiker found him. No coroner's report yet, but between us---it's a strangling." "I'll take the case," I said. Grabbing my badge off the desk. The badge insignia showed the inside of a cop car, carved in gold, a rifle slung across the seat. I was a senior detective with the Colorado Springs police department. "Humphry's going with you,"said the chief. Hump's the new kid, heart of gold, hasn't seen enough to go bad yet."Listen up. He may not be Sherlock Holmes yet but his intentions are in the right place, sometimes I wish all of ours were." "Yeah, well, you know what they say about good intentions," I said. On the road now. Humphry was going on and on. "Hey buddy,"he said."I'm real happy chief put me on this orphan case with you. Why, I'd like to find whoever done it, and ring his neck. A helpless orphan!" I patted Humphry on the knee. "Now, now," I said. These young detectives really grated on my nerves. That's partly why I decided to join the Temple of the Seven Halos. "Oh god, I think I'm gonna..." said Humphry at the crime scene, losing his lunch. Yep, a dead orphan'll do that, every time. "Lookie what we have here,"I said, picking up a scarf off the ground, splattered with Humphry's puke."Killer left a scarf at the scene. Splattered with puke. The lab will analyze this barf and we'll have our killer in no time." "That's not funny,"Humphy said, then turned his attention to the scarf. He looked at the hidden part behind the neck."These initials are yours, buddy,"he said,"wait a minute, this is..." "That's right, Hump,"I said, grabbing him in a headlock. He was no match for a veteran officer like me."I don't like your type, Hump. All high-headed, solving crimes, talking about justice. They say the road to hell is paved with good intentions. Well I happen to be part of this little group, and we're tearing up that road."Humphry's eyes were bugging out, it was despicable the way he shamed his uniform."See, we go around with bad intentions, the worst of the worst. Poor Satan must be screaming his horns off, all the damage we're doing to his road!" I must have been right, because Satan gave Humphry a hand. Contorting his body like Inspector Gadget, he got gun out somehow. I can't even remember the gunshot. As I looked down at the blood in my hands, Humphry was freaking out. "Hang in there, buddy,"his voice all remote,"medics are choppering in." I just smiled. I knew where I was going. But I was wrong. "Gotcha," said Satan, pinning me on his pitchfork. "But, the road to hell..." I protested, flailing. "Funny how that works, Detective,"said the devil."Your road to hell was paved with the good intentions behind your bad intentions!"

I am my father's son. The only thing still keeping my rifle leveled at his heart is that fact. I stand second to the left in a line of eight men I have fought, bled, and nearly died with. I wonder if any of them are as unsettled as I am. If I had been told that this job would entail ending the life of the one person, other than my squad mates, that ever gave a damn about me, I probably would have given up and died a long time ago. Admiral David Abernathy, Director of Special Tactics and Training under the Department of Naval Intelligence, along with a majority of my chain of command, has been found guilty of treason and attempting to destabilize the nation. We, his warriors, are his firing squad. Trained from childhood, mostly by him, it seems cruel that Higher demand we be his executioners. "Ready." My heart skips a beat. It's unprofessional, I look sloppy; and worst of all, I know he noticed. His eyes lock on mine. At seven meters, I could hit his heart ten time out of ten with a blindfold and a slingshot, but I feel my hands shaking; my sights waiver across his chest. "Take aim." My hand trembles. This has never happened to me before. Fear is always prevalent, but my nerves have never faltered. Why now? I lower my muzzle. The call is interrupted. I sense the eyes of my brothers on my face. I feel the Admiral's gaze burn into me. As though he were not moments from eight rounds of 7.62 through the chest, he took purposeful strides towards me and stopped when his nose was nearly touching mine. "What are you doing?" his voice was ice. I don't answer. Lightning fast, just as they were in training, an open handed strike lands across the side of my head just above the ear. "Were you given an order?" "Yes, Sir." I answer. "I may have been relieved of command, but that changes nothing about you. Who you are, what defines the man you will be come, are your actions. And right now, you are derelict of duties and responsibilities tasked by your new superiors." He looked up and down the line of men he trained- no, raised- from boys. "You were all given orders. This is my last mission for you." The Admiral reached up and adjusted my cover that had been nocked askew from his strike. Something was rubbing against his scalp now, underneath the cover. After giving his and my uniform a quick inspection and adjusting his own cover, the Admiral went back to his spot and stood at attention. "Ready," I raised my weapon. "Aim," I look through my sights, steady on my father's heart. "Fire."

By . Associated Press . Israel has cut off Middle East peace talks and threatened to impose new sanctions against the Palestinians in response to a unity agreement between Hamas and Fatah. Israel's Security Cabinet made the decision during a marathon emergency meeting convened to discuss the new Palestinian deal. The rival Palestinian factions announced a reconciliation plan meant to end a seven-year rift on Wednesday. Israel objects to any participation in Palestinian politics by Hamas, an Islamic militant group sworn to Israel's destruction. The group has killed hundreds of Israelis in suicide bombings and other attacks over the past two decades. A Palestinian security official prevents bystanders from accessing the site of an Israeli airstrike on motorcycle in Beit Lahiya, in the northern Gaza Strip on Wednesday . Benjamin Netanyahu's office released a statement said the government would not hold negotiations . A statement from Benjamin Netanyahu's office said that the government would not hold negotiations with a government that 'leans on Hamas.' 'Instead of choosing peace, Abu Mazen made a pact with a murderous terrorist organization that calls for the destruction of Israel,' it said, using Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas' nickname. 'The alliance between Abu Mazen and Hamas was signed while Israel was making efforts to promote negotiations with the Palestinians. ... He who chooses Hamas' terror does not want peace.' The statement said Israel also will respond to Mr Abbas' recent decision to join 15 international conventions 'with a series of steps,' language that typically refers to financial sanctions against the Palestinians. Israel already halted transferring tax and customs money it collects on the Palestinians behalf, worth some $100 million a month. Those funds help keep Abbas' self-rule government afloat. Mr Abbas won assurances in recent Arab League meetings that Arab countries would pay $100 million to the Palestinian Authority if Israel freezes the monthly transfers. However, some of the Arab donor countries have in the past not met their aid commitments. Palestinians waving Palestinian flags as they gather to celebrate the newly made reconciliation deal between Fatah and Hamas, ending a seven-year split . Thursday's decision was the latest â€” and perhaps final â€” blow to the embattled peace process led by U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry for the past nine months. The negotiating period had been scheduled to expire next Tuesday. After nine months of fruitless talks, the sides had been meeting in recent weeks in hopes of extending the negotiations. Saeb Erekat, the chief Palestinian peace negotiator, said Palestinian reconciliation is an internal matter. "Israel had no right to interfere in this issue," he said. He condemned any possible Israeli sanctions as "piracy," saying the tax revenues are Palestinian money. Sorry we are not currently accepting comments on this article.

Dr. Nefarious looked upon the bodies of the League of Super Protectors and cackled, "At last! I've done it! The base is mine, it belongs to me, me!" The sound of jet boosters came from above, "*Us*, doctor. Belongs to *us. *"The Techromancer landed nearby."I'll remind you this was a joint venture." Nefarious grumbled, "Yes...It was a...worthwhile alliance."He stepped over a fallen steel beam."Speaking of alliance, where i-" The nearby wall exploded as The Unstoppable Thing 'entered' the room. "DID SOMEBODY CALL FOR THE. UNSTOPPABLE. THIIIIIII-" "Yes. We had mentio-" "HAVE YOU SEEN THE SHIIIIIIIT THEY GOT IN HERE?"He held up a what looked like an ordinary van,"IT MAY LOOK LIKE AN ORDINARY VAN, BUT INSIDE,"He tore the back door off and flung it onto a nearby wall,"INSIDE I- SHIT I GRABBED THE WRONG ONE. THIS IS JUST A VAN" He then carelessly tossed the van over his shoulder. Nefarious adjusted his black gloves, "Now, if you are done tearing apart the parking garage. We have some business to atten-" He was cut short when a mop Smacked him upside the head. "Do ya *know* how long it takes to mop these floors?" The doctor turned to see what hit him. A short, old man with snow white hair and a bushy mustache stood before him, hefting a mop over a shoulder. His eyes showed nothing but rage. Nefarious clutched the back of his head, "You'd *dare* strike *the* Dr. Nefari-" He was cut off again when the mop collided with the bridge of his nose." "Six. Fucking. Hours. So spotless you can see yourself in them. But now? Now?"Continuing to smack Nefarious, he shouted,"There's dirt, ruble, entire. Fucking. Walls everywhere. How am I supposed to clean this?" Techromancer rolled his eyes, "The sole Protectors of the city are dead and you're worried about the floors?" The man stopped his assault to address the question. "I take pride in me work. Cleaning this place is a something I enjoyed." Nefarious was clutching a bruised arm, screamed, "You'll find little to enjoy in life once I ge-"A mop handle to the kneecap sent him screeching to the floor."Unfair! Unfair!" Techromancer suppressed a smirk. Unstoppable Thing observed, "YOU GETTING YOUR ASS KICKED BY THIS JANITOR IS HILARIOUS. HIT HIM AGAIN." The man turned his attention to the hulking mass of muscle, "Oh you. Don't get me started on you."He walked over to the newly made 'doorway.'"You know how long it will take to fix this?" "THING THINKS IT WILL TAKE AT LEAST 200 MAN HOURS AND ABOUT 4 TRIPS TO HOME DEPOT." Bushy eyebrows raised in surprise, "Huh...that's actually a pretty decent estimate." "I FOLLOW DO IT YOURSELF FORUMS. THEY. ARE. INNNNTERESTING." He said, flexing. Nefarious rose shakily to his feet, "The repairs to this base will be of no concern of yours. You shall long for the day that you had the comfort of moping flo-"He ducked under a swinging mop."Ha! You think I would fall for that aga- AUGH!" The doctor dropped to the floor clutching his eyes from the windex. The man tucked the windex spray bottle back into his belt. "He talks a lot. You guys notice he talks a lot." Techromancer's lip turned upward slightly, "I have noticed something of a trend there, yes."

Salahar looked over his small camp, admiring the precision of his troops as they set up tents in rows, using a knotted rope to measure exact spacing. Fires went up with regular order, each watched by an armed guard. To his east the horse line was being set up, the sleek and powerful destriers barely handled by the pages and squires. In the center was his target, the large pavilion tent decorated in silver runes, every third rune glowing blue in the night. He made a brisk pace, acknowledging salutes and approbations with barely a grunt or not. Many of his troops, engrossed in their work, didn't even notice him pass. As he neared the pavilion, he saw the wizard's apprentice emerge from the tent flap, their eyes immediately locking. From the look on his face, the news was grim indeed. "My lord, perhaps...perhaps now is not the best time to intru-" Patience. Patience let him get that far before a mailed fist sent him to the ground. "I will not remind you again, worm." There was no anger in his voice, it was simply a statement of fact. Salahar entered the tent and immediately regretted it, the soothsayer's tent always smelled of cheese and sweet wine. Behind him, the dirty apprentice scrambled through the door, his hand wiping blood from his mouth. The wizard was turned away, looking deep into a crystal bowl of water. Salahar approached him, looking over his shoulder and into the clear liquid. The wizard's words were soft, "Watch. The darkness grows." Suddenly the clear waters began to shift, growing murky and gaining splashes of color. The haze finally coalesced into an image of Salahar leading his men into battle. Each of the neatly formed rows marched in perfect order, their pristine armor glistening in reflection. Each man holding his shield and spear at an exact angle. Behind them came the archers, their white cloaks flapping in the breeze. There was no sound, but Salahar could still hear the scream from the first of his men to drop to the ground, a pin cushion of black arrow shafts. The chaos enveloped his army, and those orderly ranks crashed into chaos, each man turning to strike his own shadow, only to fall to the man beside him. "What does it mean wizard, why do they strike at each other? They are obviously under attack, he was felled by arrows!"Salahar was confused and angry, his men would never behave with such cowardice."Gorn was right, I should have had you flayed in Herzonia." Salahar's anger was interrupted by a low chant from behind him, and he turned to see the apprentice standing tall and defiant, the hood of his robe thrown back, his eyes glowing with a strange purple hue. "Wizard, what is this treachery?" Salahar looked behind him to find the wizard on the ground, convulsing and frothing blood, a look of stark terror frozen no his face. The Apprentice's chant was growing louder, an inky black fog streaming from his outstretched fingertips, snaking its way under the tent and out into the night. A final tendril of fog made its way to Salahar, who pulled his sword and slashed at in futility. "Your sword will have no effect, *My lord. *" The sarcasm dripping from The Apprentice's words was palpable, and he sneered as he stepped closer to Salahar. "My *great* Lord Salahar, fear not. This battle is yours no longer. Day will fall, and once again we will all belong to the night." As the blackness enveloped him, Lord Salahar was lulled to his rest with the muffled screams of his troops, "To arms! To arms!"

The STEPS program is a comprehensive character- and values-building curriculum covering 40 different study topics for ages 4 to 12. The program is made up of two one-year curricula: the Foundations of Faith and Character Building series. Each series includes 20 Student Books, a program Handbook and a Sing Along audio CD, containing the songs included in the series. Numerous supplementary materials are also available to enhance the program.
Gulu orphans, grateful to be given these amazing stories.
The STEPS program is the result of the dedicated efforts of volunteer missionaries who, through working with officials, teachers, parents, caregivers and children in Sierra Leone and Uganda, identified the desperate need for a Christian and character building curriculum.
In the STEPS program, meaningful and interesting stories set the stage for reflection and group discussion on the study topic.
Large picture pages illustrate the lesson, providing great colouring opportunities and bring each story to life. Study aids and supplementary materials include activity pages, project guides, interactive games, drama, songs and more!
Celly Kamoi and Tina Kapp presenting former child soldiers at HEALS these awesome Character Building materials. Jolly of Invisible Children also pictured. For details or to obtain sets of this incredible Character Building Curriculum, please see thestepsprogram.org

The swing seat, a relic of sunnier days, groaned under my weight. Its rhythmic creak was the only constant in a world that had tilted violently off its axis. My wife, Elara, was in the kitchen, and the familiar prelude had begun. The clatter of a dropped spoon, a choked sob, then the full, raw scream that tore through the quiet evening.

My fingers, calloused from years of gardening, fumbled for my phone. Sarah, Elara’s sister, answered on the first ring, her voice already laced with a weary sympathy that felt both comforting and infuriating. “She’s… she’s started,” I managed, the words catching in my throat.

Soon, the station wagon with its tell-tale dented fender would pull up our driveway. Sarah would be first, her arms already open. Then came Maria, Elara’s oldest friend, her eyes always brimming with a silent understanding. And then Chloe, the newest addition to their circle, a young woman who had lost her own child years ago and somehow, impossibly, found the strength to offer solace.

I didn’t join them. I couldn’t. The living room, with its soft lamplight and the hushed murmur of female voices, became a sanctuary I was barred from. They would form a protective huddle around Elara, a human nest woven from empathy and shared grief. They would hold her, stroke her hair, and murmur reassurances that I, her husband, could no longer provide. They’d brew chamomile tea, its aroma a ghostly counterpoint to the lingering scent of our daughter’s lavender shampoo.

Sometimes, I’d hear a faint, almost melodic hum. Elara, in a rare moment of fragile calm, would be singing a lullaby, a ghost of a tune that had once soothed our little Lily to sleep. And then, inevitably, the photos would come out. Dog-eared, faded snapshots of a gap-toothed smile, a clumsy attempt at ballet, a sand-covered face grinning at the beach. They would talk, their voices a gentle current, flowing around Elara, carrying her, for a brief time, back to a place where Lily still existed.

I stayed on the swing seat, the cool night air a balm against my own unspoken anguish. The creak was my only companion, a steady, mournful rhythm. I watched the stars emerge, one by one, cold and distant, just like the hope that had once flickered so brightly in our lives. I was the silent sentinel, guarding the periphery of their grief, a man adrift in a sea of women, each one a lighthouse I couldn’t quite reach. And as the hours stretched on, I knew that tomorrow, the swing seat would be waiting, and the cycle would begin again.

The chipped enamel of the teacup felt cold against my lips. Outside, the perpetual London drizzle painted the windowpanes with a sheen of sorrow. My name is Elara, and I brew stories, not sermons. But the recent correspondence from Ignatius, my old university roommate, had stirred a forgotten tale, one that, like a persistent phantom limb, still throbbed with a peculiar ache.

He mentioned Edgar, and the image of that monstrous nose, a veritable topographical feature, rose unbidden from the depths of my memory. Ignatius was right; it dominated, a fleshy Everest in the landscape of his face. But what Ignatius, in his month-long monastic zeal, likely never knew, was the secret Edgar carried beneath that formidable proboscis.

Edgar was a perfumer, a chemist of scent, a veritable alchemist of olfaction. He worked in a cramped, cluttered laboratory in a forgotten alleyway off Carnaby Street, a place that smelled perpetually of civet, rose absolute, and something vaguely metallic, like old coins. His anger, I always suspected, wasn't born of shame, but of a profound, almost painful sensitivity. He could dissect a fragrance down to its molecular components with a single sniff, identifying the faint trace of jasmine in a seemingly pure sandalwood, or the metallic tang of ozone before a storm.

His nose, that grotesque, magnificent instrument, was his life's work. It was both his curse and his salvation. He couldn't wear commercially produced scents; they were too crude, too loud, an assault on his hyper-attuned senses. So he created his own.

I remember one evening, after a particularly brutal critique of a new mainstream fragrance, Edgar, his brow furrowed in a familiar scowl, presented me with a tiny, unlabeled vial. "Smell this," he grunted, his voice a gravelly whisper.

I uncorked it. The first note was rain on hot asphalt, then a hint of damp earth, followed by the subtle, almost imperceptible sweetness of petrichor. It was the scent of a summer storm breaking over a parched city. It was exquisite.

"What is it?" I asked, mesmerized.

He shrugged, a rare, almost shy gesture. "Just… what a storm smells like to me. Before the rain truly hits, when the air is thick with anticipation."

That was Edgar. The man with the nose that overshadowed all else, who could translate the fleeting poetry of the world into an invisible art. Ignatius saw an "ugly motherfucker." I saw a man whose perceived deformity was, in fact, the very vessel of his extraordinary gift. He was still the only person I ever knew with a nose that told stories more eloquently than any tongue. And in that, there was a strange, undeniable beauty.

Dad drove me this time. Mom had come along in past years, but now that I was 14 and well past the most critical phase of my neurological condition, there was no real need for an extended family presence. I'd go in, complete my 24-hour observation, and then head back for our usual 'all-clear' dinner. I even had my own room this year--a generous gift from grandma--so I wouldn't have to share with other patients my age. The neurologists said focused observation was easier that way, though no one was really sure if it made a significant difference. We pulled up to the Regional Developmental Clinic around 8 PM, and I was in my room by 8:30. Dad had given me a too-tight hug while his car idled in the circular driveway. They had removed the parking lots at these clinics in our state to discourage people from driving themselves. I couldn't remember if my parents had voted for or against it. "What if there's no one left to take you?" My mom had argued. But there were buses now. Repurposed Greyhounds that slid through the suburbs. "You will never have to take that bus," mom had promised, "As long as we're alive." The room was quiet and sparsely appointed. A patient room with a bed. There were magazines and books, all coated for easy cleaning, and a small television recessed into the wall. Another recessed panel allowed for video calls, but the only numbers programmed into mine were my parents'. You had to submit a list ahead of time if you wanted more than just your immediates.

I took a service van in, hoping for good service. I got a business card for Bob, but since last Saturday, I haven't been able to reach him. Every call goes to voicemail, promising a return call – if that's the standard of service, I'm not interested.

The pricing, dependent on the type of oil, also leaves me wanting. I'll keep looking.

While the service *was* friendly, Bob, perhaps try answering your phone.

Dear Matthew, I know its been a bit since I've written, a couple days, at least. I'm sorry for that, Ellie from down the street got ill and I've been looking after her while her mother goes down to the factories to work- maybe you've received some of her shipments? I don't know where the shells they're making down there are going, I'm not sure what I'd do if I did... Thomas came back the other day, it seems he was caught on the wrong end of a mine, but to hear him tell it, you'd think he'd gone down facing some kind of monster. He's been at his parents' house since he got here(and actually, I don't know if that's the only place he's been, he was just there when I stopped by to give Mrs. Meyers a hand loading all supplies she's collected for the effort into the truck they send around every few weeks). When I walked in, he took one look at me and said I was the most beautiful thing he'd ever clapped eyes on, and then he asked me to marry him. I'd think he was just having me on, but then he mentioned how last time he'd seen you, you were being carried off on a stretcher. He said if i kept waiting, I'd waste away to nothing because 'they took him off and he ain't comin' back from that, bless him.' He said I should move on after the war is over, settle down with him, raise kids, have a life. I don't want to do any of that, not with him. I know its impossible, I have the letter they sent your mother(she gave it to me, said she didn't want it in the house), and I read it more often than I should, 'Mrs. Anderson, We are sorry to inform you that your son, Matthew Anderson, of the(there are ink spots there, from where someone's marked out the next few words) has fallen in combat at(more ink spots). Yours- Sergeant Troy'. The ink spots, they always get me. I trace them with my finger, imagining the hand that held the pen, the tremor that might have caused the blot, the swift, almost brutal efficiency of a military scribe. Sometimes, I swear I can smell the stale paper, the dust of the office where such grim news is penned, the faint scent of fear that must cling to every word. I know I should accept Thomas's proposal and move on, find a way to keep myself preoccupied, but instead I can't stop writing these letters, and I'm not sure I'll be able to stop, because I love you so much and every time I put one of these in the letter box, I can't help but hope that you'll write me back, like this is some kind of joke someone's trying to pull...look at me, I'm crying like a baby. I do that a lot, but you shouldn't worry, I'll be able to pull through, I promise. I'll write to you again sometime soon. I love you more than all the stars in the sky, my darling. Pease come home. Don't leave me like this. Yours Forever, Elayne J Mason

Spiritual Interrogations: Culture, Gender, and Community in Early African American Women's Writing
Lieferbar innert 2 Wochen
BeschreibungThe late eighteenth century witnessed an influx of black women to the slave-trading ports of the American Northeast. The formation of an early African American community, bound together by shared experiences and spiritual values, owed much to these women's voices. The significance of their writings would be profound for all African Americans' sense of their own identity as a people.Katherine Clay Bassard's book is the first detailed account of pre-Emancipation writings from the period of 1760 to 1863, in light of a developing African American religious culture and emerging free black communities. Her study--which examines the relationship among race, culture, and community--focuses on four women: the poet Phillis Wheatley and poet and essayist Ann Plato, both Congregationalists; and the itinerant preacher Jarena Lee, and Shaker eldress Rebecca Cox Jackson, who, with Lee, had connections with African Methodism.Together, these women drew on what Bassard calls a "spirituals matrix," which transformed existing literary genres to accommodate the spiritual music and sacred rituals tied to the African diaspora. Bassard's important illumination of these writers resurrects their path-breaking work. They were cocreators, with all black women who followed, of African American intellectual life.
Ch. 1The Daughters' Arrival: Histories, Theories, Vernaculars10
Ch. 2Diaspora Subjectivity and Transatlantic Crossings: Phillis Wheatley's Poetics of Recovery28
Ch. 3"The Too Advent'rous Strain": Slavery, Conversion, and Poetic Empowerment in Phillis Wheatley's Elegies58

Caltech Unveils New Virtual Exhibit for Historical Earthquake Archives
The Documenting Earthquakes virtual exhibit is replete with historical earthquake information, archives, rare photographs, and personal accounts, from as early as the 1500s to modern times. With beautifully illustrated renderings and excerpts from rare-book collections, the site is presented in an easy-to-navigate, user-friendly format. Compiled from materials in the Caltech Archives' own collections, the six-part interactive exhibit will be presented serially, over the course of the next several months.
Part I, "Documenting the 1906 Quake," offers an in-depth look at the earthquake's social, political, scientific, and economic repercussions. Included are excerpts from the Earthquake Commission Report, which not only heralded new research findings, but also introduced the public to a significant seismic feature of the local landscape that, up to that time, had largely escaped notice: the fault then described as the "San Andreas Rift."
Part I also features a selection of early, rudimentary seismograms, along with many historical photographs graphically depicting the devastation in and around San Francisco, and a recap of California's efforts at postquake "spin" for the benefit of both residents and tourists. There is also a sampling of what Caltech senior archivist Shelley Erwin calls the "sensationalist literature" that appeared in the aftermath of the quake, chronicling the "San Francisco horror" in which, among other outrages, soldiers compelled even the "fashionably attired to assist in cleaning" streets.
Caltech's pioneering contributions to earthquake science are the subject of the exhibit's next two segments, scheduled to go live later this spring and titled "The Beginnings of Seismology at Caltech" and "Charles Richter and the Earthquake Magnitude Scale."
Components four and five will showcase the fine collection of rare, historical earthquake-related books and artwork, including a series of Japanese woodblock prints, that have been donated to the Archives by George Housner (Caltech PhD 1941), the Institute's Braun Professor of Engineering, Emeritus, and internationally acknowledged to be the father of modern earthquake engineering. The Housner collection contains one of the earliest printed books on earthquakes, published in 1531 in Germany by a successor of Gutenberg and purchased by Housner in Rome from a rare-book dealer. This segment will also present the Namazu-e woodblock prints, published following the 1855 Tokyo earthquake, that recount the intriguing Japanese folklore of the "giant catfish" believed to cause earthquakes.
The final section will highlight yet more artistic and historical commentary on earthquakes, volcanoes, and other geologic hazards, and will include images from Sir William Hamilton's Campi Phlegraei, published in the 1770s. This rare, one-of-a-kind book prepared for the Royal Society in London, was a gift to the Archives from the estate of Caltech professor of physics, emeritus, Earnest Watson.
Documenting Earthquakes is curated by Erwin, in collaboration with archivists Judith Goodstein, Kevin Knox, and Elisa Piccio. Design and production are by Wayne Waller and Leslie Maxfield of Caltech's Digital Media Services.

My name is Ignatius and I've been a Jesuit monk for over one whole month. But I didn't come here to tell *my* life story. When I met Edgar he was pretty fucked up. One of the ugliest motherfuckers I knew. He had this huge ass nose right in the middle of his face, I mean, it was so huge you couldn't look at anything else while in his presence. I never asked, but I'm almost certain it was one of the reasons he was always so angry and always frowning. He is still the only person I ever knew with wrinkles at 33. I was just a novice back then, when our monastery held a program for down on their luck men that wanted to turn their life around with Lord's help. Edgar was one of them. Turning his life around proved to be harder than expected. It always is, with men like that, but he was special. He was stubborn in his unhappiness and refusal to work with us to make things better. On some level the desire was there, otherwise he would not have searched for us, but some people don't really think things can get better. First, he told us he was still a virgin, but then we found out he only ever had sex with prostitutes. Normal woman wouldn't touch him. He was still living with his mother. Same minimum wage job for the last 13 years. He came to us for about half year before his friend, well, not friend per say, cause Edgar didn't had any friends back then, more like casual acquaintance really, he was driving them back to the city, from the monastery. I told him before leaving to make Edgar sit in the back, but alas! he didn't heed my advice. I don't really know the details here, but from what I heard he was driving, Edgar by his side, and while driving he kept turning and looking at his nose. He was new, not used to its magnanimity yet. They crashed in a heard of cows. He got out of it without a scratch, but poor Edgar lost the use of his feet. There were also many cow casualties. We, the ones that knew him, were pretty sure he would commit the gravest sin and take away his own life but he surprised us all by completely his life around. He got a nose job, start working out, eat better. That lead to his first sexual encounter with a girl he didn't pay! I'm torn here. On one had it's great for him, and I'm glad, but on the other he's sinning. But I'm sure the Lord would understand, for the Lord is merciful! He then quit his job and started his own clock repair business. He is world renowned now. He became a billionaire overnight. Edgar was also the first paraplegic to escalate Mount Everest. Told you he was working out. He truly is an inspiration. Many believe him to be the second Mother Teresa, but I know he is just a man that realized the value of life in the wake of terrible tragedy. ------ -015

Earthquakes Under Pacific Ocean Floor Reveal Unexpected Circulation System
News story originally written on January 11, 2008

The Earth has a large system of underwater mountain ranges along the ocean floor that play a big part in the geology of the planet. A team of seismologists (scientists who study earthquakes) has been studying a place called the East Pacific Rise, which is on the ocean floor about 565 miles southwest of Acapulco, Mexico. These scientists have made images of this area and have made some discoveries that change what they thought they knew about these underwater ridges and vents on the ocean floor.

In the late 1970s scientists discovered a large underwater plumbing system called hydrothermal vents. These systems pull in cold seawater, heat the water underground, and then shoot it back out from openings in the seafloor. This process brings up hot water and minerals that dissolved from rocks below the ocean floor. Some unusual life forms feed off this "mixture" of dissolved minerals and hot water.

Scientists used to think that pressure forced the water into the vents through large faults, or cracks in the ridge. Now scientists have learned that the water moves much faster in and out of the vents than they had thought (maybe a billion gallons per year!). The water goes down through a large opening that is buried under the sea floor. Then the water flows underneath the ridge through a pathway above a hot magma chamber. Finally the water bubbles back up through vents that are further down the ridge.

They have also learned that tiny earthquakes on the ridge are created by the cold water passing through hot rocks and absorbing their heat. This process causes the rocks to shrink and crack, creating small quakes. Then seawater is forced down into the new spaces made by the earthquakes. This water gets heated by the magma and rises back to the seafloor. Maya Tolstoy, a marine geologist, says this process is very similar to what happens in a pot of boiling water.

These findings could help scientists understand seafloor currents, how minerals flow through the ocean, and how heat and chemicals are cycled to the seafloor and the waters above.

A few matchsticks, some paperclips, and some aluminum foil allow you to make a miniature rocket that really flies! Transport a little rocket from a model globe to a model of the moon in the comfort of your room. (Note: Don't actually do this in the comfort of your room.)
Not everyone has the same idea of fun, but there is no question that certain things are agreed to be fun by everybody. Among these are fire, shiny things, and things that fly around. These three things come together in a tiny, shiny little rocket the size of a matchstick. Making a matchstick rocket takes only a few minutes, at least some of which should be used in finding a large empty space, without any flammable objects.
First take a roughly three-quarter-inch square piece of aluminum foil and wrap it around the head of a matchstick. Insert a pin between the foil and the stick and push it up towards the match head, creating a little tunnel. Next grab a paperclip and bend the outer segment out to make a triangular base. Pull the inner curve up so it is angled upwards like a ramp. Put a large segment of aluminum foil on the ground and set the paperclip launchpad on it. Carefully place the matchstick on the ramp. Grab another match, light it, and hold it under the aluminum foil covered head of the match-rocket. The rocket should zoom off and fly around.
The chemicals on the head of the match are the fuel for this rocket. When they start to burn, they give off fast-moving gas. The gas escapes through the tunnel created by the pin, shooting backwards. The backward movement 'pushes off' on the foil and shoots the matchstick forward. And what you have is a shiny, flaming thing that flies around the room. That always has to be fun.

**Asian Markets See Mixed Results Amid Economic Uncertainty**

Asian stock markets closed with varied performance on Tuesday as investors grappled with conflicting economic signals and ongoing concerns about global growth prospects.

Tokyo's Nikkei 225 index gained 0.8% to close at 28,456, buoyed by strong earnings reports from major technology companies and a weaker yen that boosted export-oriented stocks. Honda Motor and Sony Corporation led the gains, rising 2.3% and 1.9% respectively.

However, markets in China painted a different picture. The Shanghai Composite fell 1.2% to 3,089, while the Shenzhen Component dropped 0.9%. Chinese investors remained cautious following disappointing manufacturing data released Monday, which showed factory activity contracting for the third consecutive month.

Hong Kong's Hang Seng index managed modest gains of 0.4%, supported by financial sector stocks despite ongoing concerns about the territory's economic outlook. Banking giants HSBC and Standard Chartered both posted increases of over 1%.

South Korea's KOSPI declined 0.6% as semiconductor stocks faced pressure from weakening demand forecasts. Samsung Electronics fell 1.8%, while SK Hynix dropped 2.1%.

"We're seeing investors adopt a wait-and-see approach as they try to parse through mixed economic data," said Jennifer Liu, senior market strategist at Asia Pacific Securities. "The divergence in performance across the region reflects varying local economic conditions and policy responses."

Currency markets also showed volatility, with the Japanese yen weakening 0.3% against the dollar, while the Chinese yuan remained relatively stable.

Analysts expect continued market uncertainty as investors await key inflation data from several Asian economies later this week.

So I'm walking through a field and a scarecrow says to me, 'As a theist whose views beliefs are completely representative of all religious believers, I believe that atheism is untenable as a belief system not least because without the threat of hell, purgatory, or some other sort of supernatural naughty step, everyone would rape and murder each other.' This struck me as an unusual thing for a scarecrow to say apropos of nothing. It's an unusual thing for anyone to say, being as it's demonstrably false, but it's an especially unusual thing for a scarecrow to say unprovoked. But before I could respond, he was off again: 'As a libertarian whose views and beliefs are totally representative of all free market advocates, I hate the poor. They're poor and I hate them. I like free markets specifically because they keep poor people poor and rich scarecrows rich. Fuck the poor.' All right, so clearly this scarecrow has got something wrong with him. He can't actually believe this stuff, right? I mean, there's a certain amount of evidence to suggest that free markets make the poor significantly better off. But even if that weren't the case, a bizarre hatred of the poor certainly isn't the motivation for all libertarians. What can he possibly be going on about? But before I can say anything, he's off once again: 'As a feminist whose views and beliefs are totally representative of all people who believe that the sexes should be treated equally, I believe that all men should be killed. I, like all feminists, hate men. I think we should kill them all. We should round them up, castrate them, then kill them. Then make them our slaves. The door-holding, bill-paying bastards. And this is what all feminists, we who are united under a banner of equality, think. Men are awful.' Frankly by this point I've had enough. I say to the scarecrow, 'What on Earth are you talking about? There's no way you believe any of this. It's all bollocks.' He says nothing. He just looks at me blankly. 'And another thing, right,' I say. 'All these groups you're talking about. They don't think these things at all. You're wilfully misrepresenting them.' And the scarecrow says, 'Yes. I am a straw man.'

**Title: The Last Lightkeeper**

The storm was a living thing, roaring and lashing at the old lighthouse as if it had some ancient grudge to settle. Captain Elsie Harker stood in the lantern room, her oilskin coat snapped tight against the wind, her eyes fixed on the wild black sea below. 

The radio crackled with static, the last message from the Coastguard still echoing in her mind: “Evacuate. Repeat, evacuate. No vessel can reach you until dawn.” But the lighthouse was not just a building to Captain Harker. It was her charge, her ship if ever there was one, and she had learned from her father—an old naval man himself—that you did not leave your post until every soul was safe.

Tonight, there were three of them: herself, young apprentice Jamie, and Old Mrs. Finch, the keeper’s widow who’d refused to evacuate. The storm had come sooner than expected, tossing the supply boat back to port and leaving them stranded. 

Elsie checked the fuel for the great lamp. Enough for another night, if she was careful. She made her rounds, making sure Jamie was calm, tending to Mrs. Finch’s needs, and bolting every door and window. The sea battered the rocks, and the spray leapt as high as the lantern itself. The sound was thunderous, but inside, the light burned steady.

As midnight approached, the storm reached its height. The granite tower shook, and Elsie wondered—just for a heartbeat—if the old lighthouse would stand. Then came the shriek from below: Jamie, calling out. Water was flooding the ground floor.

Elsie rushed down the winding stairs, heart pounding. Jamie was ankle-deep, struggling to close the heavy iron hatch. Together, they heaved it shut, sealing out the surging water. They climbed back to the lantern room, the safest place in the tower.

Mrs. Finch pressed her hand to the glass, watching the wild waves. “My Albert would be proud,” she whispered. “You keep the light burning.”

Elsie nodded, feeling the weight of every lighthouse keeper, every captain who’d ever stood watch through a storm. She kept the lamp burning, refueled it when it sputtered, and comforted her two charges as the tempest howled.

Dawn broke, gray and pale, and the storm finally relented. A rescue boat was spotted; the radio crackled to life. Elsie checked everything once more, then led Jamie and Mrs. Finch down the battered stairs. Only when they were safely aboard the rescue boat did Elsie look back at the lighthouse, battered but unbroken, its light still shining for any who might need it.

She understood, now, the old tradition. It wasn’t about sinking or sacrifice, not truly. It was about duty. You stayed until all were safe; you kept the light burning, through the longest night, until the storm had passed.

And so, as the boat carried them away, Captain Elsie Harker promised herself: as long as she could stand the watch, the light would never go out.

A Video Series from the Department of History
When Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria was assassinated by Serbian nationalist Gavrilo Princip on June 28, 1914, it set off a chain of events that became one of the deadliest combats in human history, known as the First World War. To mark the centennial of the start of this war, York University's Department of History has produced a documentary series, entitled The War to End All Wars: A Look Back at World War I .
Comprised of six English-language episodes and one French-language episode, the series includes 14 of York's History professors discussing various events of the war, including: The World at War, Canada at War, Women at War, Empires at War, Technologies at War, The Spoils of War and Les Canadiens français et la Première Guerre mondiale. "The series of videos in English and French, offers an opportunity to better understand the impact that the First World War had on Canadians and the world," said Marcel Martel, Chair of the Department of History.

catostomus commersonii (pictured) identifying characteristics: (native fish) both the white belly and the darker, often olive-brown to blackish dorsal side are typical of Catostomus commersonii, commonly known as the White Sucker. This widespread freshwater fish, found across North America, is a classic bottom-feeder with several distinct features making it relatively easy to identify. One of its most prominent characteristics is its subterminal, fleshy mouth, adapted for suction feeding on detritus, small invertebrates, and plant matter from the substrate. Unlike many other native fish species, its mouth is located on the underside of its head and lacks teeth on the jaws, though it possesses pharyngeal teeth. The body is cylindrical and elongated, typically ranging from 12 to 20 inches in length, though larger specimens are not uncommon. Its scales are relatively large and cycloid, feeling smooth to the touch. The dorsal fin is single and typically contains 10-13 rays, while the caudal fin is distinctly forked. Young suckers often exhibit blotchy dark spots along their sides, which tend to fade as they mature. The overall coloration can vary depending on habitat, but the general pattern of a lighter ventral surface and darker dorsal surface persists. Understanding these characteristics is crucial for anglers and conservationists alike in distinguishing this ecologically important native species from others in its diverse aquatic ecosystems.

*Silence is key*, he thinks to himself, as he stares onward, *they must not hear me*. He gazed intently as the family van pulled in the driveway from what was surely soccer practice. *Soccer practice usually ends around this time*. Sure enough, the two little boys run out in soccer uniforms, kicking along a soccer ball as their older sister gets out of the car, with her hands dug in her pockets and headphones on. The mother is the last to come out and walks to the front of the house slowly. He sighs and leans back against his branch. He had been watching them for a week. *Nothing interesting. Never. All people are all the same. They never have anything interesting going on...* He climbs down the tree and steps onto the forest floor, retreating to the far away trees. *Another week. another family, another disappointment. * He walked away into depths of the forest until he melted in with the darkness- a lonely shadow seeking someone. That's all he ever wanted, just someone to be with. Someone to understand him.
